CuratorialText:
In the early 1940s, decades into his artistic career, Henri Matisse (1869–1954) turned his focus to a new medium: cut paper. 

He had become bedridden and unable to paint following an excruciating abdominal surgery, and cut paper allowed him to continue to create in his relatively sedentary state. Encouraged by his friend, the book publisher Tériade (the pseudonym of Stratis Eleftheriades), Matisse furthered his exploration of this new technique. He mined his memories of Parisian music halls, the circus, trips to Tahiti, folktales, and mythology, and produced a series of 20 cut-paper maquettes.
persona3_adapted:
In the early 1940s, after decades of working as an artist, Henri Matisse (1869–1954) began focusing on a new type of art: cut paper.

Following a painful abdominal surgery that left him confined to bed and unable to paint, Matisse turned to cutting shapes out of colored paper as a way to keep making art while staying relatively still. Encouraged by his friend Tériade (the pen name of Stratis Eleftheriades), a book publisher, Matisse pushed further into this new approach. He drew on memories of Parisian music halls, the circus, trips to Tahiti, folktales, and mythology, and created a series of 20 cut-paper models, or maquettes, which served as designs for a book.

CuratorialText: These vibrant original compositions were then faithfully reproduced using pochoir (stencil) and combined with an original text written by Matisse to form an unbound book, Jazz, that was published in 1947—when Matisse was 77 years old. Jazz caused an immediate international sensation and reinvigorated the aging artist, setting him on a new course of artistic discovery.
persona3_adapted: These lively, original designs were carefully recreated using pochoir—a technique that involves applying color through stencils—and paired with an original text written by Matisse to create an unbound book titled *Jazz*, published in 1947, when Matisse was 77 years old. *Jazz* caused an immediate stir around the world and revitalized the aging artist, setting him on a new path of creative exploration.

CuratorialText: Matisse’s extraordinary images of Jazz are not only captivating and provocative in their own right but also reflect a practice devoted to the exploration of color and line. Throughout the artist’s decades-long career, he pushed color from the purely visual to the nearly transcendental and progressively simplified his line to the most elemental. But until his cut-outs, his color and line existed separately, one modifying the other but never fully unified—at least not to Matisse’s own satisfaction.
persona3_adapted: Matisse’s remarkable images in *Jazz* are not only striking and thought-provoking in their own right but also reveal a lifelong commitment to experimenting with color and line. Over the course of his decades-long career, he moved color beyond what is simply seen toward something almost spiritual, while gradually reducing his line work to its most basic form. However, until he began making his cut-outs, color and line remained separate in his art—each influencing the other but never fully coming together, at least not in a way that satisfied Matisse himself.

CuratorialText: This exhibition marks the first time Matisse’s Jazz will be shown in its entirety at the Art Institute; the museum acquired it in 1948. One of the most important livres d’artiste (artist books) of the 20th century, Jazz joins over 50 works from the museum’s renowned collection of paintings, sculpture, drawings, prints, illustrated books, and textiles to showcase the famed artist’s commitment across his 50-year career to continual innovation and the expressive power of color and line.
persona3_adapted: This exhibition marks the first time Matisse’s *Jazz* will be shown in its entirety at the Art Institute; the museum acquired it in 1948. One of the most important artist books (a type of book created as a work of art, often with original prints) of the 20th century, *Jazz* joins over 50 works from the museum’s renowned collection of paintings, sculpture, drawings, prints, illustrated books, and textiles. Together, they highlight the famed artist’s lifelong commitment—over a 50-year career—to constant innovation and the expressive power of color and line.

CuratorialText: From 6th-century gilt bronze Buddhist sculpture through Joseon dynasty painting and white porcelain and contemporary paintings of the late 20th century, the works of art in this exhibition demonstrate the artistic legacy produced on the Korean peninsula over millennia.
persona3_adapted: From 6th-century Buddhist sculptures covered in gold leaf, to paintings and pure white porcelain from the Joseon dynasty, to artworks from the late 20th century, the pieces in this exhibition reveal the artistic traditions that have flourished on the Korean peninsula for over a thousand years.

CuratorialText: Once privately held, these artworks now belong to the Korean people thanks to a single groundbreaking gift from the family of Lee Kun-Hee, late chairman of Samsung Group. In 2021 the family donated over 23,000 works to the Korean government for public audiences to study and appreciate in perpetuity. Lee Kun-Hee and his father, Lee Byung-Chull, collected exceptional and storied objects from throughout Korean history as a means of preserving and celebrating the nation’s cultural heritage. This exhibition marks the first time the works are on public display.
persona3_adapted: Once held in a private collection, these artworks now belong to the people of Korea, thanks to a single, extraordinary donation from the family of Lee Kun-Hee, the late chairman of Samsung Group. In 2021, the family gave over 23,000 works to the Korean government so that the public could study and appreciate them forever. Lee Kun-Hee and his father, Lee Byung-Chull, collected exceptional and historically significant objects from across Korean history, as a way to preserve and celebrate the nation’s cultural heritage. This exhibition marks the first time these works have been shown to the public.

CuratorialText: Among the 140 artworks on view—including modern and historical painting, ceramics, and objects made for Buddhist worship and scholarly study—22 are officially recognized as National Treasures or Treasures by the Korean government. These objects are recognized as remarkable examples of their type and distinguished for their exceptional historic, artistic, and academic value.
persona3_adapted: Of the 140 artworks displayed—spanning modern and historical paintings, ceramics, and objects created for Buddhist rituals and scholarly pursuits—22 are officially designated as National Treasures or Treasures by the Korean government. These pieces are acknowledged as outstanding examples of their kind and are noted for their exceptional historical, artistic, and scholarly significance.

CuratorialText: The largest Art Institute exhibition devoted to Korean art in four decades, Korean National Treasures: 2,000 Years of Art charts the ideas, values, and traditions that have shaped the country’s creative production, from the Three Kingdoms period (57 BCE–676 CE) through the 1900s, offering insights into these objects’ rich meanings across centuries.
persona3_adapted: The largest exhibition of Korean art at the Art Institute in forty years, *Korean National Treasures: 2,000 Years of Art*, traces the ideas, values, and traditions that have shaped the country’s artistic output—from the Three Kingdoms period (57 BCE–676 CE) through the 1900s. It offers a window into the deep and evolving meanings these objects have carried over the centuries.

CuratorialText: Epic and intricate, monumental and meticulous—the paintings of Kashmir-raised, London-based artist Raqib Shaw offer fantastical meditations on identity, transformation, and the redemptive power of beauty.
persona3_adapted: Epic and intricate, monumental and meticulous—the paintings of Kashmir-raised, London-based artist Raqib Shaw offer fantastical reflections on identity, change, and the healing power of beauty.

CuratorialText: Born in Calcutta and raised in the verdant Himalayan mountains of Kashmir, Shaw draws deeply on the landscapes and memories of his early life, many of which were fractured by political upheaval. Forced to leave Kashmir as a teenager, he relocated first to New Delhi and later to London, where he studied at Central Saint Martins College of Art and Design. His work is influenced by a broad range of sources—Mughal and Persian miniatures, Renaissance altarpieces, Old Masters paintings, Japanese arts of the Momoyama period, Kashmiri and Urdu poetry, and Hindu and Western mythology—and yet his visual vocabulary and technique are all his own. His signature method involves applying an acrylic liner on gesso to create a golden line almost like the leading of a stained glass window. He then applies automobile enamel paints with needle-fine syringes and manipulates those with a porcupine quill, often adding glittering inset stones and baubles that further enhance the magical and dreamlike quality of the depicted scenes.
persona3_adapted: Born in Calcutta and raised in the lush Himalayan mountains of Kashmir, Shaw draws deeply on the landscapes and memories of his early life—many of which were disrupted by political turmoil. Forced to leave Kashmir as a teenager, he moved first to New Delhi and later to London, where he studied at Central Saint Martins College of Art and Design. His work is influenced by a wide range of sources: miniature paintings from the Mughal and Persian empires, religious panel paintings from the Renaissance, works by European masters from earlier centuries, Japanese art from the Momoyama period, poetry from Kashmir and Urdu traditions, and myths from both Hindu and Western cultures. Yet his visual style and techniques are entirely his own. His signature method involves applying an acrylic liner onto a prepared surface of gesso (a white, chalky primer) to create a golden line, similar to the metal framework in a stained glass window. He then applies automobile enamel paints using syringes with needle-fine tips, manipulating the paint with a porcupine quill. He often adds glittering inset stones and small ornaments, which further enhance the magical, dreamlike quality of the scenes he depicts.

CuratorialText: At the Art Institute, Shaw debuts his more-than 100-feet-wide, 21-panel Paradise Lost (2009–25), the artist’s most ambitious and personal project to date. This magnificent allegorical painting takes viewers on a spellbinding journey, from the nocturnal solitude of the artist’s childhood in Kashmir to the frenzied daylight of the art world and the West to finally a fragile, renewed dawn. Each panel is dense with symbolism: mythical beasts, anthropomorphic hybrids, collapsing kingdoms, and natural beauty in various states of transformation. Throughout, the work is dotted with images of the artist, sometimes as a humanoid creature with different animal heads, at another time as a monkey looking with awe at the gleaming edifices and the wealth of the West, and sometimes unambiguously in full human form seated on a bed of saffron under a blossoming cherry tree, lost deep in his thoughts
persona3_adapted: At the Art Institute, Shaw presents *Paradise Lost* (2009–25), his most ambitious and personal project to date—a monumental painting made up of 21 panels, stretching over 100 feet wide. This richly symbolic work takes viewers on a captivating journey: from the quiet, nighttime isolation of the artist’s childhood in Kashmir, through the chaotic, brightly lit world of contemporary art and Western culture, and finally toward a fragile, hopeful new dawn. Each panel is filled with layered meaning: mythical creatures, human-animal hybrids, collapsing empires, and scenes of natural beauty caught in various stages of change. Throughout the work, the artist appears in different forms—sometimes as a human-like figure with the head of an animal, at other times as a monkey gazing in wonder at the gleaming buildings and wealth of the West, and occasionally in clear human form, seated on a bed of saffron beneath a blossoming cherry tree, lost in thought.

CuratorialText: The work is not a direct retelling of Milton’s 17th-century poem Paradise Lost, but rather a reflection on the many paradises lost across a lifetime: childhood innocence, creative freedom, mental tranquility, cultural belonging. “This is not just my story,” Shaw explained. “It is the story of each of us, and the story of our times.”
persona3_adapted: This work does not directly retell John Milton’s 17th-century poem *Paradise Lost*. Instead, it reflects on the many personal “paradises” we lose over a lifetime: the innocence of childhood, the freedom to create, peace of mind, and a sense of belonging to a culture. “This is not just my story,” Shaw explained. “It is the story of each of us, and the story of our times.”

CuratorialText: While Shaw has previously shown the first chapter of Paradise Lost, this exhibition marks the first time all four chapters will be displayed, showcasing the fullest extent yet of this epic masterpiece. “This painting was made not only to document a life,” Shaw shared, “but to offer something to those who come after, to offer a space of reflection for emotional recognition and perhaps for quiet rebellion against forgetfulness. I do hope that this work invites the viewer to slow down and to look carefully and to feel without haste.”
persona3_adapted: While Shaw has previously exhibited the first chapter of *Paradise Lost*, this is the first time all four chapters are shown together, revealing the most complete version yet of this ambitious, large-scale work. “This painting was made not only to document a life,” Shaw explained, “but to offer something to those who come after—a space for reflection, for emotional recognition, and perhaps for quiet rebellion against being forgotten. I hope it invites viewers to slow down, to look closely, and to feel without rushing.”

CuratorialText: Exquisitely crafted and shrouded in mystery, Saint-Porchaire ceramics are among the rarest treasures of the French Renaissance.
persona3_adapted: Exquisitely made and wrapped in mystery, Saint-Porchaire ceramics are among the rarest treasures of the French Renaissance.

CuratorialText: Only around 70 examples are known to exist worldwide, and each one dazzles and intrigues with its refined and delicate modeling and astonishingly intricate decoration. Created between about 1520 and 1570 and closely associated with the royal courts of François I and Henri II, these extraordinary works bear the emblems of power, prestige, and desire—including the interlaced cipher of Henri II and his famed mistress, Diane de Poitiers.
persona3_adapted: Only about 70 examples of these objects are known to exist worldwide. Each one impresses and fascinates viewers with its elegant, carefully crafted form and surprisingly detailed decoration. Made between roughly 1520 and 1570, these remarkable pieces are closely linked to the royal courts of King François I and King Henri II. They carry symbols of power, status, and longing—including the interwoven initials of Henri II and his famous mistress, Diane de Poitiers.

CuratorialText: Saint-Porchaire ceramics embody the sophistication and inventive spirit of Renaissance court culture. Fashioned from lead-glazed earthenware, their sculptural forms echo precious metalwork, while their ornamental patterns draw inspiration from textiles, bookbinding, and other luxury arts. Together, they express a unique and daring artistry, playful elegance, and refined taste at the height of French royal patronage.
persona3_adapted: Saint-Porchaire ceramics capture the sophistication and creative energy of Renaissance court life. Made from lead-glazed earthenware—a type of pottery coated with a shiny, glass-like finish—their sculpted shapes recall the look of fine metalwork. The decorative patterns, meanwhile, take inspiration from textiles, bookbinding, and other luxury crafts. Together, these elements reveal a bold and original artistry, a playful sense of elegance, and a refined aesthetic that flourished during the peak of French royal support for the arts.

CuratorialText: Beginning in March, the Art Institute will present an exceptional group of Saint-Porchaire ceramics from a private collection—most on public view for the first time since the late 1800s—in the Deering Family Galleries of Medieval and Renaissance Art, Arms, and Armor. These rare works will be shown alongside other notable examples of French Renaissance ceramics drawn from the Art Institute’s collection, including several recent acquisitions.
persona3_adapted: Beginning in March, the Art Institute will present an extraordinary group of Saint-Porchaire ceramics—a type of finely crafted French pottery from the 1500s—from a private collection. Most of these pieces have not been seen by the public since the late 1800s. They will be displayed in the Deering Family Galleries of Medieval and Renaissance Art, Arms, and Armor alongside other notable examples of French Renaissance pottery from the museum’s own collection, including several pieces acquired in recent years.

CuratorialText: Installed in the intimate James W. and Marilynn Alsdorf Gallery, this focused presentation invites close encounters with these remarkable jewel-like objects, while its placement within the broader context of the Deering Family Galleries highlights the rich dialogue between ceramics and the metalwork, textiles, and decorative arts that flourished during the Renaissance.
persona3_adapted: Installed in the intimate James W. and Marilynn Alsdorf Gallery, this focused presentation invites close encounters with these remarkable, jewel-like objects. Its placement within the broader context of the Deering Family Galleries highlights the rich dialogue between ceramics and the metalwork, textiles, and decorative arts that flourished during the Renaissance.

CuratorialText: Over a long and distinguished career, Japanese artist Hamanishi Katsunori (born 1949) has focused on making mezzotint prints, perhaps the most demanding of all print techniques.
persona3_adapted: Over a long and distinguished career, Japanese artist Hamanishi Katsunori (born 1949) has focused on making mezzotint prints—a printmaking technique widely considered the most challenging of all.

CuratorialText: Mezzotints are known for their dark and atmospheric appearance. This is because the starting point for any mezzotint is the creation of a roughened surface, which produces a solid black background when printed. To draw the image that emerges from this dark background, the artist uses a series of burnishers and scrapers; the deepest gouges print as white areas on the finished print.
persona3_adapted: Mezzotints are known for their dark and atmospheric look. This is because the artist begins by roughening the surface of the printing plate, so that when ink is applied and the plate is printed, the result is a solid black background. To create the image that appears to emerge from this darkness, the artist uses tools called burnishers and scrapers. The deepest scratches and cuts in the plate leave the paper white in the final print.

CuratorialText: This presentation includes earlier, smaller-format works done without color, as well as more recent larger work that boast many hues. Among the earlier monochromatic prints is Hamanishi’s 1997 Viva Chicago series, which focuses on the city’s beloved public monuments like the Picasso and Miró sculptures. As the artist recalled, “When I first came here, I was unprepared for the impact this city would leave on my mind, such a vivid impression etched deep in my memory… . This is my ode to Chicago.”
persona3_adapted: This selection includes earlier, smaller works made without color, alongside more recent, larger pieces that feature a wide range of colors. Among the earlier black-and-white prints is Hamanishi’s 1997 *Viva Chicago* series, which highlights the city’s cherished public monuments, such as the sculptures by Picasso and Miró. As the artist recalled, “When I first came here, I was unprepared for the impact this city would leave on my mind, such a vivid impression etched deep in my memory… . This is my ode to Chicago.”

CuratorialText: The exhibition also celebrates several important gifts to the museum. Hamanishi’s 2022 Four Seasons series—shown here for the first time—is a recent gift from the artist. Each work in the series is reminiscent of the panels of a folding screen and features seasonal floral imagery. Many works in the show come from the 2013 gift to the museum from the Ninion and Sheldon Landy Collection, which gave the Art Institute the largest collection of Hamanishi’s prints in the world.
persona3_adapted: The exhibition also highlights several important donations to the museum. Hamanishi’s 2022 Four Seasons series—displayed here for the first time—was recently donated by the artist himself. Each piece in the series resembles the panels of a folding screen and shows flowers associated with different seasons. Many of the works in the exhibition come from a 2013 donation by the Ninion and Sheldon Landy Collection, which gave the Art Institute the world’s largest collection of Hamanishi’s prints.

CuratorialText:
“There is no work by Carroll Dunham that does not use drawing as a foundation.”  
—Carroll Dunham. Over the course of five decades, Dunham has engaged in wide-ranging formal and thematic experimentation across various media, yet his drawings represent a distinct, interconnected body of work. Dunham’s mature artistic career began in 1970s New York amid a scene dominated by Minimalist aesthetics. Using simple elements such as line, shape, and color, he made a tremendous impact with work that nevertheless signaled a return to subject matter. In the intervening decades, Dunham has continued to produce drawings that test, and ultimately collapse, the porous boundaries between abstraction and figuration: bodies shapeshift into buildings, trees pose like models, the cellular resembles the cosmic, and internal organs or private parts are made very public.
persona3_adapted:
“There is no work by Carroll Dunham that does not use drawing as a foundation.”  
—Carroll Dunham. Over the course of five decades, Dunham has explored a wide range of styles, themes, and materials—yet his drawings form a distinct and connected body of work. His mature career began in 1970s New York, at a time when the art world was largely shaped by Minimalism, an approach that emphasized simplicity and stripped away obvious subject matter. Using basic elements like line, shape, and color, Dunham made a powerful impact with work that nonetheless signaled a return to recognizable subjects. Over the years, he has continued to create drawings that test—and ultimately break down—the blurry line between abstract and realistic imagery: bodies transform into buildings, trees strike poses like models, tiny cellular forms echo vast cosmic scenes, and internal organs or private body parts are made strikingly public.

CuratorialText: Working in series, Dunham methodically tests out every possible outcome of a drawing’s composition or content. This approach seems to spontaneously yield new imagery and ideas that link one body of work to the next. His influences are likewise wide-ranging—art history, philosophy, psychoanalysis, and evolutionary science as well as science fiction and comic books—all of which inform his explorations of the tension between male and female, nature and culture, self and other.
persona3_adapted: Working in a series of related pieces, Dunham methodically tests every possible variation of a drawing's arrangement or subject matter. This approach seems to naturally produce new images and ideas that connect one group of works to the next. His influences are similarly broad—art history, philosophy, psychoanalysis, and evolutionary science, as well as science fiction and comic books—all of which shape his explorations of the tension between male and female, nature and culture, and self and other.

CuratorialText: Dunham foregrounds the development of his work by precisely dating each drawing. This practice is evidenced in the thousands of drawings he has organized into an archive, from which this show is derived. Developed in conversation with the artist, this comprehensive survey is the first museum presentation focused exclusively on Dunham’s drawings. Encompassing a 50-year period and featuring many drawings making their public debut, it celebrates the medium central to Dunham’s expansive practice.
persona3_adapted: Dunham highlights the evolution of his work by carefully dating each drawing. This approach is visible in the thousands of drawings he has arranged into a personal collection, which serves as the source for this exhibition. Created in collaboration with the artist, this broad overview is the first museum show devoted entirely to Dunham’s drawings. Spanning 50 years and including many drawings shown publicly for the first time, it honors the artistic method at the heart of Dunham’s wide-ranging creative work.

CuratorialText: In fall of 2024, the Art Institute unveiled a newly imagined and installed gallery for the arts of Korea—our first space fully dedicated to this cultural region.
persona3_adapted: In fall of 2024, the Art Institute opened a newly designed and arranged gallery for the arts of Korea—our first space entirely devoted to this cultural region.

CuratorialText: The new installation presents a wider range of objects than previous displays—extending from ceramics to textiles and painting and spanning 2,000 years from the Three Kingdoms period (about 57 BCE–676 CE) until today. Intentionally located between the Chinese and Japanese art galleries, the thoughtful display offers insight into how these artworks both reflect the religious and material culture of Korea and how they relate to the arts of Korea’s influential neighbors, China and Japan.
persona3_adapted: This new installation includes a wider variety of objects than previous displays—ranging from ceramics to textiles and paintings, and covering 2,000 years from the Three Kingdoms period (roughly 57 BCE to 676 CE) up to the present day. Positioned deliberately between the Chinese and Japanese art galleries, the carefully arranged exhibition reveals how these artworks reflect Korea’s religious and material culture, while also showing their connections to the arts of Korea’s influential neighbors, China and Japan.

CuratorialText: Six themes introduce Korea’s long and rich history as well as its religious, political, and material cultures. The first section introduces objects that were made to help understand and spread the teachings of the Buddha. The centerpiece, an 18th-century Buddha statue, is on view for the first time since its acquisition after extensive conservation treatment. A section devoted to celadon and tea culture provides a glimpse into the practice of tea ceremonies during the Goryeo dynasty (918–1392), and objects grouped around the idea of symbolism illustrate how beautiful embellishments represent desired outcomes, such as a blissful marriage, career advancement, and long life.
persona3_adapted: Six themes introduce Korea’s long and rich history, as well as its religious, political, and material cultures. The first section presents objects created to help people understand and share the teachings of the Buddha. The centerpiece, an 18th-century statue of the Buddha, is on display for the first time since the museum acquired it, following extensive cleaning and repair work. A section focused on celadon—a type of pale green pottery—and tea culture offers a glimpse into the practice of tea ceremonies during the Goryeo dynasty (918–1392). Other objects, grouped around the idea of symbolism, show how beautiful decorations represent hopes for specific outcomes, such as a happy marriage, career success, and a long life.

CuratorialText: The remaining three sections focus on how material culture responded to a major ideological, political, and cultural shift during the Joseon dynasty (1392–1897) as evidenced in the creation of the earthy grey stoneware known as buncheong, elegant white porcelain, and scholars’ objects. The new installation also includes select contemporary works that meaningfully engage with traditional forms and materials.
persona3_adapted: The remaining three sections explore how everyday objects and artistic works reflected a major shift in ideas, politics, and culture during the Joseon dynasty (1392–1897). This change is especially visible in the creation of three types of items: buncheong—a type of stoneware with a gray, earthy tone; elegant white porcelain; and objects used by scholars. The new installation also includes a selection of contemporary works that thoughtfully connect with these traditional forms and materials.

CuratorialText: The transformed space explores aspects of life and the afterlife in the Nile Valley with the first new installation of works from the museum’s historic collection of ancient Egyptian art in a quarter-century. Striking artifacts—displayed along one wall of the gallery in a series of innovative cases that promote viewing from multiple vantage points—provide insight into the beliefs and practices of this illustrious North African culture.
persona3_adapted: The redesigned gallery examines themes of life and the afterlife in the Nile Valley, featuring the first new display of works from the museum’s historic collection of ancient Egyptian art in twenty-five years. Remarkable objects—shown along one wall in a series of innovative cases that allow visitors to view them from different angles—offer a window into the beliefs and customs of this celebrated North African civilization.

CuratorialText: Recurring themes throughout this fresh presentation of the collection consider the impact of Egypt’s natural environment, including the Nile River, on its visual culture; reveal the processes of ancient Egyptian artists; and explore the centrality of gods and goddesses to life (and death) along the Nile. Arresting sculptures, such as the statue of Shebenhor, reveal how ancient Egyptians chose to present themselves so that they would be remembered for eternity, while funerary works—such as a gilded funerary mask—unveil practices for preparing for and protecting oneself in the afterlife.
persona3_adapted: Throughout this newly arranged display of the collection, several recurring ideas come into focus: how Egypt’s natural surroundings—especially the Nile River—shaped the art and objects its people created; how ancient Egyptian artists actually made their works; and how central gods and goddesses were to both life and death along the Nile. Striking sculptures, like the statue of Shebenhor, show how ancient Egyptians chose to depict themselves so they would be remembered forever. Meanwhile, objects made for burial—such as a mask covered in gold leaf—reveal the rituals people followed to prepare for and protect themselves in the afterlife.

CuratorialText: For the first time in over 30 years, the Art Institute of Chicago unveils an elegant and thought-provoking new display of architectural fragments from buildings in and around Chicago. Since the 19th century, Chicago’s urban landscape has experienced continuous change. Over time, many  transformations of the city’s streets and neighborhoods have become invisible, preserved only in collective memory and archival photographs. Architectural fragments provide another avenue to understanding our urban past, including historical building practices and technologies, our dynamic communities, and evolving ways of life.
persona3_adapted: For the first time in over 30 years, the Art Institute of Chicago presents a refined and thought-provoking new exhibition featuring pieces taken from buildings in and around Chicago. Since the 1800s, Chicago’s cityscape has been constantly changing. Over time, many of these transformations to the city’s streets and neighborhoods have become invisible, surviving only in shared memory and old photographs. These architectural fragments—parts of buildings that have been removed or salvaged—offer another way to understand our urban past, including historical construction methods and technologies, our lively communities, and how people’s ways of life have evolved.

CuratorialText: In this new installation, sculptural elements from building facades and interiors are displayed in groups that allow visitors to learn about many architecturally or culturally significant structures and moments in Chicago. These range from celebrated buildings by well-known architects like Frank Lloyd Wright and Louis Sullivan to important local histories related to the South Side neighborhood of Bronzeville, labor, and preservation.
persona3_adapted: In this new installation, three-dimensional decorative pieces taken from building exteriors and interiors are arranged in groupings that help visitors explore many of Chicago's architecturally or culturally important structures and historical moments. These range from famous buildings by renowned architects such as Frank Lloyd Wright and Louis Sullivan to significant local stories connected to the South Side neighborhood of Bronzeville, the history of labor, and efforts to preserve buildings.

CuratorialText: Featuring well-loved works from the permanent collection alongside important new acquisitions—such as a stained-glass window by Marion Mahony Griffin and a terracotta bust of an Egyptian pharaoh by Walter Bailey—this installation, designed by the Chicago-based practice Norman Kelley, offers dynamic encounters with the museum’s rich holdings of architectural fragments, an integral part of our shared material history.
persona3_adapted: Featuring beloved works from the permanent collection alongside significant new additions—such as a stained-glass window by Marion Mahony Griffin and a terracotta bust of an Egyptian pharaoh by Walter Bailey—this exhibition, designed by the Chicago-based firm Norman Kelley, offers engaging encounters with the museum’s extensive collection of architectural fragments, which are an essential part of our shared physical history.

CuratorialText: In the textiles of Southwest Asia and North Africa, embroidery is more than surface decoration. In this region, known as SWANA, artists have used, and continue to use, centuries-old embellishment techniques to transmit cultural expressions of identity, status, and long-held belief systems. Cultural groups throughout the region wear layers of dress and adornment to protect their bodies physically and spiritually. Garments and household items are ornamented with valuable materials that function as wearable wealth, including symbolic motifs and designs constructed with coins, thread, beads, amulets, ornaments, and jewelry.
persona3_adapted: In the textiles of Southwest Asia and North Africa—a region often referred to as SWANA—embroidery is far more than just surface decoration. Here, artists have used, and continue to use, traditional techniques passed down over centuries to express cultural ideas about identity, social standing, and long-standing belief systems. Various cultural groups in the region wear multiple layers of clothing and adornments to protect themselves both physically and spiritually. Garments and household items are decorated with valuable materials that serve as a form of portable wealth. These include symbolic patterns and designs made from coins, thread, beads, protective charms, ornaments, and jewelry.

CuratorialText: The vast area that spans from Morocco and Algeria in the west to Afghanistan and Uzbekistan in the east is connected by millennia-old trade networks. The works presented reveal pathways of deep cultural exchange shaped by diverse landscapes—from mountains and deserts to seas—along with the movement of nomadic peoples and the spread and interweaving of multiple religious traditions and urban fashions.
persona3_adapted: The vast region stretching from Morocco and Algeria in the west to Afghanistan and Uzbekistan in the east has been linked for thousands of years by trade routes. The artworks on display here show how cultures deeply influenced one another across this area—shaped by varied landscapes like mountains, deserts, and seas, as well as by the journeys of nomadic peoples and the blending of many religious traditions and city-based styles.

CuratorialText: Embroidered Traditions from Morocco to Afghanistan is the first exhibition at the Art Institute of Chicago devoted exclusively to the artistic and cultural traditions of SWANA and spectacularly showcases the region’s distinctive beauty and iconic visual expressions. Drawn primarily from the Art Institute’s rich collection of textiles, jewelry, and adornment, this display presents over 70 works from the 18th through 20th century—most of which have never been exhibited publicly—including four rare face veils on loan from a private collector based in Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.
persona3_adapted: Embroidered Traditions from Morocco to Afghanistan is the first exhibition at the Art Institute of Chicago focused entirely on the artistic and cultural traditions of the SWANA region (an acronym for Southwest Asia and North Africa). The show highlights the area’s distinctive beauty and iconic visual styles in a spectacular way. Drawing mainly from the museum’s own extensive collection of textiles, jewelry, and personal ornaments, the exhibition features over 70 works dating from the 1700s to the 1900s—most of which have never been shown to the public before. It also includes four rare face veils on loan from a private collector based in Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

CuratorialText: Among the many other highlights are elaborately decorated garments from Syria, Yemen, Niger, and Egypt; silver and coral “treasure necklaces” from Morocco’s Anti-Atlas Mountains; richly embroidered linen panels from the Art Institute’s exceptional collection of Ottoman Turkish textiles; and a wedding blanket embroidered by the Marsh Arab (Ahwari) peoples of Iraq.
persona3_adapted: Other notable pieces include ornately decorated clothing from Syria, Yemen, Niger, and Egypt; silver and coral "treasure necklaces" from Morocco’s Anti-Atlas Mountains; linen panels with intricate embroidery from the Art Institute’s outstanding collection of Ottoman Turkish textiles; and a wedding blanket embroidered by the Marsh Arab (Ahwari) peoples of Iraq.

CuratorialText: Barbara Hammer’s experimental film Sanctus depicts human bodies at risk. It features archival moving X-rays taken by doctor and filmmaker James Sibley Watson Jr. (1894–1982) of people making simple gestures and engaging in everyday activities: applying lipstick, drinking liquids, shaking hands, and shaving. Hammer rephotographed the original film and spliced the images together in a visceral montage set to overwhelming requiems by Neil B. Rolnick. She further transformed the X-rays with flashes of light, hand-painted colors, and other visual effects and by speeding up, looping, and reversing the movements. In presenting images captured by medical technology that uses large amounts of radiation, Hammer underscored her idea that “science advances through the demise of animal, including human, subjects in experiments” and that imaging, and even treatment, can cause harm.
persona3_adapted: Barbara Hammer’s experimental film *Sanctus* shows human bodies in states of vulnerability. The film uses archival X-ray footage—moving images taken by doctor and filmmaker James Sibley Watson Jr. (1894–1982)—of people performing simple gestures and going about everyday activities: applying lipstick, drinking, shaking hands, and shaving. Hammer re-photographed the original film and edited the images together in a powerful sequence set to overwhelming funeral music composed by Neil B. Rolnick. She further altered the X-rays with flashes of light, hand-painted colors, and other visual effects, as well as by speeding up, repeating, and reversing the movements. By presenting images captured through medical technology that relies on high levels of radiation, Hammer emphasized her belief that “science advances through the demise of animal, including human, subjects in experiments” and that the very act of imaging—and even medical treatment—can itself cause harm.

CuratorialText: Sanctus emerged from Hammer’s initial engagement with medical themes, an interest incited in the early 1980s by the AIDS epidemic. Over the course of her 50-year career, Hammer worked in photography, 16mm film, and video with an unflinching focus on queer communities and on her encounters within the American medical system.
persona3_adapted: Sanctus grew out of Hammer’s first exploration of medical subjects, a concern sparked in the early 1980s by the AIDS crisis. Over her 50-year career, Hammer worked in photography, 16mm film, and video, maintaining a steady, direct focus on queer communities and on her own experiences within the American healthcare system.

CuratorialText: Step back in time and immerse yourself in the spiritual, domestic, and chivalric worlds of the Middle Ages and Renaissance. The Deering Family Galleries of Medieval and Renaissance Art, Arms, and Armor present nearly 700 objects from the museum’s rich holdings of art from 1200 to 1600: monumental altarpieces, exquisite jewelry, and the beloved arms and armor collection.
persona3_adapted: Step back in time and explore the spiritual, home, and knightly worlds of the Middle Ages and Renaissance. The Deering Family Galleries of Medieval and Renaissance Art, Arms, and Armor display nearly 700 objects from the museum’s extensive collection of art created between 1200 and 1600: large-scale religious paintings made for church altars, finely crafted jewelry, and the popular collection of weapons and armor.

CuratorialText: The sumptuous display begins with a large room featuring architectural sculpture, treasury objects, and early painting that convey the austere sanctity of earlier medieval art. The centerpiece of the gallery is the meticulously conserved Ayala Altarpiece, commissioned in 1396 by the chancellor of Castile for his family’s funerary chapel in northern Spain. Spanning nearly 24 feet across and eight feet high, the painted wood altarpiece has undergone extensive treatment over the last three years and makes its restored debut in brilliant form. Two linked vaulted galleries showcase altarpieces and altarpiece fragments from Italy, Spain, and northern Europe, including the Art Institute favorite Saint George and the Dragon by Bernat Martorell. Each is displayed to suggest its original religious space, highlighting the church as a center of public life.
persona3_adapted: The exhibition opens with a grand room filled with carved stone decorations from churches, objects used in religious ceremonies, and early paintings—all of which reflect the solemn, spiritual character of medieval art from centuries ago. At the heart of the room is the carefully restored Ayala Altarpiece, a large painted wooden structure commissioned in 1396 by the chancellor of Castile for his family’s burial chapel in northern Spain. Measuring nearly 24 feet wide and eight feet tall, this altarpiece has undergone extensive conservation work over the past three years and now appears in brilliant condition for the first time since its restoration. Two connected, arched galleries display similar religious artworks and fragments from Italy, Spain, and northern Europe, including the Art Institute’s beloved painting *Saint George and the Dragon* by Bernat Martorell. Each piece is arranged to evoke the original church setting, emphasizing how the church served as a central hub of community life.

CuratorialText: The spaces that follow are more intimate, focusing on the texture of late Gothic and Renaissance life. One gallery presents luxury goods and the accessories of feasting, while another displays works of art for the bedchambers of Tuscany’s merchant elite. Everyday objects from northern Europe, along with jewelry and items of personal display, complete the domestic picture of the period.
persona3_adapted: The spaces that follow feel more personal, offering a closer look at daily life during the late Gothic and Renaissance periods. One room features luxury goods and items used for feasting, while another shows artworks made for the bedrooms of Tuscany’s wealthy merchant class. Everyday objects from northern Europe, together with jewelry and personal accessories, round out the domestic scene of the time.

CuratorialText: An elegant rotunda introduces the collection of arms and armor with equipment for the bodyguards of four rulers. From here, the space opens to a gallery filled with weaponry and armor dominated by two armored figures on horseback—one dressed for battle, the other for sport—and two armed and costumed figures engaged in foot combat. Finally, a gallery lined with cabinetry features the art of the sword, luxury firearms, and equipment for the hunt extending through the Baroque era.
persona3_adapted: An elegant rotunda—a round, domed hall—introduces the collection of arms and armor, featuring equipment used by the bodyguards of four rulers. From this space, the exhibition opens into a gallery filled with weaponry and armor, dominated by two armored figures on horseback: one outfitted for battle, the other for sport. Nearby, two armed and costumed figures are shown engaged in foot combat. Finally, a gallery lined with cabinets displays the artistry of swords, luxury firearms, and hunting equipment, extending through the Baroque period (roughly the 17th to early 18th century).

CuratorialText: The new installation expands the display of art of this fascinating period sixfold, premiering several recently conserved works and many significant new acquisitions—an elegant late Gothic breastplate and backplate, a rare 15th-century German portrait, and an outstanding group of 17th- and 18th-century swords. Subtle changes in the architecture of the galleries propel visitors through time and from religious to secular works, while digital labels provide an interactive multimedia experience. At the heart of this display are the gifts of major collectors whose donations serve as the collection’s foundation: the Ayala Altarpiece and Saint George and the Dragon from Charles Deering, Sienese and Florentine gold-ground paintings and early Northern works given by Martin Ryerson, a terracotta altarpiece by Florentine Benedetto Buglioni and a South German carved and painted Saint Catherine from Kate Buckingham, and George F. Harding’s impressive collection of arms and armor. All come together in the new Deering Family Galleries, offering a transporting experience to a time that continues to enchant.
persona3_adapted: The new installation expands the display of art from this fascinating period sixfold, introducing several recently restored works and many important new additions—an elegant late Gothic chest and back armor, a rare 15th-century German portrait, and an outstanding collection of 17th- and 18th-century swords. Subtle changes in the gallery architecture guide visitors through time and from religious to non-religious works, while digital labels offer an interactive multimedia experience. At the heart of this display are the gifts of major collectors whose donations form the foundation of the collection: the Ayala Altarpiece and Saint George and the Dragon from Charles Deering; paintings with gold backgrounds from Siena and Florence, along with early Northern European works given by Martin Ryerson; a terracotta altarpiece by Florentine artist Benedetto Buglioni and a carved and painted Saint Catherine from South Germany, donated by Kate Buckingham; and George F. Harding’s impressive collection of arms and armor. All come together in the new Deering Family Galleries, offering an immersive experience of a time that continues to captivate.

CuratorialText: Jill Mulleady paints by breathing new life into unseen histories. Drawing from art-historical, cinematic, and literary sources, her memorable images can haunt the viewer long after encountering them. This exhibition—Mulleady’s first solo show at a major museum—brings together approximately 20 paintings by the artist.
persona3_adapted: Jill Mulleady paints by breathing new life into overlooked histories. Drawing from art history, film, and literature, her striking images can stay with the viewer long after seeing them. This exhibition—Mulleady’s first solo show at a major museum—brings together about 20 of her paintings.

CuratorialText: Mulleady uses various methods including staining, and at times incorporating materials such as copper, to achieve fragile, luminous surfaces that feel impossibly thin yet visually deep, holding tension between flatness and depth. Not limited to canvas or linen, she employs a range of supports, including glass and velvet. The resultant works balance different degrees of transparency and opacity and can be looked at and, sometimes, seen through.
persona3_adapted: Mulleady uses several techniques—such as staining the surface and occasionally adding materials like copper—to create delicate, glowing surfaces that seem impossibly thin but visually rich, balancing a sense of flatness with depth. She works not only on canvas or linen but also on other materials, including glass and velvet. The resulting pieces play with varying levels of transparency and opacity; some can be looked at, and others can even be seen through.

CuratorialText: Mulleady’s paintings often depict scenes that are enigmatic and dangerous, showing figures at risk or under threat—from appetite, predation, or compulsion. While these ambiguous scenes may imply narratives, they likewise refuse tidy resolutions. Her imagery draws actively on painting lineages—allegory and the Baroque, Symbolist unease, the grotesque—recasting genres and canonical images for the present. In contrast to the purity and rationality of 20th-century modernism, Mulleady prioritizes a tradition in which chaos, instinct, and history drive art making.
persona3_adapted: Mulleady’s paintings often show scenes that are mysterious and unsettling, with figures who appear to be in danger—whether from hunger, being hunted, or some uncontrollable urge. Although these puzzling scenes seem to hint at a story, they also resist offering any neat or clear conclusion. Her imagery draws heavily on older traditions in painting—such as allegory (where images stand for deeper moral meanings), the dramatic style of the Baroque period, the uneasy mood of Symbolist art, and the deliberately distorted or strange forms of the grotesque—reworking these classic styles and well-known images for today. Unlike the clean, orderly, and logical approach of 20th-century modernism, Mulleady turns to a tradition where chaos, raw instinct, and the weight of history are the forces that shape art.

CuratorialText: As Mulleady has said, she works with existing languages in order to invent a new one, and her staged tableaux are particularly resonant within the Art Institute galleries, as they rhyme with many works in the collection that have informed the artist.
persona3_adapted: As Mulleady has explained, she works with existing visual languages—the familiar styles and symbols of art history—in order to invent a new one. Her carefully arranged, theatrical scenes feel especially meaningful within the Art Institute galleries, because they echo many works in the collection that have influenced the artist.

CuratorialText: After decades as a representational painter, in her seventies Alma Thomas turned to abstraction, creating shimmering, mosaic-like fields of color with rhythmic dabs of paint that were often inspired by forms from nature. The artist had been fascinated with space exploration since the late 1960s, and her later paintings often referenced America’s manned Apollo missions to the moon. Although she had never flown, Thomas began to paint as if she were in an airplane, capturing what she described as shifting patterns of light and streaks of color. “You look down on things,” she explained. “You streak through the clouds so fast… . You see only streaks of color.”
persona3_adapted: After decades of painting recognizable subjects—people, places, and objects—Alma Thomas shifted, in her seventies, to a more abstract style. She created vibrant, shimmering fields of color that resemble mosaics, built from rhythmic dabs of paint. These works were often inspired by shapes found in nature. Thomas had been captivated by space exploration since the late 1960s, and her later paintings frequently make reference to America’s Apollo missions, which sent astronauts to the moon. Though she had never flown herself, Thomas began to paint as if she were looking down from an airplane, trying to capture what she described as shifting patterns of light and streaks of color. “You look down on things,” she explained. “You streak through the clouds so fast… . You see only streaks of color.”

CuratorialText: Starry Night and the Astronauts evokes the open expanse and celestial patterns of a night sky, but despite its narrative title, the work could also be read as an aerial view of a watery surface, playing with our sense of immersion within an otherwise flat picture plane. The viewer is immersed not only in the sense of organic expanse that this painting achieves, however, but also in an encounter with Thomas’s process: the surface here is clearly constructed stroke by stroke. Meanwhile, the glimpses of raw canvas between each primary-colored mark seem as vivid as the applied paint itself—almost as if the composition were backlit. Thomas relied on the enlivening properties of color throughout her late-blooming career. “Color is life,” she once proclaimed, “and light is the mother of color.” This painting was created in 1972, when the artist was eighty. In the same year, she became the first African American woman to receive a solo exhibition at a major art museum, the Whitney Museum of American Art in New York City.
persona3_adapted: Starry Night and the Astronauts suggests the wide-open feeling and star-filled patterns of a night sky. But despite its story-like title, the work could also be seen as a view from above of a watery surface, playing with our sense of being surrounded by the scene, even though the painting itself is flat. The viewer is drawn in not only by the sense of organic vastness the painting creates, but also by an encounter with the artist’s method: the surface is clearly built up, one brushstroke at a time. Meanwhile, the glimpses of bare canvas visible between each bright, primary-colored mark seem as vivid as the paint itself—almost as if the composition were lit from behind. Throughout her career, which flourished later in life, Thomas relied on the lively qualities of color. “Color is life,” she once declared, “and light is the mother of color.” This painting was made in 1972, when the artist was eighty years old. That same year, she became the first African American woman to have a solo exhibition at a major art museum, the Whitney Museum of American Art in New York City.

CuratorialText: Perhaps the most famous depiction of a bedroom in Western art history, this vibrant painting documents Vincent van Gogh’s sleeping quarters in his beloved “Yellow House” in Arles, France. The composition exists in three versions, the first of which Van Gogh conceived in October 1888, a month after he moved into the home. In a letter to his brother Theo, he described having painted “the walls pale lilac, the floor in a broken and faded red, the chairs and the bed chrome yellow, the pillows and the sheet very pale lemon green, the bedspread blood-red, the dressing table orange, the washbasin blue, the window green.” With its bold colors, thick and broken brushwork, and sharply receding lines, the picture might suggest a nervous energy. But the artist understood it as a calming and restful image.
persona3_adapted: Perhaps the most famous image of a bedroom in Western art, this vivid painting shows Vincent van Gogh’s sleeping quarters in his beloved “Yellow House” in Arles, France. The composition exists in three versions, the first of which Van Gogh created in October 1888, a month after he moved into the home. In a letter to his brother Theo, he described having painted “the walls pale lilac, the floor in a broken and faded red, the chairs and the bed chrome yellow, the pillows and the sheet very pale lemon green, the bedspread blood-red, the dressing table orange, the washbasin blue, the window green.” With its bold colors, thick and uneven brushstrokes, and sharply angled lines that seem to push inward, the picture might suggest a restless or nervous energy. But the artist saw it as a calm and restful scene.

CuratorialText: The painting in the Art Institute’s collection is Van Gogh’s second version of the scene, made nearly a year after the first, in September 1889. He produced a third, smaller version at the same time as a gift for his mother and sister.
persona3_adapted: The painting in the Art Institute’s collection is Van Gogh’s second version of this scene, created nearly a year after the first, in September 1889. At the same time, he also made a third, smaller version as a gift for his mother and sister.

CuratorialText: This meditating Buddha comes from the coastal town of Nagapattinam in southern India, which was, as a result of settlers from Srivijaya (Indonesia), one of the few places where Buddhism was still flourishing in the twelfth century. The Buddha—with his elongated earlobes, the wheel marks on his palms, the urna between his brows, and the cranial protuberance covered with snail-shell curls—is seated in the posture of meditation, with his hands resting on his lap (dhyanamudra), wearing a seemingly diaphanous monastic garment. As in other images from Nagapattinam, a flame emerges out of the Buddha’s cranial protuberance, probably signifying wisdom. This monumental granite sculpture originally would have graced a monastic site at Nagapattinam, which is also well known for its Buddhist bronzes. The Tamil inscription covering its back is no longer legible.
persona3_adapted: This meditating Buddha comes from the coastal town of Nagapattinam in southern India. Because of settlers from Srivijaya (in present-day Indonesia), this was one of the few places where Buddhism was still thriving in the twelfth century. The Buddha has several traditional physical features: elongated earlobes, wheel-shaped marks on his palms, a small dot (the urna) between his eyebrows, and a bump on his head covered with curls that resemble snail shells. He sits in a meditation pose, with his hands resting on his lap—a hand position known as dhyanamudra—and wears a robe that appears almost transparent. As in other images from Nagapattinam, a flame rises from the bump on his head, likely representing wisdom. This large granite sculpture originally would have adorned a monastery site in Nagapattinam, a place also famous for its Buddhist bronze statues. The Tamil inscription carved into its back is no longer readable.

CuratorialText: Many Mansions is the first in Kerry James Marshall’s series of five large-scale paintings depicting public housing projects in Chicago and Los Angeles such as Rockwell Gardens, Wentworth Gardens, or, as in Many Mansions, Stateway Gardens. Struck by the absurdity of the term “garden” to describe these failed solutions to low-income housing, Marshall was inspired to represent the profound contradictions of living in such an environment. Many Mansions is filled with ironic and startling juxtapositions of the real and artificial—from the unnaturally cheerful landscape to the three haunting, ebony-skinned figures dressed in nostalgic Sunday best. The title of the painting, visible on the red ribbon above, is a variation on Christ’s oft-quoted remark found in John [14:2]: “In my Father’s house there are many mansions.”
persona3_adapted: Many Mansions is the first in a series of five large paintings by Kerry James Marshall that show public housing projects in Chicago and Los Angeles—places like Rockwell Gardens, Wentworth Gardens, or, as in this painting, Stateway Gardens. Marshall was struck by how absurd it seemed to call these failed attempts at affordable housing “gardens,” and this inspired him to capture the deep contradictions of living in such a setting. The painting is filled with ironic and surprising contrasts between what is real and what is artificial—from the unnaturally cheerful landscape to the three haunting figures with deep black skin, dressed in old-fashioned Sunday best. The title, written on the red ribbon above, is a twist on a well-known line from the Bible, John 14:2: “In my Father’s house there are many mansions.”

CuratorialText: In Ferris Bueller’s Day Off, Ferris’s best friend Cameron Frye intensely studies this nearly ten-foot-wide painting during a scene set at the Art Institute. Featuring people of every age and social class on the banks of the River Seine, A Sunday on La Grande Jatte—1884 has captivated visitors ever since its arrival at the museum in 1924.
persona3_adapted: In *Ferris Bueller’s Day Off*, the main character’s best friend, Cameron Frye, stares intently at this nearly ten-foot-wide painting during a scene set at the Art Institute. The artwork, *A Sunday on La Grande Jatte—1884*, shows people of all ages and social classes gathered along the banks of the River Seine. Since it first arrived at the museum in 1924, it has fascinated visitors.

CuratorialText: If we, like Cameron, come closer to the painting, figures and forms dissolve into dots and dashes of complementary colors laid side by side, characteristic of Seurat’s pointillist technique. Many smaller painted and drawn sketches and several larger canvases, in which Seurat laid out the parameters for the landscape and figures, led up to this majestically composed scene. Seurat returned to the work two years after its start date, amplifying the silhouettes of some figures and adding others. Some of these, like the monkey on the leash, seem so integral to the final composition that it is hard to imagine them as add-ons, but others, like the man carrying a rolled newspaper in the furthest distance, are barely noticeable. To the artist, however, every decision was essential to his aim of making a painting of modern life equivalent to a classical Greek frieze.
persona3_adapted: If we, like Cameron, move closer to the painting, the figures and forms break apart into dots and dashes of contrasting colors placed next to each other—a hallmark of Seurat’s technique known as pointillism. Before arriving at this grandly arranged scene, Seurat created many smaller painted and drawn studies, as well as several larger canvases, to work out the details of the landscape and the people. Two years after he began the painting, Seurat returned to it, making some figures’ outlines more distinct and adding new ones. Some of these additions, such as the monkey on a leash, feel so essential to the final image that it is hard to imagine the composition without them. Others, like the man carrying a rolled newspaper in the far distance, are barely noticeable. Yet for the artist, every choice was important to his goal: to create a painting of modern life that would carry the same weight and dignity as a classical Greek frieze.

CuratorialText: Laboring at a backstrap loom in this painting is Luz Jiménez, a master weaver and Nahua, one of the largest Indigenous groups in Mesoamerica. With expertise and dexterity, Jiménez threads weft through warp, slowly building her intricately patterned textile, the completed portion resting on her lap. Diego Rivera, a leading modernist painter who came to prominence in the years after the Mexican Revolution (1910–20), promoted a vision of Mexican national identity rooted in Indigenous and folk cultures, distinct from the legacies of Spanish colonialism. By centering Jiménez in Weaving, Rivera claimed her traditions as part of his own.
persona3_adapted: Working at a backstrap loom in this painting is Luz Jiménez, a master weaver and a member of the Nahua people, one of the largest Indigenous groups in Mesoamerica. With skill and precision, Jiménez guides the horizontal threads (the weft) through the vertical threads (the warp), slowly building her richly patterned textile, the finished part resting on her lap. Diego Rivera, a leading modernist painter who rose to fame in the years after the Mexican Revolution (1910–20), promoted a vision of Mexican national identity rooted in Indigenous and folk traditions, separate from the influences of Spanish colonialism. By placing Jiménez at the center of *Weaving*, Rivera claimed her traditions as part of his own.

CuratorialText: This monumental painting is Gustave Caillebotte’s largest, most ambitious work and has been a visitor favorite since the Art Institute acquired it in 1964, well before the artist’s contributions to the Impressionist movement were widely recognized. Caillebotte captured a busy intersection of Paris that was only a short walk from his home. In fact, his paintings of Paris rarely depict areas beyond his familiar neighborhoods.
persona3_adapted: This monumental painting is Gustave Caillebotte’s largest and most ambitious work, and it has been a favorite among visitors since the Art Institute acquired it in 1964—long before the artist’s role in the Impressionist movement was widely appreciated. Caillebotte captured a busy Paris intersection just a short walk from his home. In fact, his paintings of Paris rarely show areas beyond the neighborhoods he knew well.

CuratorialText: Caillebotte demonstrated his affinity with the Impressionists through his subject of modern urban life but approached these settings with a more Realist technique. The dramatic cropping of the scene may have been inspired by the newly popularized medium of photography; the two central figures seem to be walking straight into the viewer as something captures their attention outside of the frame. At the time, artists rarely attempted to depict people in motion; Caillebotte carefully placed each of the background figures to evoke the feeling of bustling city streets. When he started exhibiting with the Impressionists, Caillebotte also focused on portraying the atmosphere of his scenes; here, instead of explicitly painting raindrops to indicate the weather, he suggests it through the incorporation of newly available retractable umbrellas and the subtle reflections on the wet cobblestones.
persona3_adapted: Caillebotte showed his connection to the Impressionists by choosing subjects from modern city life, but he painted these scenes with a more realistic style. The bold way he cuts off the edges of the image may have been inspired by photography, which had recently become popular. The two central figures appear to be walking directly toward the viewer, as if something outside the picture has caught their attention. At the time, artists rarely tried to show people in motion; Caillebotte carefully placed each person in the background to create the sense of a busy city street. When he began showing his work with the Impressionists, Caillebotte also focused on capturing the mood or feeling of a scene. Here, instead of painting raindrops clearly to show it is raining, he suggests the weather by including the newly available umbrellas that can be opened and closed, and by painting the soft reflections on the wet cobblestones.

CuratorialText: Richard Hunt created Hero Construction in 1958, one year after graduating from the School of the Art Institute of Chicago. The sculpture is composed of found objects—old pipes, bits of metal, and automobile parts—that the artist discovered in junkyards and on the street. Using a torch like a paintbrush, Hunt welded these elements into a form that is abstract yet recognizable.
persona3_adapted: Richard Hunt created *Hero Construction* in 1958, just one year after finishing his studies at the School of the Art Institute of Chicago. The sculpture is made from found objects—old pipes, scraps of metal, and car parts—that the artist collected from junkyards and the street. Using a welding torch as if it were a paintbrush, Hunt fused these pieces together into a shape that is abstract but still recognizable.

CuratorialText: Hunt was fascinated with mythology, and Hero Construction can be related to other sculptures past and present, from ancient statues of Greek heroes to modern monuments of statesmen. The modest but monumental stance of the figure suggests a contemporary hero who maintains strength in the face of the uncertainties and dangers of the present age.
persona3_adapted: Hunt was fascinated with mythology, and *Hero Construction* connects to sculptures from different eras—both ancient statues of Greek heroes and modern monuments to political leaders. The figure’s pose is both humble and grand, suggesting a present-day kind of hero who holds onto strength even when faced with the uncertainties and dangers of our time.

CuratorialText: The Mexicas, or Aztec Empire, ruled central Mexico from 1428 until 1521. This monument uses glyphs to record the history of the Mexica universe up to 1503. On the back, 1 Rabbit represents the beginning of time. On the front, the four previous cosmic eras are inscribed in the corners, while the current cosmic era 4 Movement appears in the center. 1 Crocodile (above) and 11 Reed (in the square frame) indicate the date July 15, 1503, when the ninth emperor Moctezuma Xocoyotzin was coronated. Just as these glyphs recurred throughout the Mexica capital, Tenochtitlan, they can also be found in Chicago: 4 Movement appears in a mural at the 18th Street “L” stop and on manhole covers in Pilsen—a neighborhood that has long been home to Chicago’s vibrant Mexican community.
persona3_adapted: The Mexicas, also known as the Aztec Empire, ruled central Mexico from 1428 until 1521. This monument uses picture symbols, called glyphs, to record the history of the Mexica universe up to 1503. On the back, the symbol 1 Rabbit marks the beginning of time. On the front, the four previous cosmic eras are carved into the corners, while the current cosmic era, 4 Movement, appears in the center. The symbols 1 Crocodile (above) and 11 Reed (in the square frame) represent the date July 15, 1503, the day the ninth emperor, Moctezuma Xocoyotzin, was crowned. Just as these symbols appeared throughout the Mexica capital, Tenochtitlan, they can also be found in Chicago: 4 Movement appears in a mural at the 18th Street “L” stop and on manhole covers in Pilsen—a neighborhood that has long been home to Chicago’s vibrant Mexican community.

CuratorialText: A palpable energy and sense of movement enliven Nightlife, Archibald Motley’s portrayal of a crowded cabaret in the South Side neighborhood of Bronzeville in Chicago. With stylized figures, an array of diagonal lines, and heightened colors keyed to shades of magenta and violet, the artist captured the exuberance of city dwellers out on the town. Motley created a network of gestures and glances among the people, drawing attention to the various social interactions that animate the scene. The composition is an exploration of artificial lighting. Motley was inspired, in part, to paint Nightlife after having seen Edward Hopper’s Nighthawks (1942.51), which had entered the Art Institute’s collection the prior year.
persona3_adapted: A lively energy and sense of motion bring *Nightlife* to life—Archibald Motley’s depiction of a crowded cabaret in Bronzeville, a neighborhood on Chicago’s South Side. Using simplified, slightly exaggerated figures, a variety of diagonal lines, and intense colors built around shades of magenta and violet, the artist captured the excitement of city residents enjoying a night out. Motley created a web of gestures and glances among the people, highlighting the different social interactions that make the scene feel active. The painting also explores the effects of artificial light. Motley was partly inspired to paint *Nightlife* after seeing Edward Hopper’s *Nighthawks* (1942.51), which had entered the Art Institute’s collection the year before.

CuratorialText: Although influenced by Abstract Expressionist artists in New York in the early 1950s, Joan Mitchell did not prioritize self-expression: her often exuberant abstractions were “about landscape, not about me,” she once explained. Mitchell painted large, light-filled canvases animated by loosely applied skeins of bright color—here infused with the energy of a large metropolis. The title suggests a relationship between the painting’s network of pigments and the nerves or arteries of an urban space. The sense of spontaneity conveyed in City Landscape, however, belies Mitchell’s methods. Unlike many of her contemporaries, who were dubbed “action painters,” Mitchell worked slowly and deliberately. “I paint a little,” she said. “Then I sit and look at the painting, sometimes for hours. Eventually, the painting tells me what to do.”
persona3_adapted: Although Joan Mitchell was influenced by the Abstract Expressionist artists working in New York in the early 1950s, she did not focus on expressing her own emotions. Instead, her often energetic and joyful abstract works were “about landscape, not about me,” as she once explained. Mitchell created large, light-filled canvases brought to life by loosely applied tangles of bright color—here, those colors seem to carry the energy of a big city. The title suggests that the painting’s web of paint might relate to the nerves or arteries of an urban environment. However, the sense of spontaneity in *City Landscape* hides Mitchell’s actual working process. Unlike many of her peers, who were called “action painters” for their fast, physical approach, Mitchell worked slowly and with great care. “I paint a little,” she said. “Then I sit and look at the painting, sometimes for hours. Eventually, the painting tells me what to do.”

CuratorialText: Around 1920 Georgia O’Keeffe painted a number of oils exploring, as she later recalled, “the idea that music could be translated into something for the eye.” In Blue and Green Music, O’Keeffe’s colors and forms simultaneously suggest the natural world and evoke the experience of sound. She was drawn to the theories of the Russian Expressionist painter Vasily Kandinsky, who, in his 1912 text Concerning the Spiritual in Art, argued that visual artists should emulate music in order to achieve pure expression free of literary references.
persona3_adapted: Around 1920, Georgia O’Keeffe painted a series of works in oil that explored, as she later recalled, “the idea that music could be translated into something for the eye.” In *Blue and Green Music*, O’Keeffe’s use of color and shape simultaneously calls to mind the natural world and suggests the experience of hearing sound. She was drawn to the ideas of the Russian painter Vasily Kandinsky, who was part of the Expressionist movement—a style that emphasizes emotional experience over realistic depiction. In his 1912 book *Concerning the Spiritual in Art*, Kandinsky argued that visual artists should take inspiration from music in order to achieve pure expression, free from references to stories or objects.

CuratorialText: Henri Matisse considered Bathers by a River to be one of the five most “pivotal” works of his career, and with good reason: it facilitated the evolution of the artist’s style over the course of nearly a decade. Originally, the work was related to a 1909 commission by the Russian collector Sergei Shchukin, who wanted two large canvases to decorate the staircase of his Moscow home. Matisse proposed three pastoral images, though Shchukin decided to purchase only two works, Dance II and Music (both State Hermitage Museum, Saint Petersburg).
persona3_adapted: Henri Matisse considered *Bathers by a River* to be one of the five most “pivotal” works of his career, and with good reason: it marked a major shift in the artist’s style over nearly ten years. The painting began as part of a 1909 commission from the Russian collector Sergei Shchukin, who wanted two large canvases to decorate the staircase of his Moscow home. Matisse proposed three scenes of rural life, but Shchukin decided to buy only two of them: *Dance II* and *Music* (both now in the State Hermitage Museum, Saint Petersburg).

CuratorialText: Four years later, Matisse returned to this canvas, the rejected third image, altering the idyllic scene and changing the pastel palette to reflect his new interest in Cubism. He reordered the composition, making the figures more columnar, with faceless, ovoid heads. Over the next years, Matisse transformed the background into four vertical bands and turned the formerly blue river into a thick black vertical band. With its restricted palette and severely abstracted forms, Bathers by a River is far removed from Dance II and Music, which convey a graceful lyricism. The sobriety and hint of danger in Bathers by a River may in part reflect the artist’s concerns during the terrible, war-torn period during which he completed it.
persona3_adapted: Four years later, Matisse returned to this canvas—the third version, which had been rejected. He changed the peaceful scene and shifted the soft, pastel colors to reflect his growing interest in Cubism, a style that breaks objects into geometric shapes. He reorganized the composition, making the figures more like columns, with head shapes that are oval and without facial features. Over the following years, Matisse turned the background into four vertical strips and changed what was once a blue river into a thick, black vertical band. With its limited color range and severely simplified forms, *Bathers by a River* is very different from *Dance II* and *Music*, which express a graceful, songlike quality. The seriousness and sense of threat in *Bathers by a River* may partly reflect the artist’s anxieties during the terrible, war-torn period in which he finished it.

CuratorialText: One of four posts sculpted for the palace of the king (ogoga) of Ikere, this evocative image of the Yoruba concept of a divine ruler is a masterwork of Olowe of Ise. The renowned artist’s dynamic style can be distinguished by the interrelationships between figures, their exaggerated proportions, and the use of open space. The sculptor’s diminutive portrayal of the king suggests his dependence on others, and his crown has been emphasized to evoke the spiritual power residing within it. The towering image of the king’s senior wife standing behind him underscores her superior status as a guardian of her husband’s interests, endowed with keen political understanding and supernatural knowledge. She would have placed the crown on the king’s head during his enthronement ceremony. The smaller figures at the king’s feet represent one of his junior wives and the flute-playing trickster god Eshu.
persona3_adapted: One of four carved posts made for the palace of the king (ogoga) of Ikere, this striking image of the Yoruba idea of a divine ruler is a masterpiece by Olowe of Ise. The famous artist’s lively style can be seen in how the figures relate to one another, their exaggerated sizes and shapes, and the use of empty space around them. The sculptor’s small depiction of the king suggests his reliance on others, and his crown has been made prominent to hint at the spiritual power it holds.
