From glittering stones to ruddy earth, from insects to minerals, learn about various ways that people have made and understood red through examples drawn from across the museum’s collections.
persona3_adapted: Red is a color of vitality, passion, good fortune, and celebration—it has an undeniable energy. It catches our eye and stirs something within us, demanding our attention. From sparkling gemstones to reddish earth, from insects to minerals, this exhibition explores the many ways people have created and interpreted red, using examples drawn from across the museum’s collections.

CuratorialText: Several metal compounds (that include iron, mercury, or lead) form commonly used historic colored pigments. These compounds may be found, mined, or produced. Iron compounds in earth pigments can have different colors. Red iron earths are readily found or can be manufactured and have been discovered in many applications, including pottery and paints.
persona3_adapted: Several metal-based compounds—such as those containing iron, mercury, or lead—were commonly used in the past to create colored pigments. These compounds could be found in nature, mined from the ground, or manufactured. For example, iron compounds found in earth pigments can produce a range of colors. Red iron earths, which are either easily found in nature or made artificially, have been used in many different applications, from pottery to paints.

CuratorialText: Similarly, mercury-based pigments are made from grinding the red mineral cinnabar. Synthetic versions have long been produced and are referred to as vermilion. This deep red pigment, though toxic, was used in burial ceremonies, incorporated in lacquers, and adorns paintings and documents.
persona3_adapted: Similarly, mercury-based pigments are created by grinding the red mineral cinnabar. Artificial versions have been made for centuries and are known as vermilion. This deep red pigment, though toxic, was used in burial ceremonies, mixed into lacquers, and appears in paintings and documents.

CuratorialText: While red lead has a naturally forming mineral equivalent (minimum), it was manufactured far more commonly for use as a pigment. In general, lead compounds were heated under conditions favorable to produce the desired colored material and minimizing the formation of others.
persona3_adapted: While red lead does occur naturally as a mineral (called minimum), it was much more often manufactured for use as a coloring material. Typically, lead compounds were heated under specific conditions that encouraged the production of the desired color while limiting the formation of other, unwanted substances.

CuratorialText: Red copper oxide or cuprite is found as a degradation product on bronzes and forms by the reaction of the copper from the bronze and the atmosphere.
persona3_adapted: Red copper oxide, also known as cuprite, appears as a substance that forms when bronze deteriorates. It is created through a chemical reaction between the copper in the bronze and the surrounding air.

CuratorialText: Plants and animals can also be sources of reds used in artworks. Madder root and safflower each may have their color extracted, purified, and prepared for use in objects such as textiles and prints. The conditions in which the dye or lake is prepared can change the final color. Scale insects (such as lac and cochineal) are another source. Such insects were harvested, with the red color extracted and prepared for use as a pigment or dye. When possible, determining the colorants used in artworks can shed light on historical trade routes due to the limited native habitat of these insects.
persona3_adapted: Plants and animals can also provide the reds used in artworks. For example, the color can be extracted from madder root or safflower, then purified and prepared for use in objects like textiles and prints. The way the dye or lake (a type of pigment made by binding a dye to a base) is prepared can change the final color. Scale insects—such as lac and cochineal—are another source. These insects were harvested, and their red color was extracted and turned into a pigment or dye. When possible, identifying the colorants in artworks can reveal information about historical trade routes, since these insects are only found naturally in certain regions.

CuratorialText:
Dried cochineal insects. Image courtesy of Conservation and Scientific Research, National Museum of Asian Art, Smithsonian Institution
Red also occurred naturally in the form of precious and semi-precious stones, such as rubies, garnets, spinels, and carnelians. Prized for their color and rarity, they were valuable gifts and were often worn by elites or used to adorn luxury items.
persona3_adapted:
Dried cochineal insects. Image courtesy of Conservation and Scientific Research, National Museum of Asian Art, Smithsonian Institution

Red also appeared naturally in precious and semi-precious stones, including rubies, garnets, spinels, and carnelians. Valued for their color and scarcity, these stones were given as meaningful gifts, often worn by the wealthy and powerful, or used to decorate high-status objects.

CuratorialText: Rubrication, the practice of writing portions of a text in red ink, helped visually underscore certain information and became particularly common in European manuscripts after the seventh century. Red was sometimes used for the angular Kufic script in in copies of the Qur’an made between the eighth and eleventh centuries CE to indicate vocalization; it stood out next to black- or brown-inked calligraphy. Red was also commonly used in the paste for the inked seals that were added to finished works of Chinese painting or calligraphy; it contrasted against black ink writing and images. The seal might give the name of the artist or his studio, the collector, or other private individuals, and it occasionally expresses a favorite brief saying.
persona3_adapted: Rubrication—the practice of writing parts of a text in red ink—helped draw attention to certain information and became especially common in European manuscripts after the seventh century. In copies of the Qur’an made between the eighth and eleventh centuries CE, red was sometimes used for the angular Kufic script to indicate how words should be pronounced; it stood out against black- or brown-inked calligraphy. Red was also commonly used in the paste for the inked seals that were added to finished works of Chinese painting or calligraphy, creating a contrast with black ink writing and images. The seal might give the name of the artist or his studio, the collector, or other private individuals, and occasionally it expresses a favorite brief saying.

CuratorialText: Love, passion, importance—red carries many positive associations today, and has throughout history. In ancient Egypt, pharaohs were depicted in red due to its association with royalty and passage through the afterlife. In China, red is auspicious—associated with life-generating energy (the sun, blood, and fire)—and is the color of celebrations and prosperity.
persona3_adapted: Love, passion, importance—red carries many positive associations today, and has throughout history. In ancient Egypt, pharaohs were shown in red because the color was linked to royalty and the journey through the afterlife. In China, red is considered lucky—it is connected to life-giving energy (the sun, blood, and fire)—and is the color of celebrations and wealth.

CuratorialText: Red is still associated with festivities and happiness in Chinese culture today. Learn more about Lunar New Year, when families decorate windows with red paper cuttings and adorn doors with couplets expressing auspicious wishes. Watch and learn how Chinese paper cuttings are made, from the Smithsonian Center for Folklife and Cultural Heritage.
persona3_adapted: Red continues to symbolize celebration and joy in Chinese culture today. Discover more about Lunar New Year, a time when families decorate their windows with red paper cuttings and hang couplets—poetic phrases expressing good fortune—on their doors. Watch and learn how Chinese paper cuttings are created, presented by the Smithsonian Center for Folklife and Cultural Heritage.

CuratorialText: Red can carry more negative associations as well, such as with warfare, rage, and destruction. The color of blood, there is a visceral quality to red that resonates uniquely with the human psyche. Haunted by the traces that the human body leaves behind, contemporary artist Chiharu Shiota’s 2015 installation “Over the Continents” amassed personal memories through an accumulation of nearly four hundred individual shoes. Collected by the artist, each shoe came with a note from the donor describing lost individuals and past moments. Almost four miles of red yarn wrapped around the shoes and attached to a single point in the museum’s Sackler pavilion, evoking the artist’s attention to the fundamental ties that connect humans to one another and the world.
persona3_adapted: Red can also carry more negative meanings, such as war, anger, and destruction. As the color of blood, red has a powerful, instinctive quality that affects the human mind in a unique way. Haunted by the marks the human body leaves behind, contemporary artist Chiharu Shiota created a 2015 installation called “Over the Continents.” In this work, she gathered personal memories by collecting nearly four hundred individual shoes. Each shoe came with a note from the person who donated it, describing someone they had lost or a past moment they remembered. The artist used almost four miles of red thread, wrapping it around the shoes and connecting them to a single point in the museum’s Sackler pavilion. This arrangement highlights the artist’s focus on the basic bonds that link people to one another and to the world.

CuratorialText: Whether it’s in the form of growing plants, precious stones, or deliberately blended pigments, there’s a human impulse to surround ourselves with green in the art we create, the environments we inhabit, and the spaces we build. Green evokes fertile landscapes, growth and prosperity, and even health and wellness. Learn about how green has been made and understood across various cultures and traditions represented in the museum’s collections.
persona3_adapted: Whether it appears in growing plants, precious stones, or carefully mixed paints, humans have long felt a need to surround themselves with green—in the art we make, the places we live, and the spaces we construct. Green brings to mind fertile landscapes, growth and abundance, and even health and well-being. Discover how this color has been created and understood across different cultures and traditions represented in the museum’s collections.

CuratorialText: While there aren’t many plant sources of green dyes in the art world, some Indian paintings use beetle wings to mimic shiny green emeralds. In very special works of art, emeralds could be carved to make unique objects, or were made into jewelry.
persona3_adapted: While green dyes derived from plants are relatively rare in art, certain Indian paintings incorporate beetle wings to imitate the shimmering appearance of green emeralds. In particularly exceptional artworks, emeralds themselves could be carved into one-of-a-kind objects or fashioned into jewelry.

CuratorialText: The green patina on copper and bronze objects occurs as the copper corrodes. Patina occurs naturally when copper comes into contact with salty or acidic surroundings. See, for instance, how a range of corrosion products affects the surface color of the ancient Chinese bronze ritual vessel below: most of the surface is covered with a smooth, tin oxide-stained pale green with copper compounds, but there are also numerous scattered small patches of dull red cuprite, and the bright, powdery green seen here and there represents copper chloride. Explore this object in 3D.
persona3_adapted: The green surface layer on copper and bronze objects forms as the copper reacts with its environment. This natural process, called patina, happens when copper is exposed to salty or acidic conditions. For example, look at the ancient Chinese bronze ritual vessel below: you can see how different types of chemical reactions have created a variety of surface colors. Most of the vessel is covered with a smooth, pale green layer tinted by tin oxide and copper compounds. Scattered across it are small, dull reddish patches of cuprite (a copper oxide mineral), and here and there you'll find bright, powdery green spots made of copper chloride. Explore this object in 3D.

CuratorialText: Copper corrosion products can be taken advantage of to create man-made copper green pigments; more commonly, however, the green mineral malachite can be ground to make paints. Malachite is often used in paintings in China.
persona3_adapted: The natural green mineral malachite can be ground into a powder to make paint. While it is also possible to create artificial green pigments from the corrosion (or tarnish) that forms on copper, malachite was the more common source for green paint. This pigment was frequently used in Chinese painting.

CuratorialText:
Fine-ground malachite. Image courtesy of Conservation and Scientific Research, National Museum of Asian Art, Smithsonian Institution
Copper, together with lead, is often the basis of green glazes in ceramics. Mixtures of copper with yellow particles of lead tin oxide or lead antimony oxide are also used for green enamels.
persona3_adapted:
Fine-ground malachite. Image courtesy of Conservation and Scientific Research, National Museum of Asian Art, Smithsonian Institution

Copper, often combined with lead, is a common ingredient in the green glazes used on ceramics. To create green enamels, copper is also mixed with yellow particles of lead tin oxide or lead antimony oxide.

CuratorialText: “Celadon” refers to a large family of high-fired ceramics. Their glazes are colored by small percentages of iron oxide. When fired in an oxygen-deprived, or reducing, atmosphere, the glazes turn variable shades of bluish green, traditionally described with the French word céladon. The word is commonly believed to have been derived from a seventeenth-century French drama, L’Astrée, in which a shepherd named Céladon appears dressed in a gray-green robe. Some modern scholars prefer the term “green ware,” but the poetic French name is more likely to remind a reader that celadon ceramics can exhibit a broad range of colors, from sky blue to sea green.
persona3_adapted: “Celadon” is the name for a large group of ceramics that are fired at very high temperatures. Their glossy surfaces, or glazes, get their color from small amounts of iron oxide. When these pieces are fired in a kiln with little oxygen—a process called a reducing atmosphere—the glazes turn into various shades of bluish green. This color is traditionally known by the French word céladon. The term is thought to come from a seventeenth-century French play, L’Astrée, in which a shepherd named Céladon wears a gray-green robe. Some modern scholars prefer to call these works “green ware,” but the poetic French name is more likely to remind us that celadon ceramics can range widely in color, from sky blue to sea green.

CuratorialText: Jade was the first true luxury medium to emerge in ancient China. Although the term is used to refer to a range of hard, beautifully figured stones, most Chinese Neolithic, Bronze Age, and early imperial period examples have been scientifically identified as nephrite, geologically different from the bright, apple green jadeite used in much later times. Composed of interlocked, fibrous crystals and occurring as pebbles and boulders in a range of colors (due to iron, chromium, manganese, or similar materials fused in the rock), nephrite is an incredibly hard stone. It cannot be carved or even scratched by metal but instead must be ground into shape using quartz and other tough minerals. These qualities and its rarity inspired ancient Chinese people to value it very highly and to reserve its use for special purposes, such as for jewelry and ceremonial and ritual objects.
persona3_adapted: Jade was the first truly luxurious material to become important in ancient China. While the word "jade" is often used to describe several types of hard, beautifully patterned stones, most examples from China's Neolithic period, Bronze Age, and early imperial era have been scientifically identified as nephrite. This is a different mineral from the bright, apple-green jadeite that became popular much later. Nephrite is made up of tightly interlocked, fibrous crystals and is found naturally as pebbles and boulders in many colors—these colors come from elements like iron, chromium, or manganese that are mixed into the rock. It is an extremely hard stone, so hard that it cannot be carved or even scratched by metal tools. Instead, it had to be shaped by grinding it with quartz and other tough minerals. Because of these qualities and its rarity, ancient Chinese people valued jade very highly and reserved it for special uses, such as jewelry and objects used in ceremonies and religious rituals.

CuratorialText: According to his close friend Agnes Meyer, when Charles Lang Freer was ill near the end of his life, he often held a piece of jade in his hand, believing with an almost religious faith in the comforting and restorative powers of the stone. Freer collected ancient Chinese jades in large numbers, when most other Western collectors showed little interest. Ultimately, there were more than 500 pieces in his collection, spanning more than 5,000 years.
persona3_adapted: According to his close friend Agnes Meyer, when Charles Lang Freer was ill near the end of his life, he often held a piece of jade in his hand, believing with an almost religious faith in the stone’s ability to comfort and restore him. Freer collected ancient Chinese jades in large numbers at a time when most other Western collectors showed little interest in them. In the end, his collection contained more than 500 pieces, spanning more than 5,000 years.

CuratorialText: Green is so prevalent in the flora of the natural world that the color easily conjures lush environments teeming with plants. Life, growth, and regeneration are all concepts that come to mind in the context of verdant landscapes. In Southeast Asia, during the ninth through the thirteenth century, constructing cities required carving out spaces from the verdant, tropical environment. The jungle had to be systematically cultivated; water had to be diverted into reservoirs, moats, and canals; and the landscape had to be carefully tended. When population centers shifted due to political, ecological, and religious changes, the tropical landscape readily encroached on the built environment, transforming once urban landscapes back into jungle. This environment informed Southeast Asian artists: on temple walls and sculptures, plants and flowers grow on thick stalks, vines curlicue, and sprays of foliage transform surfaces into lush landscapes.
persona3_adapted: Green is so common in the natural world that the color easily brings to mind lush environments full of plants. Ideas of life, growth, and renewal all come to mind when we think of such green landscapes. In Southeast Asia, between the ninth and thirteenth centuries, building cities meant carving out spaces from the dense, tropical environment. The jungle had to be carefully cleared; water had to be channeled into reservoirs, moats, and canals; and the landscape had to be constantly maintained. When population centers shifted due to political, environmental, or religious changes, the tropical landscape quickly reclaimed the built areas, turning what were once urban spaces back into jungle. This environment shaped the work of Southeast Asian artists: on temple walls and sculptures, plants and flowers grow on thick stems, vines curl into spirals, and clusters of leaves turn surfaces into rich, plant-filled scenes.

CuratorialText: Set in a garden, the image depicts a group of angels, referred to as huris or paris in Persian, entertaining their queen by playing music, dancing, and serving food and wine. The drawing's lively mood echoes other Persian representations of outdoor banquets or bazm (a form of celebration with wine and music) ceremonies, with the exception that this fine drawing features heavenly creatures--a favorite subject matter in sixteenth-and seventeenth-century Iran and Turkey--instead of the princely figures seen in illustrations to lyrical poetry by Hafiz and Assar.
persona3_adapted: Set in a garden, the image shows a group of angels—called huris or paris in Persian—entertaining their queen by playing music, dancing, and serving food and wine. The drawing’s lively mood is similar to other Persian depictions of outdoor banquets or bazm ceremonies (a type of celebration that includes wine and music). However, unlike the princely figures found in illustrations for the lyrical poetry of Hafiz and Assar, this fine drawing instead features heavenly beings—a popular subject in sixteenth- and seventeenth-century Iran and Turkey.

CuratorialText: Referred to as peri winged celestial creatures are among some of the most popular subjects in sixteenth-century Ottoman and Persian drawings and paintings. The exquisitely rendered composition typifies a distinct style of Ottoman drawing that combines sweeping, calligraphic lines with soft, feathery strokes, as is evident in the minute designs on the figure's robe, cup, and bottle. Light washes of color add a sense of volume and texture to the form of this heavenly being.
persona3_adapted: Known as "peri," winged celestial beings are among the most popular subjects in sixteenth-century Ottoman and Persian drawings and paintings. This beautifully detailed composition is a classic example of a distinct Ottoman drawing style, which combines flowing, calligraphy-like lines with soft, feathery strokes—visible in the tiny patterns on the figure's robe, cup, and bottle. Light washes of color give a sense of shape and surface texture to this heavenly creature.

CuratorialText: Four-part inro in form of flattened rectangle with rounded corners (probably derived from the coin called ryo). The inset rim of each of the three compartments fits into the base of the compartment above it or into the lid. The four components are secured by a blue-green silk cord passing through two holes in each of the elements, tied in a bow below and secured by the bead (ojime) above and atttached to the toggle (netsuke) at the end.
persona3_adapted: This four-part inro—a small, stacked container worn on the belt—is shaped like a flattened rectangle with rounded corners, a form likely inspired by a coin called the ryo. Each of its three compartments has a rim that fits snugly into the base of the piece above it or into the lid. The four sections are held together by a blue-green silk cord that passes through two holes in each part. The cord is tied in a bow at the bottom, secured by a small bead (called an ojime) above, and attached to a toggle (known as a netsuke) at the end.

CuratorialText: Box, small cylindrical, with cover of equal size with the box proper. Clay: hard, grayish. Glaze: pale greenish-gray celadon, with faint lilac tints. Decoration: inlaid in white and black under glaze: medallion of two flying cranes surrounded by geometric borders.
persona3_adapted: A small cylindrical box with a lid that matches the box in size. Made of hard, grayish clay. The surface is covered in a pale greenish-gray glaze, a type known as celadon, with subtle hints of lilac. The decoration is created using an inlay technique, where white and black materials are set into the clay before glazing. The design features a central medallion showing two flying cranes, framed by geometric borders.

CuratorialText: A straight-sided cup which angles inward about three-quarters of the way toward a flattened bottom. The walls of the cup are very thin and this type of ware has been referred to as egg-shell ware. The fabric is a pinkish beige color. The inside of the cup is unpainted. The details on the outside of the cup are painted in brownish-black and red on an upainted background. The same motif of a red lotus bud shape outlined in brownish-black and tied with ribbons of the same brownish-black color is repeated seven times around the cup. The ornament color is repeated seven times around the cup. The ornament extends from the edge of the cup to about the middle of the cup. Within the cup are remains of a black powdery substance.
persona3_adapted: This cup has straight sides that taper inward about three-quarters of the way down to a flattened base. Its walls are exceptionally thin, a quality that has led to such vessels being called "egg-shell ware." The material, or fabric, is a pinkish beige color. The interior of the cup is unpainted. On the outside, details are painted in brownish-black and red against an unpainted background. A single design—a red lotus bud shape outlined in brownish-black and tied with ribbons of the same color—is repeated seven times around the cup. This ornamentation runs from the rim down to about the middle of the cup. Inside the cup, traces of a black powdery substance remain.

CuratorialText: This is one of several paintings of shop fronts that Whistler completed while in St. Ives. Sympathetic critics singled it out for enthusiastic praise when Whistler exhibited it in London in 1884. One declared, it "is probably the most perfect little thing of its kind that was ever wrought by an artist who has learnt how to see….It is without detail, without apparent labour, without dramatic interest; but it is exquisite in colour, faultless in tone, and its well-considered mystery has, at least, the interest of suggestiveness."
persona3_adapted: This is one of several paintings of storefronts that Whistler made while in St. Ives. When he showed it in London in 1884, sympathetic critics singled it out for enthusiastic praise. One declared that it "is probably the most perfect little thing of its kind that was ever created by an artist who has learned how to see….It is without detail, without visible effort, without dramatic interest; but it is exquisite in color, flawless in its overall harmony of light and shade, and its carefully considered sense of mystery has, at least, the appeal of leaving something to the imagination."

CuratorialText:
Plate with a flat rim and peacock in the center. Clay: buff-colored. Glaze: transparent over blue, green, red and black painted over a white paste. Decoration: Peacock amidst flowers in the center with a scroll of half-palmettes on the rim; eight blossoms on the exterior.
Greek letters on label: (^o^) and No. 48.
persona3_adapted: This plate has a flat rim and features a peacock at its center. It is made from a light brown clay. The surface is covered with a clear, glass-like coating called a glaze, applied over white clay paste. On top of this, the artist painted colors including blue, green, red, and black. The central design shows a peacock surrounded by flowers. Around the rim, there is a repeating pattern of half-palmettes—a decorative motif shaped like a half-palm leaf. On the outside of the plate, there are eight blossoms. A label includes Greek letters and the number 48.

CuratorialText: Molded Yue-ware vessels like this were made in large quantities, but their function is unclear. They may have held a candle, or perhaps scholars dipped their writing brushes in the water-filled interior. In the Jin dynasty (265-420 CE), mythical lions, with exaggeratedly curled manes, and winged chimeras were protective images; huge stone examples guarded important tombs.
persona3_adapted: Molded vessels of this type, made from a high-fired celadon ceramic known as Yue ware, were produced in large numbers, though their exact purpose remains uncertain. They may have held a candle, or perhaps scholars dipped their writing brushes into the water-filled interior. During the Jin dynasty (265–420 CE), mythical lions—with their exaggeratedly curled manes—and winged chimeras, or hybrid creatures, were used as protective symbols; massive stone versions of these animals stood guard over important tombs.

CuratorialText: A pair of blue and white molded porcelain beaker vases [F1992.47.1 and .2] decorated with freely drawn feather, foliate scroll and scattered flowerheads, each with a leaf or conch shell "emblem" mark on base. Part of a five-piece garniture set with three jars.
persona3_adapted: A pair of blue-and-white porcelain vases, shaped like tall, flared beakers [F1992.47.1 and .2]. Their decoration features loosely painted feathers, curling leaf patterns, and scattered flower blossoms. On the base of each vase is a mark resembling a leaf or a conch shell—a type of symbolic emblem used by the maker. These vases were originally part of a five-piece decorative set, which also included three jars.

CuratorialText:
The Kiyotaki Waterfall is located along the route of the Tokaido, the great road passing through the eastern provinces of Japan and linking the city of Edo (now Tokyo), seat of the Tokugawa shogunate, with Kyoto, the capital and home of the emperor.
In this print from the series, Shokoku taki meguri (Famous Waterfalls in Various Provinces), the Kiyotaki Waterfall descends in branching rivulets over a sheer, rock face. In the foreground, pilgrims ascend from a teahouse toward a shrine dedicated to Kannon, a Buddhist deity associated with compassion. The remoteness and altitude of this scene is emphasized by the bands of clouds at the lower left.
persona3_adapted:
The Kiyotaki Waterfall is located along the route of the Tokaido, the great road that passes through the eastern provinces of Japan and connects the city of Edo (now Tokyo)—the seat of the Tokugawa shogunate, or military government—with Kyoto, the capital and home of the emperor.

In this print from the series *Shokoku taki meguri* (Famous Waterfalls in Various Provinces), the Kiyotaki Waterfall flows down a steep, bare rock face in branching streams. In the foreground, pilgrims climb from a teahouse toward a shrine dedicated to Kannon, a Buddhist deity associated with compassion. The sense of remoteness and high altitude in this scene is reinforced by the bands of clouds visible in the lower left corner.

CuratorialText: Small dish with pleated rim. Clay: buff pottery. Glaze: transparent glaze with olive greenish tone (Ofuke glaze), large irregular cracks stained brown; glaze deeper green in tone where thick. Interior of footrim glazed; glazed wiped off outside of footrim and surrounding base. Decoration: pleating on narrow everted rim. The form of this dish with fluted rim distantly echoes the shape of Chinese Longquan-ware celadon dishes.
persona3_adapted: Small dish with a ruffled edge. Made from light brown clay. Covered in a clear glaze with an olive-green tint (a type known as Ofuke glaze), which has large, uneven cracks that have been stained brown; the glaze appears a deeper green where it is thicker. The inside of the small ring at the base is glazed, while the glaze has been wiped away from the outside of the ring and the surrounding bottom area. Decoration consists of pleating along the narrow, outward-turned rim. The shape of this dish, with its fluted edge, loosely recalls the form of Chinese Longquan-style celadon dishes.

CuratorialText:
The Khamsa, or Quintet, by Nizami ranks among the great masterpieces of Persian literature. The romance of Khosraw and Shirin, one of the five poems that constitute the Khamsa, narrates the adventures of the Sasanian king Khusraw Parviz and the Armenian princess Shirin. Attributed to the early fifteenth century, the Freer Khamsa is one of the earliest known illustrated copies of this work.
Khosraw at the Gate of Shirin's Castle is among the most poignantly expressive manuscript paintings of the fifteenth century. Displeased with Khosraw's behavior and his marriage to another woman, Shirin has withdrawn to her castle. Following the death of his queen, Khosraw repeatedly attempts to see Shirin, who finally yields to his request. Shielded by the royal umbrella and standing on precious brocades and carpets, the king gestures in supplication toward Shirin, who gazes down from a window in her castle. Although the text specifies that Khosraw visited Shirin at dawn in the dead of winter, the artist has situated the reconciliation in a lush garden setting softly illuminated by a waning moon. The elegant, mustachioed Khosraw in these paintings bears a striking resemblance to known depictions of the Timurid patron Baysunghur Mirza (1399-1434).
persona3_adapted:
The *Khamsa* (or *Quintet*) by the poet Nizami is considered one of the great masterpieces of Persian literature. One of its five poems, the romance of *Khosraw and Shirin*, tells the story of the Sasanian king Khusraw Parviz and the Armenian princess Shirin. The Freer *Khamsa*, thought to date from the early fifteenth century, is one of the earliest known copies of this work to include painted illustrations.

*Khosraw at the Gate of Shirin’s Castle* is among the most emotionally powerful manuscript paintings of the fifteenth century. Unhappy with Khosraw’s behavior and his marriage to another woman, Shirin has retreated to her castle. After the death of his queen, Khosraw tries repeatedly to see Shirin, who eventually agrees to his request. Standing beneath the royal umbrella and on precious fabrics and carpets, the king gestures in a plea toward Shirin, who looks down from a castle window. Although the text says that Khosraw visited Shirin at dawn in the middle of winter, the artist has placed the scene of reconciliation in a lush garden, softly lit by a waning moon. The elegant, mustachioed Khosraw in these paintings closely resembles known images of the Timurid royal patron Baysunghur Mirza (1399–1434).

CuratorialText: Set in the London home of Whistler's brother-in-law Seymour Haden, this painting was originally titled The Morning Call. The woman reflected in the mirror at the left is Whistler's half sister Deborah Haden. The seated girl is his niece, Annie Haden. The standing woman, Miss Isabella Boott, was connected to the Haden family by marriage. Instead of trying to create a convincing illusion of three-dimensional space, Whistler adopted a color scheme and sharply angled perspective that emphasize the flatness of the picture plane.
persona3_adapted: Set in the London home of Whistler's brother-in-law Seymour Haden, this painting was originally titled *The Morning Call*. The woman reflected in the mirror on the left is Whistler's half-sister Deborah Haden. The seated girl is his niece, Annie Haden. The standing woman, Miss Isabella Boott, was connected to the Haden family by marriage. Rather than trying to create a realistic sense of depth and three-dimensional space, Whistler used a carefully chosen color scheme and a sharply tilted angle that draw attention to the flat surface of the canvas itself.

CuratorialText: Popular tales and lively images played a role in transmitting beliefs and values to the Jain community. The Shripalarasa is a narrative that recounts the wondrous adventures of the Jain Prince Shripala, who travels to distant lands in search of trade and wealth. In this unusually large and vivacious painting, the artist represents the Prince's ship as a British vessel. The inscriptions scattered across the painting, which repetively identify parts of the ship, indicate a fascination with seafaring. The depiction of the Union Jack lends immediacy to a story that took place long before the British came to India.
persona3_adapted: Popular stories and lively pictures helped pass down beliefs and values within the Jain community. The *Shripalarasa* is a tale that follows the exciting adventures of the Jain Prince Shripala as he travels to faraway lands in search of trade and wealth. In this unusually large and vibrant painting, the artist shows the Prince’s ship as a British vessel. The labels scattered across the painting, which repeatedly name parts of the ship, suggest a strong curiosity about seafaring. The presence of the British flag (the Union Jack) brings a sense of immediacy to a story that takes place long before the British arrived in India.

CuratorialText: Funeral vase: tall, slender, on hollowed, spreading feet. Wood stand. Clay: soft, light-weight, gray-white. Glaze: thin, transparent green celadon; close brown crackle. Areas of stain and of over-baked wrinkled glaze. Decoration: hieratic figures and a dragon supporting the sun-disk - in applied relief, under glaze.
persona3_adapted: This tall, slender funeral vase stands on a hollowed, flared base, which rests on a wooden stand. It is made of a soft, lightweight, gray-white clay. The surface is covered with a thin, transparent green glaze of a type known as celadon, which shows a fine network of brown cracks—a common effect in this kind of ceramic. Some areas are stained, and others have a wrinkled appearance from being over-fired in the kiln. The decoration, applied in relief (that is, raised from the surface) and then covered with glaze, shows formal, stylized figures and a dragon holding up a disk representing the sun.

CuratorialText: The Tale of Shuten Doji has long been popular in Japan; the earliest-known illustrated version of this tale dates to the fourteenth century. Minamoto no Yorimitsu (948-1021), a historical figure of great martial prowess, was transformed in popular literature into a legendary hero who rescued the realm from demonic forces. Shuten Doji, a monster who lived southwest of Kyoto, terrorized the population, dining on locals and holding young maidens captive. With divine assistance and various means of subterfuge, Yorimitsu beheaded the ogre and returned the region to tranquility. Kyosai's interest in the narrative format and his careful connoisseurship of ancient works of art are evident in his paintings and sketchbooks. Riddled with macabre humor and images of the grotesque, his own paintings catered to the tastes of late Edo (1615-1868) and early Meiji (1868-1912) audiences.
persona3_adapted: The Tale of Shuten Doji has long been popular in Japan; the earliest-known illustrated version of this story dates back to the fourteenth century. Minamoto no Yorimitsu (948-1021), a real historical figure known for his great skill in combat, was transformed in popular literature into a legendary hero who saved the land from demonic forces. Shuten Doji, a monster who lived southwest of Kyoto, terrorized the population by eating local people and holding young women captive. With help from the gods and through various acts of deception, Yorimitsu beheaded the ogre and restored peace to the region. Kyosai's interest in telling stories through sequential images, as well as his careful study of older works of art, is clear in his paintings and sketchbooks. Filled with dark humor and disturbing, exaggerated imagery, his own paintings appealed to the tastes of audiences in the late Edo period (1615-1868) and early Meiji period (1868-1912).

CuratorialText: Hassam visited Oregon during the summer of 1908, completing this view of Portland's Chinatown after his return to New York. The subject matter is unusual for Hassam, but recalls a series of shop-front scenes painted by Whistler in the 1880s. Like Whistler, Hassam treated the street as a shallow stage set, emphasizing the geometrical patterns created by the play of variously colored rectangular shaped-bricks, signs, windows, doors, walls, sidewalk, alley, and the white diagonal of broken light splayed across the near foreground.
persona3_adapted: In the summer of 1908, Hassam visited Oregon and, after returning to New York, completed this painting of Portland’s Chinatown. The scene is unusual for Hassam, but it recalls a series of storefront images painted by Whistler in the 1880s. Like Whistler, Hassam presents the street as if it were a shallow stage, drawing attention to the geometric patterns formed by the interplay of differently colored rectangular shapes—bricks, signs, windows, doors, walls, sidewalk, and alley—along with the white diagonal of broken light spreading across the foreground.

CuratorialText: Tall, blue and white glazed porcelain vase, with long neck, round bulbous body and "garlic"-shaped neck/mouth. Round ring base, slightly uneven. The body decorated with scrolling, flowering vines and animals.
persona3_adapted: A tall porcelain vase, glazed in blue and white, features a long neck, a rounded, bulbous body, and a mouth shaped like a garlic bulb. Its base is a slightly uneven circular ring. The body is decorated with winding, flowering vines and animals.

CuratorialText: Legendary and theatrical references abound in the decoration of the cover of this lacquer box for an inkstone, a water dropper, and writing brushes. The Wisteria Maiden (Fuji Musume), whose silvery facial features have oxidized to bluish black, is a common subject of Otsu-e, a type of folk painting sold in the town of Otsu near Lake Biwa. In nineteenth-century kabuki, the Wisteria Maiden became the focus of a dance expressing unrequited love to the vocal and instrumental music of a nagauta (literally, "long song"). With her is a praying demon, another figure often seen in Otsu-e. The Thunder God rushes down from above, beating his ring of drums as the couple takes shelter from a rainstorm. The ornate pictorial decoration of this box was created by an artist who had mastered maki-e techniques, which employ powders and particles of gold and other material to create intricate designs.
persona3_adapted: The decoration on the lid of this lacquer box—designed to hold an inkstone, a water dropper, and writing brushes—is filled with legendary and theatrical references. The figure of the Wisteria Maiden (Fuji Musume), whose silver facial features have darkened to a bluish black over time, is a common subject in Otsu-e, a type of folk painting sold in the town of Otsu near Lake Biwa. In nineteenth-century kabuki theater, the Wisteria Maiden became the focus of a dance expressing unrequited love, performed to the vocal and instrumental music of a nagauta (literally, "long song"). She is accompanied by a praying demon, another familiar figure in Otsu-e. Above them, the Thunder God rushes down, beating his ring of drums, as the couple takes shelter from a rainstorm. The ornate pictorial decoration of this box was created by an artist who had mastered maki-e techniques, which use powders and particles of gold and other materials to produce intricate designs.

CuratorialText: Bowl, conical, flaring; deep foot with four silica spur-marks underneath on base inside footrim. Six notches in rim. Clay: dense, sonant. Glaze: gray-green celadon; crackled in long lines. Decoration: molded and incised in paste under glaze: rosette in bottom; hibiscus flowers and foliage filling sides: single line incised below rim.
persona3_adapted: This bowl has a conical shape that flares outward at the top. It sits on a tall foot, and on the underside of the base, inside the foot rim, are four small marks left by silica supports used during firing. The rim has six notches. The clay body is dense and produces a ringing sound when tapped. The glaze is a gray-green celadon—a type of pale, jade-like finish—and it shows long, fine cracks across the surface. The decoration was created by pressing and carving patterns into the clay before glazing: a rosette at the center, with hibiscus flowers and leaves covering the sides, and a single incised line just below the rim.

CuratorialText: Depicts a hilly landscape, with a scraggly tree, and a Central Asian groom in a red jacket holding the bridle of a dappled horse with trappings and a saddle. Spurious inscription and one seal, no colophons or labels.
persona3_adapted: This painting shows a hilly landscape with a sparse, uneven tree. In the scene, a Central Asian groom wearing a red jacket holds the reins of a dappled horse, which is fitted with decorative gear and a saddle. The work includes a false or forged inscription and one seal, but it has no colophons (the artist’s or scribe’s notes often found at the end of a manuscript) or identifying labels.

CuratorialText: A rectangular, off-white frit tile with an opaque white underglaze. Overglaze painted with yellow, green, turquoise, blue and manganese black/purple outlines depicting a bird perched on a fruit tree branch. Sides and back are unglazed.
persona3_adapted: A rectangular tile made from a type of glassy ceramic material, with a matte white base layer applied before firing. On top of this, the artist painted a bird sitting on a branch of a fruit tree, using colors such as yellow, green, turquoise, blue, and a dark purple-black for the outlines. The sides and back of the tile were left unglazed, meaning they were not coated with the glassy finish.

CuratorialText: Painting is one of a series of eight views of Tokyo by Ogata Gekko. This view of a racetrack focuses on the action in the foreground, where spectators are enthralled by the competition. Contained in modern wooden box with lid.
persona3_adapted: This painting is one of eight scenes of Tokyo created by the artist Ogata Gekko. The view of a racetrack draws attention to the activity in the foreground, where the crowd is captivated by the race. The work is housed in a modern wooden box with a lid.

CuratorialText: Folio from a dispersed copy of Yusuf u Zulaykha by Jami; Persian in black nasta'liq script on gold-flecked paper; heading in red; recto: text: 2 columns, 14 lines; verso: text: 2 columns, 14 lines; one of a group of 13. Border: The recto is set in gold, green, and black rulings in a margin with birds, floral, and leaf scrolls. The verso is set in gold, green, and black rulings in a margin with lions, bulls, foxes, rabbits,and birds in a landscape.
persona3_adapted:
A single page from a dispersed copy of *Yusuf u Zulaykha*, a Persian poem by Jami. The text is written in black *nasta'liq* script—a flowing, elegant style of Persian calligraphy—on paper flecked with gold. Headings appear in red. Each side of the page contains two columns of text, with 14 lines per column. This page is one of a set of 13.

The front of the page is framed by rulings—thin decorative lines—in gold, green, and black, set within a border featuring birds and scrolling patterns of flowers and leaves. The back of the page has similar rulings, but its border depicts a landscape with lions, bulls, foxes, rabbits, and birds.

CuratorialText: Squared, hollow cylinder of the type ts’ung [cong] 琮; narrow projecting collar at both ends; rich cream and tans; decoration: incised and in low relief, stylized masks and double bands of fine lines on corners. (Collar chipped.)
persona3_adapted: A squared, hollow cylinder known as a *cong* (a type of ancient Chinese ritual object), with a narrow projecting rim at both ends. The surface is a rich cream color with tan tones. The decoration is carved into the surface and slightly raised, featuring stylized face-like designs and double bands of fine lines along the corners. (The rim is chipped.)

CuratorialText: One of a pair of blue and white vases, Kangxi period, each of trumpet shape form with flared foot, after a bronze Ku, each of the three sections with large loose lotus scroll, the central lobed section encircled by a diaper filled band.
persona3_adapted: One of a pair of blue-and-white vases from the Kangxi period (1661–1722). Each vase has a trumpet-like shape with a flared base, modeled after an ancient Chinese bronze vessel known as a *gu*. The three sections of each vase are decorated with large, flowing lotus scrolls. The central, lobed section is encircled by a band filled with a repeating diamond pattern, a design often called "diaper" in decorative arts.

CuratorialText: Pitcher, of a full-bodied pear form, on a high spreading foot; plaster restorations including handle and foot. Clay: soft, sandy, white. Glaze: turquoise green, partly disintegrated and having a silver iridescence.
persona3_adapted: This pitcher, shaped like a full, rounded pear, sits on a tall base that widens toward the bottom. The handle and base have been repaired with plaster. The clay body is soft, sandy, and white. The glaze is a turquoise green, though parts of it have broken down over time, giving the surface a shimmering, silvery quality.

CuratorialText: Bowl, deep ovoidal; low foot with kiln-slag attachments. Clay: hard. Glaze: brilliant olive-gray, finely crackled. Decoration: molded in the paste in light relief, under glaze; single large floral motif (peony?) filling bottom, lower half of walls, difficult to decipher under glaze.
persona3_adapted: This bowl has a deep, egg-like shape and sits on a short base, where small bits of kiln material remain attached from the firing process. The clay body is hard. The glaze is a brilliant olive-gray with a fine network of tiny cracks across its surface. The decoration was pressed into the clay itself, creating a subtle raised pattern beneath the glaze. A single large floral design—possibly a peony—fills the bottom and lower sides, though the details are hard to make out through the glaze.

CuratorialText: Clay: dense, resonant, buff stoneware. Glaze: transparent, crackled, over white slip; unglazed area stained blackish-brown. Decoration: in cobalt blue, under glaze: linear patterns representing maple leaves and water or pampas grass; geometric bands on sides of lid and base. Square seal on underside of lid.
persona3_adapted: This object is made from a dense, sturdy type of clay called stoneware, which has a warm, tan color and produces a deep, ringing sound when tapped. Its surface is covered with a clear, glass-like coating that has a fine network of tiny cracks, applied over a layer of white liquid clay (known as slip). The areas left unglazed have turned a dark brownish-black. The decoration, painted in a rich blue pigment beneath the glaze, consists of flowing lines that suggest maple leaves and either water or tall grasses. Geometric bands run along the edges of the lid and base. A small square stamp is pressed into the underside of the lid.

CuratorialText: Tall blue and white glazed porcelain vase, with bulbous lower body, cylindrical center body with pinched "snake" handles and slightly outflaring rim. Porcelain body with dark blue cobalt underglaze with gray-blue-white glaze. Floral decorative patterns.
persona3_adapted: A tall porcelain vase, glazed in shades of blue and white. It has a rounded lower section, a straight central body, and a slightly flared rim. The vase features two narrow, pinched handles that curve outward like snakes. The porcelain surface is decorated with floral patterns, painted in a dark blue pigment applied beneath the glaze, which gives the piece a gray-blue-white finish.

CuratorialText: Dish, low, with rounded sides; one chip on rim; hair-line crack. Clay: white porcelain. Glaze: clear, feldspathic; a few pinholes and impurity flecks. Decoration: painted in underglaze cobalt blue of deep tone, copying a traditional design of the Ch'eng-hua period. See F1951.10 and .11. Center inside, "three friends of winter" (pine, plum, and bamboo) with garden rock, and fungus, enclosed by double line band which is repeated at rim. Outside, following model, a scene of figures on a garden terrace with plants and trees.
persona3_adapted: This shallow, round-sided dish has a small chip on its rim and a fine, hairline crack. It is made of white porcelain. The clear glaze, which contains feldspar (a mineral that helps create a glassy surface), shows a few tiny pinholes and small specks of impurities. The decoration is painted in a deep blue pigment applied before the glaze was fired, a technique known as underglaze blue. The design copies a traditional style from the Ch'eng-hua period (a famous era in Chinese porcelain history). Inside the dish, at the center, is a motif called the "three friends of winter"—pine, plum, and bamboo—along with a garden rock and a fungus. This central image is enclosed by a double line, a pattern repeated around the rim. On the outside, following the original model, a scene shows figures on a garden terrace surrounded by plants and trees.

CuratorialText: Individual serving bowl (mukozuke [JPN]). Black lacquer rim. Red lacquer repairs. Clay: hard, light-fawn. Shiny brown on surface. Glaze: yellow and gray over blue-green; inside--brilliant brown and blue. Outside: compounded with iron and rice-straw ash. Inside: clear glaze.
persona3_adapted: Individual serving bowl (mukozuke, a type of Japanese dish). The rim is finished with black lacquer, and there are visible repairs done in red lacquer. The clay body is hard and a light fawn color, with a shiny brown surface. The glaze—a glassy coating applied to the pottery—appears in shades of yellow and gray over a blue-green base. Inside the bowl, the glaze is a brilliant combination of brown and blue. On the outside, the glaze is made from a mixture that includes iron and rice-straw ash. Inside, a clear glaze has been used.

CuratorialText: White clay, pinkish buff where exposed. Decoration of grasses incised under clear brown wash; hatching incised under clear glaze (body indented in each area); and flower and grasses painted in black overglaze enamel. Kiln mark stamped on base inside foot rim. Thick iron wash over half, appearing dull yellowish brown; clear glaze on other half of body, appearing gray where it partially overlaps wash. Five spur marks (from clay wads) on foot rim.
persona3_adapted: This vessel is made of white clay, which appears pinkish-buff in areas where the surface is exposed. The decoration combines several techniques: grasses are carved into the clay beneath a clear brown wash (a thin, liquid clay coating); hatching (fine, closely spaced lines) is carved under a clear glaze (a glassy coating that fuses to the clay during firing), with the body indented in each hatched area; and flowers and grasses are painted on top of the glaze using a black enamel (a type of glassy paint applied after the first firing). A kiln mark—a stamp identifying the workshop—is pressed into the base inside the foot rim (the raised ring on which the vessel rests). Half of the body is covered with a thick iron wash, which appears dull yellowish-brown; the other half has a clear glaze, which looks gray where it partially overlaps the wash. Five spur marks—small scars left by clay supports used during firing—are visible on the foot rim.

CuratorialText: Bowl, small, ovoidal, on a bold, spreading foot. Cracked and repaired. Clay: soft, grayish-white. Glaze: inside, a deep aubergine; outside now covered with an iridescent patina. Decoration: painted in black under glaze.
persona3_adapted: This small, egg-shaped bowl sits on a wide, flared base. It has been cracked and repaired. The clay body is soft and grayish-white. The inside is glazed a deep purple, while the outside is now covered with a shimmering, rainbow-like surface layer that developed over time. The decoration was painted in black before the glaze was applied.

CuratorialText: Mounted with F1937.39. Detached folio from a dispersed copy of Nuzhatnama-yi ala'i (Excellent book of counsel) by Shahmardan ibn Abi al-Khayr; text: Persian in black and red naskhi script; recto: illustration and text: Medicinal property of the mulberry tree, 2 lines, 3 paintings; verso: illustration and text: Pharmacological property of the olive tree, its leaves and fruits, 4 lines, 2 paintings; one of a group of three folios.
persona3_adapted: This detached page comes from a dispersed copy of the *Nuzhatnama-yi ‘Ala’i* (The Excellent Book of Counsel), written by Shahmardan ibn Abi al-Khayr. The text is in Persian, written in black and red ink in a style of calligraphy known as *naskhi*. On the front side, there is an illustration and text describing the medicinal uses of the mulberry tree, with two lines of script and three paintings. On the back side, the illustration and text explain the pharmacological properties of the olive tree, including its leaves and fruit, with four lines of script and two paintings. This folio is one of three from the same original manuscript.

CuratorialText: Both Corvus, "The Raven," and Centaurus, "The Centaur," are constellations of the southern hemisphere. Corvus is composed of seven stars while Centaurus, whose forepart is made up of a man and hindpart of a horse, consists of 37. Al-Kazwini records that the last two stars of Centaurus are called "the two sworn upon," and according to tradition one of these passes very close to the path of Corvus. Some people, writes al-Kazwini, believe that the star belongs to "the Raven" while others insist that it is part of "The Centaur."
persona3_adapted: Both Corvus ("The Raven") and Centaurus ("The Centaur") are constellations found in the southern hemisphere. Corvus is made up of seven stars, while Centaurus—whose front half is shaped like a man and back half like a horse—contains 37 stars. The medieval scholar al-Kazwini notes that the last two stars of Centaurus are known as "the two sworn upon," and according to tradition, one of these stars passes very close to the path of Corvus. Al-Kazwini writes that some people believe this star belongs to "The Raven," while others insist it is part of "The Centaur."

CuratorialText: Bowl, large, deep, ovoidal; heavy foot; cracked. Clay: heavy, coarse, gray. Glaze: white, with tinges of green; crackled and discolored to a brownish tone. Chipped along the rim. Decoration: painted in blue and black under glaze.
persona3_adapted: This large, deep bowl has an egg-like shape and a thick, sturdy base. It is cracked. The clay body is heavy, rough, and gray in color. The surface is covered in a white glaze with hints of green; the glaze has developed a fine network of cracks and has discolored to a brownish tone. There are chips along the rim. The decoration was painted in blue and black before the glaze was applied.

CuratorialText: Black-lacquered (shin nuri) cypress-wood utensil stand (shin daisu) with four black-lacquered posts and two shelves; four black-lacquered (shin nuri) cypress boards (shikiita) of various sizes for use with formal tea procedures.
persona3_adapted: A utensil stand made of cypress wood, coated in black lacquer (a glossy, protective finish), designed for use in formal tea ceremonies. It has four black-lacquered posts and two shelves, along with four black-lacquered cypress boards of different sizes.

CuratorialText: Vessel in the shape of a Shang dynasty ceremonial "ku;" copper decorated with cloisonne enamels in light blue, dark blue, green, red, yellow, and white; inside the foot is an inscription of eight characters in relief giving a K'ang-hsi date. A separate flower holder fits inside the mouth. During the Qing dynasty (1644-1911), the display of ancient objects as well as contemporary ones made in an archaistic style was considered a sign of learning and refined taste. The shape and decoration of this vase, including the mask-like faces on the base, reproduce aspects of a Bronze Age ritual vessel called a gu.
persona3_adapted:
This vessel takes the form of a *gu*, a type of ceremonial container from the Shang dynasty (roughly 1600–1046 BCE). It is made of copper and decorated with *cloisonné enamel*—a technique in which thin metal wires are attached to the surface to create small compartments, which are then filled with colored enamel paste and fired. Here, the colors include light blue, dark blue, green, red, yellow, and white. Inside the foot of the vessel, an inscription of eight raised characters gives a date corresponding to the reign of the Kangxi emperor (1661–1722) of the Qing dynasty.

A separate flower holder fits inside the mouth of the vase. During the Qing dynasty (1644–1911), displaying both ancient objects and contemporary pieces made in an antique style was seen as a mark of learning and refined taste. The shape and decoration of this vase—including the mask-like faces on its base—reproduce features of a Bronze Age ritual vessel known as a *gu*.

CuratorialText: Polychrome. Clay: fine white porcelain. Glaze: transparent, high fired. Decoration: dragons amid clouds incised in the paste and covered with brilliant green enamel; six-character Hung-chih mark on base. (Pair with F1952.20).
persona3_adapted: This object is made of fine white porcelain clay, coated with a transparent glaze that was fired at a high temperature. The decoration shows dragons among clouds, carved into the clay surface and then covered with a bright green enamel. On the base is a six-character mark identifying it as from the Hung-chih period. This piece is one of a pair, the other being F1952.20.

CuratorialText: Bulb-bowl: circular, on three cloud-scroll feet. Two rows of bosses. Incised numeral 1 (one) and spur-marks on base. Clay: hard, fine, light-gray. Glaze: inside:olive, strong lavender-blue and lighter green-blue; outside: rose-purple, blue, gray and olive.
persona3_adapted: This bowl, shaped like a bulb, is circular and rests on three feet carved to resemble curling clouds. Its surface is decorated with two rows of rounded, raised bumps. On the base, the number "1" has been carved into the clay, and small marks from the kiln supports used during firing are visible. The clay body is hard, fine-grained, and light gray in color. The glaze—the glassy coating applied to the pottery—varies dramatically between the inside and outside. Inside, the colors are olive, a strong lavender-blue, and a lighter green-blue. Outside, the glaze shifts to rose-purple, blue, gray, and olive.

CuratorialText:
The brilliant paintings on this pair of folding screens are regarded as masterpieces among only six surviving sets of screens by Sotatsu, a talented and innovative artist who headed a painting workshop known as Tawaraya. As a townsman, Sotatsu produced paintings such as fans for popular consumption. By the late 1620s, however, Sotatsu was painting for the imperial court, and his works survive in the collections of the Kyoto imperial palace. For his artistic merit, he was granted the honorary Buddhist ecclesiastical title Hokkyo (Bridge of the Law), which is included in his signature on these screens.
Matsushima (Pine Islands) is the name of a famous site (meisho) near Sendai, a city in Miyagi Prefecture in northeastern Japan. Its beauty inspired both poets and painters. In Sotatsu's screens the rock from which pine trees grow are rendered in brilliant mineral colors of green, blue, and brown, highlighted with gold. Waves in animated forms are delineated in alternating lines of ink and gold, producing a luminous effect. Clouds and embankments are rendered in particles of gold leaf accented with silver, which has darkened over time to a soft black tone.
Sotatsu's innovative composition creates a dynamic interplay among the land and cloud forms, the bending pines, and churning waves. His lifelong interest in pictorial composition left a lasting legacy. Later painters of the Rimpa school, such as Ogata Korin (1658-1716), repeated the Matsushima theme in their work. The Matsushima screens were probably painted in the early Kan'ei era (1624-44), a period of cultural efflorescence in Kyoto.
persona3_adapted:
The brilliant paintings on this pair of folding screens are considered masterpieces among only six surviving sets of screens by Sotatsu, a talented and innovative artist who ran a painting workshop known as Tawaraya. As a townsman, Sotatsu initially produced items like painted fans for the general public. By the late 1620s, however, he was painting for the imperial court, and his works remain in the collections of the Kyoto imperial palace. In recognition of his artistic skill, he was awarded the honorary Buddhist title Hokkyo (meaning "Bridge of the Law"), which he included in his signature on these screens.

Matsushima (Pine Islands) is the name of a famous scenic site near Sendai, a city in Miyagi Prefecture in northeastern Japan. Its beauty inspired both poets and painters. In Sotatsu's screens, the rocks from which pine trees grow are painted with vivid mineral colors—green, blue, and brown—highlighted with gold. Waves in lively, swirling forms are drawn with alternating lines of ink and gold, creating a glowing effect. Clouds and embankments are made with particles of gold leaf accented with silver, which has darkened over time to a soft black tone.

Sotatsu's inventive composition creates a dynamic interplay among the land and cloud forms, the bending pines, and the churning waves. His lifelong interest in how images are arranged on a surface left a lasting influence. Later painters of the Rimpa school, such as Ogata Korin (1658–1716), revisited the Matsushima theme in their own work. The Matsushima screens were likely painted in the early Kan'ei era (1624–44), a period of rich cultural flourishing in Kyoto.

CuratorialText:
The Tale of Shuten Doji has long been popular in Japan; the earliest-known illustrated version of this tale dates to the fourteenth century. Minamoto no Yorimitsu (948-1021), a historical figure of great martial prowess, was transformed in popular literature into a legendary hero who rescued the realm from demonic forces. Shuten Doji, a monster who lived southwest of Kyoto, terrorized the population, dining on locals and holding young maidens captive. With divine assistance and various means of subterfuge, Yorimitsu beheaded the ogre and returned the region to tranquility.
Kyosai's interest in the narrative format and his careful connoisseurship of ancient works of art are evident in his paintings and sketchbooks. Riddled with macabre humor and images of the grotesque, his own paintings catered to the tastes of late Edo (1615-1868) and early Meiji (1868-1912) audiences.
persona3_adapted:
The Tale of Shuten Doji has long been a popular story in Japan; the earliest-known illustrated version of this tale dates back to the fourteenth century. Minamoto no Yorimitsu (948-1021), a historical figure known for his great skill in combat, was transformed in popular literature into a legendary hero who saved the land from demonic forces. Shuten Doji, a monster who lived southwest of Kyoto, terrorized the population by eating local people and holding young women captive. With divine help and various acts of deception, Yorimitsu cut off the ogre's head and restored peace to the region.

Kyosai's interest in storytelling through images, as well as his careful study of older works of art, is clear in his paintings and sketchbooks. Filled with dark humor and disturbing, grotesque imagery, his own paintings appealed to the tastes of audiences in the late Edo period (1615-1868) and early Meiji period (1868-1912).

CuratorialText: Ceremonial sickle in four parts: three of bronze inlaid with turquoise, one (the blade) of jade decorated in linear relief with notched back and ground edge. No objects of this type have yet been scientifically excavated in China, so it is difficult to know the purpose of this extremely rare axe. It consists of four separate pieces: a tubular bronze handle, two caps with dragon motifs, and an unusual serrated jade blade that might not be original. Traces of wood inside the handle suggest it was lengthened with additional elements that are now missing.
persona3_adapted: This ceremonial sickle is made up of four separate pieces: three are bronze decorated with turquoise, and one—the blade—is carved from jade. The jade blade has a notched back and a ground edge, with a design carved in low relief (meaning the pattern stands out slightly from the surface). No objects like this have ever been found in a scientifically controlled excavation in China, so it is difficult to know the purpose of this extremely rare axe. The four parts include a tubular bronze handle, two caps decorated with dragon designs, and an unusual serrated jade blade that may not have originally belonged with the rest. Traces of wood found inside the handle suggest it was once extended with additional pieces that are now missing.

CuratorialText: This is one of a pair of folding screens illustrating two of the fifty-four chapters in The Tale of Genji, a narrative composed in the early eleventh century by a lady of the court known as Murasaki Shikibu. This fictional biography of Prince Genji, whose life was filled with romance and tragedy, was a frequent subject of Japanese paintings. This screen represents chapter fifty-one, Ukifune. The lady Ukifune and her lover Niou, drift together in a boat on a wintry day, sheltered by a folding screen and temporarily isolated in their tragic romance. The painter Kano Naonobu chose monochromatic ink and thin color washes for a theme that was more commonly illustrated in lavish color and gold. The dreamlike effect of the mists and washes is poetically evocative of the mood of each episode. Naonobu was an official painter who served the Tokugawa shogun.
persona3_adapted: This is one of a pair of folding screens that depict two of the fifty-four chapters from *The Tale of Genji*, a story written in the early eleventh century by a court lady known as Murasaki Shikibu. The tale, a fictional biography of Prince Genji, whose life was filled with romance and tragedy, was a frequent subject of Japanese paintings. This screen illustrates chapter fifty-one, titled *Ukifune*. In the scene, the lady Ukifune and her lover Niou drift together in a boat on a wintry day, sheltered by a folding screen and temporarily isolated in their tragic romance. The painter Kano Naonobu chose to work in black ink and thin, transparent layers of color—a restrained approach—for a theme that was more commonly shown in rich, vibrant colors and gold. The dreamlike quality of the mist and the delicate washes of paint poetically captures the mood of each episode. Naonobu was an official painter who served the Tokugawa shogun, the military ruler of Japan at the time.

CuratorialText: Ornament of roughly rectangular shape; translucent mottled tan and cream color with areas of white film of incipient disintegration; blunt blade edge is indicated by lightly incised line and beveling; upper edge has irregular notches and a narrow projection; satin smooth surface. (One small chip). Acquired with a box, now lost.
persona3_adapted: This small rectangular ornament is made of a translucent stone with a mottled tan and cream color. Some areas show a white film, which is a sign that the surface is beginning to break down with age. The blunt edge of the blade is suggested by a lightly scratched line and a slight slope or bevel. The upper edge has irregular notches and a narrow projection. The surface is smooth, like satin. There is one small chip. The piece was originally acquired with a box, which is now lost.

CuratorialText: Jar, large, ovoidal, on a low, thick foot rim. Clay: soft, grayish. Glaze: variable aubergine, frosted with whitish areas of partial disintegration, with a slight iridescence. Earthy attachments. Decoration: impressed, incised and modelled in the paste.
persona3_adapted: This large, egg-shaped jar sits on a short, thick base. It is made of soft, grayish clay. The surface is covered with a glaze that shifts in shades of purple, with frosty white patches where the glaze has partly worn away, giving it a faint, shimmering quality. There are earthy-looking attachments on the surface. The decoration was created by pressing, carving, and shaping the clay itself before it was fired.

CuratorialText: Bowl: deep, ovoidal; low foot. Clay: hard, white. Glaze: pale green, mottled with blue-violet inside; gray-olive on rim and edge of flow; finely pitted.
persona3_adapted: This bowl is deep and egg-shaped, with a short base. It is made of hard, white clay. The glaze—a glassy coating—is a pale green, speckled with blue-violet on the inside. On the rim and where the glaze drips, the color shifts to a gray-olive. The surface is finely pitted, like tiny dimples.

CuratorialText: Chinese Han Glazed pottery " Hill-jar"; cylindrical vessel with cover; three bear-shaped feet; spur marks. Clay: reddish, sandy texture; fired medium. Glaze: lead silicate; deep green with areas of silvery iridescence, earth incrustations. Decoration: horizontal panels moulded in relief with animals a nd horsemen; two simulated mask and ring handles; cover represents mountains with animals in relief.
persona3_adapted: This is a Chinese jar from the Han dynasty, known as a "hill jar." It is a cylindrical container with a lid, resting on three feet shaped like bears. The jar shows small marks on its surface from the supports used during firing. The clay body is reddish and sandy in texture, and it was fired at a medium temperature. The glaze is made from lead and silica, giving it a deep green color. In some areas, the glaze has developed a silvery, rainbow-like sheen, and there are also crusts of earth from being buried. The decoration is arranged in horizontal bands, molded in relief—meaning the designs stand out from the surface. These bands show animals and horsemen. There are also two handles shaped like masks holding rings, though these are decorative and not functional. The lid represents a mountain landscape, with animals also molded in relief.

CuratorialText: Dive into the artistic process of one of history’s most beloved and influential artists. A true titan of the Italian Renaissance, Raffaello di Giovanni Santi (1483–1520)—better known as Raphael—matched ambition with lyricism to create works with both intellectual heft and emotional depth, a necessary skill in the complex political landscape of Renaissance courts. In his short life of only 37 years, he achieved such profound success as a painter, designer, and architect that he was regarded as the pinnacle of artistic perfection for centuries after his death. Raphael: Sublime Poetry is the first comprehensive exhibition on Raphael in the United States, bringing together more than 170 of the artist’s greatest masterpieces and rarely seen treasures to illuminate the brilliance of Raphael’s extraordinary creativity. The son of a painter and poet, Raphael engaged with the foremost writers and thinkers of his age in Rome, displaying a poetic sensibility that captivated his peers and generations that followed. Follow the full breadth of his life and career, from his origins in Urbino to his rise in Florence, where he began to emerge as a peer of Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo, to his final, prolific decade at the papal court in Rome. To underscore the range of his genius, this presentation brings together important drawings, paintings, and tapestries from public and private collections across Europe and the United States, many of which have never been shown together. With particular attention to Raphael’s portrayal of women—from his use of nude female models for the first time in Western art to his tender depictions of the Madonna and Child—and recent scientific discoveries made using state-of-the-art technology, this exhibition offers a rare opportunity to experience the genius of an artist who helped shape the course of art history.
persona3_adapted: Explore the creative process of one of history’s most beloved and influential artists. A towering figure of the Italian Renaissance, Raffaello di Giovanni Santi (1483–1520)—better known as Raphael—combined bold ambition with a graceful, poetic style to produce works that are both intellectually rich and emotionally powerful. This was an essential skill in the complex political world of Renaissance courts. In his short life of only 37 years, he achieved such remarkable success as a painter, designer, and architect that for centuries after his death, he was seen as the ultimate standard of artistic perfection. *Raphael: Sublime Poetry* is the first major exhibition in the United States devoted entirely to Raphael, bringing together more than 170 of his greatest works and rarely seen treasures to reveal the brilliance of his extraordinary imagination. The son of a painter and poet, Raphael engaged with the leading writers and thinkers of his time in Rome, displaying a poetic sensitivity that captivated his contemporaries and generations to come. Follow the full arc of his life and career—from his early years in Urbino, to his rise in Florence, where he began to rival Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo, and finally to his last, highly productive decade at the papal court in Rome. To highlight the breadth of his talent, this exhibition brings together important drawings, paintings, and tapestries from public and private collections across Europe and the United States, many of which have never been displayed together. With special focus on Raphael’s portrayal of women—from his groundbreaking use of nude female models in Western art to his tender images of the Madonna and Child—and recent scientific discoveries made using advanced technology, this exhibition offers a rare chance to experience the genius of an artist who helped shape the course of art history.

CuratorialText: One of the greatest influencers of all time, Raphael (1483 –1520) was idolized for more than three centuries, in Europe and beyond, as the painter of supreme perfection and the role model for artists in training. During his short life, his contemporaries celebrated his enormous creative facility, and later his fame towered above that of other Italian Renaissance stars. Born Raffaello di Giovanni Santi, the ambitious young artist soon left behind his modest origins in the Marche region of central Italy in search of important patrons in Umbria and Tuscany. In Florence, then the capital of the European art world, he emerged as a peer to the elder Leonardo and Michelangelo. Raphael spent his final decade as the favorite artist of the popes in Rome, where he was praised as the “prince of painters.” A savvy businessman and impresario, he worked with well-organized teams of assistants and collaborators to propagate his inventions and style, spawning a new generation of noteworthy artists.
persona3_adapted: One of the most influential figures in art history, Raphael (1483–1520) was admired for over three hundred years, across Europe and beyond, as the painter of flawless beauty and the ideal model for artists learning their craft. During his short life, his contemporaries celebrated his remarkable natural talent, and later his reputation surpassed that of other great Italian Renaissance stars. Born Raffaello di Giovanni Santi, the ambitious young artist soon left behind his modest roots in the Marche region of central Italy to seek out important supporters in Umbria and Tuscany. In Florence, then the center of the European art world, he became an equal to the older masters Leonardo and Michelangelo. Raphael spent his final ten years as the favorite artist of the popes in Rome, where he was hailed as the “prince of painters.” A shrewd businessman and organizer, he worked with well-coordinated teams of assistants and collaborators to spread his ideas and style, inspiring a new generation of notable artists.

CuratorialText: This first comprehensive U.S. exhibition on Raphael provides an immersive look at his meteoric career. Together, the drawings, paintings, prints, and tapestries on view present a portrait of his dashing artistic personality and astonishingly creative mind. This is also the story of an artist who was born to a poet-painter, became an intimate friend to literary figures, and tried his hand at composing sonnets. Raphael lived and worked in a culture accustomed to thinking of painting and poetry as intertwined sister arts.
