The elegance and dramatic force of his imagery still have the power to invoke the ancient dictum, much discussed in his time, that “painting is a mute poetry, and poetry is a blind painting.”
persona3_adapted: This first comprehensive U.S. exhibition on Raphael offers an in-depth look at his remarkably swift rise to fame. Together, the drawings, paintings, prints, and tapestries on display reveal both his bold artistic personality and his astonishingly inventive mind. This is also the story of an artist who was born to a poet-painter, became a close friend of literary figures, and even tried his hand at writing sonnets. Raphael lived and worked in a culture that saw painting and poetry as deeply connected forms of art—often called "sister arts." The elegance and dramatic power of his images still have the ability to bring to mind the ancient saying, much debated in his day, that "painting is a silent poetry, and poetry is a blind painting."

CuratorialText: Raphael was born on April 6, 1483, in Urbino, a hill town set in a spectacular, rugged landscape in the Marche region, in east central Italy. The artist’s roots in Urbino, with its refined court life and unique intellectual heritage, played a defining role in his life and career. He proudly identified with his birthplace and signed his paintings “RAPHAEL VRBINAS” to the end of his days.
persona3_adapted: Raphael was born on April 6, 1483, in Urbino, a hill town set in a spectacular, rugged landscape in the Marche region of east central Italy. The artist’s roots in Urbino, with its refined court life and unique intellectual heritage, played a defining role in his life and career. He proudly identified with his birthplace and signed his paintings “RAPHAEL VRBINAS” to the end of his days.

CuratorialText: Raphael received his earliest training from his father, Giovanni Santi (ca. 1439–1494), a court painter and prolific poet. During Raphael’s childhood, Giovanni was busy composing an epic poem on Federico III da Montefeltro (1422–1482), the famous ruler who had brought peace, wealth, and fame to the Duchy of Urbino. A formidable general, scholar, and patron of art and architecture, Federico transformed his court into an internationally celebrated center for the arts and humanist learning. He founded a fabled library and rebuilt Urbino’s imposing Palazzo Ducale, whose rhythmic arcades and harmonic geometry embodied the humanist dedication to mathematics that distinguished the culture of Urbino. Those same ideals are evident in the vibrant architectural visions—enlivened with perspectival effects and classical references—created by Urbino painters.
persona3_adapted: Raphael’s earliest training came from his father, Giovanni Santi (ca. 1439–1494), who worked as a painter for the court and was also a highly productive poet. During Raphael’s childhood, Giovanni was writing a long epic poem about Federico III da Montefeltro (1422–1482), the famous ruler who brought peace, wealth, and fame to the Duchy of Urbino. Federico was a powerful military leader, a scholar, and a generous supporter of art and architecture. He turned his court into an internationally admired center for the arts and for humanist learning—a way of thinking that focused on human potential, classical knowledge, and education. He built a legendary library and rebuilt Urbino’s grand Palazzo Ducale (the Duke’s palace), whose repeating arched walkways and balanced, mathematically harmonious design reflected the humanist belief in the importance of mathematics—a value that set Urbino’s culture apart. These same principles can be seen in the lively architectural scenes created by Urbino’s painters, which are brought to life with techniques that create a sense of depth and include references to classical styles.

CuratorialText: Raphael’s father soon brought his precocious son to study with Pietro Perugino (1446/50–1523), an artist he likely knew through local projects. A brilliant, prolific painter and draftsman with active workshops in Perugia and Florence, Perugino relied heavily on collaboration with assistants to meet the demands of patrons all over Italy. Raphael coursed quickly through the stages of pupil and apprentice to become first a collaborator to Perugino and then an independent artist. Perugino’s elegant figures, breathtaking command of technique, and efficient methods for reproducing designs left their mark on the younger artist.
persona3_adapted: Raphael’s father soon brought his exceptionally talented son to study with Pietro Perugino (1446/50–1523), an artist he likely knew through local projects. Perugino was a brilliant and highly productive painter and draftsman (someone who creates detailed drawings as part of their artistic process). He ran busy workshops in Perugia and Florence, where he relied heavily on assistants working together with him to fulfill the many commissions he received from patrons across Italy. Raphael moved quickly through the stages of student and assistant, first becoming a collaborator with Perugino and then an independent artist in his own right. Perugino’s graceful figures, stunning mastery of his craft, and efficient methods for reproducing his designs left a lasting influence on the younger artist.

CuratorialText: Ambitious, studious, and disciplined, the young Raphael first sought to establish himself as an independent professional in Città di Castello, a small, wealthy town near Perugia, where he painted notable church altarpieces. Confraternities, religious institutions for regular citizens devoted to works of charity, were also major sources of patronage for artists in Umbria and the Marche. Gathered here are paintings and related studies done for confraternities by Perugino and the young Raphael—including the first painting fully in Raphael’s hand, as confirmed by its recent conservation treatment.
persona3_adapted: Ambitious, studious, and disciplined, the young Raphael first sought to establish himself as an independent professional in Città di Castello, a small, wealthy town near Perugia, where he painted notable works for church altars. Confraternities—religious organizations for ordinary citizens dedicated to charitable work—were also major sources of support for artists in the regions of Umbria and the Marche. Gathered here are paintings and related preparatory studies made for confraternities by Perugino and the young Raphael, including the first painting executed entirely by Raphael himself, as confirmed by its recent conservation treatment.

CuratorialText: From 1500 to 1507, the young Raphael endeavored to cultivate patrons by painting both monumental altarpieces and small-scale devotional works in the regions of the Marche, Umbria, and Tuscany. An iconic example is the large, multipart painting at the center of this gallery, made for nuns in Perugia and now known as the Colonna Altarpiece (after a later owner). The full ensemble is reunited here as a complete work for the first time since it was dismembered around 1663 and the individual paintings dispersed. Other notable projects include a mammoth altarpiece for the Baronci family chapel in Città di Castello, which was later destroyed in an earthquake and survives only in fragments and preliminary drawings, and a remarkable altarpiece for the Oddi family chapel in Perugia, represented here by studies and the painting that provided its base.
persona3_adapted: From 1500 to 1507, the young Raphael worked to build relationships with wealthy supporters by creating both large, formal religious paintings for church altars and smaller, personal devotional pieces in the regions of the Marche, Umbria, and Tuscany. A key example is the large, multi-panel painting at the center of this gallery, made for nuns in Perugia and now known as the Colonna Altarpiece (named after a later owner). The complete set of panels is brought together here as a single work for the first time since it was taken apart around 1663 and the individual paintings scattered. Other important projects include a massive altarpiece for the Baronci family chapel in Città di Castello, which was later destroyed in an earthquake and survives only in fragments and early sketches, and a notable altarpiece for the Oddi family chapel in Perugia, shown here through preparatory drawings and the painting that served as its base.

CuratorialText: Full-scale drawings for the Oddi Altarpiece reveal the workshop practices that Raphael had absorbed during his training and collaboration with Perugino. Dazzling studies on paper also show how he worked in a variety of media—black chalk, pen and ink, and metalpoint on prepared paper—often with a virtuosic command of technique. Drawings such as these likely served as his calling card and undoubtedly impressed his patrons.
persona3_adapted: Full-scale preparatory drawings for the Oddi Altarpiece reveal the studio methods that Raphael had learned during his training and collaboration with Perugino. Brilliant studies on paper also show how he worked in a variety of materials—black chalk, pen and ink, and a technique called metalpoint (using a metal stylus on specially coated paper)—often with a masterful command of his craft. Drawings like these likely served as his professional calling card and certainly impressed his patrons.

CuratorialText: Raphael may have been drawn to Florence after hearing fellow painters rave about rival full-scale designs (cartoons) for spectacular murals there by Leonardo and Michelangelo. Related works in this gallery convey the electrifying novelty and arresting sculptural power of those unrealized projects. While in Florence between 1504 and 1508, Raphael also studied Michelangelo’s sculptures and Leonardo’s paintings and drawings, promptly and seamlessly adapting aspects of their styles and techniques in his work.
persona3_adapted: Raphael may have been drawn to Florence after hearing fellow painters praise the rival full-scale preparatory drawings (called cartoons) that Leonardo and Michelangelo had created for spectacular wall paintings there. Works in this gallery related to those projects convey their electrifying novelty and striking, sculpture-like power—even though the murals themselves were never completed. During his time in Florence, from 1504 to 1508, Raphael also studied Michelangelo’s sculptures and Leonardo’s paintings and drawings, quickly and smoothly incorporating elements of their styles and techniques into his own work.

CuratorialText: Raphael also learned from his peers’ revolutionary approaches to inventing compositions. Leonardo’s theories about “sketching quickly,” in the fervor of artistic inspiration, transformed Raphael’s methods. Asserting that the effort to finish drawings and puzzle out details could dry up fresh ideas, Leonardo advised working out concepts in “rough sketches,” like a poet scrawling the first draft of a verse in messy handwriting with crossed-out words. Before Florence, Raphael had tended to develop compositions in a more artisanal way, figure by detailed figure, as he had learned from Perugino. Now, spontaneous sketching led him to more organic compositions pulsating with movement and vivid expression.
persona3_adapted: Raphael also learned from his peers’ groundbreaking ways of creating compositions. Leonardo’s ideas about “sketching quickly,” in the heat of artistic inspiration, changed how Raphael worked. Leonardo believed that spending too much effort finishing drawings and working out small details could stifle fresh ideas. He advised developing concepts in “rough sketches,” much like a poet writing a first draft in messy handwriting with crossed-out words. Before his time in Florence, Raphael had tended to build his compositions in a more step-by-step, craft-based way—working figure by detailed figure, as he had learned from his teacher Perugino. Now, spontaneous sketching led him to create more natural, flowing compositions full of movement and lively expression.

CuratorialText: The exposure to Leonardo’s and Michelangelo’s art infused Raphael’s compositions with a sense of space, sculptural monumentality, and expressive force. This was the result of an enormous amount of experimentation on paper. Sequences of drawings—from a sheet of sketches to studies of an arrangement based on three-dimensional clay or wax models—reveal Raphael’s increasingly disciplined approach to the design process. Working in orderly steps, for example, he gradually animated a pyramidal composition (favored by Leonardo), which could seem static, by building a dynamic interplay of gestures and gazes among the figures.
persona3_adapted: Exposure to the art of Leonardo and Michelangelo gave Raphael’s works a greater sense of space, a more solid, almost sculptural presence, and a powerful emotional impact. This came from an enormous amount of experimentation on paper. Sequences of drawings—from rough sketches to studies of arrangements based on small clay or wax models—show Raphael’s increasingly methodical approach to designing his works. Working in careful steps, for example, he gradually brought life to a pyramid-shaped composition (a structure favored by Leonardo), which could otherwise feel static, by creating a lively exchange of gestures and glances among the figures.

CuratorialText: In 1507 the twenty-four-year-old Raphael painted his most significant work before his move to Rome, an altarpiece of Christ’s entombment for the Baglioni family chapel in Perugia. The preliminary drawings show him in full command of his powers as a storyteller, and demonstrate how he constructed a dramatic composition with a climactic narrative. Through arduous study, aided by his knowledge of classical sculpture, the artist evolved a static arrangement of figures into an emotionally arresting action scene. Raphael had absorbed Leonardo’s principle that the physical gestures of the body express the “motions” of the mind and soul.
persona3_adapted: In 1507, at the age of twenty-four, Raphael painted what is considered his most important work before moving to Rome. It was an altarpiece—a painting created to stand above an altar in a church—depicting the burial of Christ. This piece was made for the Baglioni family chapel in the city of Perugia. His early sketches show him fully in control of his ability to tell a story, and reveal how he built a powerful scene with a clear, dramatic high point in the narrative. Through intense study, and drawing on his knowledge of ancient Roman sculpture, Raphael transformed a still, balanced arrangement of figures into an emotionally gripping moment of action. He had taken to heart Leonardo da Vinci’s idea that the physical movements of the body reveal the inner thoughts and feelings of the mind and soul.

CuratorialText: For centuries Raphael has been widely admired for Madonna and Child paintings that include a display of tenderness. From about 1300, Byzantine icons of the Eastern Orthodox faith had featured a form of the Madonna known as the Virgin Eleousa, from the Greek for “compassionate,” in which Mary embraces the Christ Child as he touches his cheek to hers. Disseminated widely, the imagery inspired numerous medieval and Renaissance artists, including Italian sculptors and painters. Raphael drew upon this model and thoroughly transformed it.
persona3_adapted: For centuries, Raphael has been widely admired for his paintings of the Madonna and Child—the Virgin Mary and the infant Jesus—that convey a sense of tenderness. Around the year 1300, religious icons from the Eastern Orthodox Christian tradition featured a version of the Madonna known as the Virgin Eleousa, a Greek word meaning “compassionate.” In these images, Mary holds the Christ Child close as he presses his cheek against hers. This type of image spread widely and inspired many artists of the medieval and Renaissance periods, including Italian sculptors and painters. Raphael drew on this earlier model but thoroughly reimagined it.

CuratorialText: Raphael’s ability to capture a mood of vitality and innocent playfulness, especially while maintaining a superior command of anatomical realism, is striking in light of the historical realities of childbirth, infancy, and parenthood in his time. The rare account book displayed here records funerary expenses for Raphael’s mother, who died of childbirth-related complications in 1491, and for two of his siblings who died in infancy. Knowledge of gynecology and obstetrics was scarce, pregnancy and childbirth extremely dangerous, and infant mortality rates astronomically high. These facts, along with a desire among Renaissance artists to humanize religious subjects, all contributed to the appeal of the tender Madonna and Child as votive figures and subjects in art.
persona3_adapted: Raphael’s skill in conveying a sense of energy and innocent playfulness—while also demonstrating an exceptional grasp of realistic human anatomy—is striking when we consider the harsh realities of childbirth, infancy, and parenthood during his era. The rare financial record displayed here lists burial costs for Raphael’s mother, who died from complications related to childbirth in 1491, and for two of his siblings who died as infants. At the time, knowledge of women’s health and childbirth was limited, pregnancy and delivery were extremely dangerous, and the rate of infant death was shockingly high. These circumstances, combined with a trend among Renaissance artists to make religious figures feel more relatable and human, help explain why the gentle image of Mary with the infant Jesus became so popular—both as a focus for prayer and as a subject for art.

CuratorialText: Despite his great ambitions, Raphael was largely thwarted in his attempts to break into the competitive art market in Florence. He found his most feasible source of patronage among the city’s wealthy merchants, who commissioned portraits and tender devotional paintings, mainly of the Madonna and Child. Raphael continued to produce works on this subject for private patrons well after his move to Rome in 1508, and many of his collaborators followed his example.
persona3_adapted: Despite his great ambitions, Raphael was largely unsuccessful in his efforts to establish himself in Florence’s competitive art market. His most realistic opportunities for support came from the city’s wealthy merchants, who hired him to paint portraits and gentle religious scenes, most often showing the Virgin Mary with the infant Jesus. Raphael continued to create works on this subject for private clients even after he moved to Rome in 1508, and many of his assistants and fellow artists followed his lead.

CuratorialText: Raphael sought to bring the lauded ethereal physical beauty of the Madonna into radiant being. He was not immune to the convention, sung by poets both within and beyond his circle, that favored an elegant, aristocratic lady with dainty facial features and golden hair, but he also strove for long-established Christian ideals. Sermons, prayers, songs, and poetry inspired by early church writings had praised the Virgin’s beauty and grace as mirrors of her unique spiritual perfection. He infused his representations with a humanity and psychological presence revealed through gestures and reactions. Raphael’s paintings display an arresting command of light, color, space, and geometry, and his renderings of infants are more naturalistic in their proportions, poses, and expressions than those of many of his peers.
persona3_adapted: Raphael aimed to bring the celebrated, almost heavenly physical beauty of the Madonna to vivid life. He was influenced by the popular ideal—celebrated by poets both in his circle and beyond—that favored an elegant, aristocratic woman with delicate facial features and golden hair. At the same time, he also pursued long-standing Christian ideals. Sermons, prayers, songs, and poetry inspired by early church writings had praised the Virgin’s beauty and grace as reflections of her unique spiritual perfection. Raphael added a sense of humanity and inner life to his portrayals, shown through gestures and reactions. His paintings demonstrate a striking mastery of light, color, space, and geometry, and his depictions of infants are more lifelike in their proportions, poses, and expressions than those of many other artists of his time.

CuratorialText: Raphael’s portraits communicate a profound empathy and reflect years of exercising his hand in drawings to achieve an intimate, concerted contemplation of the sitter. The regal pose of Leonardo’s Mona Lisa, begun in 1503, offered a revolutionary model for the likeness of a luxuriously dressed person: a three-quarter view, head turned to the left, torso erect, and hands resting gracefully at the waist. The elegance of the poses here also suggests a striving for the cultivated manners of the Italian Renaissance courts and the ideals of beauty celebrated by famous poets. Raphael was an intimate friend of Baldassarre Castiglione (1478–1529), whose book on fashionable behavior at court promoted a model of comportment and grace that prized sprezzatura, a studied nonchalance or insouciance.
persona3_adapted: Raphael’s portraits convey a deep sense of human connection and reflect years of practicing his drawing skills to achieve an intensely focused, thoughtful study of the person being portrayed. The dignified posture of Leonardo’s Mona Lisa, begun in 1503, offered a groundbreaking model for depicting an elegantly dressed individual: a three-quarter view, with the head turned to the left, the torso upright, and the hands resting gracefully at the waist. The refined poses seen here also suggest an aspiration toward the polished social manners of Italian Renaissance courts and the standards of beauty celebrated by well-known poets. Raphael was a close friend of Baldassarre Castiglione (1478–1529), whose book on proper behavior at court promoted an ideal of poise and elegance that valued *sprezzatura*—a deliberate, effortless nonchalance or casual grace.

CuratorialText: Many Renaissance portraits of women were painted at the time of betrothal and marriage, a financial-dynastic arrangement in which romantic love had no place. Elegant fashion and jewelry were meant to impress the future husband’s family, as dowries and property were negotiated. Overt appraisals of women’s appearances by poets and artists also contributed to a culture in which young women could become innamorate—platonic beloveds or actual mistresses—of a male aristocracy.
persona3_adapted: Many Renaissance portraits of women were created at the time of their engagement or marriage, which was a financial and family-based arrangement where romantic love was not a factor. The elegant clothing and jewelry in these paintings were intended to impress the future husband’s family, as dowries and property were being negotiated. Open assessments of women’s looks by poets and artists also helped create a culture in which young women could become *innamorate*—meaning either idealized, non-romantic beloveds or actual mistresses—of men in the aristocratic class.

CuratorialText: At some point in 1508, Raphael arrived in Rome, where he would become the favorite court artist of Popes Julius II (r. 1503–13) and Leo X (r. 1513–21). An intriguing question is how exactly the twenty-five-year-old Raphael managed to supersede an earlier generation of painters working in the Vatican Palace and take control of the fresco decoration of the most important of the rooms, the Stanza della Segnatura. Surely artistic genius and personal savvy were key factors, but research shows that networks of political influence between Rome and his hometown of Urbino also paved the way, including support from his friend and countryman Donato Bramante (1444–1514), the pope’s favorite architect.
persona3_adapted: By 1508, Raphael had arrived in Rome, where he would go on to become the favorite artist at the courts of Popes Julius II (who ruled from 1503 to 1513) and Leo X (who ruled from 1513 to 1521). A fascinating question is how this twenty-five-year-old artist managed to overtake an older generation of painters already working in the Vatican Palace and take charge of painting the frescoes—wall paintings done on fresh plaster—in the most important room, the Stanza della Segnatura. Certainly, his artistic brilliance and personal charm were key, but research shows that political connections between Rome and his hometown of Urbino also helped clear the way, including support from his friend and fellow Urbino native Donato Bramante (1444–1514), the pope’s favorite architect.

CuratorialText: Gathered here are Raphael’s studies for those Vatican frescoes: the School of Athens, with its gathering of philosophers, and the Disputa, representing Roman Catholic theology. These works show an artist in full command of the expressive potential of different drawing media. Rapidly sketched pen-and-ink sheets teem with the energy of ideas in the making, while studies in black chalk appear monumental despite their small size. In the next gallery, a digital video conveys the power of Raphael’s finished frescoes in four rooms of the Vatican Palace.
persona3_adapted: Here are Raphael’s preparatory drawings for his famous Vatican frescoes—large wall paintings on fresh plaster. One of these, the *School of Athens*, shows a gathering of ancient philosophers; the other, the *Disputa*, depicts Roman Catholic theology. Together, these works reveal an artist who fully controls the expressive range of different drawing materials. Sheets drawn quickly with pen and ink buzz with the energy of ideas still being formed, while studies made with black chalk feel monumental, even though they are small. In the next gallery, a digital video conveys the impact of Raphael’s completed frescoes across four rooms in the Vatican Palace.

CuratorialText: By about 1510 Raphael was collecting accolades as the most visible and prolific painter at the papal court in Rome; meanwhile, shut away in the Sistine Chapel, the secretive Michelangelo labored to paint his ceiling frescoes, which would deeply influence the younger artist, who snuck in to see them. Drawings here relate to projects that occupied Raphael as he concluded his first series of frescoes in the Vatican Palace and began the second, in the Stanza di Eliodoro, which communicate with greater monumentality and dramatic force. After Pope Julius II died in 1513 and Leo X ascended to the throne, some learned imagery vanished in favor of blunter papal messaging.
persona3_adapted: By around 1510, Raphael was earning widespread praise as the most famous and productive artist working for the pope in Rome. At the same time, the more private Michelangelo was working in isolation on the ceiling of the Sistine Chapel. His frescoes—paintings made on wet plaster—would deeply influence the younger Raphael, who secretly entered the chapel to see them. The drawings on display here relate to the projects Raphael worked on as he finished his first series of frescoes in the Vatican Palace and began his second series, in a room called the Stanza di Eliodoro. These later works communicate with greater grandeur and emotional intensity. After Pope Julius II died in 1513 and Leo X became pope, some of the more scholarly imagery was replaced by more direct messages supporting the papacy.

CuratorialText: Other drawings and prints illustrate Raphael’s collaboration with Marcantonio Raimondi (ca. 1480–1534), a Bolognese printmaker and close friend. In their fruitful partnership, beginning in 1510, they created engravings of tremendous technical skill and artistic power. This medium enabled the reproduction of imagery in multiples and ensured a long afterlife for Raphael’s compositions. The prints were copied by artists well into the late 1800s and repurposed in other media, such as the maiolica plates in a nearby case.
persona3_adapted: Other drawings and prints show how Raphael worked with Marcantonio Raimondi (ca. 1480–1534), a printmaker from Bologna and a close friend. In their productive partnership, which began in 1510, they produced engravings—images carved into metal plates for printing—that displayed remarkable technical skill and artistic strength. This method allowed images to be reproduced in multiple copies, helping Raphael’s compositions survive and remain influential long after his lifetime. Artists continued to copy these prints well into the late 1800s and reused them in other art forms, such as the painted ceramic plates (called maiolica) displayed in a nearby case.

CuratorialText: In the next gallery is a digital video showing Raphael’s monumental fresco cycles in four rooms of the Vatican Palace. The display provides a sense of scale and context for the many related drawings in this exhibition. Between 1508 and 1524, the frescoes were painted by Raphael and his workshop in the nearly square rooms now known as the Stanza della Segnatura, Stanza di Eliodoro, and Stanza dell’Incendio, and in the gargantuan oblong Sala di Costantino (completed by his assistants after his death in 1520).
persona3_adapted: In the next gallery, a digital video shows Raphael’s large-scale wall paintings in four rooms of the Vatican Palace. The video helps visitors understand the size and setting of these works, which is useful for appreciating the many related drawings in this exhibition. Between 1508 and 1524, Raphael and his assistants painted these wall paintings in rooms that are nearly square—now called the Stanza della Segnatura, Stanza di Eliodoro, and Stanza dell’Incendio—and in the enormous, long rectangular Sala di Costantino, which was finished by his helpers after his death in 1520.

CuratorialText: The durable but difficult fresco technique requires expertise, the use of full-scale drawings (cartoons), and a willingness to anticipate challenges. Water-based colors applied to moist surface plaster (intonaco) dry and carbonate in a chemical process, permanently bonding the colors to the wall. Executed primarily by Raphael between 1508 and 1512, the frescoes in the Stanza della Segnatura are the most famous and include the School of Athens, the Disputa, the personifications of Justice, and the Parnassus. In the following three rooms Raphael worked with a greater involvement of collaborators who followed his designs closely.
persona3_adapted: The fresco technique is both durable and demanding, requiring great skill, the use of full-scale preparatory drawings (called cartoons), and a willingness to plan ahead for potential problems. In this method, water-based paints are applied to wet plaster (known as intonaco). As the plaster dries, a chemical reaction called carbonation occurs, which permanently binds the colors to the wall. The frescoes in the Stanza della Segnatura, painted mainly by Raphael between 1508 and 1512, are his most famous works. They include the School of Athens, the Disputa, symbolic figures representing Justice, and the Parnassus. In the three rooms that followed, Raphael worked more closely with assistants, who followed his designs carefully.

CuratorialText: The paintings here represent the breathtaking visual eloquence of Raphael’s later years. New harmonies of color are transformed by a dark chiaroscuro reminiscent of Leonardo. Sculptural forms seem to project from dense compositions. Charged gazes, poses, and gestures communicate a sense of impending drama.
persona3_adapted: These paintings capture the stunning visual expressiveness of Raphael’s later work. He creates new harmonies of color, deepened by a dark, dramatic contrast between light and shadow—a technique that recalls Leonardo da Vinci. Figures appear almost sculptural, as if they are pushing forward from the dense, layered compositions. Intense looks, poses, and gestures convey a feeling of drama about to unfold.

CuratorialText: Between 1514 and 1520, the demanding Pope Leo X rained project after project upon Raphael. He produced few paintings independently; using his drawings as a guide, skilled assistants painted most of the frescoes in the Vatican’s Stanza dell’Incendio. He was often disparaged for this practice by rivals, including his fierce enemy Michelangelo. But Raphael and his well-organized workshop completed an astonishing number of large-scale projects in his final six years. Executive help freed the artist to concentrate his creative energies on inventing new designs and exploring new forms. The involvement of workshop assistants in paintings and in certain types of drawings has inevitably led to debates about authorship. Labels in this exhibition attempt to indicate instances of their likely intervention, and a nearby display allows comparison of studies by Raphael and his fellow artists in an effort to characterize their individual styles.
persona3_adapted: Between 1514 and 1520, the demanding Pope Leo X overwhelmed Raphael with one major project after another. During this period, Raphael produced few paintings entirely on his own. Instead, he created detailed drawings that served as guides, and his skilled assistants used them to paint most of the frescoes—wall paintings done on fresh plaster—in the Vatican’s Stanza dell’Incendio (Room of the Fire). This way of working drew criticism from rivals, including his fierce enemy Michelangelo. Yet Raphael and his well-organized workshop (the team of assistants working under him) completed an astonishing number of large-scale projects in his final six years. Having executive help freed Raphael to focus his creative energy on inventing new designs and exploring new artistic forms. The involvement of workshop assistants in paintings and in certain types of drawings has inevitably led to debates about who actually created what. Labels in this exhibition try to indicate where assistants likely contributed, and a nearby display allows visitors to compare studies by Raphael and his fellow artists, helping to identify the distinct qualities of each person’s style.

CuratorialText: The imperious Pope Leo X charged Raphael with the daunting task of designing a set of monumental tapestries to hang in the Sistine Chapel during special occasions—a project that put Raphael in direct competition with Michelangelo and his ceiling frescoes above. Starting in 1515, Raphael began drawing small preliminary studies. Aided by workshop assistants, he then painted in color gouaches on paper the enormous full-scale cartoons that would guide the weavers. Woven in Brussels using rich materials, the stratospherically expensive tapestries contributed to the bankruptcy of Leo’s papacy. With their inventive compositions, striking color harmonies, and monumental scale, they were soon the envy of monarchs across Europe. Kings commissioned second editions woven from Raphael’s cartoons, and the set owned by King Philip II of Spain (1527–1598) is represented here by three stunning examples.
persona3_adapted: The powerful Pope Leo X gave Raphael the enormous task of designing a set of large, woven wall hangings (tapestries) for the Sistine Chapel, to be displayed on special occasions. This project placed Raphael in direct competition with Michelangelo, who had painted the chapel’s ceiling with frescoes (paintings applied to wet plaster). Beginning in 1515, Raphael created small initial sketches. With help from his workshop assistants, he then painted the full-scale paper patterns (called cartoons) in colored gouache—a type of opaque watercolor—that would guide the weavers. The tapestries were woven in Brussels using costly materials, and their sky-high expense helped push the pope’s finances into bankruptcy. With their original designs, striking color combinations, and grand size, these tapestries soon became the envy of monarchs across Europe. Kings ordered second versions woven from Raphael’s original patterns, and the set owned by King Philip II of Spain (1527–1598) is represented here by three stunning examples.

CuratorialText: As the tapestry designs progressed, the Raphael team largely took over the fresco work in the Vatican Palace, endeavoring to complete the Stanza dell’Incendio and assisting Raphael as he began to conceive decorations for the final space. Giulio Romano (1499?–1546) acted as his studio foreman and went on to enjoy a brilliant independent career.
persona3_adapted: As the designs for the tapestries advanced, Raphael’s workshop team took on most of the fresco painting in the Vatican Palace. They worked to finish the Stanza dell’Incendio (a room named after a famous fire scene painted there) and helped Raphael as he started planning decorations for the last room. Giulio Romano (1499?–1546) served as the head of Raphael’s studio and later built a highly successful career on his own.

CuratorialText: Raphael’s last and largest altarpiece is the enormous Transfiguration, commissioned by Cardinal Giulio de’ Medici (the future Pope Clement VII) around 1517. Spurred by competition with artistic rivals, Raphael produced a work markedly more dramatic, ambitious, and complex than his earlier altarpieces. Although its execution was interrupted by his untimely death in 1520, the Transfiguration became Raphael’s artistic testament. The main event is Christ’s Transfiguration on Mount Tabor, during which he transformed before the stunned apostles Peter, James, and John to reveal his divinity.
persona3_adapted: Raphael’s final and largest altarpiece—a painted panel placed behind an altar—is the monumental *Transfiguration*. It was commissioned around 1517 by Cardinal Giulio de’ Medici, who would later become Pope Clement VII. Driven by rivalry with other leading artists of his time, Raphael created a work that is noticeably more dramatic, ambitious, and complex than his earlier altar paintings. Although his sudden death in 1520 interrupted its completion, the *Transfiguration* came to be seen as Raphael’s artistic legacy—a final statement of his genius. The central scene depicts Christ’s Transfiguration on Mount Tabor, the moment when he was transformed before the astonished apostles Peter, James, and John, revealing his divine nature.

CuratorialText: Raphael’s studies for the Transfiguration, displayed in the niche at left, represent the culmination of his career as a draftsman. They are reunited here to show the range of his design process. Raphael used red chalk primarily when drawing nude models from life. He reserved black chalk for the full-scale studies of apostles’ heads and hands, a type of drawing called an “auxiliary cartoon” because the design starts from a dotted chalk outline transferred from another sheet.
persona3_adapted: Raphael’s preparatory drawings for the Transfiguration, shown in the alcove to the left, mark the peak of his skill as a designer. These works are brought together here to reveal the variety in his creative process. Raphael mainly used red chalk when sketching nude models from life. He saved black chalk for the life-sized studies of the apostles’ faces and hands—a kind of drawing known as an “auxiliary cartoon,” because the design begins with a dotted chalk outline that was transferred from another sheet of paper.

CuratorialText: Raphael rarely interrupted his projects for Popes Julius II and Leo X to make room for other commissions. The notable exception was his powerful friend Agostino Chigi (1466–1520), whom he met in about 1510. Born in Siena, Chigi became the richest banker in Europe, and his legendary wealth financed the popes’ cultural and military enterprises. Raphael’s work for Chigi included fresco paintings, as well as designs for his remodeled stables and the multimedia ensembles of his two chapels.
persona3_adapted: Raphael seldom set aside his work for Popes Julius II and Leo X to take on other projects. The one significant exception was his influential friend Agostino Chigi (1466–1520), whom he met around 1510. Born in Siena, Chigi rose to become the wealthiest banker in Europe, and his legendary fortune helped fund the popes’ cultural and military efforts. Raphael’s work for Chigi included wall paintings (known as frescoes), along with designs for Chigi’s remodeled stables and the combined artistic elements—such as painting, sculpture, and architecture—of his two chapels.

CuratorialText: Displayed here are a variety of Raphael’s preparatory drawings for Chigi’s projects. In chalk studies of nude figures, Michelangelo’s language of contorted poses and powerful muscularity is transformed with an effortless grace and the sensuality of living flesh. The drawings also allude to the legacy of Raphael’s approach to the human figure, which led to the style known as Mannerism after his death. Male and female forms here anticipate prototypical Mannerist poses—as with Raphael’s primeval personification of an earthquake in a Sistine Chapel tapestry design, which spawned the figural vocabulary that his former assistant Giulio Romano would exploit to spectacular effect in his later frescoes.
persona3_adapted: Displayed here are a variety of Raphael’s preparatory drawings for Chigi’s projects. In chalk studies of nude figures, Michelangelo’s approach—with its twisted poses and powerful muscularity—is transformed into something effortlessly graceful and sensuously alive. The drawings also hint at the lasting influence of Raphael’s treatment of the human figure, which after his death gave rise to a style known as Mannerism. Male and female forms here anticipate the characteristic poses of early Mannerism—for example, Raphael’s ancient, symbolic figure of an earthquake in a design for a Sistine Chapel tapestry. That figure created a visual language of the body that his former assistant Giulio Romano would later use to dramatic effect in his own wall paintings.

CuratorialText: Rome and its ancient monuments transformed Raphael’s art, and his work likewise changed the face of Rome. Following his arrival in the Eternal City in 1508, he threw himself into archaeological studies and made drawings of monuments that demonstrate a nearly scientific rigor. Pope Leo X tasked Raphael with creating an enormous drawing of ancient Rome, “making those members that are entirely ruined and are completely invisible correspond with those that are still standing and can be seen.” A letter to Leo, written on Raphael’s behalf, attests to the artist’s deep archaeological knowledge and technical skill, including a highly original method for using a magnetic compass in conducting surveys of ruins.
persona3_adapted: Rome and its ancient monuments profoundly influenced Raphael’s art, and in turn, his work reshaped the appearance of Rome. After arriving in the city in 1508, he immersed himself in the study of ancient ruins, creating drawings with a level of precision that approached scientific accuracy. Pope Leo X commissioned Raphael to produce a large-scale reconstruction of ancient Rome, aiming to “make the parts that are completely destroyed and invisible correspond with those that are still standing and can be seen.” A letter written on Raphael’s behalf to the Pope reveals the artist’s deep understanding of archaeology and his technical abilities, including an innovative method for using a magnetic compass to survey ruins.

CuratorialText: Around 1503 Pope Julius II had charged Bramante, Raphael’s mentor and close friend from Urbino, to design a new Saint Peter’s Basilica that would convey the majesty of the papacy in the aesthetic language of ancient Rome. Drawings and prints here illuminate Raphael’s career as an architect after he took over this massive project upon Bramante’s death. In 1517 Raphael bought the Palazzo Caprini, designed by Bramante and now destroyed but pictured in two works here, where he lived out his final years in Rome as the “prince of painters.”
persona3_adapted: Around 1503, Pope Julius II commissioned Bramante—Raphael’s mentor and a fellow native of Urbino—to design a new Saint Peter’s Basilica. The goal was to express the grandeur of the papacy using the visual style of ancient Rome. Drawings and prints in this section show Raphael’s work as an architect after he took over this enormous project following Bramante’s death. In 1517, Raphael bought the Palazzo Caprini, a building designed by Bramante that no longer exists but is shown in two works here. Raphael spent his final years in Rome living in this house, known at the time as the “prince of painters.”

CuratorialText: The Met acquired its first Chinese painting in 1902. Since then, the Museum has added more than two thousand works of painting and calligraphy, building one of the most comprehensive collections in the world. Spanning fifteen hundred years of cultural production and featuring a variety of genres, techniques, and styles, The Met's collection has become a key resource for the study of Chinese painting and calligraphy. This exhibition presents a rich selection of works from the collection arranged in a largely chronological display. The final gallery tells the story of Wen C. Fong (1930–2018), chairman of the Museum’s Department of Asian Art from 1971 to 2000, and his teacher Li Jian (1881–1956). As a young calligraphy prodigy in Shanghai in the 1940s, Fong studied brush arts with Li. The display centers on a group of fans, painted and written by Li, that were intended to transmit the canonical models of the classical tradition from teacher to student. Brought to the United States in 1949 and treasured by the Fong family since then, these fans are presented publicly for the first time.
persona3_adapted: The Met acquired its first Chinese painting in 1902. Since then, the Museum has added more than two thousand works of painting and calligraphy (the art of beautiful handwriting), building one of the most comprehensive collections in the world. Spanning fifteen hundred years of cultural production and featuring a variety of genres, techniques, and styles, The Met's collection has become a key resource for the study of Chinese painting and calligraphy. This exhibition presents a rich selection of works from the collection arranged in a largely chronological display. The final gallery tells the story of Wen C. Fong (1930–2018), chairman of the Museum’s Department of Asian Art from 1971 to 2000, and his teacher Li Jian (1881–1956). As a young calligraphy prodigy in Shanghai in the 1940s, Fong studied brush arts with Li. The display centers on a group of fans, painted and written by Li, that were intended to pass on the classic models of the tradition from teacher to student. Brought to the United States in 1949 and treasured by the Fong family ever since, these fans are presented publicly for the first time.

CuratorialText: The hand-held fan was an unexpected muse for some of the most innovative artists in 19th-century Europe. Fans became hugely popular across many levels of society during this period, serving as functional and fashionable objects of adornment and communication. Well-known French Impressionists such as Edgar Degas and Camille Pissarro not only featured this feminine accessory in their work but also adopted it as an experimental format for their art. Fanmania investigates why avant-garde artists incorporated fans into their work and sheds light on themes of gender, courtship, consumerism, and appropriation. Artists were attracted to the semicircular form for myriad reasons, including fascination with fans from Asia and Spain, commercial ambition, and their interest in formal and technical innovation. Displaying more than 75 artworks from across The Met collection, this multimedia exhibition features painted and printed fans from Europe and Asia as well as artworks that depict women wielding fans to explore the phenomenon of “fanmania.”
persona3_adapted: The hand-held fan served as an unexpected source of inspiration for some of the most groundbreaking artists in 19th-century Europe. During this period, fans became extremely popular across many levels of society, functioning both as practical and stylish accessories and as tools for nonverbal communication. Well-known French Impressionists such as Edgar Degas and Camille Pissarro not only included this feminine object in their paintings but also used it as an experimental surface for their own art. *Fanmania* explores why forward-thinking artists incorporated fans into their work and sheds light on themes of gender, courtship, consumerism, and the borrowing of cultural styles. Artists were drawn to the fan's half-circle shape for many reasons, including a fascination with fans from Asia and Spain, commercial ambition, and their interest in new approaches to form and technique. Featuring more than 75 artworks from across The Met collection, this multimedia exhibition includes painted and printed fans from Europe and Asia, as well as artworks showing women using fans, to investigate the phenomenon of “fanmania.”

CuratorialText: During the nineteenth century, handheld fans achieved unprecedented popularity throughout European society. A newfound artistic appreciation for these objects also emerged, fueled by cultural crazes for all things Japanese and Spanish as well as by exhibitions and publications devoted to fan making and its history. “Almost all the great artists of the century have contributed . . . to the decoration of the best fans,” wrote the author Octave Uzanne in 1882. For the fourth Impressionist exhibition of 1879, Edgar Degas envisioned an entire gallery devoted to fans. Though the fan-shaped paintings his colleagues submitted were ultimately dispersed throughout the show, they nevertheless elicited strong responses from the public, including bewilderment, as illustrated below. Today, these works continue to raise questions—mainly, why did ambitious European artists adopt this accessory as a format for their work? Bringing together artworks from across The Met, Fanmania explores the reasons artists were drawn to this semicircular form, including its commercial potential, its fashionability, and the opportunities it offered for formal and technical innovation. Although avant-garde fans were primarily designed for display rather than use, they retain many of the associations of functional fans. Themes of gender, courtship, appropriation, and experimentation unfold in this examination of fans as objects of adornment, decoration, and communication in the nineteenth century.

People also began to appreciate these objects as art, driven by widespread fascination with Japanese and Spanish culture, as well as by exhibitions and books focused on fan-making and its history. “Almost all the great artists of the century have contributed . . . to the decoration of the best fans,” wrote the author Octave Uzanne in 1882. For the fourth Impressionist exhibition of 1879, the artist Edgar Degas imagined an entire room dedicated to fans. Although the fan-shaped paintings his fellow artists submitted were eventually spread throughout the show, they still provoked strong reactions from the public—including confusion, as shown below. Today, these works continue to raise questions, mainly: why did ambitious European artists choose this accessory as a format for their art? Bringing together artworks from across The Met, *Fanmania* explores the reasons artists were drawn to this half-circle shape, including its potential for profit, its trendiness, and the chance it offered for new approaches to form and technique. Although avant-garde (experimental, forward-thinking) fans were mainly made for display rather than actual use, they still carry many of the meanings of functional fans. Themes of gender, courtship, cultural borrowing, and artistic experimentation emerge in this look at fans as objects of decoration, communication, and personal adornment in the nineteenth century.

CuratorialText: Handheld folding fans are multimedia objects that involve many types of artistry and artisanal skill. This show focuses primarily on the role of painters and printmakers in the production of the fan’s semicircular leaf. In Europe, prior to 1700, most of these designs were unsigned. In the nineteenth century, major artists grew increasingly interested in making fans and began signing their work, as did preeminent fan-making houses, which evolved out of earlier guilds. Some of the precedents for these practices are on display here.
persona3_adapted: Handheld folding fans are objects that combine multiple forms of artistic and craft-based skill. This exhibition focuses mainly on the work of painters and printmakers who created the fan’s semicircular central panel. In Europe, before 1700, most of these designs were not signed by their creators. During the nineteenth century, major artists became more interested in making fans and began signing their work, as did the leading fan-making companies, which developed from earlier trade organizations. Some examples of the earlier traditions that led to these practices are shown here.

CuratorialText: Historically, European fans have been most closely associated with the eighteenth century, when their use became widespread. The examples here show the various roles fans played, including as tools for flirtation, amusement, education, propaganda, observation, and masquerade. Fan leaves communicated through their subjects and style, while users brought them to life through their gestures. Women were not only the consumers of fans but also among their producers, as fan making was considered an art form appropriate for female practitioners, whether amateur or professional.
persona3_adapted: Historically, European fans are most strongly linked to the eighteenth century, when they became widely used. The examples here show the many different roles fans played—they could be tools for flirtation, entertainment, learning, spreading political messages, observing others, or participating in costume parties. The decorated surfaces of fans communicated meaning through their images and artistic style, while the people using them brought the fans to life through their movements. Women were not only the buyers of fans but also helped make them, since fan-making was seen as an art form suitable for women, whether they worked as amateurs or professionals.

CuratorialText: Handheld fans found perhaps their most prominent public display in the opera house. The atmosphere of spectatorship and performance—present both onstage and among the audience—heightened attention to fans in the hands of female theatergoers. The emphasis on seeing and being seen even gave rise to fan designs that incorporated opera glasses, or lorgnettes (from the French lorgner, “to ogle”), and small mirrors. A lorgnette fan is on view in the case nearby. Fan makers also developed a genre of fans devoted to the opera. These fans incorporated scores, lyrics, and homages to celebrated composers.
persona3_adapted: Handheld fans were perhaps most prominently displayed in public at the opera house. The atmosphere of watching and being watched—both onstage and among the audience—drew special attention to the fans held by women attending the opera. This focus on seeing and being seen even inspired fan designs that included opera glasses (small binoculars used in theaters) or lorgnettes (from the French word *lorgner*, meaning “to ogle”), as well as small mirrors. A fan with a built-in lorgnette is displayed in the nearby case. Fan makers also created a category of fans specifically dedicated to the opera. These fans featured musical scores, lyrics, and tributes to famous composers.

CuratorialText: As theatergoing grew more popular later in the century, Impressionist artists seized on the novel subject of spectators in the theater loge. The fashionability and ubiquity of fans at the opera had become fully apparent by the early 1880s, as evidenced by the cartoon below.
persona3_adapted: As theater attendance increased in popularity later in the 1800s, Impressionist artists began focusing on a fresh subject: people watching performances from theater boxes, the private seating areas on the sides of the stage. By the early 1880s, it was clear just how fashionable and widespread hand-held fans had become at the opera—a trend that the cartoon below clearly captures.

CuratorialText: Movement is an essential aspect of the handheld fan. The nineteenth-century critic Charles Blanc wrote that the fan is “above all things . . . a means or motive of gracious movements, under pretext of agitating the air for the sake of coolness.” The expressive qualities of a fan’s motions are among the ways it can convey meaning and reveal the user’s personality, mood, or intentions. Fans can flutter, vibrate, waft, snap, open, shut, rise, and even fall (or be deliberately dropped) at a particularly opportune moment before a potential suitor.
persona3_adapted: Movement is a key feature of the handheld fan. The nineteenth-century critic Charles Blanc observed that the fan is “above all things . . . a means or motive of gracious movements, under pretext of agitating the air for the sake of coolness.” The way a fan moves can express meaning and reveal the user’s personality, mood, or intentions. Fans can flutter, vibrate, waft, snap, open, shut, rise, and even fall—or be deliberately dropped—at a particularly well-chosen moment in front of a potential suitor.

CuratorialText: Artists in the late nineteenth century understood the fan as a moving object and attempted to capture its dynamism in myriad ways. The fleeting and ephemeral nature of its movement surely made it an appealing subject for the Impressionists. Fan designs frequently incorporate metallic paints, gold leaf, feathers, spangles, and sequins, the visual impacts of which are heightened when in motion.
persona3_adapted: Artists in the late nineteenth century saw the fan as an object in motion and tried to capture its energy and liveliness in many different ways. The quick, passing quality of its movement likely made it an attractive subject for the Impressionists, a group of artists who focused on capturing fleeting moments and changing light. Fan designs often include metallic paints, thin sheets of gold, feathers, shiny decorations, and sequins, whose visual effects become more striking when the fan is moving.

CuratorialText: The term “Japonisme” was first used by French critics in the early 1870s to describe the passion for Japanese art and culture they observed among their contemporaries. Japanese goods became increasingly available in Europe after the United States forced Japan to open its ports to international trade in 1853–54. The 1867 and 1878 Universal Exhibitions in Paris were pivotal events in Europeans’ exposure to the arts of Japan. Many Impressionist artists and their friends collected such works, including fans, acquiring them from newly established specialist dealers and shops. By the late 1880s, Japanese fans were imported in the millions to France, where they were used as women’s accessories and decoration.
persona3_adapted: The term “Japonisme” was first used by French critics in the early 1870s to describe the widespread fascination with Japanese art and culture they noticed among people of their time. Japanese goods became more available in Europe after the United States pressured Japan to open its ports to international trade in 1853–54. Two major world’s fairs in Paris—the Universal Exhibitions of 1867 and 1878—were key moments in introducing Europeans to Japanese arts. Many Impressionist painters and their friends collected Japanese objects, such as fans, buying them from newly opened specialist shops and dealers. By the late 1880s, millions of Japanese fans were being imported into France, where they were used both as fashion accessories for women and as decorative items.

CuratorialText: European artists focused on the aesthetic value of these objects and borrowed from them liberally, often with little understanding of their original contexts. Today, some would call this cultural appropriation, though subtleties of interpretation varied. Certainly, the history of Japonisme is fraught, but the admiration for Japanese fans freed European artists to imagine new techniques and strategies for their art.
persona3_adapted: European artists valued these objects for their beauty and freely borrowed ideas from them, often without understanding the cultures or traditions they came from. Today, some would call this cultural appropriation, though opinions on the matter were more complex at the time. The history of Japonisme—the influence of Japanese art on Western artists—is certainly complicated, but the admiration for Japanese fans allowed European artists to explore new methods and approaches in their own work.

CuratorialText: In the nineteenth century, Spain became a center of fan production at the same time that the country’s tourism industry emerged and its culture was increasingly exported throughout Europe. Reporting from his travels to the Iberian Peninsula in the early 1840s, the French writer Théophile Gautier claimed, “A woman without a fan is something I have not yet seen in this happy country.” The fan’s prominent role in Spanish flamenco dance is likely another reason it became inextricably linked with Spain in the nineteenth-century imagination, and remains so today. In Paris at mid-century, Spanish culture was in vogue, a phenomenon known as Hispagnolisme. Spanish-born empress Eugénie de Montijo, who married Napoleon III in 1853, exerted considerable influence on fashion and taste. Spanish dance troupes and musicians performed often in the capital, and avant-garde artists, such as Edouard Manet, took them up as subjects. For some artists who made fans in this period, it was Spain that they had foremost in mind. Manet and Degas both chose Spanish themes for their earliest fan designs.

Writing about his travels to the Iberian Peninsula in the early 1840s, the French writer Théophile Gautier claimed, “A woman without a fan is something I have not yet seen in this happy country.” The fan’s important role in Spanish flamenco dance is likely another reason it became deeply linked with Spain in the nineteenth-century imagination, and it remains so today. In Paris around the middle of the century, Spanish culture was very fashionable, a trend known as Hispagnolisme. The Spanish-born empress Eugénie de Montijo, who married Napoleon III in 1853, had a significant influence on fashion and taste. Spanish dance troupes and musicians often performed in the capital, and cutting-edge artists, such as Edouard Manet, chose them as subjects for their work. For some artists who made fans during this period, Spain was the country they had foremost in mind. Manet and Degas both selected Spanish themes for their earliest fan designs.

CuratorialText: The theme of the bourgeois woman with her fan in a domestic setting proliferated in art of the last quarter of the nineteenth century. In his 1882 book The Fan, the French writer Octave Uzanne described the role of fans to his female readers: “In all solitude, in every despair, it will remain your confidante.” Whether at home or on ventures out, the handheld fan served as a companion, trusted protector, and elegant accoutrement.
persona3_adapted: The image of a middle-class woman holding a fan in a home setting became increasingly common in art during the last quarter of the nineteenth century. In his 1882 book *The Fan*, the French writer Octave Uzanne described the fan’s role to his female readers: “In all solitude, in every despair, it will remain your confidante.” Whether used at home or while out and about, the handheld fan acted as a companion, a trusted protector, and an elegant accessory.

CuratorialText: As fashion accessories, fans signaled facets of their user’s identity through their materials and stylistic form as well as in the way they were handled. “One wave of the Fan is sufficient by itself to distinguish between a princess and a countess, a marchioness and a plebeian,” remarked one female observer cited by Uzanne. A Japanese uchiwa fan in the hands of the Impressionist Berthe Morisot or a plumed fan held by one of Mary Cassatt’s sitters displayed their alignment with current taste and confirmed their social status.
persona3_adapted: As fashion accessories, fans communicated aspects of their owner's identity—not only through the materials they were made of and their visual style, but also through the way they were held and moved. “One wave of the Fan is sufficient by itself to distinguish between a princess and a countess, a marchioness and a plebeian,” remarked one female observer quoted by the writer Uzanne. A Japanese-style uchiwa fan (a flat, non-folding fan) in the hands of the Impressionist painter Berthe Morisot, or a feathered fan held by one of Mary Cassatt’s subjects, showed that these women were in tune with fashionable taste and confirmed their place in society.

CuratorialText: Fans were among the first souvenirs, used since the seventeenth century to commemorate events and to remember visits to important sites. As travel increased in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries, fans became popular as small, practical, and artistic objects that could serve as mementos. The Universal Exhibitions of the second half of the nineteenth century played a crucial role in promoting the French fan industry and exposing the European public to fans from other cultures. Souvenir fans were produced for each of these events, providing visitors with information and maps while also recording the remarkable buildings erected specifically for these occasions.
persona3_adapted: Fans were among the earliest souvenirs, used since the 1600s to mark special events and to remember visits to important places. As travel became more common in the 1700s and 1800s, fans grew popular as small, practical, and decorative objects that could serve as keepsakes. The World's Fairs of the late 1800s—large international exhibitions showcasing industry and culture—played a key role in promoting the French fan-making industry and introducing European audiences to fans from other parts of the world. Special souvenir fans were made for each of these fairs, giving visitors information and maps while also showing the impressive buildings that were constructed specifically for these events.

CuratorialText: This monumental work was one of a series of carved architectural supports designed for the exterior courtyard of a Yoruba palace. It was commissioned by a king from the most renowned master sculptor in the history of Yoruba art, Olowe of Ise. The two-tiered composition embodies a formal dynamism that established Olowe's exceptional artistic reputation. Its principal personage, an equestrian warrior, is depicted frontally above a female caryatid with arms raised in three-quarter view. Through this contrast in their alignment, the figures at once reflect distinct attitudes while relating to each other fluidly. The compressed style of the upper half also contrasts with the greater degree of openness below. At the base two smaller figures radiate outward at oblique angles. In addition to the inventive dynamism of their overall design, works by Olowe are noted for the attention given to surface detail. The mounted warrior holds a spear and pistol in either hand, and his vest, saddle, and horse's headgear are articulated through deeply carved linear motifs. While innovative in its formal interpretation, the subject of this work is a classic emblem of regional leadership. In Yoruba art such equestrian figures identify their patrons with martial conquest achieved through physical might.
persona3_adapted: This monumental work was one of a series of carved wooden supports designed for the exterior courtyard of a Yoruba palace. It was commissioned by a king from the most celebrated master sculptor in the history of Yoruba art, Olowe of Ise. The two-level composition embodies a sense of formal energy and movement that established Olowe’s exceptional artistic reputation. The main figure, a warrior on horseback, is shown facing forward, positioned above a female figure with her arms raised, depicted at a three-quarter angle—turned slightly to the side rather than fully front-facing. Through this contrast in their poses, the two figures express different attitudes while still relating to each other in a fluid, connected way. The compressed, tightly packed style of the upper half also contrasts with the more open, spacious arrangement below. At the base, two smaller figures extend outward at diagonal angles. In addition to the inventive energy of their overall design, works by Olowe are noted for the careful attention given to surface detail. The mounted warrior holds a spear and pistol in either hand, and his vest, saddle, and the horse’s headgear are marked by deeply carved linear patterns. While innovative in its artistic approach, the subject of this work is a classic symbol of regional leadership. In Yoruba art, such horseback figures identify their patrons with military conquest achieved through physical strength.

CuratorialText: This saltcellar is both an extraordinary example of skilled workmanship and an artifact that epitomizes a singularly important convergence of cultures. In the second half of the fifteenth century, Portuguese explorers and traders were impressed by the considerable talent of ivory carvers along the coast of West Africa. As a result, they were inspired to commission works of this kind for their patrons, which ingeniously combine both European and African aesthetics and forms. During this period, salt and pepper were costly commodities and elaborate receptacles were appropriate for their storage in princely homes. This work contains imagery relating to early Temne and Bullom worldviews. The four snakes, associated with mystical wealth, appear to confront four growling dogs. According to regional traditions, dogs are considered spiritually astute animals able to see spirits and ghosts that are invisible to humans. This depiction of the dogs, with teeth bared, hair bristling, and ears laid back, may relate to that ability. However, the level of animation in this scene could also derive from chivalric hunting scenes in European woodcuts, which were furnished to local African artists by their European patrons.
