CuratorialText: The imagery on this plate represents the earliest known depiction of a well-known episode from the story of Bahram Gur or Bahram V, the Sasanian king (r. 420 –438). The story seems to have been popular for centuries—both in Sasanian times and under the Islamic rule that followed—but was only recorded in the Shahnamah, or Book of Kings, by Firdausi in the early eleventh century A.D., centuries after this plate was created. The Shahnamah recounts how Bahram Gur, challenged to feats of archery by his favorite musician, Azadeh, changed a male gazelle to a female and a female to a male and pinned the hind leg of an animal to its ear. This plate directly refers to the story of Bahram Gur because the horns of the male have been shot off with an arrow, transforming his appearance into that of a female, while the two arrows embedded in the head of the female make her appear to have horns like those of a male gazelle. This plate was likely hammered into shape and then decorated to highlight its varied surface contours and colors, making its human and animal forms stand out in relief. It was partially gilded using a mixture of mercury and gold, which could be painted onto the surface. Large numbers of gold and silver vessels made during Sasanian rule in Iran and Mesopotamia have survived, demonstrating an ancient Near Eastern tradition that spread westward to the Hellenistic and Roman world, where vessels made of precious metals also became symbols of power and prestige. These objects, presumably official state products, were presented as royal gifts to persons of importance within Sasanian Iran and beyond its borders.

The story appears to have remained popular for centuries—both during the Sasanian period and under later Islamic rule—but it was not written down until the early eleventh century AD, in the *Shahnamah* (the *Book of Kings*) by the poet Firdausi, hundreds of years after this plate was made. The *Shahnamah* tells how Bahram Gur, challenged to a series of archery feats by his favorite musician, Azadeh, turned a male gazelle into a female and a female into a male, and pinned a gazelle’s hind leg to its ear. This plate directly refers to that story: the male gazelle’s horns have been shot off by an arrow, making it look like a female, while two arrows stuck in the female’s head make it appear to have horns like a male. The plate was probably hammered into shape and then decorated to emphasize its varied surface textures and colors, making the human and animal figures stand out in raised form. It was partially covered with gold leaf using a mixture of mercury and gold, which could be painted onto the surface. Many gold and silver vessels from the Sasanian period in Iran and Mesopotamia have survived, showing an ancient tradition from the Near East that spread westward to the Hellenistic and Roman worlds, where vessels made of precious metals also became symbols of power and status. These objects, likely official state products, were given as royal gifts to important people within Sasanian Iran and beyond its borders.

CuratorialText: This solid-cast sculpture is one of a pair of nearly identical images of a hero or a demon wearing the upturned boots associated with highland regions, his power enhanced by the mighty horns of the ibex on his head and the body and wings of a bird of prey draped around his shoulders. It was created at the time the first cities emerged in ancient Sumer. A new world view conceived of human figures in realistic terms, through accurate proportions and highly modeled forms with distinctive features. The blending of human and animal forms to visualize the supernatural world and perhaps to express shamanistic beliefs, however, is more characteristic of the contemporary arts of Proto-Elamite Iran, where a remarkable tradition of metalworking developed during this period.
persona3_adapted: This solid-cast sculpture is one of a pair of nearly identical figures depicting a hero or a demon. The figure wears upturned boots, a style associated with highland regions, and its power is enhanced by the large horns of an ibex on its head, along with the body and wings of a bird of prey draped over its shoulders. The piece was created during the time when the first cities were emerging in ancient Sumer (in what is now southern Iraq). During this period, a new way of thinking led artists to represent human figures in a more realistic style, using accurate body proportions and highly detailed, three-dimensional forms with distinctive features. However, the combination of human and animal elements to represent the supernatural world—and possibly to express shamanistic beliefs—is more typical of the art of Proto-Elamite Iran (in what is now western Iran), where a remarkable tradition of metalworking developed at this time.

CuratorialText: This fragmentary piece takes the form of the face of a roaring lion. It was found in a storeroom at Fort Shalmaneser, a royal building at Nimrud that was probably used to store booty and tribute collected by the Assyrians while on military campaign. During the final defeat of Assyria at the end of the seventh century B.C., the royal complexes at Nimrud were sacked and burned, and the lion was likely blackened through exposure to fire at this time. The flat back suggests that this piece may have originally been attached to a piece of wooden furniture such as the arm of a chair. The right cheek, eyebrows, and incised muzzle are detailed with incised dots and the eyes are deeply carved for now-lost semiprecious stone or colored glass inlays. A pattern of painted white triangles outlined with raised ribs is preserved on the right side at the back of the face, perhaps to represent a mane. Scenes of the Assyrian king hunting lions are depicted on royal reliefs from the Assyrian palaces. Because it shares the same subject matter, this piece has been attributed to an Assyrian style. Built by the Assyrian king Ashurnasirpal II, the palaces and storerooms of Nimrud housed thousands of pieces of carved ivory. Most of the ivories served as furniture inlays or small precious objects such as boxes. While some of them were carved in the same style as the large Assyrian reliefs lining the walls of the Northwest Palace, the majority of the ivories display images and styles related to the arts of North Syria and the Phoenician city-states. Phoenician style ivories are distinguished by their use of imagery related to Egyptian art, such as sphinxes and figures wearing pharaonic crowns, and the use of elaborate carving techniques such as openwork and colored glass inlay. North Syrian style ivories tend to depict stockier figures in more dynamic compositions, carved as solid plaques with fewer added decorative elements. However, some pieces do not fit easily into any of these three styles. Most of the ivories were probably collected by the Assyrian kings as tribute from vassal states, and as booty from conquered enemies, while some may have been manufactured in workshops at Nimrud. The ivory tusks that provided the raw material for these objects were almost certainly from African elephants, imported from lands south of Egypt, although elephants did inhabit several river valleys in Syria until they were hunted to extinction by the end of the eighth century B.C.
persona3_adapted: This fragmentary piece is shaped like the face of a roaring lion. It was discovered in a storage room at Fort Shalmaneser, a royal building in the ancient city of Nimrud. This fort was likely used to hold the loot and tribute that the Assyrians collected during their military campaigns. When the Assyrian Empire was finally defeated at the end of the seventh century B.C., the royal buildings at Nimrud were looted and set on fire. The lion’s darkened surface probably comes from being exposed to that fire. The back of the piece is flat, which suggests it was originally attached to a piece of wooden furniture, perhaps the arm of a chair. The right cheek, eyebrows, and the area around the muzzle are decorated with small carved dots. The eyes are deeply carved, likely to hold inlays of semiprecious stone or colored glass that have since been lost. On the right side, near the back of the face, a pattern of painted white triangles outlined with raised ridges has survived, possibly meant to suggest a mane. Scenes of the Assyrian king hunting lions appear on stone reliefs from Assyrian palaces. Because this piece shares that same subject, experts have identified it as being made in an Assyrian style. The palaces and storerooms of Nimrud, built by the Assyrian king Ashurnasirpal II, once held thousands of carved ivory objects. Most of these ivories were used as furniture inlays or as small precious items like boxes. Some were carved in the same style as the large Assyrian stone reliefs that lined the walls of the Northwest Palace. However, the majority of the ivories show images and styles that come from North Syria and the Phoenician city-states. Phoenician-style ivories are recognized by their use of imagery borrowed from Egyptian art—such as sphinxes and figures wearing Egyptian-style crowns—and by elaborate carving techniques like openwork (cutting away parts of the surface to create a pattern) and inlays of colored glass. North Syrian-style ivories tend to show heavier, more solid figures in more active poses, carved as flat plaques with fewer added decorations. Still, some pieces do not fit neatly into any of these three styles. Most of the ivories were probably collected by Assyrian kings as tribute from allied states or as loot from conquered enemies, though some may have been made in workshops at Nimrud itself. The elephant tusks that provided the raw material for these objects almost certainly came from African elephants, imported from lands south of Egypt. Elephants did live in several river valleys in Syria, but they were hunted to extinction there by the end of the eighth century B.C.

CuratorialText: Of the many legacies left by the ancient civilizations of southern Mesopotamia, the invention of writing is paramount. At the end of the fourth millennium B.C., written language developed in the region, first as pictographs and then evolving into abstract forms called cuneiform. The pictographs, like the ones on this tablet, are called proto-cuneiform and were drawn in the clay with a pointed implement. Circular impressions alongside the pictographs represented numerical symbols. Cuneiform (meaning wedge-shaped) script was written by pressing a reed pen or stylus with a wedge-shaped tip into a clay tablet. Clay, when dried to a somewhat hardened state, made a fine surface for writing, and when fired the records written on it became permanent. Early writing was used primarily as a means of recording and storing economic information. This tablet most likely documents grain distributed by a large temple, although the absence of verbs in early texts makes them difficult to interpret with certainty. In addition to the writing that appears on this tablet, the imagery of the cylinder seal, which was incompletely impressed on both faces and the edges of the tablet before it was inscribed, also records information. This seal apparently has not survived. The seal impression depicts a male figure guiding two dogs on a leash and hunting or herding boars in a reed marsh. He is the so-called priest-king, a male figure who can be identified by his dress and pose. Here he appears in his role as the good shepherd who protects flocks from wild predators.
persona3_adapted: Of the many contributions left by the ancient civilizations of southern Mesopotamia, the invention of writing is the most important. At the end of the fourth millennium B.C., written language developed in the region, first as picture-based symbols (pictographs) and then evolving into abstract forms called cuneiform. The pictographs, like the ones on this tablet, are called proto-cuneiform and were drawn into the clay with a pointed tool. Circular marks next to the pictographs represented numbers. Cuneiform (meaning wedge-shaped) script was made by pressing a reed pen or stylus with a wedge-shaped tip into a clay tablet. Clay, when dried to a somewhat hardened state, made a good surface for writing, and when fired in a kiln, the records written on it became permanent. Early writing was used mainly as a way to record and store economic information. This tablet most likely documents grain distributed by a large temple, although the lack of verbs in early texts makes them difficult to interpret with certainty. In addition to the writing on this tablet, the images from a cylinder seal—a small carved cylinder rolled across the clay to leave an impression—also record information. The seal was incompletely pressed onto both sides and the edges of the tablet before the writing was added. This seal apparently has not survived. The seal impression shows a male figure guiding two dogs on a leash and hunting or herding wild pigs in a marsh of reeds. He is what scholars call the priest-king, a male figure who can be identified by his clothing and posture. Here he appears in his role as the good shepherd who protects flocks from wild predators.

CuratorialText:
In ancient Mesopotamia, a cylinder-shaped seal could be rolled on a variety of objects made of clay. When seals were impressed on tablets or tablet cases the seal impressions served to identify the authority responsible for what was written in the documents, much as a signature does today. When seals were impressed on sealings — lumps of clay that were used to secure doors and the lids of storage jars— the seal impressions served to identify their owner and protect against unauthorized opening. Many cylinder seals have survived because they were made of durable materials, particularly stone, but also metal and fired clay. Perforated through the middle like a bead, seals were also believed to have apotropaic, or protective, functions and were worn as jewelry or pinned on garments.

The modern impression of a seal is shown here so that the entire design can be seen. The scene is composed of two basic groupings that form an overall continuous design unified by the stylized landscape setting. In one group, two tall trees flank a hunter grasping an ibex. Above the man a cuneiform inscription gives the name of the seal owner, Balu-ili, who was a court official, and his profession, cupbearer. In the other group, ibexes stand on mountains, facing each other and flanking three trees. This seal was made during the Akkadian period, when the iconography used by the seal engraver expanded to include a variety of new mythological, thematic, and narrative subjects. Here, the already ancient motif of the hunt is given a new setting in a clearly defined landscape. If there was a relationship between this imagery and the inscribed text, its meaning is no longer understood.

Sacred and secular ideas, fundamental to the beliefs of ancient Mesopotamian peoples, were visualized in the miniature images carved on the seals. The carver used intaglio, a technique in which the forms were cut into the stone, to create the raised impression. The challenge of the seal carver was to create a design that would maintain its balance and clarity when rolled out only half its length on a small surface or twice its length on a larger surface. On the left and right edges of the impression one can see how the rolled out design begins to repeat. Unlike much of the art of the ancient Near East, which survives only in a fragmentary state, cylinder seals are in the unique position of appearing almost exactly as they would have looked to the ancient people who used them.

In ancient Mesopotamia, a cylinder-shaped seal could be rolled across various clay objects. When pressed onto tablets or tablet cases, the seal’s imprint served to identify the person or authority responsible for the document’s contents—much like a signature does today. When used on sealings, which were lumps of clay that secured doors or the lids of storage jars, the impressions identified the owner and helped prevent unauthorized opening. Many cylinder seals have survived because they were made from durable materials, especially stone, but also metal and fired clay. Pierced through the center like a bead, these seals were also believed to have protective, or apotropaic, powers and were worn as jewelry or pinned onto clothing.

The modern impression of a seal is shown here so that the entire design can be seen. The scene consists of two main groupings that together form a continuous pattern, unified by a stylized landscape. In one group, two tall trees stand on either side of a hunter who is grasping an ibex (a type of wild goat). Above the man, a cuneiform inscription—a system of writing using wedge-shaped marks—gives the name of the seal’s owner, Balu-ili, who was a court official, and his profession, cupbearer. In the other group, ibexes stand on mountains, facing each other with three trees between them. This seal was made during the Akkadian period, a time when the images used by seal carvers expanded to include a wide range of new mythological, thematic, and narrative subjects. Here, the already ancient theme of the hunt is placed in a clearly defined landscape. If there was any connection between this imagery and the inscribed text, its meaning is no longer known.

Sacred and everyday ideas, central to the beliefs of ancient Mesopotamian peoples, were brought to life in the tiny images carved onto the seals. The carver used intaglio, a technique in which the forms are cut into the stone, so that when rolled, the design appears raised. The challenge for the seal carver was to create a design that would remain balanced and clear whether rolled out only half its length on a small surface or twice its length on a larger one. On the left and right edges of the impression, you can see how the design begins to repeat. Unlike much of the art from the ancient Near East, which survives only in fragments, cylinder seals are unique in that they appear almost exactly as they would have looked to the people who used them long ago.

CuratorialText: This type of figurine known as an eye idol, made of stone and having incised eyes, has been excavated at Tell Brak, where thousands were found in a building now called the Eye Temple. They were probably dedicated there as offerings. Many are incised with multiple sets of eyes, others with jewelry, and still others with representations of "children"—smaller eyes and body carved on the body of the larger idol. Wide eyes demonstrate attentiveness to the gods in much of Mesopotamian art.
persona3_adapted: This type of figurine, called an eye idol, is made of stone and has eyes carved into its surface. Examples have been uncovered at Tell Brak, where thousands were discovered in a building now referred to as the Eye Temple. They were likely left there as offerings. Many of the idols are carved with several pairs of eyes, some with jewelry, and others with images of "children"—smaller eyes and bodies carved onto the larger idol's body. In much of Mesopotamian art, wide eyes show a person's attentiveness to the gods.

CuratorialText: Some of the most elaborate representations of females in the art of the ancient Near East are images of divine and cult figures whose association with certain aspects of life made them essential to the welfare of humanity. Fertility, procreation, and the growth of crops and livestock were among the basic concepts identified with female divinities. Representations of nude females in clay, stone, and metal are the simplest and most obvious expression of these concepts, and such figures appear throughout antiquity in many regions. This striking example in clay from the South Caspian region of northwestern Iran is hollow and probably served as a cult vessel as well as a sacred image.
persona3_adapted: Some of the most detailed depictions of women in ancient Near Eastern art show divine and religious figures whose connection to key aspects of life made them vital to human well-being. Fertility, childbirth, and the successful growth of crops and livestock were among the basic ideas linked to female deities. Representations of nude women made from clay, stone, and metal are the simplest and most direct expressions of these ideas, and such figures appear across many regions throughout the ancient world. This striking clay example, from the South Caspian region of northwestern Iran, is hollow and likely served both as a container used in religious rituals and as a sacred image.

CuratorialText: Distinctive clay female figures like this one were produced at sites belonging to the Halaf culture. This example displays a strong stylization with an emphasis on the sexual features. She sits with her large thighs extended, supporting her breasts with her arms; neither hands nor feet are shown. Her head is missing; in other figures of this type when the head is intact, it is elongated into a large noselike projection but otherwise is featureless. Remains of paint may represent jewelry. The meaning of such representations is unknown but may be connected with fertility. The stylized depiction of the nude female form remained an artistic convention in northern Syria, Anatolia, and the Aegean for several millennia.
persona3_adapted: Clay female figures with distinctive features, like this one, were made at settlements of the Halaf culture. The figure is highly simplified and stylized, with a strong focus on sexual characteristics. She sits with her large thighs spread apart, and her arms support her breasts; neither hands nor feet are shown. Her head is missing—in other similar figures where the head survives, it is stretched into a long, nose-like shape but has no other facial features. Traces of paint may indicate that she once wore jewelry. We do not know what these figures meant, but they may have been linked to ideas about fertility. This simplified way of depicting the nude female body remained a common artistic practice in northern Syria, Anatolia, and the Aegean for thousands of years.

CuratorialText: Furniture inlaid with carved ivory plaques was highly prized by the Assyrian kings. During the ninth to seventh centuries B.C., vast quantities of luxury goods, often embellished with carved ivory in local, Syrian, and Phoenician styles, accumulated in Assyrian palaces, much of it as booty or tribute. This object belongs to a group of plaques depicting animals and stylized plants. They were made by master carvers in a delicate openwork technique characteristic of Phoenician ivory carving. However, the style and subjects depicted have close parallels on stone relief sculptures from Tell Halaf, in northern Syria, and a debate exists over which tradition produced these fine panels.
persona3_adapted: Furniture decorated with carved ivory panels was highly valued by Assyrian kings. Between the ninth and seventh centuries B.C., enormous quantities of luxury goods—often adorned with carved ivory in local, Syrian, and Phoenician styles—were brought into Assyrian palaces, much of it taken as war spoils or tribute. This piece belongs to a group of panels showing animals and stylized plants. They were created by master carvers using a delicate technique that cuts through the ivory to create a lace-like pattern, a method typical of Phoenician ivory work. However, the style and subject matter closely resemble stone relief carvings from Tell Halaf in northern Syria, and scholars continue to debate which artistic tradition actually produced these fine panels.

CuratorialText: This statuette group, carved in the round, was found with five other statuettes with similar imagery. The six statuettes were excavated in and just below two arched niches built into the wall of a room at Fort Shalmaneser, a royal building at Nimrud that was probably used to store booty and tribute collected by the Assyrians while on military campaign. Those found just below the niches could have fallen when the palaces were destroyed during the the final defeat of Assyria at the end of the seventh century B.C. Originally, these objects were attached to long ivory plinths and exhibited in the arched niches, perhaps arranged in a procession of foreigners bringing different animals and animal skins as tribute to the Assyrian king. A frontally facing male, striding to the right with his lower body in profile, grasps the horns of an oryx (a species of desert-dwelling antelope) that strides behind him. The male figure’s eyes, necklace, and armlet were carved to receive colored glass or semiprecious stone inlays. He wears a short kilt belted with a long sash, embroidered with decoration including two uraei (mythical, fire-spitting serpents), zig-zags, wavy lines, small squares, rosettes, diamonds, and circles. A monkey, whose fur is rendered with short incisions, sits erect on his left shoulder and grasps his short curly hair. Although his right arm does not survive, it was probably extended to support the elaborately patterned leopard skin draped over his right shoulder. Built by the Assyrian king Ashurnasirpal II, the palaces and storerooms of Nimrud housed thousands of pieces of carved ivory. Most of the ivories served as furniture inlays or small precious objects such as boxes. While some of them were carved in the same style as the large Assyrian reliefs lining the walls of the Northwest Palace, the majority of the ivories display images and styles related to the arts of North Syria and the Phoenician city-states. Phoenician style ivories are distinguished by their use of imagery related to Egyptian art, such as sphinxes and figures wearing pharaonic crowns, and the use of elaborate carving techniques such as openwork and colored glass inlay. North Syrian style ivories tend to depict stockier figures in more dynamic compositions, carved as solid plaques with fewer added decorative elements. However, some pieces do not fit easily into any of these three styles. Most of the ivories were probably collected by the Assyrian kings as tribute from vassal states, and as booty from conquered enemies, while some may have been manufactured in workshops at Nimrud. The ivory tusks that provided the raw material for these objects were almost certainly from African elephants, imported from lands south of Egypt, although elephants did inhabit several river valleys in Syria until they were hunted to extinction by the end of the eighth century B.C.

This small group of statues, carved so that they can be viewed from all sides, was discovered alongside five other statuettes with similar themes. The six pieces were found in and just below two arched niches built into the wall of a room at Fort Shalmaneser, a royal building in Nimrud. This fort was likely used to store the loot and tribute that the Assyrians collected during their military campaigns. The statues found just below the niches may have fallen when the palaces were destroyed during the final defeat of Assyria at the end of the seventh century B.C. Originally, these objects were attached to long ivory bases and displayed in the arched niches, possibly arranged as a procession of foreigners bringing different animals and animal skins as tribute to the Assyrian king.

One figure shows a male facing forward, striding to the right with his lower body turned to the side, as he grasps the horns of an oryx (a type of desert antelope) that walks behind him. The man’s eyes, necklace, and armband were carved to hold colored glass or semi-precious stone inlays. He wears a short kilt fastened with a long sash, embroidered with decorations that include two uraei (mythical, fire-spitting serpents), zig-zags, wavy lines, small squares, rosettes, diamonds, and circles. A monkey, whose fur is indicated by short carved lines, sits upright on his left shoulder and grips his short, curly hair. Although his right arm is missing, it was probably extended to support the elaborately patterned leopard skin draped over his right shoulder.

Built by the Assyrian king Ashurnasirpal II, the palaces and storerooms of Nimrud housed thousands of carved ivory pieces. Most of these ivories were used as furniture inlays or as small precious objects like boxes. While some were carved in the same style as the large Assyrian stone reliefs that lined the walls of the Northwest Palace, the majority show images and styles related to the art of North Syria and the Phoenician city-states. Phoenician-style ivories are recognizable by their use of imagery borrowed from Egyptian art, such as sphinxes and figures wearing pharaonic crowns, as well as elaborate carving techniques like openwork (cutting away parts of the material to create a pattern) and colored glass inlay. North Syrian-style ivories tend to depict stockier figures in more dynamic compositions, carved as solid plaques with fewer added decorative elements. However, some pieces do not fit neatly into any of these three styles.

Most of the ivories were probably collected by the Assyrian kings as tribute from vassal states or as loot from conquered enemies, though some may have been made in workshops at Nimrud. The ivory tusks that provided the raw material for these objects almost certainly came from African elephants, imported from lands south of Egypt. However, elephants did inhabit several river valleys in Syria until they were hunted to extinction by the end of the eighth century B.C.

CuratorialText:
This bronze monumental statue is one of the very few nearly complete Roman bronze statues of the third century preserved today. It is a reminder that many of the disembodied portrait heads displayed in these galleries were once attached to freestanding sculptures, whose original appearances were undoubtedly quite different. Portraits of the emperor served the highly important function in imperial propaganda of presenting the ruler to the public and of projecting the personality with which he wished to be perceived. Leadership and military strength are evoked in the heroic nudity of the figure and in the pose, which recalls the famous statue of Alexander the Great with the Lance by Lysippos. In contrast to the idealized body, the portrait head represents the emperor with brutish realism. Trebonianus likely would have cradled a parazonium, or short sword, in his left arm, and held a spear in his upraised right hand.
The statue has undergone several campaigns of restoration since its discovery in the early nineteenth century and was examined in great detail as part of the conservation treatment in preparation for its display here. Visual examination inside and out, combined with x-radiography, made possible the clear identification of ancient and restored areas, which are indicated in the accompanying illustration. At least three-quarters of the statue is ancient. Despite the discrepancy in scale, the head belongs to the body. Although the mantle draped over his left shoulder is a modern restoration, cast edges beneath it confirm that the statue had a similar embellishment in antiquity. The left foot with its elaborately decorated open-fronted boot appears to be ancient but may not belong.
persona3_adapted:
This large bronze statue is one of the very few nearly complete Roman bronze figures from the third century that have survived to the present day. It serves as a reminder that many of the head-only portraits displayed in these galleries were once attached to free-standing sculptures, whose original appearances were likely quite different. Portraits of the emperor played a crucial role in imperial propaganda—the deliberate shaping of public opinion—by presenting the ruler to the people and projecting the image he wanted them to see. Leadership and military strength are suggested by the figure's heroic nudity and by its pose, which echoes the famous statue of Alexander the Great holding a lance, created by the sculptor Lysippos. In contrast to the idealized body, the portrait head shows the emperor with a blunt, almost harsh realism. Trebonianus probably would have held a short sword, known as a parazonium, in his left arm, and a spear in his raised right hand.

Since its discovery in the early nineteenth century, the statue has been restored several times. It was examined in great detail as part of the conservation work done to prepare it for display here. By looking closely at the statue inside and out, and using X-ray imaging, experts were able to clearly identify which parts are ancient and which are later additions—these are marked in the accompanying illustration. At least three-quarters of the statue is original. Although the head and body are different in size, the head does belong to this body. The cloak draped over the statue's left shoulder is a modern addition, but cast edges beneath it show that the statue had a similar decoration in ancient times. The left foot, with its elaborately decorated open-fronted boot, appears to be ancient but may not originally have been part of this statue.

CuratorialText: Eirene, the daughter of Zeus and Themis, was one of the three Horai (Seasons), maidens closely associated with the fertility of the earth and the nurturing of children. The original bronze was erected in the Agora (marketplace) of Athens between 375/374 and 360/359 B.C. Rarely can an ancient monument be dated so exactly. We know from literary sources that the cult of Eirene was introduced to Athens in 375/374, and six recently found Panathenaic amphorae dated to 360/359 show an image of the statue. The Greek traveler Pausanius saw the work in the Agora in the second century A.D. and reported that it was by the sculptor Kephisodotos. Eirene was represented as a beautiful young woman wearing a peplos and himation (cloak), holding a scepter in her right hand, and carrying the young child Ploutos (the personification of wealth) and a cornucopia on her left arm. The figure brings to mind images of Demeter, the major goddess of agricultural plenty and the mother of Ploutos.
persona3_adapted: Eirene, the daughter of Zeus and Themis, was one of the three Horai (the goddesses of the seasons), young women closely linked to the fertility of the earth and the care of children. The original bronze statue was set up in the Agora (the central public square and marketplace) of Athens between 375/374 and 360/359 B.C. It is rare for an ancient monument to be dated with such precision. We know from ancient writings that the worship of Eirene was introduced to Athens in 375/374, and six recently discovered Panathenaic amphorae—ceramic jars used as prizes in a major Athenian festival—dated to 360/359 show an image of the statue. The Greek traveler Pausanius saw the work in the Agora in the second century A.D. and recorded that it was made by the sculptor Kephisodotos. Eirene was shown as a beautiful young woman wearing a peplos and himation (a type of cloak), holding a scepter in her right hand, and carrying the young child Ploutos (the embodiment of wealth) and a cornucopia (a horn-shaped symbol of abundance) on her left arm. The figure brings to mind images of Demeter, the great goddess of agricultural plenty and the mother of Ploutos.

CuratorialText: Many private summer villas were located along the coast near Naples. One of the most sumptuous must have been the villa built by Agrippa, friend of the emperor Augustus and husband of his daughter, Julia. It stood overlooking the Bay of Naples from a spot near the modern town of Boscotrecase. The villa was partially excavated between 1903 and 1905 after its accidental discovery during work on a railway. Wall decorations that still survived in four bedrooms were removed. The Metropolitan Museum acquired sections from three rooms and the Archaeological Museum at Naples received the rest. Agrippa died in 12 B.C. and his son, Agrippa Postumus became the villa's proprietor in 11 B.C. as inscriptions found there indicate. The frescoes must have been painted during renovations begun at the time. Painted by artists working for the imperial household, they are among the finest existing examples of Roman wall painting. The so-called Black Room was one of a sequence of bedrooms facing south toward the downward slope of the mountain and the sea. The source of light was a wide doorway giving onto a terrace or promenade. This ambiguous and sophisticated decoration is a masterpiece of the so-called third style of Roman wall painting, which flourished during the reign of Augustus. The theme is a playful rendition of architectural motif. A low red dado serves as the base from which a skeleton of thin white columns appears to rise against a black background. There almost weightless columns support pavilions, candelabra, tripods, and a narrow cornice that runs around the room. They were embellished with jewel-like decorations. On the back wall tiny swans, the bird of Apollo, patron god of Augustus, perch improbably on threadlike spirals, and yellow panels with Egyptianizing motifs must have brought to mind the recent annexation of Egypt after the death of Cleopatra in 30 B.C. This architectural scheme creates almost no sense of depth or volume. The black walls behind appear at once to be flat and to dissolve into limitless space. Tiny landscape vignettes float like islands in the middle of this blackness. Burnished to a high polish, these walls must have appeared magical indeed when illuminated by lamps at night.
persona3_adapted:
Many wealthy Romans owned summer villas along the coast near Naples. One of the most luxurious must have been the villa built by Agrippa, a close friend of Emperor Augustus and husband to his daughter, Julia. It overlooked the Bay of Naples from a spot near the modern town of Boscotrecase. The villa was partly excavated between 1903 and 1905 after being discovered by accident during railway construction. Wall decorations that still survived in four bedrooms were removed. The Metropolitan Museum acquired sections from three rooms, while the Archaeological Museum in Naples received the rest. Agrippa died in 12 B.C., and inscriptions found at the site indicate that his son, Agrippa Postumus, became the villa's owner in 11 B.C. The frescoes were likely painted during renovations that began around that time. Created by artists working for the imperial household, they are among the finest surviving examples of Roman wall painting.

The so-called Black Room was one of a series of bedrooms facing south, toward the downward slope of the mountain and the sea. Light entered through a wide doorway that opened onto a terrace or walkway. This room’s decoration is ambiguous and refined—a masterpiece of what art historians call the "third style" of Roman wall painting, a style that flourished during Augustus’s reign. The design plays with architectural forms in a lighthearted way. A low red baseboard (called a dado) supports a delicate framework of thin white columns that seem to rise against a black background. These almost weightless columns hold up small pavilions, candelabras, tripods, and a narrow ledge that runs around the room, all decorated with jewel-like details. On the back wall, tiny swans—birds sacred to Apollo, the patron god of Augustus—perch improbably on threadlike spirals. Yellow panels with Egyptian-style motifs would have reminded viewers of Egypt’s recent annexation after Cleopatra’s death in 30 B.C.

This architectural arrangement creates almost no sense of depth or volume. The black walls behind appear both flat and as if they dissolve into endless space. Tiny landscape scenes float like islands in the middle of this blackness. Polished to a high shine, these walls must have seemed truly magical when lit by lamps at night.

CuratorialText:
From the villa of Agrippa Postumus at Boscotrecase, the Mythological Room (19)
Landscape with Perseus and Andromeda This fresco from the Imperial villa at Boscotrecase depicts two consecutive events from the myth of Perseus and Andromeda. Perseus is about to rescue Andromeda from the ketos, a snaky sea monster painted in a brilliant blue-green palette. The creature raises his head with gigantic open jaws and frightful teeth toward Andromeda, who stands with outstretched arms in the center of the panel. One hand appears to be chained to the crag; the other elegantly placed on the rocks. Perseus flies in from the left with his lyre in one hand, winged shoes on his feet, and a windblown cloak over his shoulder. In the upper right portion of the fresco, he is greeted by Andromeda's grateful father, a scene that alludes to the myth's happy endingâ€“the marriage of hero and princess. The fortunes of love and the ever-present sea are the themes that link this fresco and that of Polyphemus and Galatea (20.192.17) from the same cubiculum at Boscotrecase. The translucent blue-green background of both frescoes also unifies the disparate episodes combined in each painting, and must have brought a sense of coolness to the room.
persona3_adapted:
From the villa of Agrippa Postumus at Boscotrecase, the Mythological Room (19)

Landscape with Perseus and Andromeda

This wall painting from the imperial villa at Boscotrecase shows two moments from the story of Perseus and Andromeda happening one after the other. Perseus is about to rescue Andromeda from the *ketos*—a snake-like sea monster painted in a vivid blue-green color scheme. The creature lifts its head, jaws wide open with terrifying teeth, toward Andromeda, who stands in the center of the panel with her arms stretched out. One hand appears chained to the rocky cliff; the other rests gracefully on the stones. Perseus flies in from the left, carrying a lyre (a stringed instrument) in one hand, wearing winged shoes, and with a cloak billowing over his shoulder. In the upper right part of the painting, he is greeted by Andromeda’s grateful father—a scene that hints at the story’s happy ending: the marriage of the hero and the princess. The themes of love’s fortunes and the ever-present sea connect this painting to the one of Polyphemus and Galatea (20.192.17) from the same bedroom at Boscotrecase. The translucent blue-green background of both paintings also ties together the different scenes combined in each work, and would have brought a sense of coolness to the room.

CuratorialText: This noble image of a woman brings to mind the philosopher Aristotle's description of commonly held beliefs about the dead: "In addition to believing that those who have ended this life are blessed and happy, we also think that to say anything false or slanderous against them is impious, from our feeling that it is directed against those who have already become our betters and superiors" (Of the Soul, quoted in Plutarch, A Letter to Apollonius 27). Larger than life and seated on a thronelike chair, this figure assumes almost heroic proportions.
persona3_adapted: This dignified portrait of a woman recalls the philosopher Aristotle's account of how people commonly view the dead: "We not only consider those who have passed away to be blessed and fortunate, but we also believe that speaking falsely or maliciously about them is a kind of disrespect—because we feel such words are aimed at those who have become, in a sense, our moral superiors" (from *Of the Soul*, as quoted in Plutarch's *A Letter to Apollonius* 27). Larger than life and seated on a chair resembling a throne, the figure takes on an almost heroic stature.

CuratorialText: A stylized octopus flanked by fish covers each side of this stirrup jar, named for the shape of its handles. Mycenaean pottery often reflects Minoan-inspired themes and techniques that originated on the island of Crete. Animated marine motifs adopted from Minoan art began to appear on Mycenaean vessels in about 1500 BCE and were initially rendered in a very naturalistic manner. Here, the symmetrical composition and abstract depiction of the sea creatures are characteristically Mycenaean. Such jars were commonly used to transport liquids.
persona3_adapted: A stylized octopus, with fish on either side, appears on each side of this stirrup jar—so named because its handles resemble the shape of a stirrup. Mycenaean pottery often reflects themes and techniques inspired by the Minoan civilization, which originated on the island of Crete. Lively sea-life designs borrowed from Minoan art began to appear on Mycenaean vessels around 1500 BCE and were initially shown in a very lifelike way. Here, the balanced arrangement and simplified, non-realistic portrayal of the sea creatures are typical of Mycenaean style. Such jars were commonly used for transporting liquids.

CuratorialText:
On the shoulder, women dancing between musicians playing flute and lyre
On the body, wedding procession. This is the earliest and most complete known representation of an Attic wedding. The bridal couple with the best man behind them sit in a cart drawn by two donkeys. A mule cart with four guests follows. Other members of the procession are on foot. The woman in the lead holds two torches, indicating that the scene takes place at night. The procession heads toward the bridegroom's house where a woman, probably the mother of the groom, awaits. The architecture of the house is carefully indicated; the white columns of the porch may be painted wood.
persona3_adapted: On the shoulder of the vase, women are shown dancing between musicians playing the flute and the lyre. On the body of the vase, a wedding procession is depicted. This is the earliest and most complete known representation of a wedding in ancient Attica (the region around Athens). The bride and groom, with the best man seated behind them, ride in a cart pulled by two donkeys. A mule cart carrying four guests follows behind. Other members of the procession walk on foot. The woman leading the group holds two torches, which tells us the scene takes place at night. The procession moves toward the bridegroom's house, where a woman—likely the groom's mother—waits. The details of the house's architecture are carefully shown; the white columns of the porch may have been made of painted wood.

CuratorialText: This highly ornate and extremely well-preserved Roman marble sarcophagus came to the Metropolitan Museum from the collection of the Dukes of Beaufort and was formerly displayed in their country seat, Badminton House in Gloucestershire, England. An inscription on the unfinished back of the sarcophagus records that it was installed there in 1733. In contrast to the rough and unsightly back, the sides and front of the sarcophagus are decorated with forty human and animal figures carved in high relief. The central figure is that of the god Dionysos seated on a panther, but he is somewhat overshadowed by four larger standing figures who represent the four Seasons (from left to right, Winter, Spring, Summer, and Fall). The figures are unusual in that the Seasons are usually portrayed as women, but here they are shown as sturdy youths. Around these five central figures are placed other Bacchic figures and cultic objects, all carved at a smaller scale. On the rounded ends of the sarcophagus are two other groups of large figures, similarly intermingled with lesser ones. On the left end, Mother Earth is portrayed reclining on the ground; she is accompanied by a satyr and a youth carrying fruit. On the right end, a bearded male figure, probably to be identified with the personification of a river-god, reclines in front of two winged youths, perhaps representing two additional Seasons. The sarcophagus is an exquisite example of Roman funerary art, displaying all the virtuosity of the workshop where it was carved. The marble comes from a quarry in the eastern Mediterranean and was probably shipped to Rome, where it was worked. Only a very wealthy and powerful person would have been able to commission and purchase such a sarcophagus, and it was probably made for a member of one of the old aristocratic families in Rome itself. The subjects - the triumph of Dionysos and the Seasons - are unlikely, however, to have had any special significance for the deceased, particularly as it is clear that the design was copied from a sculptor's pattern book. Another sarcophagus, now in the Hessisches Landesmuseum in Kassel, Germany, has the same composition of Dionysos flanked by the four Seasons, although the treatment and carving of the figures is quite different. On the Badminton sarcophagus the figures are carved in high relief and so endow the crowded scene with multiple areas of light and shade, allowing the eye to wander effortlessly from one figure to another. One must also imagine that certain details were highlighted with color and even gilding, making the whole composition a visual tour de force. Very few Roman sarcophagi of this quality have survived. Although the Badminton sarcophagus lacks its lid, the fact that it was found in the early eighteenth century and soon thereafter installed in Badminton Hall means that it has been preserved almost intact and only a few of the minor extremities are now missing.
persona3_adapted: This highly decorated and remarkably well-preserved Roman marble coffin—known as a sarcophagus—came to the Metropolitan Museum from the collection of the Dukes of Beaufort. It was once displayed at their country estate, Badminton House in Gloucestershire, England. An inscription on the unfinished back of the sarcophagus records that it was installed there in 1733. In contrast to the rough and unfinished back, the sides and front are covered with forty human and animal figures carved so that they stand out prominently from the stone surface (a technique called high relief). The central figure is the god Dionysos, seated on a panther, but he is somewhat overshadowed by four larger standing figures representing the four Seasons (from left to right: Winter, Spring, Summer, and Fall). These figures are unusual because the Seasons are typically shown as women, but here they appear as sturdy young men. Around these five central figures are other figures and objects associated with the worship of Dionysos (known as Bacchic imagery), all carved at a smaller scale. On the rounded ends of the sarcophagus are two more groups of large figures, similarly mixed with smaller ones. On the left end, Mother Earth is shown reclining on the ground, accompanied by a satyr (a half-human, half-goat creature from mythology) and a youth carrying fruit. On the right end, a bearded male figure—probably meant to represent a river god—reclines in front of two winged youths, who may stand for two additional Seasons. This sarcophagus is an outstanding example of Roman funerary art, displaying the full skill of the workshop where it was carved. The marble comes from a quarry in the eastern Mediterranean and was likely shipped to Rome, where it was worked. Only a very wealthy and powerful person could have commissioned and purchased such a piece, and it was probably made for a member of one of the old aristocratic families in Rome itself. However, the subjects—the triumph of Dionysos and the Seasons—probably did not have any special meaning for the person buried in it, especially since the design appears to have been copied from a sculptor's pattern book. Another sarcophagus, now in the Hessisches Landesmuseum in Kassel, Germany, has the same arrangement of Dionysos flanked by the four Seasons, though the treatment and carving of the figures are quite different. On the Badminton sarcophagus, the figures are carved in high relief, giving the crowded scene many areas of light and shadow, which allows the eye to move easily from one figure to another. We should also imagine that certain details were highlighted with color and even gold leaf, making the whole composition a stunning visual achievement. Very few Roman sarcophagi of this quality have survived. Although the Badminton sarcophagus is missing its lid, the fact that it was discovered in the early eighteenth century and soon after installed in Badminton Hall means it has been preserved almost completely intact, with only a few small extremities now missing.

CuratorialText:
On the neck, obverse, battle of centaurs and Lapiths; reverse, youths and women
Around the body, Amazonomachy (battle between Greeks and Amazons). The ancient Greeks almost never depicted contemporary or historical events in art. Thus, while literary works of the fifth century B.C. make clear that the Greeks understood the magnitude of their victory in the Persian Wars, there was no concern among artists to illustrate major events or personalities. Instead, their preference was for grand mythological battles between Greeks and eastern adversaries, notably Amazons. The Amazons were a mythical race of warrior women whose homeland lay far to the east and north. The most celebrated Amazonomachies in Athens during the first half of the fifth century were large-scale wall paintings that decorated the Theseion and the Stoa Poikile (the Painted Portico). Their influence was considerable and underlies the representation here and on the adjacent krater, 06.286.86.
persona3_adapted:
On the neck of the vase, one side shows a battle between centaurs (creatures that are half-human, half-horse) and Lapiths (a mythical Greek tribe). The other side shows young men and women. Around the body of the vase is an Amazonomachy—a battle between Greeks and Amazons, who were a mythical race of warrior women from lands far to the east and north.

The ancient Greeks almost never showed real historical events or people in their art. Even though writings from the 400s B.C. make it clear that the Greeks understood how important their victory was in the wars against Persia, artists did not try to illustrate major events or famous figures. Instead, they preferred to show grand mythological battles between Greeks and eastern enemies, especially the Amazons.

In Athens during the first half of the 400s B.C., the most famous Amazon battle scenes were large wall paintings that decorated two important buildings: the Theseion (a temple dedicated to the hero Theseus) and the Stoa Poikile (a public building known as the Painted Portico). These paintings had a strong influence on later artists, and their style can be seen in the scene on this vase, as well as on a nearby mixing bowl (a type of vase called a krater), numbered 06.286.86.

CuratorialText: Approximately 230 paintings in the collection of the Rijksmuseum are still lifes. As early as the 16th century, Flemish painters specialized in this genre. These are arranged displays featuring, for example, shells, books, food and drink, tableware, skulls, or dead animals, but also flowers, insects, and sometimes a person in the background. Life literally stands still for a moment. Separate specializations soon emerged within the genre. For instance, Rachel Ruysch primarily painted flowers, while Pieter Claesz and Willem Heda enjoyed creating arrangements with food and eating and drinking utensils. Another variant, the vanitas still life, which often depicts a skull, refers to the transience of life and the material world.
persona3_adapted: Around 230 paintings in the Rijksmuseum’s collection are still lifes—a type of artwork that shows arranged objects rather than people or landscapes. As early as the 1500s, painters from Flanders (now part of Belgium) began to specialize in this kind of picture. These works carefully arrange items such as shells, books, food and drink, tableware, skulls, or dead animals, as well as flowers, insects, and occasionally a person in the background. In these scenes, life truly seems to pause for a moment. Over time, different subcategories of still-life painting developed. For example, Rachel Ruysch focused mainly on flowers, while Pieter Claesz and Willem Heda enjoyed composing scenes with food and dining utensils. Another type is the vanitas still life, which often includes a skull and is meant to remind viewers of the shortness of life and the emptiness of material things.

CuratorialText: Orange above! With the coronation of King William I (1772-1843), the Netherlands became a monarchy for the first time in 1815. This first king is the son of the last Stadtholder, William V, who had fled to England years earlier. King William I has canals and roads constructed in the Netherlands and invests in trade, shipping, and industry. However, with his sights firmly set on the economy, William I pays less attention to other affairs of state. Discontent in the Southern Netherlands grows. The population rises in revolt, and in 1831, the secession of Belgium becomes a reality. The king resists, but attempts to regain the territory come to nothing. After years of struggle, William I recognizes independent Belgium in 1839. Disappointed, he abdicates the throne a year later, and his son, William II, takes over.
persona3_adapted: Orange above! With the coronation of King William I (1772–1843), the Netherlands became a monarchy for the first time in 1815. This first king was the son of the last Stadtholder—a title for a leader or governor in the Dutch Republic—William V, who had fled to England years earlier. King William I had canals and roads built across the Netherlands and invested in trade, shipping, and industry. However, with his attention focused almost entirely on the economy, William I paid less attention to other matters of state. Discontent grew in the Southern Netherlands. The population rose in revolt, and in 1831, Belgium broke away and became an independent country. The king resisted, but his attempts to reclaim the territory failed. After years of struggle, William I officially recognized an independent Belgium in 1839. Disappointed, he gave up the throne a year later, and his son, William II, took over.

CuratorialText: The extensive costume collection of the Rijksmuseum, the oldest in the Netherlands, tells a wealth of stories. From women's and men's to children's clothing: these pieces reveal stories about the wearer and their taste, the methods of the makers, and local and global influences. Many garments are custom-made for a special occasion, while others showcase the technological advancements of ready-to-wear fashion. From the Frisian branch of the Orange dynasty in the 17th century to Yves Saint Laurent: the collection features highlights of Dutch and international fashion. The collection is a remarkable reflection of fashion developments throughout the centuries.
persona3_adapted: The Rijksmuseum’s extensive costume collection—the oldest in the Netherlands—offers a rich array of stories. Spanning women’s, men’s, and children’s clothing, these pieces reveal details about the wearer’s personal taste, the techniques used by the makers, and the blend of local and global influences. Many garments were custom-made for special events, while others highlight technological advances in ready-to-wear fashion. From the Frisian branch of the Orange dynasty in the 17th century to Yves Saint Laurent, the collection includes highlights of both Dutch and international fashion. It serves as a remarkable reflection of how fashion has evolved over the centuries.

CuratorialText: At the end of the 19th century, a new art style emerges. It is a reaction by artists who rebelled against the historicism prevailing at the time, which harked back to the styles of previous centuries. The new art, aptly named Art Nouveau, is characterized by natural forms, such as flowers, leaves, animals, and insects. The emphasis lies on flowing lines, geometric shapes, and asymmetrical compositions. With Paris, Brussels, and Munich as centers, Art Nouveau spread as an international movement in Western Europe and the United States. During this period, which lasted from approximately 1890 until the beginning of the First World War in 1914, Art Nouveau became visible in all fields of the arts.
persona3_adapted: In the late 1800s, a new artistic style emerged. It was a response by artists who rejected the dominant trend of the time—historicism, which simply imitated the styles of earlier centuries. This new style, fittingly called Art Nouveau, drew inspiration from natural forms like flowers, leaves, animals, and insects. Its key features were flowing lines, geometric shapes, and unbalanced (asymmetrical) arrangements. With major centers in Paris, Brussels, and Munich, Art Nouveau spread as an international movement across Western Europe and the United States. During this period, which lasted from about 1890 until the start of World War I in 1914, Art Nouveau appeared in every field of the arts.

CuratorialText: Hendrick Goltzius (1558-1617) was born in Germany. Trained as a draftsman, engraver, and glass painter, he also developed into a renowned painter. Goltzius followed his teacher Dirck Volkertsz. Coornhert to Haarlem. There, together with Karel van Mander and Cornelis Cornelisz. van Haarlem, he introduced the Mannerism of Bartholomäus Spranger to the Netherlands. The style is recognizable by muscular bodies, twisted poses, and small heads in intricate compositions. During a trip through Italy in 1590-91, Goltzius was inspired by the work of the Italian masters. Back in the Netherlands, he left Mannerism behind and opted for a more classicist style with classical proportions in his work. His technically complex engravings, in particular, were admired throughout Europe. Goltzius's oeuvre provides a fine reflection of the developments in Dutch painting around 1600.
persona3_adapted: Hendrick Goltzius (1558-1617) was born in Germany. Trained as a draftsman, engraver, and glass painter, he also became a well-known painter. Goltzius followed his teacher Dirck Volkertsz. Coornhert to Haarlem. There, together with Karel van Mander and Cornelis Cornelisz. van Haarlem, he introduced a style called Mannerism—brought from Bartholomäus Spranger—to the Netherlands. This style is recognizable by its muscular bodies, twisted poses, and small heads within complex compositions. During a trip through Italy in 1590-91, Goltzius was inspired by the work of Italian masters. After returning to the Netherlands, he moved away from Mannerism and adopted a more classicist approach, emphasizing balanced proportions in his work. His technically intricate engravings, in particular, were admired across Europe. Goltzius's body of work offers a clear reflection of the changes taking place in Dutch painting around 1600.

CuratorialText: Rembrandt van Rijn (1606-1669) decided at a young age that he wanted to become a painter. He first apprenticed with Jacob van Swanenburgh in his hometown of Leiden and subsequently continued his training with Pieter Lastman in Amsterdam. Rembrandt allowed himself to be unconstrained by rules and experimented freely with daring compositions, paint handling, and rendering of textures. He enhanced the drama in his depictions with strong light-dark contrasts. His early works are painted quite precisely; later, his brushstrokes became increasingly loose. He regularly received important commissions, including his most famous and largest painting, The Night Watch. Instead of a static portrait, Rembrandt painted a lively moment in which all the figures are depicted in action. In addition to this masterpiece, the Rijksmuseum holds 24 other paintings, 64 drawings, and approximately 1,300 prints by Rembrandt in its collection.
persona3_adapted: Rembrandt van Rijn (1606-1669) decided at a young age that he wanted to become a painter. He first trained with Jacob van Swanenburgh in his hometown of Leiden and later continued his studies with Pieter Lastman in Amsterdam. Rembrandt refused to be limited by traditional rules and experimented freely with bold compositions, the way he handled paint, and how he depicted different surfaces and textures. He heightened the sense of drama in his scenes by using strong contrasts between light and dark. His early works are painted quite precisely; later, his brushstrokes became increasingly loose and free. He regularly received important commissions, including his most famous and largest painting, *The Night Watch*. Rather than creating a formal, still portrait, Rembrandt painted a lively moment in which all the figures are shown in action. In addition to this masterpiece, the Rijksmuseum holds 24 other paintings, 64 drawings, and about 1,300 prints by Rembrandt in its collection.

CuratorialText: Although Johannes Vermeer (1632-1675) is now world-famous, little is known about him. It is clear, however, that the native of Delft spent his days as an art dealer, father of a large family, and, naturally, a painter. His early work includes a Biblical and a mythological scene. Other early works reveal influences from the Utrecht Caravaggists. Vermeer primarily paints interiors and tronies (portrait-like studies of people). His most famous works are the intimate genre pieces, snapshots of everyday life: a lady reading a letter, a woman engaged in a household chore. All the scenes share a serene calm, which sometimes even feels somewhat mysterious. Vermeer is admired for his exceptional use of light, powerful colors, and incredible precision. During his lifetime, he rose to a certain fame and commanded high prices for his paintings. Afterward, Vermeer fell into obscurity and was not rediscovered until the mid-19th century.
persona3_adapted: Although Johannes Vermeer (1632-1675) is now world-famous, surprisingly little is known about his life. What is clear is that he lived in the Dutch city of Delft, worked as an art dealer, raised a large family, and, of course, painted. His earliest works include a scene from the Bible and one from classical mythology. Other early paintings show the influence of a group of artists known as the Utrecht Caravaggists—followers of the Italian painter Caravaggio, who used dramatic lighting and realistic detail. Vermeer mostly painted indoor scenes and what are called "tronies"—portrait-like studies of people, often with exaggerated expressions or costumes. His most famous works are intimate scenes of everyday life: a woman reading a letter, another doing a household chore. These paintings share a quiet calm, which sometimes feels even a little mysterious. Vermeer is admired for his masterful handling of light, his bold use of color, and his extraordinary attention to detail. During his lifetime, he achieved a degree of fame and sold his paintings for high prices. After his death, however, his work was largely forgotten, and he was not rediscovered until the mid-19th century.

CuratorialText: Rachel Ruysch (1664-1750) is one of the most successful artists of her time. Her flower still lifes are very detailed. Roses, poppies, carnations, and irises: all almost indistinguishable from the real thing. Her work bears witness to a vast knowledge of nature. Ruysch acquires this knowledge at the Amsterdam Botanical Garden, where her father, Frederik Ruysch, teaches as a professor. She also studies his collection of prepared flowers and insects. She learns the tricks of the trade from still-life painter Willem van Aelst. In 1701, Ruysch becomes the first woman to join the Hague artists' society Pictura. From 1708 to 1716, she works as court painter for the German Elector John William of the Palatinate, a particularly honorable position. He is by no means the only admirer of her work. Ruysch's flower still lifes are collected by enthusiasts far beyond the national borders.
persona3_adapted: Rachel Ruysch (1664-1750) is one of the most successful artists of her time. Her flower paintings, known as still lifes, are extraordinarily detailed. Roses, poppies, carnations, and irises appear so lifelike that they are nearly indistinguishable from the real thing. Her work reveals a deep understanding of the natural world. Ruysch gained this knowledge at the Amsterdam Botanical Garden, where her father, Frederik Ruysch, taught as a professor. She also studied his collection of preserved flowers and insects. She learned the techniques of her craft from Willem van Aelst, a painter who specialized in still lifes. In 1701, Ruysch became the first woman to join Pictura, an artists' society in The Hague. From 1708 to 1716, she worked as a court painter for the German Elector John William of the Palatinate—a particularly prestigious role. He was far from the only admirer of her work. Ruysch's flower still lifes were collected by enthusiasts well beyond the borders of her own country.

CuratorialText: Thérèse Schwartze (1851-1918) was born into the artistic family of painter Johann Georg Schwartze and grew into a talented portrait painter, draftsman, and printmaker. Trained in her father's studio and after art education in Munich and Paris, she develops into a true talent. When her father dies prematurely, the 23-year-old Schwartze becomes the new breadwinner of the family. Her style is powerful, with rapid brushstrokes and large areas of color, which make her portraits full of character yet flattering. Schwartze's work is well received, and she becomes increasingly famous both at home and abroad. Around the year 1900, Schwartze is the most successful portraitist in the Netherlands, and she portrays members of the Royal Family. She works quickly and creates hundreds of portraits in pastel and oil paint during her career, of which the Rijksmuseum holds a number of artist portraits in its collection.
persona3_adapted: Thérèse Schwartze (1851-1918) was born into an artistic family—her father, Johann Georg Schwartze, was a painter—and she grew up to become a talented portrait painter, as well as a skilled draftswoman and printmaker. She trained in her father’s studio and later studied art in Munich and Paris, where her abilities truly blossomed. When her father died unexpectedly, the 23-year-old Schwartze became the main financial provider for her family. Her painting style is bold and energetic, marked by quick, visible brushstrokes and broad areas of color. This approach gives her portraits a strong sense of character while still being flattering to her subjects. Schwartze’s work was warmly received, and she grew increasingly famous both in the Netherlands and abroad. By around 1900, she was the most successful portrait painter in the country, and she painted members of the Dutch royal family. She worked quickly and produced hundreds of portraits in pastel (a soft, chalk-like medium) and oil paint over the course of her career. The Rijksmuseum holds a number of her portraits of artists in its collection.

CuratorialText: Anyone walking through Amsterdam towards the end of the 19th century has a good chance of encountering George Hendrik Breitner (1857-1923). For days on end, the painter roams through his beloved Amsterdam with a sketchbook. He calls himself 'the painter of the people' and primarily draws and paints 'ordinary' city dwellers. Breitner also uses his camera to capture life. He subsequently applies his typical photographic style, in which figures partially fall outside the frame, to his paintings as well. This style, combined with thick brushstrokes and smudges, is not understood by everyone. Journalists are critical of his work, but enthusiasts love it. To this day, Breitner remains one of the most famous Impressionists of his time.
persona3_adapted: If you were walking through Amsterdam in the late 1800s, you would have had a good chance of running into George Hendrik Breitner (1857–1923). The painter spent days on end wandering through his beloved city with a sketchbook. He called himself "the painter of the people," and focused his drawings and paintings on ordinary city dwellers. Breitner also used a camera to capture everyday life. He then brought a photographic style into his paintings—one where figures are often cut off at the edge of the frame, as if caught in a snapshot. This approach, combined with thick brushstrokes and smudged paint, was not understood by everyone. Journalists were critical of his work, but his fans loved it. To this day, Breitner remains one of the most famous Impressionists—artists who aimed to capture fleeting moments and the effects of light—of his time.

CuratorialText: He drew extensively and received his first painting lessons from Anton Mauve, a cousin by marriage and a renowned painter. In his early years, Van Gogh primarily drew and painted many peasant scenes in dark colors. After a brief period of study in Antwerp, Van Gogh left for Paris, where he was influenced by Impressionism and Pointillism. He developed a style characterized by short brushstrokes and bright, unmixed colors applied side by side. In 1888, he moved to sunny Southern France. Van Gogh struggled with mental health issues, for which he was hospitalized. However, this did not prevent him from continuing to paint. It was only after his death, in 1890, that his work reached the general public and Van Gogh became world-famous.
persona3_adapted: He drew extensively and received his first painting lessons from Anton Mauve, a relative by marriage and a well-known painter. In his early years, Van Gogh mainly drew and painted many scenes of peasant life using dark colors. After a short period of study in Antwerp, he moved to Paris, where he was influenced by two art movements: Impressionism, which focused on capturing the effects of light and everyday scenes, and Pointillism, a technique that uses small dots of color to build an image. He developed a style marked by short brushstrokes and bright, pure colors placed next to each other without blending. In 1888, he moved to sunny southern France. Van Gogh struggled with mental health issues and was hospitalized at times, but this did not stop him from continuing to paint. It was only after his death in 1890 that his work reached the general public and he became world-famous.

CuratorialText: Born into a Utrecht family of silversmiths, the career path of Adam van Vianen (1568/69-1627) was determined early on. Around 1590, Van Vianen must have completed his master's examination, as he worked independently from that moment on and joined the blacksmiths' guild. His imaginative objects tell a story through lobe-like ornaments and forms. In 1614, Van Vianen received a commission from the Amsterdam guild to create a jug in memory of his own brother Paulus, who was also a silversmith and had passed away shortly before. The jug became a tribute to his brother's work and creativity. In this jug, the ornament plays the leading role for the first time.
persona3_adapted: Born into a family of silversmiths in Utrecht, Adam van Vianen (1568/69-1627) had his career path set from an early age. Around 1590, he likely passed his master's examination, as he began working independently from that point and joined the blacksmiths' guild. His imaginative objects tell a story through decorative shapes that resemble lobes or rounded forms. In 1614, the Amsterdam guild commissioned Van Vianen to create a jug in memory of his own brother Paulus, also a silversmith, who had recently died. The jug became a tribute to his brother's work and creativity. In this piece, the decorative forms take center stage for the first time.

CuratorialText: Visual artist and photographer Viviane Sassen (1972) visited the village of Pikin Slee, deep in the rainforest of Suriname, in 2012. Here, people live without running water, electricity, roads, or the internet. But however remote the village may be, the influence of the modern world is clearly noticeable. For instance, this community also possesses mobile phones and plastic household items. The photo series that Sassen created in Pikin Slee captures the beauty of everyday life in abstract compositions. Largely in black and white, but with the colorful splashes characteristic of Sassen's earlier work. In 2013, the Pikin Slee series was added to the collection of the Rijksmuseum as a modern addition to the collection of photographs of Suriname, which until then had been primarily historical in character.
persona3_adapted: Visual artist and photographer Viviane Sassen (born 1972) visited the village of Pikin Slee, deep in the rainforest of Suriname, in 2012. The people living there have no running water, electricity, roads, or internet. Yet despite the village’s isolation, the influence of the modern world is clearly visible—this community, for example, also has mobile phones and plastic household items. The photo series Sassen created in Pikin Slee captures the beauty of everyday life through images that arrange shapes, patterns, and forms in a deliberately non-literal way—what art specialists call "abstract compositions." Most of the photographs are in black and white, but they include bursts of bright color, a feature that has become a signature of Sassen’s earlier work. In 2013, the Pikin Slee series entered the collection of the Rijksmuseum, adding a modern perspective to the museum’s photographs of Suriname, which until then had been mostly historical in nature.

CuratorialText: William of Orange (1533–1584) was an ambitious nobleman who rose to become the leader of the Revolt and was revered as the 'father of the fatherland'. At the age of 11, William inherited the French principality of Orange and received the title 'Prince of Orange'. He led a turbulent life with an impressive career. Philip II, the son of Charles V, appointed William as Stadtholder of Holland, Zeeland, and Utrecht, the most important Dutch provinces. The two men clashed. Philip strived for an empire with central authority and one religion: Roman Catholicism. William, on the other hand, stood for freedom of religion and conscience for everyone. This struggle cost him his life on July 10, 1584, from a gunshot.

At the age of 11, William inherited the French principality of Orange and received the title 'Prince of Orange'. He led a turbulent life with an impressive career. Philip II, the son of Charles V, appointed William as Stadtholder—a kind of governor or provincial leader—of Holland, Zeeland, and Utrecht, the most important Dutch provinces. The two men clashed. Philip strived for an empire with central authority and one religion: Roman Catholicism. William, on the other hand, stood for freedom of religion and conscience for everyone. This struggle cost him his life on July 10, 1584, from a gunshot.

CuratorialText: Who is that girl in the painting by Simon Maris, anyway? Little is known about her. However, it is clear that around the year 1906, she modeled for Simon Maris in his studio at Keizersgracht 498. She was about 12 years old at the time. This is evident from photographs Maris took of the young lady in his studio. Documents have been preserved in Simon Maris's archive in which he refers to her as 'Isabella'. Some other paintings Maris made of the girl were also given this name. Nevertheless, it is not certain that this young lady was really named 'Isabella'. It is possible that Maris invented this name for her. Or perhaps she used a pseudonym herself, as models often did at that time.
persona3_adapted: Who is that girl in Simon Maris’s painting? We know very little about her. What is clear is that around 1906, she posed for Maris in his studio at Keizersgracht 498. She was about 12 years old at the time. We know this from photographs Maris took of her in that same studio. In Maris’s personal archive, documents have survived in which he refers to her as “Isabella.” Some of his other paintings of the girl also carry this name. Still, we cannot be sure that “Isabella” was her real name. It is possible that Maris invented the name. Or perhaps she used a false name herself, as models often did in that period.

CuratorialText: Amalia of Solms (1602-1675) played a crucial role in increasing the prestige of the House of Orange and the international positioning of the Netherlands.
