She serves as a sounding board and advisor to her husband, Stadtholder Frederik Hendrik. When he falls ill, she assumes some of his duties and charts a course that heralds the end of the Eighty Years' War. Three years after her husband's death, her son passes away, marking the beginning of the first stadtholderless era. To safeguard the future of the House of Orange, Van Solms exerts national and international influence. She uses art as a political tool. Her collection of paintings, furniture, and Asian objects competes with other European courts. In doing so, she firmly establishes the Netherlands, with the House of Orange at its head, as a European center of art and trade.
persona3_adapted: Amalia of Solms (1602-1675) played a crucial role in raising the status of the House of Orange and strengthening the Netherlands' position on the world stage. She acted as a trusted advisor and sounding board for her husband, Stadtholder Frederik Hendrik—a Stadtholder being a kind of political and military leader in the Dutch Republic. When he fell ill, she took on some of his responsibilities and helped steer a course that led to the end of the Eighty Years' War. Three years after her husband's death, her son also died, marking the start of a period known as the first stadtholderless era, when the country had no official leader from the House of Orange. To protect the future of her family, Van Solms used her influence both within the Netherlands and abroad. She turned to art as a political tool. Her collection of paintings, furniture, and objects from Asia was designed to rival the displays of other European royal courts. In this way, she helped firmly establish the Netherlands—with the House of Orange at its head—as a major European center for both art and trade.

CuratorialText: Between 1650 and 1672, there was no Stadtholder. The most powerful citizens governed the country. As Grand Pensionary of Holland, Johan de Witt rose to become the most influential politician. He cunningly plays England and France, the Republic's enemies, against each other. Nevertheless, these countries, together with the prince-bishoprics of Münster and Cologne, attack the Netherlands in 1672. His brother, Cornelis de Witt, oversees the military activities as a deputy of the States General. As the foreign troops gain ground, the call for the return of the Stadtholder grows ever louder. Cornelis is arrested on suspicion of conspiracy against the Prince of Orange. When Johan visits him in prison, an incited mob drags the brothers out and kills them. Long after their deaths, both are remembered as champions of 'true freedom'.
persona3_adapted: Between 1650 and 1672, the Netherlands had no Stadtholder—a position similar to a head of state or military leader, usually held by a member of the House of Orange. Instead, the country was governed by its most powerful citizens. Johan de Witt, serving as Grand Pensionary (the highest political office in the province of Holland), became the most influential politician of the time. He skillfully played England and France—both enemies of the Dutch Republic—against each other. Despite his efforts, these countries, along with the prince-bishoprics of Münster and Cologne, attacked the Netherlands in 1672. His brother, Cornelis de Witt, oversaw military operations as a deputy of the States General, the national assembly. As foreign forces advanced, public demand for the return of a Stadtholder grew louder. Cornelis was arrested on suspicion of plotting against the Prince of Orange. When Johan visited him in prison, an angry mob dragged both brothers out and killed them. Long after their deaths, they were remembered as defenders of what was called 'true freedom'—a vision of republican governance without a single powerful ruler.

CuratorialText: At the beginning of the 19th century, Hortense de Beauharnais (1783-1837) and Louis Napoleon became the first Queen and King of Holland, appointed by Emperor Napoleon Bonaparte. The royal couple had three sons together, including the future Emperor Napoleon III, but it was an unhappy marriage. Queen Hortense was often present at festivities and official occasions and, partly due to her visits to local businesses, became increasingly popular with the people. That was until her husband, displeased with her popularity, forced her into the background. After Napoleon's defeat, she settled in Switzerland without her husband. At her impressive Arenenberg estate, De Beauharnais, herself a talented musician and composer, organized cultural gatherings that were talked about for a long time afterwards.
persona3_adapted: In the early 1800s, Hortense de Beauharnais (1783-1837) and Louis Napoleon became the first King and Queen of Holland, appointed by Emperor Napoleon Bonaparte. The royal couple had three sons together—one of whom would later become Emperor Napoleon III—but their marriage was an unhappy one. Queen Hortense frequently appeared at celebrations and official events, and partly because she also visited local businesses, she grew increasingly popular with the Dutch people. That changed when her husband, displeased by her popularity, pushed her out of the public eye. After Napoleon’s defeat, she settled in Switzerland without her husband. At her impressive estate in Arenenberg, De Beauharnais—herself a talented musician and composer—hosted cultural gatherings that people talked about for a long time afterward.

CuratorialText: On July 13, 1885, the new Rijksmuseum officially opened its doors after a construction period of nearly ten years. The building was designed by the architect Pierre Cuypers (1827–1921), a prominent member of the Catholic Church. On the exterior of the Rijksmuseum, Cuypers depicted himself, just as the master builders did in the decoration of cathedrals during the Middle Ages. Over the years, many original decorations in the museum have disappeared. During the last renovation, however, Cuypers' work was restored to its former glory. The Rijksmuseum was not Cuypers' only showpiece. In 1889, 'his' Central Station in Amsterdam was unveiled. In addition to these famous projects, Cuypers focused primarily on churches and monuments.
persona3_adapted: On July 13, 1885, the new Rijksmuseum officially opened its doors after nearly ten years of construction. The building was designed by the architect Pierre Cuypers (1827–1921), a prominent member of the Catholic Church. On the exterior of the Rijksmuseum, Cuypers included a depiction of himself, following a tradition used by master builders in the Middle Ages, who often placed their own images in the decorations of cathedrals. Over the years, many of the museum’s original decorative features were lost. During the most recent renovation, however, Cuypers’ work was restored to its original appearance. The Rijksmuseum was not Cuypers’ only major achievement. In 1889, his Central Station in Amsterdam was also unveiled. Beyond these famous projects, Cuypers focused mainly on designing churches and monuments.

CuratorialText: Willem Drees (1886-1988) made a career in The Hague as a member of the Social Democratic Workers' Party, which would later become the PvdA. The politician climbed a long ladder of various positions: municipal councilor, alderman, Member of Parliament. In the first cabinet elected after the war, Drees was appointed Minister of Social Affairs. With his Drees Emergency Act, a provision for fixed benefits, he helped the elderly out of financial distress. Drees was popular and became Prime Minister in 1948. It was his task to effectively handle the reconstruction of the Netherlands. Drees proved to be the right man in the right place. With a sense of solidarity, the Netherlands was rebuilt. Under Drees's leadership, until 1958, the foundation of the modern Dutch welfare state was laid. To this day, the statesman is appreciated for his efforts for the Netherlands.
persona3_adapted: Willem Drees (1886–1988) built his political career in The Hague as a member of the Social Democratic Workers' Party, which later became the Labour Party (PvdA). Over time, he rose through a series of roles: city councilor, deputy mayor, and member of parliament. After World War II, Drees was appointed Minister of Social Affairs in the first elected government. There, he introduced the Drees Emergency Act, a law that provided fixed financial support to help elderly citizens escape poverty. Popular among the public, Drees became Prime Minister in 1948. His main task was to oversee the reconstruction of the Netherlands effectively. He proved to be exactly the right person for the job. Guided by a sense of shared responsibility, the country was rebuilt. Under Drees’s leadership, which lasted until 1958, the foundations of the modern Dutch welfare state were established. Even today, this statesman is remembered and respected for his contributions to the Netherlands.

CuratorialText: At the end of the 16th century, there was a preference in Italy for Mannerism, a style full of complex compositions and figures in intricate, twisted poses. Around 1600, the Italian Baroque artist Michelangelo Merisi da Caravaggio broke radically with this art form. His figures are actually very realistic. Wrinkled faces, unkempt hair, dirty feet: gods and saints are depicted as very ordinary people. Caravaggio has a preference for strong light-dark contrasts, dramatic effects, and compositions with a clear structure. His method spreads rapidly throughout Europe. Especially in Utrecht, around 1620, many followers, known as Caravaggists, can be found. Artists such as Gerard van Honthorst, Hendrick ter Brugghen, and Dirck van Baburen travel to Rome to study Caravaggio's style. And although he never visited Italy, Rembrandt was also inspired by Caravaggio's lighting effects.
persona3_adapted: In the late 1500s, Italian artists favored a style called Mannerism, which featured crowded, complicated arrangements and figures in twisted, unnatural poses. Around 1600, the Italian artist Caravaggio broke sharply with this tradition. His figures are strikingly realistic. Wrinkled faces, messy hair, dirty feet: gods and saints appear as ordinary people. Caravaggio favored strong contrasts between light and dark, dramatic effects, and compositions with a clear, straightforward structure. His approach spread quickly across Europe. In Utrecht, around 1620, many followers—known as the Caravaggists—emerged. Artists like Gerard van Honthorst, Hendrick ter Brugghen, and Dirck van Baburen traveled to Rome to study Caravaggio’s style. And although he never visited Italy, Rembrandt was also inspired by Caravaggio’s dramatic use of light and shadow.

CuratorialText: The 15th and 16th centuries are broadly regarded as the period of the Renaissance, a term that literally means 'rebirth'. In Europe, the arts, literature, and sciences flourish due to a new appreciation of the classical antiquity of the Romans and Greeks. Artists and architects seek ideal proportions. They study Roman architecture and adopt formal languages ​​such as round arches, columns, and pediments. The visual arts increasingly focus on a realistic representation of nature and man, using perspective and anatomical studies. Alongside religious themes, secular themes and mythological representations become popular. The Renaissance spreads from Italy throughout Europe, thanks to the rise of printmaking and printing in the mid-15th century.
persona3_adapted: The 15th and 16th centuries are widely considered the Renaissance, a word that means 'rebirth.' During this time, the arts, literature, and sciences thrived across Europe, driven by a renewed interest in the classical cultures of ancient Greece and Rome. Artists and architects sought out what they considered perfect proportions. They studied Roman buildings and adopted features like rounded arches, columns, and triangular gables (called pediments). In painting and sculpture, there was a growing focus on depicting nature and the human body realistically, using techniques like perspective (creating the illusion of depth on a flat surface) and careful study of human anatomy. While religious subjects remained important, non-religious themes and stories from mythology also became popular. The Renaissance began in Italy and spread throughout Europe, helped by the development of printmaking and the printing press in the mid-1400s.

CuratorialText: The period between the First and Second World Wars is dominated by Art Deco, an international art movement in which tradition and industrial progress go hand in hand. Emerging from Art Nouveau, Art Deco also draws inspiration from stylized plant and floral motifs as well as linear, geometric designs. Additionally, artists look to folk art and art forms from Africa, East Asia, and Central America, a source of inspiration that is further fueled by archaeological finds. The term Art Deco is derived from the 1925 World's Fair in Paris, the Exposition Internationale des Arts Décoratifs et Industriels Modernes.
persona3_adapted: The period between the First and Second World Wars is defined by Art Deco, an international artistic movement that blends tradition with industrial progress. Building on the earlier style known as Art Nouveau, Art Deco also takes inspiration from simplified plant and flower patterns, as well as designs based on straight lines and geometric shapes. Artists of this era also looked to folk art and the visual traditions of Africa, East Asia, and Central America—a source of ideas that was further enriched by new archaeological discoveries. The name "Art Deco" comes from the 1925 World's Fair in Paris, officially called the Exposition Internationale des Arts Décoratifs et Industriels Modernes.

CuratorialText: From around 1150, increasingly tall churches rose in and around Paris, with Notre-Dame as the most famous example. It is seen as the beginning of Gothic architecture, a style that spread rapidly throughout the rest of Europe and would continue until shortly after 1500. Church walls became airier thanks to tall pointed arch windows fitted with colorful stained glass; the vaults rested on slender columns and were strikingly ribbed, and on the exterior, the masonry was supported by slender flying buttresses with pinnacles. Under the influence of the Gothic style, sculptors also gave their human, animal, and plant figures a stylized appearance with elegant poses, calligraphic drapery, and graceful gestures. Meanwhile, the forms of Gothic architecture also seeped into other art forms, for example, painting, illuminated manuscripts, metalwork, and furniture. The Rijksmuseum possesses numerous ecclesiastical objects in this style, such as painted altars, silver monstrances, wooden prayer beads, and a small nativity scene.
persona3_adapted: From around 1150, increasingly tall churches began to appear in and around Paris, with Notre-Dame being the most famous example. This is considered the beginning of Gothic architecture, a style that spread quickly across the rest of Europe and remained popular until shortly after 1500. Church interiors became lighter and more open, thanks to tall, pointed arch windows filled with colorful stained glass. The ceilings, or vaults, were supported by slender columns and featured visible, rib-like lines that added structure and decoration. On the outside, the stone walls were reinforced by graceful, arch-shaped supports called flying buttresses, often topped with small, pointed spires known as pinnacles. Under the influence of the Gothic style, sculptors also gave their figures—whether human, animal, or plant—a stylized look, with elegant poses, flowing, calligraphy-like folds in clothing, and graceful gestures. At the same time, the forms of Gothic architecture spread into other art forms, such as painting, hand-painted books (illuminated manuscripts), metalwork, and furniture. The Rijksmuseum holds many religious objects in this style, including painted altarpieces, silver containers for the Eucharist (monstrances), wooden prayer beads, and a small nativity scene.

CuratorialText: The term Impressionism, which means snapshot, was initially used in the early 1870s as a negative label for the work of a group of Parisian painters. Claude Monet, Edgar Degas, and Berthe Morisot depict reality as seen through the eyes of the artist. The French Impressionists are inspired by the realistic style of the French Barbizon School and the work of the slightly older Dutch artist Johan Barthold Jongkind. They work with bright colors and short, loose brushstrokes. They choose their themes from modern life. Due to the sketchy approach, it sometimes appears as if their paintings were created quickly and spontaneously. This often leads critics to say that the artworks are unfinished. From the late 1880s onwards, appreciation for French Impressionism also grows in the Netherlands.
persona3_adapted: The term Impressionism—meaning something like a "snapshot"—was first used in the early 1870s as a critical label for the work of a group of Parisian painters. Artists such as Claude Monet, Edgar Degas, and Berthe Morisot aimed to capture reality as it appeared through the artist's own eyes. The French Impressionists were inspired by the realistic approach of the Barbizon School (a group of French painters who focused on natural landscapes) and by the slightly older Dutch artist Johan Barthold Jongkind. They worked with vivid colors and used short, loose brushstrokes—meaning their paint application was quick and visible rather than smooth and blended. Their subjects were drawn from modern, everyday life. Because their style often looked sketchy or unfinished, it sometimes seemed as though their paintings were made quickly and spontaneously. This led many critics to claim the works were incomplete. Starting in the late 1880s, appreciation for French Impressionism also began to grow in the Netherlands.

CuratorialText: At the end of the 19th century, a new art style emerges. It is a reaction by artists who rebelled against the historicism prevailing at the time, which harked back to the styles of previous centuries. The new art, aptly named Art Nouveau, is characterized by natural forms, such as flowers, leaves, animals, and insects. The emphasis lies on flowing lines, geometric shapes, and asymmetrical compositions. With Paris, Brussels, and Munich as centers, Art Nouveau spread as an international movement in Western Europe and the United States. During this period, which lasted from approximately 1890 until the beginning of the First World War in 1914, Art Nouveau became visible in all fields of the arts.
persona3_adapted: At the end of the 19th century, a new artistic style emerged. It was a response by artists who rejected the dominant trend of historicism—the practice of imitating styles from earlier centuries. This new style, aptly named Art Nouveau (meaning "new art" in French), is defined by its use of natural forms, such as flowers, leaves, animals, and insects. The focus is on flowing lines, geometric shapes, and arrangements that are not symmetrical. With major centers in Paris, Brussels, and Munich, Art Nouveau spread as an international movement across Western Europe and the United States. During this period, which lasted from roughly 1890 until the start of World War I in 1914, Art Nouveau appeared in every field of the arts.

CuratorialText: Rembrandt is not only a successful painter, but also exceptionally talented as a draftsman and etcher. The Rijksmuseum collection contains approximately 1,300 prints by his hand, including sometimes multiple copies and different states of a single print. The subjects of his etchings range from biblical and mythological scenes to everyday scenes, landscapes, and (self-)portraits. Rembrandt's development in technique, style, and artistic approach can be clearly seen in these works. His loose drawing style and the high-contrast rendering of light and shadow are unique. The choice to depict the most emotional moments is also remarkable for his time. It makes his work human and relatable. Rembrandt has thus had a major influence on artists of his own time and for a long time thereafter.
persona3_adapted: Rembrandt was not only a highly successful painter but also an exceptionally skilled draftsman and printmaker. The Rijksmuseum holds around 1,300 prints made by his hand, including multiple copies and different versions of the same print. His etchings cover a wide range of subjects, from biblical and mythological stories to everyday scenes, landscapes, and portraits of himself and others. In these works, we can clearly see how his technique, style, and artistic approach evolved over time. His loose, sketch-like drawing style and the dramatic contrast between light and dark are distinctive. Another notable choice for his era was his focus on capturing the most emotionally charged moments. This makes his work feel deeply human and relatable. As a result, Rembrandt had a major influence on artists of his own time and for many years afterward.

CuratorialText: Under the patronage of Augustus the Strong, Elector of Saxony, alchemist Johann Friedrich Böttger succeeded in becoming the first in Europe to produce porcelain in 1708. Not long afterwards, the first European porcelain factory was established in Meissen. The factory played an important role in the dream of August the Strong: a Japanese Palace, in which he wanted to exhibit his collection of Asian and Meissen porcelain. Since the end of the 16th century, porcelain, also known as 'white gold', had been imported from Asia and collected by the wealthy members of European courts and the elite. The fact that porcelain was now also being produced in Europe is one of the most important moments in the history of the European applied arts. The Rijksmuseum holds one of the most important collections of Meissen porcelain, including pieces from the factory's early production from the collection of Margarethe and Franz Oppenheimer.
persona3_adapted: Under the sponsorship of Augustus the Strong, the Elector of Saxony, the alchemist Johann Friedrich Böttger became the first person in Europe to successfully produce porcelain in 1708. Shortly after, the first European porcelain factory was established in Meissen. This factory was central to Augustus the Strong’s grand vision: a Japanese Palace where he intended to display his collection of both Asian and Meissen porcelain. Since the late 1500s, porcelain—often called 'white gold'—had been imported from Asia and collected by wealthy members of European royal courts and the elite. The fact that Europe could now produce its own porcelain marks a major turning point in the history of European decorative arts. The Rijksmuseum holds one of the most significant collections of Meissen porcelain, including early pieces from the factory that once belonged to the collection of Margarethe and Franz Oppenheimer.

CuratorialText: The Rijksmuseum has been collecting Asian ceramics since its founding in 1885. The largest part of this is Chinese (approx. 2,300 pieces) and Japanese (approx. 600), plus another 50 pieces from other Asian countries. After all, the Dutch have been importing porcelain from Asia since the founding of the Dutch East India Company (VOC, 1602). From the 17th century onwards, thin-fired porcelain with Chinese blue decorations became immensely popular. Consequently, Chinese and Japanese porcelain play an important role in Dutch interiors. Since 1952, the collection of the Royal Society of Friends of Asian Art (KVVAK) has also been housed in the Rijksmuseum, comprising pieces that are part of art production in Asia: tea ceramics from Japan, Song bowls and Tang funerary sculptures from China, and ceramics from Southeast Asia and Korea.
persona3_adapted: The Rijksmuseum has been collecting ceramics from Asia since it opened in 1885. The largest part of this collection is Chinese (about 2,300 pieces) and Japanese (about 600), along with another 50 pieces from other Asian countries. This makes sense because the Dutch have been importing porcelain from Asia ever since the Dutch East India Company (VOC) was founded in 1602. Starting in the 17th century, thin, hard-fired porcelain decorated with blue patterns from China became extremely popular. As a result, Chinese and Japanese porcelain became an important feature in Dutch homes. Since 1952, the collection of the Royal Society of Friends of Asian Art (KVVAK) has also been kept at the Rijksmuseum. This collection includes objects that represent different artistic traditions across Asia: Japanese ceramics made for tea ceremonies, Chinese bowls from the Song dynasty and sculptures made for tombs during the Tang dynasty, as well as ceramics from Southeast Asia and Korea.

CuratorialText: From 1610 onwards, around one hundred thousand pieces of Chinese porcelain were imported into the Netherlands annually. To withstand the Chinese competition, Dutch potters began to refine their products. The earthenware becomes thinner, whiter, and shinier. Blue paintings are applied to a base of white tin glaze, a color scheme strongly associated with Asian porcelain. This earthenware is called 'plateel' or 'faience', but in the 17th and 18th centuries it acquires the name 'Hollants porceleyn'. From 1650 onwards, Delft becomes the center of earthenware production, hence the eventual name 'Delft earthenware'. During the same period, trade in Chinese porcelain comes to a virtual standstill due to the civil war in China (1644-1684). This gives Delft earthenware the opportunity to flourish and gain popularity among the European elite.
persona3_adapted: Starting in 1610, about one hundred thousand pieces of Chinese porcelain were imported into the Netherlands each year. To compete with this influx, Dutch potters began to improve their own ceramic products. Their earthenware became thinner, whiter, and shinier. They applied blue designs onto a base of white tin glaze—a color scheme that strongly reminded people of Asian porcelain. This type of earthenware was called 'plateel' or 'faience', but during the 1600s and 1700s, it became known as 'Hollants porceleyn' (Dutch porcelain). After 1650, the city of Delft emerged as the main center for this production, which is why it eventually became known as 'Delft earthenware'. Around the same time, trade in Chinese porcelain nearly stopped due to a civil war in China (1644–1684). This pause gave Delft earthenware a chance to thrive and grow popular among Europe’s wealthy and influential classes.

CuratorialText: The bronze collection of the Rijksmuseum contains a collection of small sculptures from the Renaissance, primarily from Italy. A number of these originated in the late 15th century in the university city of Padua. These are small bronze utilitarian objects inspired by classical antiquity: candlesticks, perfume burners, oil lamps, and inkwells, all decorated with mythological figures. Many small workshops cast such luxury utensils in series, but unique sculptures and objects were also made in bronze. A good example is the large candlestick that Verrocchio designed in 1469 for Palazzo Vecchio, the city hall of Florence. Bronze casting in the style of classical antiquity also flourished in other Italian cities, such as Rome and Venice. In the 16th and 17th centuries, these figurines found their way into the studies of humanist scholars, art cabinets of wealthy citizens, and Kunstkammern of princes and nobility.
persona3_adapted: The Rijksmuseum’s bronze collection includes small sculptures from the Renaissance, mostly from Italy. Several of these were made in the late 1400s in the university city of Padua. They are small, practical bronze objects inspired by ancient Greek and Roman art: candlesticks, perfume burners, oil lamps, and inkwells, all decorated with figures from mythology. Many small workshops produced such luxury items in series, but unique bronze sculptures and objects were also created. A notable example is the large candlestick that Verrocchio designed in 1469 for Palazzo Vecchio, the city hall of Florence. Bronze casting in the style of ancient Greece and Rome also thrived in other Italian cities, such as Rome and Venice. During the 1500s and 1600s, these small figures made their way into the private studies of humanist scholars, the art collections of wealthy citizens, and the cabinets of curiosities owned by princes and nobility.

CuratorialText: Originally, artists in seascapes primarily captured historical events at sea, so that posterity could relive the heroic deeds. 'War correspondent' and artist Willem van de Velde I created eyewitness paintings of famous Dutch naval battles. His son Willem was a master at capturing calm or, conversely, stormy seas. In the 18th and 19th centuries, Ludolf Bakhuyzen and Wijnand Nuijen opted for a dramatized depiction of ships in distress. Bakhuyzen even went out to sea to study weather effects up close. Thanks to painters such as Andreas Schelfhout, Louis Meijer, and father and son Schotel, Dutch art abroad became almost synonymous with the seascape. For the artists of the Hague School, the effect of light and cloudy skies played a major role, and they paid more attention to the harsh life of fishermen.
persona3_adapted: Originally, artists who painted seascapes focused mainly on recording historical events at sea, so that future generations could experience heroic moments again. Willem van de Velde I, who worked like a war correspondent, created paintings based on what he saw of famous Dutch naval battles. His son, Willem van de Velde II, was especially skilled at capturing either calm waters or, on the other hand, stormy seas. In the 1700s and 1800s, Ludolf Bakhuyzen and Wijnand Nuijen chose to portray ships in trouble in a dramatic, heightened way. Bakhuyzen even went out to sea himself to study weather effects up close. Thanks to painters such as Andreas Schelfhout, Louis Meijer, and the father-and-son team Schotel, Dutch art abroad became almost synonymous with the seascape. For the artists of the Hague School, the play of light and cloudy skies became very important, and they paid more attention to the harsh lives of fishermen.

CuratorialText: In the 17th century, the marksmen of the civic guard were regularly immortalized in group portraits. At that time, the powerful cities themselves were responsible for security. The militia ensured the maintenance of order and security. Every adult man up to the age of 60 is expected to join, although he must be able to purchase his own weaponry. The group portraits are a costly undertaking, as each marksman pays for his own portrait. The earliest marksman portraits date from the 16th century and are rather static. In the course of the 17th century, painters opted for a somewhat looser composition, with Rembrandt's Night Watch and the lively Banquet of the Marksmen by Bartholomeus van der Helst as highlights. Not only marksmen's guilds commission group portraits; other urban, provincial, and national organizations and individuals also commission them.
persona3_adapted: In the 17th century, members of the civic guard—citizens who served as a local militia—were frequently depicted together in group portraits. During this period, cities themselves were responsible for maintaining safety and order. The militia helped ensure security, and every adult man up to age 60 was expected to join, provided he could afford to buy his own weapons. These group portraits were expensive projects, since each guardsman paid for his own likeness to be included. The earliest examples of such portraits date back to the 16th century and tend to be quite stiff and formal. Over the course of the 1600s, however, painters began to arrange the figures more naturally and dynamically. Two outstanding examples are Rembrandt’s *The Night Watch* and Bartholomeus van der Helst’s lively *Banquet of the Marksmen*. It wasn’t only the marksmen’s guilds that commissioned these group portraits; other city, regional, and national organizations, as well as private individuals, also ordered them.

CuratorialText: Approximately 230 paintings in the collection of the Rijksmuseum are still lifes. As early as the 16th century, Flemish painters specialized in this genre. These are arranged displays featuring, for example, shells, books, food and drink, tableware, skulls, or dead animals, but also flowers, insects, and sometimes a person in the background. Life literally stands still for a moment. Separate specializations soon emerged within the genre. For instance, Rachel Ruysch primarily painted flowers, while Pieter Claesz and Willem Heda enjoyed creating arrangements with food and eating and drinking utensils. Another variant, the vanitas still life, which often depicts a skull, refers to the transience of life and the material world.
persona3_adapted: Around 230 paintings in the Rijksmuseum’s collection are still lifes—a type of artwork that focuses on inanimate objects. As early as the 1500s, painters in Flanders (now part of Belgium) began to specialize in this category. These works show carefully arranged items such as shells, books, food and drink, tableware, skulls, or dead animals, as well as flowers, insects, and sometimes a person in the background. In these scenes, life is literally frozen for a moment. Over time, distinct subcategories emerged within this genre. For example, Rachel Ruysch focused mainly on painting flowers, while Pieter Claesz and Willem Heda enjoyed composing scenes with food and dining utensils. Another type, known as the vanitas still life—which often includes a skull—serves as a reminder of the fleeting nature of life and the material world.

CuratorialText: The Rijksmuseum has an enormous collection of portraits from the late Middle Ages to the present day. In addition to their artistic value, these more than 1,700 portraits give the past a face. They describe what people looked like: what clothing and jewelry they wore, what their hairstyles were like, and sometimes what the interiors looked like at the time. There are also significant differences, as each century emphasizes different aspects. In the Renaissance, portraits seem to become increasingly realistic, whereas artists from the 20th and 21st centuries move away from that realism. Throughout the centuries, governments, organizations, and individuals have commissioned portraits for all kinds of occasions: to confirm profession or status, for a wedding, or as a memorial after someone's death. The Rijksmuseum collection also contains many self-portraits by famous painters such as Jan Steen, Rembrandt, and Van Gogh.
persona3_adapted: The Rijksmuseum holds a vast collection of portraits spanning from the late Middle Ages to the present day. Beyond their artistic merit, these more than 1,700 portraits give a human face to history. They show us what people looked like—the clothing and jewelry they wore, their hairstyles, and sometimes the interiors of their homes. Yet there are also striking differences across the centuries, as each era highlights different concerns. During the Renaissance, portraits appear to become increasingly lifelike, while artists from the 20th and 21st centuries deliberately move away from that kind of realism. Over time, governments, organizations, and individuals have commissioned portraits for many reasons: to affirm a person’s profession or social standing, to mark a wedding, or to serve as a memorial after death. The Rijksmuseum collection also includes many self-portraits by famous painters such as Jan Steen, Rembrandt, and Van Gogh.

CuratorialText: Genre paintings are paintings depicting scenes from everyday life. They are lively scenes that often take place in kitchens, living rooms, inns, and markets. They capture people in their natural environment: children romping, a family huddled together in the kitchen, or a merchant hawking his wares. The first genre paintings emerged in 16th-century Flemish painting, but in the Netherlands, genre art did not flourish until a century later. The works of Johannes Vermeer, Jan Steen, and Judith Leyster became famous. They often concealed a mischievous content or, conversely, a moral message in their work. The term 'genre paintings' did not emerge until the 19th century, when the popularity of genre art reached a peak. The Rijksmuseum collection also includes works by 19th-century painters such as Jozef Israëls, known for his lively scenes of farmers and fishermen.
persona3_adapted: Genre paintings show scenes from everyday life. These lively images often take place in kitchens, living rooms, inns, or markets, capturing people in their natural surroundings: children playing, a family gathered in the kitchen, or a merchant selling his goods. The first such paintings appeared in 16th-century Flemish art, but in the Netherlands, this style did not flourish until a century later. The works of Johannes Vermeer, Jan Steen, and Judith Leyster became well known. These artists often hid a playful or, alternatively, a moral message within their scenes. The term "genre paintings" itself was not used until the 19th century, when this type of art reached the height of its popularity. The Rijksmuseum collection also includes works by 19th-century painters such as Jozef Israëls, who is known for his vivid depictions of farmers and fishermen.

CuratorialText: A history piece depicts a memorable historical story, which often originates from a written source. This can be a story from the Bible or mythology, but also events from history. Multiple figures are often visible in these narrative pieces. For instance, around 1530, Jan Jansz. Mostaert depicted a scene from the conquest of America, while in 1824, Jan Willem Pieneman captured The Battle of Waterloo , the largest painting in the Rijksmuseum. In the 17th century, painters also chose Roman scenes as a metaphor for the political situation in the Netherlands. For instance, the Batavian revolt against the Romans, depicted by Ferdinand Bol in the painting Claudius Civilis, refers to the struggle against the Spaniards.
persona3_adapted: A history painting tells a memorable story from the past, often based on a written source. This could be a tale from the Bible or mythology, or an actual historical event. These narrative works usually show several figures. For example, around 1530, Jan Jansz. Mostaert painted a scene from the conquest of America, while in 1824, Jan Willem Pieneman created The Battle of Waterloo, the largest painting in the Rijksmuseum. In the 17th century, artists also used Roman scenes as a way to comment on the political situation in the Netherlands. For instance, the Batavian revolt against the Romans, shown by Ferdinand Bol in his painting Claudius Civilis, was meant to echo the Dutch struggle against the Spanish.

CuratorialText: “We, the people of Indonesia, hereby declare the independence of Indonesia.” By uttering these words, the nationalist leader Sukarno declared his country, Indonesia, independent on August 17, 1945. Former colonizer the Netherlands refuses to recognize that independence and attempts to reoccupy Indonesia. In defense of its independence, Indonesia wages war against the Netherlands on its own soil. From the beginning, Indonesia seeks and receives support from the international community. Under international pressure, the Netherlands is forced to recognize Indonesia's independence in 1949. In 1950, Indonesia becomes a member state of the United Nations, and the international confirmation of independence becomes a reality.
persona3_adapted: “We, the people of Indonesia, hereby declare the independence of Indonesia.” With these words, the nationalist leader Sukarno proclaimed his country’s independence on August 17, 1945. The former colonial power, the Netherlands, refused to accept this declaration and attempted to reclaim control over Indonesia. To defend its newly declared independence, Indonesia fought a war against the Netherlands on its own land. From the very beginning, Indonesia sought and received support from other nations around the world. Faced with growing international pressure, the Netherlands was ultimately forced to recognize Indonesia’s independence in 1949. The following year, in 1950, Indonesia became a member of the United Nations, marking the moment when its independence was formally acknowledged by the global community.

CuratorialText: Water is crucial for the development of the Netherlands as a trading nation. The economy grows thanks to the raw materials that enter via rivers and the world's oceans. Consider bulk goods such as grain and timber from the Baltic region, wool from England, wine from France, and raw salt from the Iberian Peninsula. Using these raw materials, the Dutch produce items such as beer, cloth (woolen fabric), fine salt (for the herring trade), weapons, and ships. Many of these products are subsequently exported, often again by water. Cities are important links in this international trade. In the 15th and 16th centuries, Antwerp is the largest port city of the Netherlands. After the (re)conquest by the Spaniards during the Dutch Revolt, Holland, and especially Amsterdam, takes over the role as a staple market. International trade leads to the emergence of a financial market, significant employment, and strong urban growth.
persona3_adapted: Water played a central role in the Netherlands' rise as a trading nation. The economy grew thanks to raw materials arriving by river and sea from around the world. These included bulk goods like grain and timber from the Baltic region, wool from England, wine from France, and raw salt from the Iberian Peninsula. Dutch manufacturers turned these materials into products such as beer, cloth (woolen fabric), refined salt (used in the herring trade), weapons, and ships. Many of these finished goods were then exported, often again by water. Cities served as vital hubs in this international network. In the 15th and 16th centuries, Antwerp was the largest port city in the Netherlands. However, after Spanish forces recaptured the city during the Dutch Revolt, the region of Holland—and especially Amsterdam—took over as the main center for trade and storage. This international commerce gave rise to a financial market, created many jobs, and fueled rapid urban growth.

CuratorialText: What is money actually worth? Why does a small piece of metal worth a few euro cents suddenly have a much higher value due to the '2 euro' imprint? Whoever holds the right to mint coins chooses the appearance, the amount of precious metal, and the value. Through trade contacts, gold and silver coins spread across the world for centuries. Coins with little gold or silver circulated primarily locally, because once abroad, the metal value was of paramount importance. The Amsterdam Exchange Bank was for a long time a key hub for the trade in precious metals. As early as the Eighty Years' War, a large portion of the silver that Spain obtained from Spanish America ended up in Amsterdam. Until well into the 18th century, veritable silver fleets sailed from Spain to the Republic. From Amsterdam, money traveled the world, such as to Asia via the VOC.
persona3_adapted: What is money actually worth? How can a small piece of metal worth just a few euro cents suddenly be valued much higher simply because it bears the imprint "2 euro"? The authority that holds the right to mint coins decides their design, the amount of precious metal they contain, and their face value. For centuries, gold and silver coins spread across the world through trade. Coins with little gold or silver mostly circulated locally, because once they left their home region, the value of the metal itself became the most important factor. The Amsterdam Exchange Bank was for a long time a central hub for the trade in precious metals. As early as the Eighty Years' War, a large portion of the silver that Spain obtained from Spanish America ended up in Amsterdam. Until well into the 18th century, entire fleets of silver ships sailed from Spain to the Dutch Republic. From Amsterdam, money traveled around the world—for example, to Asia through the Dutch East India Company (VOC).

CuratorialText: William of Orange is regarded as the 'Father of the Fatherland' because of his leading role in the struggle for independence. The House of Orange owes its princely title to the insignificant French city-state of Orange on the Rhône, yet manages to carve out a place for itself among the great European royal houses through its wealth and prestige. The wives of the stadtholders play an important role in these networks. Nevertheless, it seems to be the end for the House of Orange at the end of the 18th century: Stadtholder William V flees to England, and the Batavian Republic is proclaimed. But in 1813, his eldest son returns and becomes King William I. From that moment on, the Netherlands is a 'constitutional monarchy,' and a member of the House of Orange-Nassau has always sat on the throne.
persona3_adapted: William of Orange is considered the 'Father of the Fatherland' for his key role in the Dutch fight for independence. The House of Orange gets its princely title from the small French city-state of Orange, located on the Rhône River, yet through its wealth and influence, it manages to earn a place among Europe's great royal families. The wives of the stadtholders—the country's leaders at the time—play an important part in building these political and social connections. Still, by the end of the 18th century, the House of Orange seems to be finished: Stadtholder William V flees to England, and the Batavian Republic is established. However, in 1813, his eldest son returns and becomes King William I. From then on, the Netherlands is a 'constitutional monarchy,' meaning the monarch's powers are limited by law, and a member of the House of Orange-Nassau has always occupied the throne.

CuratorialText: Whose object was it before it came to the museum? The lives, relationships, and circumstances of the owners tell something about the significance of the object. Provenance research reveals stories of pre-war owners and reminds us of the people who were persecuted during the Holocaust. Even if objects were not looted, their provenance provides insight into their history. The Rijksmuseum collection primarily tells the stories of collectors from the period between 1933 and 1945. Research into the collection can therefore not possibly provide a complete picture of all persecuted owners. After all, many everyday objects did not end up in museums or are more difficult to identify. Nevertheless, the objects in the Rijksmuseum remind us of the diversity of pre-war Europe. Provenance research shows that the people persecuted by the Nazis actively contributed to Dutch culture and history. Objects in museums are still the silent witnesses of this today.
persona3_adapted: Whose object was it before it came to the museum? The lives, relationships, and circumstances of the owners tell us something about why the object mattered. Research into an object’s history—called provenance—reveals the stories of people who owned it before the war and reminds us of those who were persecuted during the Holocaust. Even when objects were not stolen, knowing where they came from gives us insight into their past. The Rijksmuseum collection mainly tells the stories of collectors from the years 1933 to 1945. Because of this, research into the collection cannot give a complete picture of all persecuted owners. Many everyday objects never ended up in museums, or are harder to identify. Still, the objects in the Rijksmuseum remind us of the diversity of pre-war Europe. Provenance research shows that the people the Nazis persecuted actively contributed to Dutch culture and history. Objects in museums remain the silent witnesses of this today.

CuratorialText: Before the Europeans arrive, centuries-old trade routes already exist around the Indian Ocean. The Dutch East India Company (VOC) forcibly expands these networks and exploits areas such as the Moluccas and Sri Lanka for spices. Batavia (Jakarta) and the Cape of Good Hope become important centers for sugar, coffee, and rice. In the Atlantic region, Dutch merchants trade in products such as sugar and coffee, which leads to the establishment of the Dutch West India Company (WIC). Over time, slave labor and the slave trade take place at all trading posts. Even for a craftsman in the Netherlands, there is money to be made from colonial trade, for example as a supplier of products for the slave trade or for daily life in the colonies. The transport of the products to Europe and their further processing also generates revenue. In this way, colonial trade stimulates the national economy.
persona3_adapted: Before the arrival of Europeans, trade routes that had existed for centuries already connected regions around the Indian Ocean. The Dutch East India Company, known as the VOC, forcefully expanded these networks and took control of areas such as the Moluccas and Sri Lanka to obtain spices. Batavia (present-day Jakarta) and the Cape of Good Hope grew into important centers for producing sugar, coffee, and rice. In the Atlantic region, Dutch merchants traded goods like sugar and coffee, which led to the creation of the Dutch West India Company, or WIC. Over time, the use of enslaved laborers and the trading of enslaved people became common at all of the company’s trading posts. Even craftsmen living in the Netherlands could profit from colonial trade—for instance, by supplying goods used in the slave trade or items needed for daily life in the colonies. Transporting these products back to Europe and processing them further also brought in income. In this way, colonial trade helped stimulate the Dutch national economy.

CuratorialText: The Netherlands was a colonial power for more than 350 years and established trading posts and colonies worldwide in the 17th century, including in Indonesia, South Africa, Sri Lanka, South America, and the Caribbean. Dutch colonial settlement often took place through the conclusion of trade contracts, but also through military force. The Rijksmuseum possesses many (art) objects that were brought to the Netherlands during and after this period, ranging from war booty to items acquired through trade. It is important to be open about the history of this collection and to reflect on the role of the Netherlands in the colonial past. Through research and collaboration with countries of origin, the Rijksmuseum aims to provide a nuanced and diverse picture of this shared past.
persona3_adapted: The Netherlands was a colonial power for more than 350 years, establishing trading posts and colonies around the world during the 1600s. These included territories in Indonesia, South Africa, Sri Lanka, South America, and the Caribbean. Dutch colonial settlement often happened through trade agreements, but also through the use of military force. The Rijksmuseum holds many objects—both artworks and everyday items—that were brought to the Netherlands during and after this colonial period. Some of these objects were taken as war booty, while others were acquired through trade. It is important to be open about the history of this collection and to think carefully about the Netherlands' role in its colonial past. Through research and collaboration with the countries where these objects originally came from, the Rijksmuseum aims to present a balanced and many-sided view of this shared history.

CuratorialText: For 750 years, Amsterdam has been a city that grows, flourishes, and buzzes. Residents from all corners of the world have made the city what it is today. The first inhabitants of Amsterdam were farmers and fishermen, living along the River Amstel. In the late Middle Ages, Amsterdam grew into a small port city amidst the peatlands. Partly due to the establishment of the trading companies VOC and WIC, the city transformed rapidly into a global metropolis with more than 200,000 inhabitants during the 17th century, where almost every Amsterdammer was directly or indirectly involved in colonial trade. From rope makers to cartographers, from notaries to confectioners. To manage the population growth, a plan was drawn up to expand the city. It was the beginning of the famous canal belt.
persona3_adapted: For 750 years, Amsterdam has been a city that grows, thrives, and hums with activity. People from all over the world have shaped it into what it is today. The earliest residents were farmers and fishermen, living along the Amstel River. In the late Middle Ages, Amsterdam developed into a small port town set among peat bogs. Largely because of the founding of the Dutch East India Company (VOC) and the Dutch West India Company (WIC)—two powerful trading corporations—the city rapidly transformed into a global hub with more than 200,000 inhabitants during the 1600s. During this time, nearly every Amsterdammer was directly or indirectly involved in colonial trade: from rope makers to mapmakers, from notaries to candy makers. To manage the growing population, a plan was created to expand the city. This marked the beginning of the famous ring of canals.

CuratorialText: The tulip originates from Turkey and was introduced to Europe in the late 16th century. The flower proved to thrive particularly well in the sandy soil of the Dutch coastal region. In the 17th century, traders of tulips and other exotic flowers began publishing 'Florilegia', books with detailed illustrations serving as catalogues of their wares. Enthusiasts of unusual flowers and plants could also turn to painters and draftsmen. Flower still lifes became a popular genre in art. Real flowers wilt, but paintings could serve as durable substitutes. Moreover, these flower still lifes sometimes cost even less than the flowers depicted on the canvas.
persona3_adapted: The tulip originally comes from Turkey and was brought to Europe in the late 1500s. The flower grew especially well in the sandy soil of the Dutch coastal region. During the 1600s, traders who sold tulips and other exotic flowers began publishing 'Florilegia'—books filled with detailed illustrations that acted as catalogues of what they had for sale. People who were passionate about unusual flowers and plants could also turn to painters and sketch artists. Paintings of flowers, known as still lifes, became a popular type of art. Real flowers eventually wither and die, but paintings could serve as lasting substitutes. Interestingly, these flower paintings sometimes cost even less than the actual flowers shown in the artwork.

CuratorialText: Throughout history, the role of pets changes significantly, from useful working animals to beloved companions. It was not until between 500 and 1500 that noblemen began keeping dogs for their company. In paintings from the 17th century, these four-legged creatures often appear in that role. For a long time, cats were primarily used as mouse exterminators and had a bad reputation due to their medieval association with witchcraft. It was not until the Victorian era that people accepted cats as pets. A beautiful pedigree cat, such as a British Shorthair or the Persian Longhair, became a status symbol for wealthy citizens. From the 19th century onwards, dogs and cats, and later birds and fish, became everyday pets for all social classes. Artist Henriëtte Ronner-Knip (1821–1909) specialized in painting cats, which brought her fame. She was regularly commissioned to immortalize the beloved pets of aristocratic and bourgeois families.
persona3_adapted: Throughout history, the role of pets has changed significantly, from being working animals to becoming beloved companions. It was only between the years 500 and 1500 that noblemen began keeping dogs simply for their company. In paintings from the 1600s, these four-legged animals often appear in that role. For a long time, cats were mainly used to catch mice, and they had a bad reputation because people in the Middle Ages associated them with witchcraft. It was not until the Victorian era—the period of Queen Victoria's reign in the 1800s—that people began to accept cats as pets. A beautiful purebred cat, such as a British Shorthair or a Persian Longhair, became a status symbol for wealthy citizens. From the 1800s onward, dogs and cats, and later birds and fish, became everyday pets for people of all social classes. The artist Henriëtte Ronner-Knip (1821–1909) specialized in painting cats, which brought her fame. Wealthy families from the aristocracy and upper middle class often hired her to create lasting images of their beloved pets.

CuratorialText: Sculpture. From the Middle Ages to the present day, in every shape and size and encompassing a wide range of materials. This expanding online catalog presents a comprehensive overview of the latest scholarly insights into the Rijksmuseum's vast and highly diverse collection of European sculpture. This online catalog includes every work of European sculpture in the Rijksmuseum's art collection. The most recently published catalog of sculpture dates from 1973. Prompting a thorough update of this valuable, but by now outdated reference work was the ever-growing body of art historical research and the long list of acquisitions made in recent decades.   The Rijksmuseum (and its precursors: the Royal Cabinet of Rareities and later the Dutch Museum of History and Art) began acquiring sculpture on a systematic basis only in the last quarter of the nineteenth century. Additions to the collection prior to this time are few in number. One early example is the Dutch government's purchase ofArtus Quellinus's magnificent marble bust portrait of Andries de Graeff in 1817. Initially, the museum's sculpture acquisition policy was primarily oriented to works of national interest. It was not until the twentieth century that this focus gradually began to shift, largely thanks to the efforts of Adriaan Pit (museum director from 1898 to 1917) and Jaap Leeuwenberg (curator of sculpture from 1948 to 1969 and compiler of the 1973 catalogue), who sought to actively acquire international works. As a result, sculptures of non-Netherlandish origin today make up approximately half of the collection. This international scope received an additional impulse when, in 1952, the Dutch government charged the Rijksmuseum with the care of Italian and German works formerly in the possession of Fritz Mannheimer and recovered from Germany after the Second World War. This enhancement of the collection has since served to stimulate the museum's policy of acquiring international works.
persona3_adapted:
Sculpture. From the Middle Ages to today, in every shape and size and made from a wide range of materials. This growing online catalog offers a thorough look at the latest scholarly understanding of the Rijksmuseum's large and very diverse collection of European sculpture. It includes every European sculpture in the museum's art collection. The last printed catalog of sculpture was published in 1973. What prompted this major update of that valuable but now outdated reference work was the steadily increasing body of art-historical research and the many new pieces acquired in recent decades.

The Rijksmuseum (and its earlier forms: the Royal Cabinet of Rareities and later the Dutch Museum of History and Art) only began collecting sculpture in a planned way during the last quarter of the nineteenth century. Very few pieces were added to the collection before that time. One early example is the Dutch government's purchase in 1817 of Artus Quellinus's impressive marble portrait bust of Andries de Graeff. At first, the museum's approach to acquiring sculpture focused mainly on works of national importance. It was not until the twentieth century that this focus gradually began to change, largely thanks to the efforts of Adriaan Pit (museum director from 1898 to 1917) and Jaap Leeuwenberg (curator of sculpture from 1948 to 1969 and author of the 1973 catalog), who actively sought to acquire works from other countries. As a result, sculptures from outside the Netherlands now make up about half of the collection. This international dimension received an additional boost in 1952, when the Dutch government gave the Rijksmuseum responsibility for Italian and German works that had belonged to Fritz Mannheimer and were recovered from Germany after the Second World War. This strengthening of the collection has since encouraged the museum's policy of acquiring works from around the world.

CuratorialText:
The Rijksmuseum holds a world-class collection of Japanese works on paper: woodblock prints, illustrated books and drawings. Among the almost 4000 prints, more than 800 are exclusive, privately published surimono. This online catalog features 203 surimono donated to the museum between 1991 and 1999 by the collector JHW Goslings (1943-2011). The entries in this catalog are taken from Matthi Forrer's 2013 collection catalog Surimono in the Rijksmuseum Amsterdam.
Poetry and image combined
“Surimono (literally 'printed things') constitute one of the most delicate genres in Japanese printmaking. This genre fascinates because it combines poetry and image and because it presents a pictorial puzzle, which provides the viewer with a particular insight into the intellectual and literary world of the late 18th- and early 19th-century Edo (today's Tokyo). Major artists such asKatsushika Hokusai,Utagawa Kunisada,Toyota HokkeiandYashima Gakutei, to name but a few, provided imagery to accompany the poetic exploits of poetry club members.
persona3_adapted:
The Rijksmuseum holds a world-class collection of Japanese works on paper: woodblock prints, illustrated books and drawings. Among the almost 4,000 prints, more than 800 are exclusive, privately published surimono—a special type of print made for private occasions. This online catalog features 203 surimono donated to the museum between 1991 and 1999 by the collector JHW Goslings (1943-2011). The entries in this catalog are taken from Matthi Forrer's 2013 collection catalog Surimono in the Rijksmuseum Amsterdam.

Poetry and image combined
“Surimono (literally 'printed things') are among the most delicate and refined types of Japanese printmaking. This genre fascinates because it combines poetry and image, and because it presents a visual puzzle—one that offers the viewer a unique window into the intellectual and literary world of late 18th- and early 19th-century Edo (today's Tokyo). Major artists such as Katsushika Hokusai, Utagawa Kunisada, Toyota Hokkei, and Yashima Gakutei, to name just a few, created images to accompany the poetic achievements of poetry club members.

CuratorialText: Portrait of Maria Trip (1619-83), sister of Jacobus Trip and later the wife of Balthasar Coymans. Standing, half-length, facing left. Her left arm rests on a banister, holding a fan.
persona3_adapted: Portrait of Maria Trip (1619–83), sister of Jacobus Trip and later the wife of Balthasar Coymans. She is shown standing, from the waist up, facing left. Her left arm rests on a banister as she holds a fan.

CuratorialText: He sits with outstretched wings on a cloud placed on a round plinth, holding his right index finger to the mouth of his mischievously smiling face, while reaching with his left hand for an arrow in the quiver lying beside him. His left leg is drawn up, and the foot of his right leg, positioned diagonally in front of him, extends over the round plinth. Beside him lies a flowering rose branch. The quiver filled with arrows displays, among other things, fluting, a wreath of laurel leaves, and godrons. The pedestal, a short column, has a profiled base decorated with a wreath of laurel leaves, a shaft with curved fluting containing culots transitioning into a smooth upper section interrupted by a profiled edge, and furthermore, a profiled, overhanging cover plate decorated with egg-and-dart molding, which is rotatable by means of two brass knobs.
persona3_adapted: He sits with outstretched wings on a cloud placed atop a round base, holding his right index finger to the lips of his mischievously smiling face, while reaching with his left hand toward an arrow in the quiver lying beside him. His left leg is drawn up, and the foot of his right leg, positioned diagonally in front of him, extends over the round base. Beside him lies a flowering rose branch. The quiver filled with arrows is decorated with, among other things, vertical grooves, a wreath of laurel leaves, and a pattern of curved, ribbed ornaments. The pedestal, a short column, has a base with layered contours, also decorated with a wreath of laurel leaves, and a shaft with curved grooves that end in leaf-shaped ornaments near the bottom. Above this, the shaft becomes smooth, interrupted by a raised band, and is topped with a projecting, layered cap decorated with a repeating pattern of oval and dart shapes. This cap can be rotated using two brass knobs.

CuratorialText: Indoor room with a mother removing lice from her child's hair, known as 'Mother's Duty'. Interior with mother and child sitting in front of a box bed on the right. A high chair on the right; on the left, a dog sits on the tiled floor and looks at the open window in the room behind. Above the door is a small painting of a landscape.
persona3_adapted: An indoor scene shows a mother removing lice from her child's hair, a task referred to as "Mother's Duty." The two sit in front of a box bed on the right side of the room. To the right stands a high chair; on the left, a dog rests on the tiled floor, gazing toward an open window in the room behind. Above the door hangs a small landscape painting.

CuratorialText: This portrait of Alida Assink presents her in her finest attire, depicted from head to toe. That is quite exceptional, as the painter Kruseman typically reserved such a full-length portrait exclusively for royalty. Assink was 23 years old when this likeness was commissioned by her guardian, a wealthy landowner. The rural setting with hunting dog and garden vase harks back to the English portrait style, which Kruseman admired.
persona3_adapted: This portrait shows Alida Assink from head to toe, dressed in her finest clothing. That is quite unusual, because the painter Kruseman normally created such a full-length portrait only for royalty. Assink was 23 years old when her guardian—a wealthy landowner—commissioned this painting. The countryside setting, complete with a hunting dog and a garden vase, recalls the English style of portraiture that Kruseman admired.

CuratorialText: The Dordrecht artist Aert Schouman was no scientist, but he was deeply interested in true-to-life depictions of the bird realm. His keen powers of observation are demonstrated by the way in which this red-billed toucan grasps the branch, namely with two, rather than three toes like other species. The landscape airily painted in transparent shades of green and blue evokes the bird’s native regions in Guyana and North Brazil.
persona3_adapted: The Dordrecht artist Aert Schouman was not a scientist, but he was deeply committed to portraying birds as realistically as possible. His sharp observational skills are evident in the way this red-billed toucan grips the branch—using two toes instead of three, as most other bird species do. The landscape, painted with a light touch in translucent shades of green and blue, suggests the bird’s natural habitat in Guyana and northern Brazil.

CuratorialText: Still life with roses. Also included are a butterfly and two snails.
persona3_adapted: This painting, titled *Still Life with Roses*, depicts a bouquet of roses alongside a butterfly and two snails.

CuratorialText: Here the Duke of Wellington is receiving the message that Prussian forces are coming to his aid. Wellington, commander of the Anglo-Dutch troops, is the central figure in this group portrait of the major players at Waterloo. Lying wounded on a stretcher in the left foreground is the Dutch Crown Prince, later King William II. Originally intended for Wellington, the painting remained in the Netherlands thanks to William I who bought the picture for his son.
persona3_adapted: Here the Duke of Wellington is receiving the message that Prussian forces are coming to his aid. Wellington, commander of the Anglo-Dutch troops, is the central figure in this group portrait of the major players at Waterloo. Lying wounded on a stretcher in the left foreground is the Dutch Crown Prince, later King William II. Originally intended for Wellington, the painting remained in the Netherlands thanks to William I, who bought the picture for his son.

CuratorialText: Of the noblemen who rebelled against Phillip II’s Spanish rule of the Netherlands, William of Orange rose to become the great leader of the Dutch Revolt. He defended his own interests, strove for greater independence and advocated freedom of religion. In 1580 Phillip II put a price on his head, and he was assassinated four years later.
persona3_adapted: Of the noblemen who rebelled against Philip II's Spanish rule of the Netherlands, William of Orange emerged as the key leader of the Dutch Revolt. He fought to protect his own interests, pushed for greater independence, and supported the idea of religious freedom. In 1580, Philip II placed a bounty on his head, and he was assassinated four years later.

CuratorialText: This still life invites close examination of all the details and elicits marvel at the richness of God’s creation: the colourful flowers, the exotic shells, but certainly also the beautiful insects. At the same time, decay is spreading. Fruit shows the first rotten spots, leaves are eaten away by caterpillars and snails and ants drag a dead fly. The painting is thus also about impermanence.
persona3_adapted: This still life painting encourages you to look closely at every detail and admire the richness of God’s creation: the colorful flowers, the exotic shells, and certainly the beautiful insects. At the same time, signs of decay are appearing. Fruit shows the first rotten spots, leaves are being eaten by caterpillars and snails, and ants drag a dead fly. So the painting is also about the fleeting nature of life.

CuratorialText: Presented here are two squirrel monkeys from Central America, two white sulphur-crested cockatoos from Australia, a grey parrot from Africa and a purple-naped lory – on a chain at the lower left – from Indonesia. Hondecoeter combined these creatures and several other splendid birds in this painting, which was destined for Het Loo, the palace of William III. It hung above the door of the king’s private apartment.
persona3_adapted: Here are two squirrel monkeys from Central America, two white sulphur-crested cockatoos from Australia, a grey parrot from Africa, and a purple-naped lory—shown on a chain at the lower left—from Indonesia. In this painting, Hondecoeter brought together these animals and several other magnificent birds. The work was created for Het Loo, the palace of William III, and was hung above the door of the king’s private apartment.

CuratorialText: This is a panoramic, sun-drenched river landscape – somewhat hazy in the distance – with sketchily drawn clouds. Two Dutch officers halt at the river, and water their horses next to a herder and his resting cattle. This idyllic scene does not unfold in Italy, but along the Waal between Nijmegen and Cleves, where Cuyp made landscape drawings that he took back to his workshop in Dordrecht.
persona3_adapted: This is a wide, sunlit river scene, with the distance appearing slightly hazy and the clouds loosely suggested rather than painted in fine detail. Two Dutch officers stop by the river to water their horses, standing near a herder and his resting cattle. Although this peaceful countryside view might seem Italian, it actually takes place along the Waal River, between the cities of Nijmegen and Cleves. Cuyp made drawings of this landscape on location and later brought them back to his studio in Dordrecht to use as references for his paintings.

CuratorialText: Ice entertainment near a village. Winter village scene with many figures skating, playing, and bowling on the ice. On the left, two people have fallen through the ice. In the distance, a drawbridge and a windmill.
persona3_adapted: On a frozen river near a village, a lively winter scene unfolds as many people skate, play, and bowl on the ice. To the left, two figures have broken through the ice and fallen into the water. In the background, a drawbridge and a windmill can be seen, adding depth to the rural landscape.

CuratorialText: This dolls’ house is exceptionally realistic. All the contents have been made of authentic materials, and the proportions are exactly correct. The fine cabinet, of tortoiseshell decorated with pewter inlays, was made by a cabinetmaker from France, who worked in Amsterdam for several years. Petronella Oortman was married to the Amsterdam merchant Johannes Brandt.
persona3_adapted: This dolls’ house is remarkably lifelike. Every object inside is made from real materials, and the scale is perfectly accurate. The elegant cabinet, crafted from tortoiseshell and decorated with pewter inlays, was built by a French cabinetmaker who worked in Amsterdam for several years. Petronella Oortman was married to Johannes Brandt, a merchant from Amsterdam.

CuratorialText: This scene is painted on a copper panel. Its smooth surface was well suited to meticulous and detailed finish. Van Kessel made dozens of such little ‘coppers’ of insects and other animals, which he would arrange in a larger composition. They may have also served as decorative panels for a small cabinet of curiosities.
persona3_adapted: This scene is painted on a copper panel. The smooth surface of the metal allowed the artist to work with great precision and fine detail. Van Kessel created dozens of these small copper paintings of insects and other animals, which he would later arrange into a larger composition. They may have also been used as decorative panels for a small cabinet of curiosities—a type of room or piece of furniture where collectors displayed unusual or fascinating objects.

CuratorialText: 18 June 1648: a banquet is taking place at the Amsterdam crossbowmen’s guild. The occasion was the signing of the Treaty of Münster, which marked an end to the war with Spain. The captains of the civic guard company shake hands as a sign of peace, and the drinking horn is passed around. The poem on the drum proclaims the joy of Amsterdam’s armed militia that their weapons can henceforth be laid to rest.
persona3_adapted: On June 18, 1648, a banquet was held at Amsterdam’s crossbowmen’s guild—a kind of social and military club for local citizens. The event celebrated the signing of the Treaty of Münster, which ended the war between the Dutch Republic and Spain. The leaders of the city’s volunteer militia shake hands as a gesture of peace, while a ceremonial drinking horn is passed among the guests. A poem written on the drum expresses the militia’s joy that their weapons can now be put away for good.

CuratorialText: The Fishing for Souls: an allegory of the jealousy of the various religions during the Twelve Years' Truce (1609-1621) between the Dutch Republic and Spain. In a river landscape, large groups of people are situated on both banks of the river; several rowboats are on the water. On the horizon, a rainbow. On the left side are the Protestants and their supporters: the Princes of Orange, Maurice and Frederick Henry, Frederick V of the Palatinate, James I, King of England, and the youthful Louis XIII, King of France, with his mother, Maria de' Medici. In the boat in the foreground sit the Protestant fishermen; their nets are marked with Fides, Spes, and Charitas. On the right side stand the Catholics and their supporters: Archdukes Albert and Isabella, General Ambrosio Spinola, and the Pope carried by cardinals. In the small Catholic boat, priests are fishing under the leadership of a bishop.
persona3_adapted: In *Fishing for Souls*, the artist uses a symbolic scene to comment on the religious tensions during the Twelve Years' Truce (1609–1621), a pause in the war between the Dutch Republic and Spain. The painting shows a river with large crowds gathered on both banks, several rowboats on the water, and a rainbow on the horizon. On the left side are the Protestants and their allies: the Princes of Orange (Maurice and Frederick Henry), Frederick V of the Palatinate, King James I of England, and the young King Louis XIII of France with his mother, Maria de' Medici. In the boat closest to us, Protestant fishermen are at work; their nets are labeled with the Latin words *Fides* (Faith), *Spes* (Hope), and *Charitas* (Charity)—three Christian virtues. On the right side stand the Catholics and their supporters: Archdukes Albert and Isabella, General Ambrosio Spinola, and the Pope, who is being carried by cardinals. In a small Catholic boat, priests are fishing under the direction of a bishop.

CuratorialText: Wedding portrait of Isaac Abrahamsz Massa and Beatrix van der Laen, married in Haarlem on April 25, 1622. The couple is seated full-length on a raised platform under a tree. On the left, a thistle; on the right, ivy. On the right in the background, elegant couples are walking in a garden with a fountain at a country house.
persona3_adapted: This full-length portrait shows Isaac Abrahamsz Massa and Beatrix van der Laen, who were married in Haarlem on April 25, 1622. The couple sits on a raised platform beneath a tree. To the left, a thistle is visible; to the right, ivy. In the background on the right, well-dressed couples stroll through a garden beside a fountain at a country house.

CuratorialText: Figure of painted porcelain. The figure depicts a parrot (macaw) sitting on a tree stump with leaves. The parrot has a blue-scaled neck and head and a white body. The tail and the tips of the wings are painted blue, yellow, and white. A branch with cherries lies beneath the right leg. The figure is unmarked.
persona3_adapted: This small sculpture is made of painted porcelain. It shows a parrot—specifically a macaw—perched on a tree stump with leaves. The bird’s neck and head are covered in blue, scale-like markings, while its body is white. The tail and wing tips are painted in blue, yellow, and white. Beneath the parrot’s right leg rests a branch with cherries. The piece has no maker’s mark or signature.

CuratorialText: Formal long-sleeved kimono for an unmarried young woman (furisode), decorated all over with more than two hundred flying cranes. Black silk crepe (chirimen) with yuzen decoration in white, red, and grey tones, with gold foil detailing. Red silk lining. Single bamboo crest (mon).
persona3_adapted: Formal long-sleeved kimono intended for an unmarried young woman, known as a furisode. Its entire surface is decorated with over two hundred flying cranes. The kimono is made of black silk crepe, a fabric with a slightly crinkled texture, and features a traditional Japanese dyeing technique called yuzen, which allows for detailed hand-painted or stenciled patterns in white, red, and grey tones, accented with gold foil. The lining is red silk. A single bamboo family crest, or mon, appears on the garment.

CuratorialText: Still life with flowers. A bouquet of flowers in a metal vase standing in a niche. The bouquet contains roses, irises, poppies, a wheat stalk, tulips, and other flowers. A snail, a caterpillar, and a beetle crawl among the flowers and over the niche. Pendant of SK-A-337.
