persona3_adapted: A still life arrangement of flowers. A bouquet of roses, irises, poppies, tulips, a stalk of wheat, and other blooms sits in a metal vase placed within a recessed alcove. Small insects—a snail, a caterpillar, and a beetle—crawl among the flowers and along the edges of the alcove. This painting is the companion piece to another work in the collection, numbered SK-A-337.

CuratorialText: Mary Magdalene sitting under a tree with the ointment jar on her lap. The saint is seated in a landscape with mountains and rugged rock formations.
persona3_adapted: Mary Magdalene sits beneath a tree, holding the ointment jar on her lap. She is positioned in a landscape of mountains and rough rock formations.

CuratorialText: Portrait of the twenty-year-old Marten Soolmans standing full-length on a checkered tile floor in an undefined space. He is dressed in an expensive black suit with a lace collar, bows, cuffs, and garters, wears a black hat and shoes with rosettes. In his left hand, he holds a glove.
persona3_adapted: This full-length portrait shows twenty-year-old Marten Soolmans standing on a checkered tile floor in a space that is not clearly defined. He wears an expensive black suit, complete with a lace collar, decorative bows, cuffs, and garters, along with a black hat and shoes adorned with rosettes. In his left hand, he holds a single glove.

CuratorialText: Portrait of 23-year-old Oopjen Coppit standing full-length on a tiled floor in an otherwise undefined space. She is dressed in an expensive black dress, lace collar and cuffs, and a black veil. She wears precious jewelry. In her right hand, she holds a fan of black ostrich feathers.
persona3_adapted: This full-length portrait shows 23-year-old Oopjen Coppit standing on a tiled floor, with no other background details to define the space around her. She wears a costly black dress, a lace collar and cuffs, and a black veil, along with valuable jewelry. In her right hand, she holds a fan made of black ostrich feathers.

CuratorialText: Still life with a golden ceremonial goblet, a silver holder on which stands a Chinese dish, an orange, a peeled lemon, grapes, peaches, a lobster, a plate of oysters and a roll.
persona3_adapted: A still life arrangement featuring a golden ceremonial goblet, a silver stand holding a Chinese dish, an orange, a peeled lemon, grapes, peaches, a lobster, a plate of oysters, and a roll.

CuratorialText: Portrait of Marie Fargues, the artist's wife, in Turkish costume. Full-length, seated on a divan. Her head resting on her right hand. On the divan lie a book and a basket containing a mirror, comb, and bonnet. On the floor are an Oriental rug and a small vase with carnations
persona3_adapted: This full-length portrait shows Marie Fargues, the artist’s wife, dressed in a Turkish costume. She is seated on a low sofa, resting her head on her right hand. Beside her on the sofa are a book and a basket holding a mirror, a comb, and a bonnet. On the floor lie an Oriental rug and a small vase filled with carnations.

CuratorialText: A cheerful violinist holding a birch flask. A laughing man leans out of a window covered by an Oriental rug. In his right hand, he holds up a birch flask filled with wine; in his left hand, a violin with a bow. On his head, a feathered hat.
persona3_adapted: A cheerful violinist holds a birch flask. A laughing man leans out of a window draped with an Oriental rug. In his right hand, he raises a birch flask filled with wine; in his left, he holds a violin and bow. On his head, he wears a hat with a feather.

CuratorialText: View of Olinda in Dutch Brazil. On the left, on a hill, a group of people by the ruins of the church. In the foreground, tropical plants, fruits, and animals (armadillo, monkey, sloth, anteater, lizard).
persona3_adapted: A view of Olinda, in the part of Brazil then under Dutch control. On the left, a group of people stands on a hill near the ruins of a church. In the foreground, the artist has depicted tropical plants, fruits, and animals—including an armadillo, monkey, sloth, anteater, and lizard.

CuratorialText: Winter landscape. View of a wide, snow-covered lane leading past a forest. In the foreground, three boys with sleds. Further back, a wagon drawn by two horses stands on the road in front of a farm.
persona3_adapted: A winter scene unfolds before us: a broad, snow-covered lane stretches into the distance, passing alongside a forest. In the foreground, three boys stand with sleds, while farther down the road, a horse-drawn wagon waits in front of a farmhouse.

CuratorialText: Italian landscape with draftsman. A road through the landscape with a bridge over a mountain stream. On the road, a man with a mule. In the foreground, some seated figures.
persona3_adapted: An Italian landscape, featuring a draftsman—an artist sketching the scene. A road winds through the countryside, crossing a bridge over a mountain stream. Along the road, a man walks with a mule. In the foreground, a few figures are seated.

CuratorialText: Still life with turkey pie. Table with a white damask tablecloth on which stand pewter plates with a pie, oysters, lemon, salt and pepper, and olives (?). Also a pewter pitcher, roemer, knife, nuts, bread rolls, a nautilus cup, and a Wan Li bowl with fruit. In the background stands a large pie on which a turkey has been placed.
persona3_adapted: A still life painting titled *Still Life with Turkey Pie*. It shows a table covered with a white damask tablecloth. On the table are pewter plates holding a pie, oysters, lemon, salt and pepper, and what appear to be olives. Also present are a pewter pitcher, a type of wine glass called a roemer, a knife, nuts, bread rolls, a cup made from a nautilus shell, and a bowl from the Chinese Wan Li period, filled with fruit. In the background, a large pie sits with a turkey placed on top.

CuratorialText: The stone bridge. Landscape with a stone bridge over a river or canal. On the water are two small boats with figures. Various people are walking on the road, a wagon on the left. In the middle, a hay barn between trees, a church tower in the distance on the right.
persona3_adapted: A stone bridge spans a river or canal in this landscape scene. Two small boats carrying figures float on the water. Along the road, several people are walking, and a wagon appears on the left. In the middle ground, a hay barn sits nestled among trees, while a church tower rises in the distance on the right.

CuratorialText: A stone bust of Flora in an ornamental cartouche surrounded by a festoon of various types of flowers, including tulips, roses, delphinium, foxglove, hollyhocks, carnations, and ivy.
persona3_adapted: A carved stone portrait of Flora, the Roman goddess of flowers, is set within an ornate, decorative frame. Surrounding the frame is a garland-like arrangement of various flowers, including tulips, roses, delphinium, foxglove, hollyhocks, carnations, and ivy.

CuratorialText: The overturned bouquet. A cat, playing with a mousetrap, knocks over a vase containing a bouquet of flowers. In the bouquet: tulips, irises, poppies, and carnations. Flying among the flowers are butterflies, a spider, a dragonfly, and snails.
persona3_adapted: An overturned bouquet. A cat, playing with a mousetrap, has knocked over a vase of flowers. The bouquet includes tulips, irises, poppies, and carnations. Among the flowers, butterflies, a spider, a dragonfly, and snails are scattered or in motion.

CuratorialText: The Fall of Man. Adam and Eve standing before the Tree of Knowledge. A snake offers Eve an apple. Surrounding the figures are various animals: monkey, cat, dog, slugs, hedgehog, frog, fox, and owl. In the background on the left, Adam and Eve are warned by God not to eat from the tree.
persona3_adapted: In *The Fall of Man*, we see Adam and Eve standing before the Tree of Knowledge. A serpent offers Eve an apple. Around them, various animals are gathered—including a monkey, cat, dog, slugs, hedgehog, frog, fox, and owl. In the background on the left, God warns Adam and Eve not to eat fruit from the tree.

CuratorialText: View of an orchard with plum blossoms, seen through the branches of a plum tree in the foreground; picnicking visitors in the background; part of the entrance gate on the left in the foreground; against a bright red sky.
persona3_adapted: A view of an orchard filled with blooming plum trees, framed by the branches of a plum tree in the foreground. In the background, visitors are picnicking, while part of the entrance gate is visible on the left side of the foreground. The scene is set against a vivid red sky.

CuratorialText: The painting is executed in a pointillist technique. The sitter is positioned on the right, facing the viewer, while studying a book in the light of a kerosene lamp. She is dressed in a pink gown, known as reform clothing, and has her hair simply styled in an updo. The kerosene lamp is partially visible on the table on the left, placed next to a small vase of roses on a small table covered with a floral tablecloth. Flowers in a large vase in the background are visually incorporated into the pattern of the decorative wallpaper.
persona3_adapted: The painting uses a technique called pointillism, where the artist builds the image from many small dots of color rather than brushstrokes. The woman in the painting sits on the right, facing us, as she reads a book by the light of a kerosene lamp. She wears a pink gown—a style known as reform clothing, which was designed to be more comfortable and practical than the restrictive fashions of the time—and her hair is simply pinned up. The kerosene lamp is partly visible on the table to the left, placed beside a small vase of roses on a table covered with a floral cloth. In the background, flowers in a large vase blend visually into the pattern of the decorative wallpaper.

CuratorialText: Kitten game. A small cat plays with dominoes on a table. There is also an ashtray with a cigar on the table.
persona3_adapted: A small cat plays with dominoes on a table, where an ashtray holding a cigar also rests.

CuratorialText: Interior in which a family celebrates the Feast of Saint Nicholas. In the center, a girl holding a John the Baptist doll and a bucket of candy in her arms. On the left, a crying boy who has received a rod in his shoe. Next to the mother with outstretched arms stands a laughing boy with a club. On the floor is a basket of cakes and rolls; by the fireplace, several young people are singing a Sinterklaas song. In the background, an older man and woman.
persona3_adapted: In this interior scene, a family is celebrating the Feast of Saint Nicholas, a Dutch holiday similar to Christmas. At the center, a girl holds a doll of John the Baptist—a biblical figure—along with a bucket of candy. On the left, a boy cries because he has received a rod in his shoe, a traditional punishment for misbehavior. Next to the mother, who has her arms outstretched, stands a laughing boy holding a club. On the floor, there is a basket of cakes and rolls; near the fireplace, several young people sing a Sinterklaas song. In the background, an older man and woman can be seen.

CuratorialText: Jeremiah mourning over the destruction of Jerusalem. The old prophet sits on the rocky slope of a mountain, his head resting on his left hand. On a stone beside him lie a golden bowl, a pitcher, and books, sanctuaries from the destroyed temple. Below to the left, the burning buildings of the city and soldiers can be seen.
persona3_adapted: Jeremiah mourns the destruction of Jerusalem. The elderly prophet sits on a rocky mountainside, his head resting on his left hand. Beside him on a stone lie a golden bowl, a pitcher, and books—objects saved from the ruined temple. Below to the left, the city’s buildings are burning, and soldiers can be seen.

CuratorialText: Still life with fruit, oysters, and a porcelain bowl. On a stone table, a silver plate with oysters, fruit, a porcelain bowl, an inverted roemer, a glowing wick, and a bunch of grapes on a wooden box. Also snails and insects.
persona3_adapted: A still life arrangement featuring fruit, oysters, and a porcelain bowl. Resting on a stone table, a silver plate holds oysters, fruit, a porcelain bowl, an overturned wine glass (known as a roemer), a glowing wick, and a cluster of grapes placed on a wooden box. The composition also includes snails and insects, adding a sense of life and decay to the scene.

CuratorialText: Painting titled 'View at the Geestbrug'. River landscape with the Trekvliet near Rijswijk, with De Binckhorst Castle in the distance on the left and the Laakmolen on the right. A woman with children stands on the road to the farm, and boats are sailing on the water.
persona3_adapted: A painting titled 'View at the Geestbrug' shows a river landscape along the Trekvliet near Rijswijk. In the distance, De Binckhorst Castle appears on the left, while the Laakmolen windmill is visible on the right. On the road leading to a farm, a woman stands with children, and boats glide across the water.

CuratorialText: The well-stocked kitchen, with Jesus with Martha and Mary in the background. In the kitchen, two maidservants are busy preparing food. One is plucking a chicken, the other is skewering a chicken. To the left, others are sitting by the hearth. In the foreground, all kinds of game and poultry, vegetables, and fruits are laid out. Among these are also various glasses, jugs, pitchers, and plates.
persona3_adapted: The kitchen is well-stocked with food and supplies, while in the background, a scene from the Bible—Jesus with Martha and Mary—can be seen. In the kitchen, two maidservants are busy preparing a meal: one is plucking a chicken, and the other is skewering one. To the left, other figures are seated near the hearth. In the foreground, an array of game, poultry, vegetables, and fruits is displayed, along with various glasses, jugs, pitchers, and plates.

CuratorialText: Still life with flowers and fruit. On a plinth stands a stone vase with flowers, fruit, and vines. On the plinth is a glass, and lying are a pineapple, peaches, grapes, and a rose. On the wall hangs a painting of a woman with a flower basket handing a child a flower wreath.
persona3_adapted: A still life arrangement of flowers and fruit. On a stone pedestal sits a carved vase overflowing with blossoms, fruit, and twisting vines. Resting on the pedestal are a glass, a pineapple, peaches, grapes, and a single rose. Hanging on the wall behind them is a painting of a woman holding a flower basket, as she reaches down to offer a flower wreath to a child.

CuratorialText: Six warships lie on a vast, calm sea. The sails are being hoisted, the anchors weighed. The stern of the ship on the right in the foreground is decorated with the coat of arms of Amsterdam, supported by two standing lions. This likely depicts part of a war fleet preparing for departure. A sloop with dignified figures, probably from the Admiralty, sails past the ships to the sound of trumpets and salutes, while a little further away the last provisions and crew are still being taken on board. The enormous accuracy in the rendering of details—rigging, sails, and cannons—is striking.
persona3_adapted: Six warships rest on a wide, calm sea. The crew is raising the sails and lifting the anchors. On the front right, the back of one ship displays Amsterdam's coat of arms, held up by two standing lions. This scene likely shows part of a war fleet getting ready to leave. A small boat carrying important-looking people—probably officials from the Admiralty—moves past the ships, accompanied by trumpet sounds and gun salutes. A bit farther away, the last supplies and crew members are still being loaded on board. What stands out is the incredible precision in how every detail is shown—the ropes, sails, and cannons are all rendered with remarkable accuracy.

CuratorialText: Still life with a hammered bronze vase containing a bouquet of flowers: tulips, roses, anemones, and marigolds. On the tabletop lie a dead frog, a cluster of berries, and a caterpillar.
persona3_adapted: A still life painting shows a hammered bronze vase holding a bouquet of flowers—tulips, roses, anemones, and marigolds. Resting on the tabletop are a dead frog, a cluster of berries, and a caterpillar.

CuratorialText: Portrait of Giuliano da Sangallo (c. 1445-1516). Architect, son of Francesco Giamberti da Sangallo. Bust facing right, with a landscape in the background. A compass and pen lie on the plinth at the bottom of the image. Part, with SK-C-1367, of a diptych.
persona3_adapted: Portrait of Giuliano da Sangallo (c. 1445-1516). He was an architect, the son of Francesco Giamberti da Sangallo. The portrait shows his head and upper chest, facing to the right, with a landscape visible behind him. A compass and pen rest on the base at the bottom of the image. This work, together with SK-C-1367, forms a two-panel painting (a diptych).

CuratorialText: An old woman, probably Rembrandt's mother, Neeltgen Willemsdr van Zuydtbroeck (died 1640), presumably depicted as the prophetess Anna. A seated old woman, reading a large book lying open on her lap.
persona3_adapted: An elderly woman—likely Rembrandt's mother, Neeltgen Willemsdr van Zuydtbroeck (who died in 1640)—is shown here, probably in the role of the prophetess Anna. She sits reading a large book that lies open on her lap.

CuratorialText: Allegorical musical group. On the left, a man in Oriental clothing and a turban plays a viola da gamba. Next to him stands a young man with a harp. A young woman sits beside a small table with an open songbook on her lap and sings. Behind her, an old woman with a cloth over her head looks on. In the foreground lie musical instruments (lute, violin) and stacks of books. On the wall is a painting of Lot's Flight from Sodom.
persona3_adapted: A symbolic musical scene. On the left, a man wearing Eastern-style clothing and a turban plays a viola da gamba—an early string instrument held between the legs, similar to a cello. Next to him stands a young man with a harp. A young woman sits beside a small table, an open songbook on her lap, and sings. Behind her, an older woman with a cloth covering her head watches. In the foreground lie musical instruments (a lute and a violin) alongside stacks of books. On the wall hangs a painting depicting Lot's Flight from Sodom, a biblical story.

CuratorialText: Venus and Adonis. The half-naked Venus and Cupid try to prevent Adonis from going hunting. Two white doves in the lower right. Adonis's quiver hangs from the tree. Adonis has two hunting dogs with him.
persona3_adapted: Venus and Adonis. Venus, partially undressed, and Cupid attempt to stop Adonis from leaving for the hunt. In the lower right corner, two white doves appear. Adonis’s quiver—the container for his arrows—hangs from a tree. He is accompanied by two hunting dogs.

CuratorialText: The Cannon Shot. A warship in calm with slack sails fires a cannon shot. Two longboats on either side, in the distance another warship with furled sails. Pendant to SK-A-1848.
persona3_adapted: A warship sits motionless on calm waters, its sails hanging loose and slack. A single cannon is fired, sending a plume of smoke across the scene. Two longboats flank the vessel, while in the distance, another warship appears with its sails tightly furled. This painting is a companion piece to SK-A-1848.

CuratorialText: The word Sufi often conjures up images of Turkish whirling dervishes, turning gracefully round themselves in a mystical ceremony. But outside the world of Islamic mysticism, not much is known of the beliefs and practices in which such ceremonies are rooted, or about the communities that embrace them.
persona3_adapted: The term *Sufi* frequently brings to mind the image of Turkish whirling dervishes, spinning gracefully in a mystical ceremony. Yet, beyond the realm of Islamic mysticism, little is widely understood about the beliefs and practices that give rise to such rituals, or about the communities that follow them.

CuratorialText: This display will explore Sufi life and art through a selection of objects that represent Sufi communities in the Middle East, North and Sub-Saharan Africa, and northern India. This mystical movement, which first appeared in the Islamic world during the eighth century AD, has been defined as a pathway to a personal experience with God. Among the British Museum objects on display will be Islamic artefacts imbued with Sufi connotations. Some, such as alms bowls, are linked overtly to dervishes, while others have a more subtle connection, such as calligraphic works, miniature paintings and wall tiles. Sufis come from all walks of life, crossing the boundaries of class, ethnicity and regionality. The display will explore such themes as mystical love, poverty and asceticism, as well as Sufi influence upon the wider societies they belong to.
persona3_adapted: This display explores Sufi life and art through a selection of objects representing Sufi communities in the Middle East, North and Sub-Saharan Africa, and northern India. Sufism is a mystical movement that first appeared in the Islamic world during the eighth century AD. It can be understood as a path toward having a personal, direct experience with God. Among the British Museum objects on display are Islamic artifacts that carry Sufi meanings. Some, like alms bowls, are clearly linked to dervishes—members of Sufi groups known for their vows of poverty and spiritual practices. Others have a more subtle connection, such as works of calligraphy, miniature paintings, and wall tiles. Sufis come from all walks of life, crossing boundaries of class, ethnicity, and region. The display will explore themes such as mystical love, poverty, and self-denial for spiritual reasons, as well as the influence Sufis have had on the wider societies they belong to.

CuratorialText: Hawaiʻi: a kingdom crossing oceans is a dazzling celebration of the artistry and history of Hawaiʻi. The exhibition brings together remarkable objects – from feathered cloaks worn by chiefs and finely carved deities, to powerful shark-toothed weapons and bold contemporary works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists. In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu travelled across vast oceans on a journey that would mark a turning point in the history of their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. The exhibition commemorates over 200 years since this ill-fated royal visit and sheds light on Hawaiʻi's history and culture through an exploration of the complex and enduring relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations. Building on years of collaboration with Native Hawaiian artists, practitioners and scholars, the show centres on indigenous knowledge, shining new light on exceptional objects and extraordinary stories. Shaped together with Hawaiian knowledge-bearers, the exhibition showcases seldom-seen international loans alongside the remarkable collections at the British Museum – one of the largest in the world outside of Hawaiʻi.
persona3_adapted: Hawaiʻi: a kingdom crossing oceans is a stunning tribute to the artistry and history of Hawaiʻi. The exhibition brings together remarkable objects—from feathered cloaks worn by chiefs and finely carved figures of gods, to powerful weapons made with shark teeth and bold contemporary works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists. In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu traveled across vast oceans on a journey that would mark a turning point in the history of their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. The exhibition commemorates over 200 years since this tragic royal visit and sheds light on Hawaiʻi's history and culture by exploring the complex and lasting relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations. Built on years of collaboration with Native Hawaiian artists, practitioners, and scholars, the show centers on indigenous knowledge, offering fresh perspectives on exceptional objects and extraordinary stories. Shaped together with Hawaiian knowledge-bearers, the exhibition features rarely seen international loans alongside the British Museum’s remarkable collections—one of the largest in the world outside of Hawaiʻi.

CuratorialText: Explore prints by Scottish poet, writer, artist and gardener Ian Hamilton Finlay (1925–2006) on the theme of revolution and its relationship to contemporary society. Finlay became known from the late 1950s for his poetry but in the 1960s his output expanded to include the production of prints and three-dimensional works of art in stone, metal, neon and glass, which he produced in collaboration with various craftspeople. He also became a dedicated gardener, developing a conceptual garden at his remote home on the edge of the Pentland Hills, southwest of Edinburgh, which incorporated many of his works of art. He named his garden Little Sparta after the ancient Greek city, a militaristic rival to Athens, reflecting his deep interest in classical history.
persona3_adapted: Explore prints by Scottish poet, writer, artist and gardener Ian Hamilton Finlay (1925–2006) that focus on the idea of revolution and its connection to modern society. Finlay first gained attention in the late 1950s for his poetry, but by the 1960s his creative work expanded to include prints and three-dimensional pieces made from materials such as stone, metal, neon and glass. He created these works in partnership with various skilled artisans. Finlay was also a devoted gardener, developing a garden with artistic and intellectual intentions at his remote home on the edge of the Pentland Hills, southwest of Edinburgh. This garden incorporated many of his artworks. He named it Little Sparta after the ancient Greek city, a militaristic rival to Athens, reflecting his deep interest in classical history.

CuratorialText: In the 1980s, Finlay's interest in the French Revolution began to dominate his work. He was fascinated by revolutions and ruptures, and how high ideals could easily lead to violence and corruption. The 12 prints in this display, made between 1984 and 1992, form part of Finlay's engagement with the French Revolution as a subject. They are from a group of 26 prints donated in 2025 by Prof Stephen Bann, an art historian who has written extensively on Finlay and who corresponded with the artist for many years.
persona3_adapted: In the 1980s, Finlay’s work became increasingly focused on the French Revolution. He was drawn to the idea of revolutions and sudden breaks with the past, and to how noble ideals could easily turn into violence and corruption. The 12 prints in this display, created between 1984 and 1992, are part of Finlay’s exploration of the French Revolution as a theme. They come from a set of 26 prints donated in 2025 by Professor Stephen Bann, an art historian who has written extensively about Finlay and corresponded with the artist over many years.

CuratorialText: In a career that lasted from the 1760s to the 1830s, Pierre Alexandre Wille (1748–1837) experienced some of the most turbulent events in French history. A pupil of Jean Baptiste Greuze (1725–1805), Wille established himself by imitating the genre scenes that won Greuze renown at the Salons of the 1760s, showing an inclination for delicacy that suited the tastes of the ancien régime.
persona3_adapted: In a career that spanned from the 1760s to the 1830s, Pierre Alexandre Wille (1748–1837) lived through some of the most dramatic upheavals in French history. As a student of Jean Baptiste Greuze (1725–1805), Wille first made his name by imitating the everyday-life scenes that had brought Greuze fame at the official art exhibitions of the 1760s. His work showed a preference for a gentle, refined style that appealed to the tastes of the pre-Revolutionary French monarchy and aristocracy.

CuratorialText: Yet Wille the man embraced the Revolution, even joining the republican army, and he busied himself chronicling the personalities of the day. Ultimately, however, the Revolution proved more of a challenge than an opportunity. The younger Wille had embraced a variety of styles but, in later life, he found it difficult to adapt to the more austere taste of Napoleonic France. By the time he died, he had long been reduced to poverty. During his lifetime and since, Wille was renowned more for his drawings than his paintings. The British Museum has been acquiring his work since the 19th century, and its collection has recently doubled in size following a bequest from the dealer and philanthropist Colin Clark (1935–2020). This gift included some of the finest finished drawings by Wille; one of the most powerful is the portrait which claims to show the 'Angel of Assassination' Charlotte Corday, made in her prison cell days or even hours before her execution. This celebration of Clark's bequest illustrates Wille's world through his drawings and those of his peers, revealing him to have been a talented draughtsman whose works offer a fascinating insight into French society before, during and after the Revolution.
persona3_adapted: Yet Wille the man embraced the Revolution, even joining the republican army, and he occupied himself with recording the notable figures of the era. Ultimately, however, the Revolution proved more of a challenge than an opportunity. The younger Wille had experimented with many different artistic approaches, but in later life, he found it difficult to adapt to the more restrained tastes of Napoleonic France. By the time he died, he had long been reduced to poverty. During his lifetime and since, Wille was more famous for his drawings than his paintings. The British Museum has been acquiring his work since the 1800s, and its collection has recently doubled in size after a gift from the dealer and philanthropist Colin Clark (1935–2020). This gift included some of the finest completed drawings by Wille; one of the most striking is the portrait that claims to show the 'Angel of Assassination' Charlotte Corday, made in her prison cell days or even hours before her execution. This celebration of Clark's gift illustrates Wille's world through his drawings and those of his contemporaries, revealing him to have been a highly skilled artist whose works offer a fascinating window into French society before, during, and after the Revolution.

CuratorialText: Made in England between 1340 and 1405, the Asante Ewer later travelled to West Africa. In the 1880s it was photographed in a courtyard of the royal palace of the Asantehene, king of the Asante people, in Kumasi (in present-day Ghana). During the Anglo-Asante War of 1896 it was looted by British forces and subsequently purchased by the British Museum. A second ewer, also taken in 1896 and shown in the same photograph, was presented to the Prince of Wales's Own (West Yorkshire) Regiment. This display reunites these two jugs. A major new research project into the Asante Ewer – one of the finest examples of late medieval English bronze casting – is helping to further trace the story of its journey to Ghana, and its status and significance in West Africa. Its location in the centre of the palace courtyard, in what appears to be a shrine, embedded in the roots of sacred trees, suggests it was an object of significant cultural or ritual value. Remarkable for surviving the long-distance journey from Europe to West Africa and back, the Asante Ewer has had vastly different lives, from a luxury English jug to sacred West African vessel, to colonial loot taken by British troops.
persona3_adapted: Made in England between 1340 and 1405, the Asante Ewer later traveled to West Africa. In the 1880s, it was photographed in a courtyard of the royal palace of the Asantehene, the king of the Asante people, in Kumasi (in present-day Ghana). During the Anglo-Asante War of 1896, it was taken by British forces and later purchased by the British Museum. A second ewer, also taken in 1896 and visible in the same photograph, was given to the Prince of Wales's Own (West Yorkshire) Regiment. This display brings these two jugs back together. A major new research project into the Asante Ewer—one of the finest surviving examples of late medieval English bronze casting—is helping to further trace its journey to Ghana, as well as its status and meaning in West Africa. Its placement in the center of the palace courtyard, within what appears to be a shrine, embedded in the roots of sacred trees, suggests it was an object of significant cultural or ritual importance. Remarkable for surviving the long journey from Europe to West Africa and back, the Asante Ewer has led vastly different lives: from a luxury English jug, to a sacred West African vessel, to colonial loot taken by British troops.

CuratorialText: This display forms part of a three-year research project, The age of copper, ivory and gold, supported by The British Academy and Wolfson Foundation, exploring the connections between Western Europe and West Africa during the Middle Ages and Early Modern period. It is the result of close collaboration with colleagues at the Manhyia Palace Museum in Kumasi, York Army Museum and many others across Ghana, the US and the UK.
persona3_adapted: This display is part of a three-year research project called *The Age of Copper, Ivory and Gold*, funded by The British Academy and Wolfson Foundation. The project investigates the links between Western Europe and West Africa during the Middle Ages and the Early Modern period (roughly from the 5th to the 18th centuries). It was developed through close partnership with colleagues at the Manhyia Palace Museum in Kumasi, the York Army Museum, and many other institutions across Ghana, the United States, and the United Kingdom.

CuratorialText: In the 1400s and 1500s, the Low Countries were a powerhouse of artistic innovation. This was a transformative period for Netherlandish drawings, as they evolved from preparatory studies to works of art in their own right.
persona3_adapted: In the 1400s and 1500s, the region now known as Belgium, the Netherlands, and Luxembourg was a major center of artistic creativity. During this time, drawings from this area underwent a significant change: they were no longer just rough sketches made in preparation for paintings or other finished works, but instead came to be valued as complete, independent pieces of art.

CuratorialText: Featuring around 120 works by artists including Rogier van der Weyden, Lucas van Leyden, Pieter Bruegel the Elder and Hendrick Goltzius, this exhibition charts the development of drawing in the Low Countries (present-day Belgium, Luxembourg and the Netherlands) before 1600. The exceptional breadth and quality of the British Museum's holdings provides a unique opportunity to present a comprehensive account of drawing from this region.  Early Netherlandish drawings are extremely rare, as they were made as functional objects in the workshop and were not typically retained. This was unlike Italy or the German-speaking lands, where the taste for collecting drawings in the 1500s ensured the survival of a higher number of works from this period. Drawings were central to the design and production of works of art in different media, including tapestries, paintings, stained glass and prints – and surviving examples shed light on the creativity and collaboration of Netherlandish artists.
persona3_adapted: Featuring around 120 works by artists including Rogier van der Weyden, Lucas van Leyden, Pieter Bruegel the Elder and Hendrick Goltzius, this exhibition traces the development of drawing in the Low Countries (present-day Belgium, Luxembourg and the Netherlands) before 1600. The British Museum’s collection is unusually broad and of high quality, offering a rare chance to present a thorough overview of drawing from this region. Early Netherlandish drawings are extremely scarce, because they were created as practical tools within the artist’s workshop and were not usually kept. This contrasts with Italy or the German-speaking regions, where a growing interest in collecting drawings during the 1500s helped preserve a larger number of works from that time. Drawings played a central role in designing and producing artworks in various forms—such as tapestries, paintings, stained glass, and prints—and the examples that survive give us insight into the creativity and collaborative practices of Netherlandish artists.

CuratorialText: Based on a research project and supported by a grant from the International Music and Art Foundation, the exhibition highlights new findings that have come from combining curatorial and conservation expertise along with scientific analysis. New research into drawings previously only connected to Rogier van der Weyden and his circle, for instance, reveals they originate directly from Rogier's workshop and must have been drawn by his pupils as part of their training.
persona3_adapted: Based on a research project and supported by a grant from the International Music and Art Foundation, the exhibition highlights new findings that have come from combining curatorial and conservation expertise along with scientific analysis. New research into drawings previously only connected to Rogier van der Weyden and his circle, for instance, reveals they originate directly from Rogier's workshop and must have been drawn by his pupils as part of their training.

CuratorialText: Be among the first to view designs for a permanent memorial to Queen Elizabeth II in central London.
persona3_adapted: Be among the first to see the proposed designs for a permanent memorial to Queen Elizabeth II, to be located in central London.

CuratorialText: Featuring a 10ft bronze statue of Elizabeth as a young Queen, on a 14ft plinth, the memorial in St James' Park will commemorate the late monarch and her lifelong commitment to public service. The memorial is expected to be completed in 2028. Designed by acclaimed sculptor Martin Jennings, to be sculpted in bronze, the statue will show Her Majesty in an early stage of her reign, dressed in the robes of the Order of the Garter, using a famous painting by Pietro Annigoni as inspiration.
persona3_adapted: The memorial in St James’s Park will feature a 10-foot bronze statue of Elizabeth as a young Queen, set atop a 14-foot stone base called a plinth. It is designed to honor the late monarch and her lifelong dedication to public service, with completion expected in 2028. The statue, created by the highly regarded sculptor Martin Jennings, will be cast in bronze and depict Her Majesty early in her reign, wearing the ceremonial robes of the Order of the Garter. The design draws inspiration from a well-known painting by Pietro Annigoni.

CuratorialText: Mr Jennings will also sculpt a statue of Prince Philip, the country's longest-serving consort, at a similar age wearing a uniform of Admiral-of-the-Fleet. His statue will be located close to Queen Elizabeth's, in recognition of the support he gave her during her 70-year reign. Other elements of the memorial have been designed by Foster + Partners in collaboration with Michel Desvigne Paysagiste. The British Museum display features maquettes of the statues and a scale model of the wider memorial park. The King and Queen, other Members of the Royal Family and the Prime Minister viewed the designs at the Museum on 21 April to mark the 100th anniversary of Queen Elizabeth II's birth. The permanent memorial will also feature: a new cast-glass bridge inspired by Queen Mary's Fringe Tiara, which was worn by Queen Elizabeth on her wedding day; a bust of Her Majesty in her later years by sculptor Karen Newman; and The Commonwealth Wind Sculpture, a new abstract work by Yinka Shonibare.
persona3_adapted: Mr Jennings will also create a statue of Prince Philip, the country's longest-serving royal spouse, shown at a similar age and dressed in the uniform of Admiral-of-the-Fleet. This statue will be placed near Queen Elizabeth's, recognizing the support he gave her throughout her 70-year reign. Other parts of the memorial have been designed by Foster + Partners in collaboration with Michel Desvigne Paysagiste. The British Museum exhibition includes small-scale models of the statues, known as maquettes, as well as a scaled-down version of the entire memorial park. On 21 April, to mark the 100th anniversary of Queen Elizabeth II's birth, the King and Queen, other members of the Royal Family, and the Prime Minister viewed the designs at the Museum. The permanent memorial will also include: a new bridge made of cast glass, inspired by Queen Mary's Fringe Tiara—the tiara Queen Elizabeth wore on her wedding day; a bust of Her Majesty in her later years, created by sculptor Karen Newman; and The Commonwealth Wind Sculpture, a new abstract artwork by Yinka Shonibare.

CuratorialText: The design also includes gardens dedicated to the Commonwealth and to the nations of the United Kingdom, creating spaces for relaxation and reflection. The memorial has been developed in consultation with the Queen Elizabeth Memorial Committee following a design competition held last year. As well as the memorial park, recommendations made by the Queen Elizabeth Memorial Committee include a new charitable trust for regenerating community assets across the UK, and an online Digital Memorial(Opens in new window) to allow people to submit their own memories of the late Queen.
persona3_adapted: The design also includes gardens dedicated to the Commonwealth and to the nations of the United Kingdom, creating spaces for relaxation and reflection. The memorial was developed in consultation with the Queen Elizabeth Memorial Committee, following a design competition held last year. In addition to the memorial park, the Committee’s recommendations include establishing a new charitable trust to help regenerate community assets across the UK, and creating an online Digital Memorial (opens in a new window) where people can share their own memories of the late Queen.

CuratorialText:
Discover how people lived and died in ancient Egypt and explore their hopes and aspirations for the afterlife. 
From human remains, painted coffins, amulets, shabtis and tomb models of daily life to spectacular wall paintings and papyri; investigate what tombs, human remains and burial goods tell us about the lives and afterlives of ancient Egyptian people. The tour will explore the preparation of the deceased for burial, including mummification, the furnishing of the tomb for an eternal restful existence, and the use of magic to guide and protect people on their perilous journey to the afterlife. It will also show how funerary remains can tell us about the lives of the ancient Egyptians. This tour visits displays that include human remains. The British Museum is committed to curating human remains with care, respect and dignity. Find out more about our principles governing the holding, display and study of human remains.
persona3_adapted: Discover how people lived and died in ancient Egypt, and learn about their hopes and dreams for what comes after death. Through human remains, painted coffins, protective charms (amulets), small servant figurines (shabtis), and miniature models of daily life—alongside stunning wall paintings and papyrus scrolls—you will examine what tombs, bodies, and burial objects reveal about the lives and afterlives of ancient Egyptians. The tour explores how the dead were prepared for burial, including the process of mummification (preserving the body), how tombs were furnished to ensure an eternal, peaceful existence, and how magical practices were used to guide and protect people on their dangerous journey to the afterlife. It also shows how burial remains can tell us about the everyday lives of the ancient Egyptians. Please note that this tour includes displays of human remains. The British Museum is committed to treating these remains with care, respect, and dignity. Learn more about our principles for holding, displaying, and studying human remains.

CuratorialText:
Discover the fascinating history of ancient Egypt and explore one of the most iconic spaces in the Museum before it opens to the public.
In the Egyptian sculpture gallery (Room 4), you can see impressive statues of kings and gods, monumental tomb architecture and ancient tomb reliefs spanning 3,000 year. These include the imposing colossal bust of Ramesses II and the Gayer-Anderson Cat. Learn about the importance of large-scale sculpture in ancient Egyptian temples, tombs and much more in this special guided tour.
persona3_adapted:
Discover the fascinating history of ancient Egypt and explore one of the most iconic spaces in the Museum before it opens to the public.

In the Egyptian sculpture gallery (Room 4), you can see impressive statues of kings and gods, monumental tomb architecture, and ancient stone carvings from tombs that span 3,000 years. These include the imposing colossal bust of Ramesses II and the Gayer-Anderson Cat. Learn about the importance of large-scale sculpture in ancient Egyptian temples, tombs, and much more in this special guided tour.

CuratorialText: Founded in 1753, the British Museum opened its doors to visitors in 1759. The Museum tells the story of human cultural achievement through a collection of collections. This room celebrates some of the collectors who, in different ways, have shaped the Museum over four centuries, along with individuals and organisations who continue to shape its future. The adjoining galleries also explore aspects of collecting. Room 1: Enlightenment, tells the story of how, in the early Museum, objects and knowledge were gathered and classified. Room 2a: The Waddesdon Bequest displays the collection of Renaissance and Baroque masterpieces left to the British Museum by Baron Ferdinand Rothschild MP at his death in 1898.
persona3_adapted: Founded in 1753, the British Museum opened its doors to visitors in 1759. The Museum tells the story of human cultural achievement through a collection of collections. This room celebrates some of the collectors who, in different ways, have shaped the Museum over four centuries, along with individuals and organisations who continue to shape its future. The adjoining galleries also explore aspects of collecting. Room 1: Enlightenment, tells the story of how, in the early Museum, objects and knowledge were gathered and classified. Room 2a: The Waddesdon Bequest displays the collection of Renaissance and Baroque masterpieces left to the British Museum by Baron Ferdinand Rothschild MP at his death in 1898.

CuratorialText: The British Museum has successfully raised £3.5 million, which means the Tudor Heart Pendant will remain on public display for generations to come.  In December 2019 a metal detectorist in Warwickshire found a spectacular Tudor Heart jewel associated with Henry VIII and his first wife Katherine of Aragon in the ground after centuries of rest. The Tudor Heart Pendant is unlike any object in the British Museum collection or elsewhere in the UK. This highly accomplished jewel with enamelled motifs is attached to a 75-link gold chain by a suspension clasp in the form of a hand emerging from a cloud. Few objects survive to tell the story of Henry's life with the devoutly Catholic Katherine, a consequence of the annulment of their marriage in 1533. Their union was the longest of all of Henry's, lasting 24 years it decisively shaped the first three decades of his reign.
persona3_adapted: The British Museum has raised £3.5 million, ensuring that the Tudor Heart Pendant will remain on public display for future generations. In December 2019, a metal detectorist in Warwickshire discovered a remarkable Tudor heart-shaped jewel, linked to Henry VIII and his first wife, Katherine of Aragon, after it had lain buried for centuries. The Tudor Heart Pendant is unlike any other object in the British Museum’s collection—or anywhere else in the UK. This finely crafted piece, decorated with enamel designs, is attached to a 75-link gold chain by a clasp shaped like a hand emerging from a cloud. Very few objects survive that tell the story of Henry’s life with the deeply Catholic Katherine, largely because their marriage was annulled in 1533. Their union was the longest of all Henry’s marriages, lasting 24 years, and it profoundly shaped the first three decades of his reign.

CuratorialText: In the 18th century, the collections were divided into 'Natural and Artificial Rarities' – objects found in nature or made by people. Only a few rooms in the early Museum had manmade objects but case after case was filled with natural specimens from Sloane's collection and his library which included the 265 volumes of his herbarium. In 1735, the Swedish botanist Carl Linnaeus revolutionised the way plants, animals and other objects from the natural world were named and classified. His pupil, Daniel Solander, was a curator in the Museum and applied the system to Sloane's collections and to the natural history specimens Solander himself collected with Sir Joseph Banks on the first Cook voyage.  Many of these original specimens are included in the exhibition, as well as a number of fossils. By 1880 there were so many natural history specimens that they needed a museum of their own – the Natural History Museum in South Kensington.
persona3_adapted: In the 18th century, the collections were divided into 'Natural and Artificial Rarities' – objects found in nature or made by people. Only a few rooms in the early Museum contained manmade objects, but case after case was filled with natural specimens from Sloane's collection and his library, which included the 265 volumes of his herbarium (a dried plant collection). In 1735, the Swedish botanist Carl Linnaeus transformed the way plants, animals, and other natural objects were named and organized. His student, Daniel Solander, worked as a curator in the Museum and applied this system to Sloane's collections, as well as to the natural history specimens Solander himself gathered with Sir Joseph Banks on the first Cook voyage. Many of these original specimens are included in the exhibition, along with a number of fossils. By 1880, there were so many natural history specimens that they required their own museum – the Natural History Museum in South Kensington.

CuratorialText: Antiquaries collected books, manuscripts, drawings and artefacts from the past, and travelled the country studying ruins in order to learn more about Britain's Roman past and its earlier history. It was fairly easy to learn about medieval times because many of these objects had survived, including religious artefacts, armour, books and buildings. But antiquaries also began to survey and map Roman and earlier sites, such as Stonehenge, in a more scientific and systematic way and to learn more about the earliest Britons.  This knowledge, combined with the new study of rock strata, led archaeologists to question the accepted date of the world's creation – 4004 BC – calculated from readings of the Bible.
persona3_adapted: Antiquaries—early scholars of the past—collected books, handwritten manuscripts, drawings, and historical objects. They traveled across the country studying ruins to learn more about Britain's Roman era and its even earlier history. Learning about the medieval period was relatively straightforward, since many objects from that time had survived, including religious items, armor, books, and buildings. However, these scholars also began to survey and map Roman and prehistoric sites—such as Stonehenge—in a more scientific and organized way, helping them understand the earliest inhabitants of Britain. This growing body of knowledge, combined with the emerging study of rock layers (known as stratigraphy), led archaeologists to question the widely accepted date of the world's creation—4004 BC—which had been calculated from readings of the Bible.

CuratorialText: In 1824, Charles Townley's famous collection of Greek and Roman sculptures, acquired on his Grand Tours in Italy, came to the British Museum. They joined the collections of prints and drawings, gems, coins, bronzes, vases and other classical antiquities from fellow connoisseurs such as Sir William Hamilton and Richard Payne Knight. These men studied each other's collections to learn about the 'progress' of art, from 'primitive' beginnings in early civilisations to what they considered to be the height of artistic achievement – the sculpture and architecture of classical Greece. This became the standard against which to measure all art and led, in late 18th-century Britain, to a classical revival in architecture and the decorative arts.
persona3_adapted: In 1824, Charles Townley's renowned collection of Greek and Roman sculptures—acquired during his educational travels through Italy—arrived at the British Museum. These works joined existing collections of prints, drawings, gemstones, coins, bronze objects, vases, and other classical artifacts from fellow enthusiasts and collectors, such as Sir William Hamilton and Richard Payne Knight. These men studied one another's collections to trace what they saw as the 'progress' of art: a journey from what they considered the 'primitive' beginnings of early civilizations to the peak of artistic achievement, which they believed was found in the sculpture and architecture of classical Greece. This Greek ideal became the standard by which all other art was judged, and in late 18th-century Britain, it sparked a revival of classical styles in both architecture and the decorative arts.

CuratorialText: Everything gathered in the 18th century had to be classified and organised so that it could be more easily understood and used.  King George III's library was presented to the nation by George IV and was housed in the room that is now the Enlightenment Gallery (his library is now in the British Library). The coins and medals collection was among the first part of the library to arrive at the Museum and they were arranged by country and ruler to help bring past history alive. George III was also keenly interested in the navy, navigation and scientific discoveries and collected instruments and working models. Sir William Hamilton arranged his vases found in the area around Naples according to the styles of painting, attempting to establish a chronology for them. He later published them in beautifully illustrated folios. In the early part of the century, Sir Hans Sloane filled his cabinets with curious 'artificial' (manmade) objects from around the world, including those collected from enslaved peoples in Jamaica. These objects were catalogued and displayed in many different ways – by type, material, date or purpose.
persona3_adapted: Everything collected during the 1700s had to be sorted and organized so that it could be more easily studied and put to use. King George III’s library was given to the nation by his son, George IV, and was placed in the room that now houses the Enlightenment Gallery (the library itself is now kept at the British Library). The collection of coins and medals was among the first items from the library to arrive at the Museum; they were arranged by country and ruler to help bring the past to life. George III was also very interested in the navy, navigation, and scientific discoveries, and he gathered instruments and working models. Sir William Hamilton organized the ancient vases he found near Naples according to their painting styles, trying to establish a timeline for them. He later published these vases in beautifully illustrated books. In the early part of the century, Sir Hans Sloane filled his cabinets with curious manmade objects from around the world, including items collected from enslaved people in Jamaica. These objects were recorded and displayed in many different ways—by type, material, date, or purpose.

CuratorialText: The Enlightenment led to a fevered interest in deciphering the mysterious forms of Egyptian hieroglyphs and the cuneiform inscriptions found in lands described in the Bible as well as early forms of Sanskrit in India. Scholars 'cracked the codes' in the 19th century, opening up thousands of years of history. At the same time, antiquaries deciphered runes and other early forms of writing, revealing more of our own history. This was a period when significant numbers of antiquities, acquired from collectors, diplomats and through excavation, entered the British Museum.
persona3_adapted: The Enlightenment sparked an intense curiosity about ancient writing systems—such as Egyptian hieroglyphs, the wedge-shaped cuneiform inscriptions from biblical lands, and early forms of Sanskrit in India. In the 19th century, scholars successfully decoded these scripts, unlocking thousands of years of previously unknown history. Meanwhile, antiquarians (early historians and collectors) deciphered runes and other early forms of writing, revealing more about our own past. During this era, the British Museum acquired a large number of ancient artifacts, obtained from collectors, diplomats, and through archaeological excavations.

CuratorialText: Christian mistrust of superstition and idols, rituals and magic led to ignorance about other religions. Enlightenment scholars attempted to dispel this by studying the ancient and modern religions they encountered. They researched rituals and cults in different cultures, collected religious artefacts, grouped gods into families and examined their attributes and functions. They wanted to find similarities that might shed light on the origins of the world's religions. Much of this activity took place within the context of colonialism.
persona3_adapted: Christian suspicion of superstition, idols, rituals, and magic resulted in widespread ignorance about other religions. During the Enlightenment, scholars tried to overcome this by studying both ancient religions and those they encountered in the modern world. They researched ceremonies and worship practices across different cultures, gathered religious objects, organized gods into categories, and analyzed their characteristics and roles. Their goal was to identify commonalities that might reveal the origins of the world's religions. Much of this work happened within the framework of colonialism.

CuratorialText: In this era of colonialism and global trade, travellers, traders, scientists, explorers and diplomats created collections of ethnographic objects, including ceremonial and everyday items, thought representative of customs and cultures of people around the globe. They collected artefacts and published illustrated accounts about the lives and cultures of people around the world, from a European perspective. Alongside Sir Hans Sloane's collection, the British Museum also houses objects from the voyages of Captain James Cook and Sir Joseph Banks to Australia, New Zealand, Tahiti and other Pacific islands. A significant number of objects were acquired through employees of the East India and Royal Africa Companies, whose wealth was made possible through the use of enslaved labour and the exploitation of people and resources.
persona3_adapted: During the age of colonialism and expanding global trade, travelers, traders, scientists, explorers, and diplomats assembled collections of objects from different cultures—both ceremonial items and everyday tools—that they believed represented the customs and ways of life of people around the world. They gathered these objects and published illustrated books describing the lives and traditions of various peoples, always from a European point of view. Alongside the collection of Sir Hans Sloane, the British Museum also holds objects brought back from the voyages of Captain James Cook and Sir Joseph Banks to Australia, New Zealand, Tahiti, and other Pacific islands. A large number of these objects came from employees of the East India Company and the Royal Africa Company, whose wealth was built on the use of enslaved labor and the exploitation of people and natural resources.

CuratorialText: During his leadership, from 336–323 BC, Alexander the Great conquered the Greek city-states, the Persian Empire and founded more than 70 cities creating an empire that stretched across three continents and covered about two million square miles.  The Hellenistic period dates from the death of Alexander the Great in 323 BC to the defeat of Cleopatra VII and Mark Antony at Actium in 31 BC. After his death, Alexander's empire was divided up into kingdoms, ruled by his generals. The major dynasties sponsored major architectural programmes and were great patrons of the arts and culture. Objects on display in Room 22 include a colossal carved column drum from the Temple of Artemis at Ephesos (one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World), the Demeter from Knidos in south-west Turkey and Greek bronzes. Displays of smaller objects show the skill of ancient goldsmiths, metalworkers, potters and coroplasts (makers of clay or terracotta figurines) and gem cutters.
persona3_adapted: During his leadership, from 336 to 323 BC, Alexander the Great conquered the Greek city-states and the Persian Empire, and founded more than 70 cities. In doing so, he created an empire that stretched across three continents and covered about two million square miles. The Hellenistic period—the era of Greek-influenced culture that followed—is dated from Alexander’s death in 323 BC to the defeat of Cleopatra VII and Mark Antony at Actium in 31 BC. After Alexander died, his empire was divided into kingdoms ruled by his generals. The major royal dynasties of this time funded large-scale building projects and were strong supporters of the arts and culture. Objects on display in Room 22 include a huge carved column drum from the Temple of Artemis at Ephesos (one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World), the statue of Demeter from Knidos in southwestern Turkey, and Greek bronzes. Smaller displays reveal the skill of ancient goldsmiths, metalworkers, potters, makers of clay or terracotta figurines, and gem cutters.

CuratorialText: The exhibition is a dazzling celebration of the artistry and history of Hawaiʻi. It brings together remarkable objects – from feathered cloaks worn by chiefs and finely carved deities, to powerful shark-toothed weapons and bold contemporary works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists. In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu travelled across vast oceans on a journey that would mark a turning point in the history of their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. The exhibition commemorates over 200 years since this ill-fated royal visit and sheds light on Hawaiʻi's history and culture through an exploration of the complex and enduring relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations.
persona3_adapted: This exhibition is a vibrant tribute to the artistry and history of Hawaiʻi. It brings together remarkable objects—from feathered cloaks worn by chiefs and finely carved figures of gods, to powerful weapons edged with shark teeth and bold contemporary works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists. In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu traveled across vast oceans on a journey that would mark a turning point in the history of their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. The exhibition marks over 200 years since this tragic royal visit and sheds light on Hawaiʻi's history and culture by exploring the complex and lasting relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations.
