CuratorialText: This fashion show presents contemporary clothing of the Moana-Nui-a-Kiwa (the great ocean of Kiwa/the Pacific Ocean), which has been facilitated in the UK by Interisland Collective and curated by Lyall Hakaraia. The collection has been conceived and made by Māori designer Lyall Hakaraia in collaboration with venerated practitioners from across the Pacific. These practictioners are tohunga (experts) who are building and continue to grow Pacific cultural knowledge. They consider themselves as guides and strive to be 'good ancestors' through their daily practices, powerful intentions and vision. They are building resilient futures for mokopuna (grandchildren) and generations to come. The show will act as a focal point for the Pacific diaspora community as they activate a VĀ (portal) to their ancestors. The show is an offering to those ancestors that remain at the British Museum and are embodied through Pacific collections. It aims to 'warm' them in slumber. The collective will sing, chant and offer prayers to them during the show with Aroha (love) from across the Pacific.
persona3_adapted: This fashion show presents contemporary clothing from Moana-Nui-a-Kiwa (the great ocean of Kiwa, also known as the Pacific Ocean). The event has been organized in the UK by Interisland Collective and curated by Lyall Hakaraia. The collection was designed and created by Māori designer Lyall Hakaraia, working together with highly respected practitioners from across the Pacific. These practitioners are tohunga—experts who are building and continuing to grow Pacific cultural knowledge. They see themselves as guides and aim to be 'good ancestors' through their daily actions, strong intentions, and vision. They are working to create resilient futures for mokopuna (grandchildren) and for generations yet to come. The show will serve as a gathering point for the Pacific diaspora community as they open a VĀ (a portal or spiritual doorway) to their ancestors. The show is an offering to those ancestors who remain at the British Museum and are present through Pacific collections. Its purpose is to 'warm' them as they rest. During the show, the collective will sing, chant, and offer prayers to them with Aroha (love) from across the Pacific.

CuratorialText: One half of the gallery presents the histories of China from 5000 BC to the present: from iconic Ming dynasty blue-and-white porcelain to delicate handscrolls, from magnificent Tang dynasty tomb figurines to modern works of art. The displays feature the richness of art and material culture in China, including painting, prints, jade, bronze, lacquer and ceramics.
persona3_adapted: One half of the gallery traces China’s history from 5000 BC to the present day. The objects on view range from famous blue-and-white porcelain of the Ming dynasty and finely painted handscrolls, to impressive tomb figures from the Tang dynasty and modern artworks. Together, these displays highlight the depth and variety of China’s artistic and material traditions, including painting, prints, jade, bronze, lacquerware, and ceramics.

CuratorialText: The other half of the gallery presents South Asia's many histories chronologically and by region, from early human occupation to the present. Highlights include seals from the Indus civilisation, superb south Indian sculptures of Shiva and one of the finest statues of the goddess Tara from Sri Lanka. Sophisticated paintings and objects from the courts of the Mughal emperors can be seen alongside 20th-century paintings, including by the Nobel laureate Rabindranath Tagore.
persona3_adapted: The other half of the gallery presents South Asia's many histories in chronological order and by region, from the earliest human settlements to the present day. Among the highlights are engraved seals from the Indus civilisation, exceptional stone sculptures of the Hindu god Shiva from southern India, and one of the finest statues of the goddess Tara from Sri Lanka. Highly refined paintings and decorative objects from the courts of the Mughal emperors are displayed alongside 20th-century paintings, including works by the Nobel Prize-winning poet and artist Rabindranath Tagore.

CuratorialText: China is one of the world's oldest civilisations. Today it covers a vast territory the size of Europe and is home to a quarter of the world's population. China has produced a highly distinctive culture with beautifully crafted objects made on an industrial scale from the earliest times. Room 33 examines the past 7,000 years of China's history, exploring the themes of writing systems, rituals, beliefs, war, international trade and more. Room 33 also explores South Asia's history, from 1.5 million years ago to the present day. Trade, exchange, migration and political power are some of the themes examined. South Asia spans India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Bhutan, Nepal, the Tibetan plateau and Sri Lanka. The region is home to numerous ancient and modern languages, such as Sanskrit, Tamil, Hindi and Urdu. The Buddhist, Hindu, Jain, Sikh and Islamic faiths have inspired magnificent architecture, sculpture, painting, literature and music.
persona3_adapted: China is one of the world's oldest civilizations. Today, it covers a vast area roughly the size of Europe and is home to a quarter of the global population. From very early times, China developed a unique culture, producing beautifully made objects on a large, almost industrial scale. Room 33 looks at 7,000 years of Chinese history, exploring topics such as writing systems, religious and ceremonial practices, beliefs, warfare, international trade, and more. The gallery also examines the history of South Asia, from 1.5 million years ago to the present day. Key themes include trade, cultural exchange, migration, and political power. South Asia includes modern-day India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Bhutan, Nepal, the Tibetan plateau, and Sri Lanka. The region is home to many ancient and modern languages, including Sanskrit, Tamil, Hindi, and Urdu. Major religions such as Buddhism, Hinduism, Jainism, Sikhism, and Islam have inspired remarkable achievements in architecture, sculpture, painting, literature, and music.

CuratorialText:
Africa's rich cultural heritage and histories are represented at the Museum both in Africa (Room 25), The Sainsbury Galleries, and across the galleries. Join our volunteer-led tours, developed in collaboration with community members, to explore their personal selection of objects ranging from the ancient world to the present day. Each guide will deliver their own personal tour exploring one of the following themes:
Out of Africa: stories of migration, materials and mythology
The Igbo worldview: artefacts from the past in the British Museum
African metal artefacts: tradition, technique and trade
Ancient objects from African kingdoms
persona3_adapted:
Africa’s rich cultural heritage and histories are represented at the Museum both in Africa (Room 25), The Sainsbury Galleries, and across the other galleries. Join our volunteer-led tours, created in partnership with community members, to explore their personal selection of objects from the ancient world to the present day. Each guide will offer their own unique tour focusing on one of the following themes:

- **Out of Africa: stories of migration, materials and mythology**  
- **The Igbo worldview: artefacts from the past in the British Museum**  
- **African metal artefacts: tradition, technique and trade**  
- **Ancient objects from African kingdoms**

CuratorialText:
Step into spring and the month of May in this creative play session for children aged five and under.
Meet May Queens, Green Men and woodland creatures in this child-led, open-ended creative session. Stop and smell some floral scents, draw and scribble with a range of art materials and make crowns and masks to wear for spring festivities! Working at your child's pace, together we'll explore textures, colours and scents through a range of materials.
persona3_adapted:
Step into spring and the month of May in this creative play session for children aged five and under.  
Meet May Queens, Green Men, and woodland creatures in this child-led, open-ended creative session—meaning your child can explore freely, following their own interests.  
Stop and smell some floral scents, draw and scribble with a range of art materials, and make crowns and masks to wear for spring festivities!  
Working at your child’s pace, together we’ll explore textures, colours, and scents through a variety of materials.

CuratorialText: Sennacherib was the king of Assyria from 704–681 BC and was famous for his building projects. The rooms and courtyards of his Neo-Assyrian Southwest Palace at Nineveh were decorated with a series of detailed carved stone panels. Many of these stone panels are on display in Room 9. The panels depict a variety of scenes, including the transport of huge sculptures of human-headed winged bulls (lamassu) that weigh up to 30 tons and were intended for the main entrances to the palace. These illustrations provide an insight into ancient quarrying and transport techniques, as well as Sennacherib's keen interest in his building projects. Other panels on display depict the king's military campaigns.
persona3_adapted: Sennacherib ruled Assyria from 704 to 681 BC and was well known for his ambitious building projects. The rooms and courtyards of his Neo-Assyrian Southwest Palace at Nineveh were decorated with a series of detailed stone panels, carved with images. Many of these panels are now on display in Room 9. They show a variety of scenes, including the transport of enormous sculptures of human-headed winged bulls, called lamassu, which weighed up to 30 tons and were intended to guard the main entrances to the palace. These images offer insight into ancient methods of quarrying and moving heavy stone, as well as Sennacherib's strong personal interest in his construction work. Other panels on display depict the king's military campaigns.

CuratorialText: Split across three rooms, these galleries display life during this period in Assyrian culture. On display are lion hunting reliefs, the siege of the city of Lachish and scenes from inside the palace of King Sargon II at Khorsabad.  Visitors to Room 10a are greeted by a pair of colossal human-headed winged bulls. Intended as a supernatural guardians, these gigantic figures guarded the palace of King Sargon II at Khorsabad, and were intended to deter evil of all kinds from entering.
persona3_adapted: Spread across three rooms, these galleries show what daily life was like in Assyrian culture during this period. On display are carved stone panels showing lion hunts, the military attack on the city of Lachish, and scenes from inside the palace of King Sargon II at Khorsabad. Visitors to Room 10a are greeted by a pair of enormous human-headed winged bulls. These giant figures were meant to serve as supernatural protectors, guarding the palace of King Sargon II at Khorsabad and keeping all forms of evil from entering.

CuratorialText: In ancient Assyria, hunting lions was considered the sport of kings, symbolic of the ruling monarch's duty to protect and fight for his people. The sculpted reliefs in Room 10a illustrate the sporting exploits of the last great Assyrian king, Ashurbanipal (668–631 BC) and were created for his palace at Nineveh (in modern-day northern Iraq). The hunt scenes, full of tension and realism, rank among the finest achievements of Assyrian Art. They depict the release of the lions, the ensuing chase and subsequent killing.
persona3_adapted: In ancient Assyria, hunting lions was seen as a sport fit for kings, symbolizing the ruler's duty to protect and fight for his people. The carved stone panels in Room 10a show the hunting achievements of the last great Assyrian king, Ashurbanipal (668–631 BC), and were made for his palace at Nineveh (in what is now northern Iraq). These hunting scenes, full of tension and a lifelike quality, are considered among the finest works of Assyrian art. They show the release of the lions, the chase that follows, and the final killing.

CuratorialText: Lachish was one of the chief cities of the kingdom of Judah in the southern Levant and in 701 BC it was captured by the Assyrian King Sennacherib (704–681 BC). The siege followed the refusal of Lachish to pay tribute to the Assyrian Empire (based in modern northern Iraq) and is mentioned in the Bible. Many of the relief sculptures on display in Room 10b depict the capture of the city, alongside a selection of items and weaponry used in the siege. A 'prism' inscribed with an Assyrian account of the campaign is also on show.
persona3_adapted: Lachish was one of the most important cities in the kingdom of Judah, located in the southern part of the region known as the Levant (the area along the eastern Mediterranean). In 701 BC, it was captured by the Assyrian King Sennacherib (who ruled from 704 to 681 BC). The siege happened because Lachish refused to pay tribute—essentially a form of forced payment or tax—to the Assyrian Empire, which was based in what is now northern Iraq. This event is also mentioned in the Bible. Many of the carved stone panels, or relief sculptures, displayed in Room 10b show scenes from the city's capture, along with a selection of objects and weapons used during the siege. Also on display is a clay prism inscribed with the Assyrian version of the campaign's events.

CuratorialText: The city and palace at Khorsabad (in modern northern Iraq), was built for the Assyrian King Sargon II (721–705 BC). The palace entrances were originally dominated by pairs of colossal human-headed winged bulls, which were intended as guardians, accompanied by protective spirits with magical powers. Two of these impressive statues now stand in Room 10c, along with carvings depicting the king and crown prince, royal courtiers and hunting scenes. Inscriptions on display in the gallery come from a similar winged bull from the palace of Sennacherib (704–681 BC) at nearby Nineveh and were badly burnt when the city was destroyed in 612 BC.
persona3_adapted: The city and palace at Khorsabad (in modern northern Iraq) were built for the Assyrian King Sargon II, who ruled from 721 to 705 BC. At the palace entrances, pairs of enormous winged bulls with human heads originally stood guard, accompanied by protective spirits believed to possess magical powers. Two of these impressive statues are now displayed in Room 10c, alongside carvings that show the king and crown prince, royal courtiers, and scenes of hunting. Also on display in the gallery are inscriptions taken from a similar winged bull that once stood in the palace of Sennacherib (who ruled from 704 to 681 BC) at nearby Nineveh. These inscriptions were severely damaged by fire when the city was destroyed in 612 BC.

CuratorialText: Rooms 57–59 present the material culture of the region from the Neolithic farmers of the eighth millennium BC to the fall of the Neo-Babylonian Empire in 539 BC, within the context of major historical events. Objects on display illustrate the continuity of the Canaanite culture of the southern Levant throughout this period. They highlight the indigenous origins of both the Israelites and the Phoenicians. The display compares this culture with that of the people of central inland Syria, the Amorites and the Aramaeans.
persona3_adapted: Rooms 57–59 showcase the everyday and ceremonial objects—what scholars call "material culture"—from this region, spanning from the Neolithic farmers of the eighth millennium BC to the fall of the Neo-Babylonian Empire in 539 BC. These items are presented within the framework of major historical events. The objects on display reveal how the Canaanite culture of the southern Levant (roughly modern-day Israel, Palestine, Jordan, Lebanon, and southern Syria) remained remarkably consistent over thousands of years. They also emphasize that both the Israelites and the Phoenicians had their roots in this local, indigenous culture. The exhibition draws a comparison between this Canaanite tradition and the cultures of people from central inland Syria—specifically the Amorites and the Aramaeans.

CuratorialText: Ancient Anatolia and Urartu form an important land link between Europe and Asia. They lie where the modern Republic of Turkey, Armenia, Georgia and north-west Iran are located today.  Examples of Early Bronze Age craftsmanship on display include silver bull and cup. Business archives of Middle Bronze Age merchants illustrate trading between central Anatolia and Mesopotamia (modern Iraq). Delicate gold jewellery and figurines date from the Hittite period. Iron Age objects from Urartu, include winged bulls and griffins used to decorate furniture.
persona3_adapted: Ancient Anatolia and Urartu served as a crucial land connection between Europe and Asia, located in what is now the modern countries of Turkey, Armenia, Georgia, and northwestern Iran. Among the objects on display from the Early Bronze Age are a silver bull and a cup, showcasing the skilled craftsmanship of that period. Records from Middle Bronze Age merchants reveal the trade networks between central Anatolia and Mesopotamia (present-day Iraq). From the Hittite period, there are finely made gold jewelry and small statues. Objects from the Iron Age kingdom of Urartu include winged bulls and griffins—mythical creatures with the body of a lion and the head of an eagle—that were used as decorative elements on furniture.

CuratorialText: Reaching back more than 2,000 years, this exhibition explored the origins of Hindu, Jain and Buddhist sacred art in the ancient and powerful nature spirits of India, and the spread of this art beyond the subcontinent.  One of the first major exhibitions in the world to look at the early devotional art of India from a multi-faith, contemporary and global perspective, it highlighted the inspiration behind now-familiar depictions of the deities and enlightened teachers of these world religions – and how they were shared across the Indian Ocean to Southeast Asia and along the Silk Roads to East Asia.
persona3_adapted: Spanning more than 2,000 years, this exhibition explored the origins of sacred art in Hinduism, Jainism, and Buddhism. It traced these traditions back to ancient and powerful nature spirits in India, and followed how this art spread beyond the subcontinent. As one of the first major exhibitions worldwide to examine early devotional art from India through a multi-faith, contemporary, and global lens, it revealed the inspiration behind now-familiar images of gods, goddesses, and enlightened teachers in these world religions. The exhibition also showed how these artistic traditions traveled across the Indian Ocean to Southeast Asia and along the Silk Roads to East Asia.

CuratorialText: Colourful, multi-sensory and atmospheric, this exhibition was developed in collaboration with an advisory community panel of practising Hindus, Buddhists and Jains. These living religious traditions and their sacred art are now integral to the daily lives of almost two billion people around the world including in the UK. Key loans from our community partners helped to tell this contemporary story.
persona3_adapted: Vivid, immersive, and rich in sensory detail, this exhibition was created in partnership with an advisory panel of practicing Hindus, Buddhists, and Jains from the community. These living faith traditions and their sacred artworks are now a central part of daily life for nearly two billion people worldwide, including in the UK. Important objects lent by our community partners helped bring this contemporary story to life.

CuratorialText: The exhibition showcased more than 180 objects – including sculptures, paintings, drawings and manuscripts – from the South Asian collection at the British Museum as well as generous loans from national and international partners. It highlighted provenance, examining the stories, from creation to acquisition by museums, of every object in the show.
persona3_adapted: The exhibition featured more than 180 items—such as sculptures, paintings, drawings, and manuscripts—drawn from the British Museum’s South Asian collection, along with significant loans from museums and institutions in the UK and abroad. It focused on the history of each object’s journey, from its creation to its acquisition by museums, exploring the stories behind how and why these works came to be in the collections on display.

CuratorialText: From the symbolic footprints which preceded portrayals of the Buddha in human form to the cosmic serpents incorporated into Hindu art and the nature spirits who attend Jain enlightened teachers, this compelling exhibition told the ancient stories behind these living traditions.
persona3_adapted: From the symbolic footprints that once stood in for images of the Buddha, before artists began showing him in human form, to the cosmic serpents woven into Hindu art and the nature spirits that accompany Jain enlightened teachers, this compelling exhibition revealed the ancient stories behind these living traditions.

CuratorialText: The first exhibition on Hiroshige to be held at the British Museum, and the first on the artist in London for more than a quarter of a century, this was a visually stunning portrait of a country about to change forever. Born during an unsettled time in Japan's history, Utagawa Hiroshige (1797–1858) went on to become one of the country's most talented, prolific and popular artists. As Japan confronted the encroaching outside world, Hiroshige's calm artistic vision connected with – and reassured – people at every level of society. From fashionable figures and energetic city views to remote landscapes and impressions of the natural world, Hiroshige captured many aspects of life in the Japan of his time. Stunning bird-and-flower prints reveal his poetic feeling for nature while his evocative landscapes reflected the growing interest in travel across Japan. Hiroshige portrayed his world sometimes as it was, but often the way he imagined it could be.
persona3_adapted: This is the first exhibition on Hiroshige at the British Museum, and the first in London dedicated to the artist in over twenty-five years. It offers a visually striking portrait of a country on the verge of profound change. Born during a period of instability in Japan, Utagawa Hiroshige (1797–1858) became one of the nation's most skilled, productive, and beloved artists. As Japan faced growing pressure from the outside world, Hiroshige's serene artistic perspective connected with—and offered comfort to—people across all levels of society. His work captures many facets of life in Japan during his era, from fashionable figures and lively city scenes to distant landscapes and depictions of the natural world. Stunning prints of birds and flowers reveal his poetic sensitivity to nature, while his evocative landscapes reflect the increasing interest in travel throughout Japan. Hiroshige portrayed his world sometimes as it truly was, but often as he imagined it could be.

CuratorialText: Possessed of remarkable technical skills, both as a colourist and draftsman, Hiroshige had a sympathetic regard for people from all walks of life. Unlike most other print designers of his day, he came from a samurai family, but crossed social boundaries to devote himself to depicting popular customs. His work was affordable, too – along with celebrated landscape prints, he also designed hundreds of hand-held, disposable fans that were available to all.
persona3_adapted: Hiroshige was an exceptionally skilled artist, both in his use of color and in his drawing ability. He showed a warm, understanding interest in people from every level of society. Unlike most other print designers of his time, he was born into a samurai family—a class of warriors—yet he moved beyond social divisions to focus on portraying everyday customs and ordinary life. His work was also affordable: in addition to his famous landscape prints, he created hundreds of hand-held fans that were meant to be used and thrown away, making his art available to nearly everyone.

CuratorialText: The exhibition featured prints, drawings, illustrated books and paintings from the British Museum collection, as well as a significant gift and loan of prints from Alan Medaugh, a major US collector of Hiroshige's work, and other important loans. As well as exploring Hiroshige's incredible body of work, this show considered his global legacy, which spans from Japan's Edo period (1615–1868) through to Vincent van Gogh and contemporary artists such as Julian Opie.
persona3_adapted: The exhibition included prints, drawings, illustrated books, and paintings from the British Museum's collection, along with a major gift and loan of prints from Alan Medaugh, a prominent American collector of Hiroshige's work, as well as other important loans. In addition to exploring Hiroshige's remarkable artistic output, the show examined his lasting influence around the world—from Japan's Edo period (1615–1868) to artists like Vincent van Gogh and contemporary figures such as Julian Opie.

CuratorialText: A stone figure of 'Hara-Pārvatī' (Śiva seated with Pārvatī on his knee). Also known as 'Umamahesvaramurti'. Accompanied by a lion and a bull (Nandi) and surrounded by many flying and standing attendants (musicians, garland-bearers), there is also a row of ritual equipment: two conch-shell trumpets on stands, a lamp and a bell.
persona3_adapted: A stone sculpture depicts the Hindu god Shiva seated with his wife Parvati on his knee. This type of image is also called 'Umamahesvaramurti.' The couple is accompanied by a lion and a bull (known as Nandi, Shiva's sacred vehicle). Around them are many attendants, some flying and some standing, including musicians and figures carrying garlands. Below them is a row of ritual objects: two conch-shell trumpets mounted on stands, a lamp, and a bell.

CuratorialText: Pair of bride's slippers (tarkasin) made of red-dyed leather embroidered in silk thread and decorated with metal studs. Tongues cut in the shape of the khamsa ('five') or 'Hand of Fatima'.
persona3_adapted: Pair of bride's slippers (tarkasin) made from red-dyed leather, embroidered with silk thread and decorated with metal studs. The tongues of the slippers are cut in the shape of the khamsa (meaning 'five' in Arabic), also known as the 'Hand of Fatima'—a palm-shaped symbol believed to offer protection.

CuratorialText: Head and upper body of pink/grey granite monumental statue of Ramses II (one of a pair placed before the door of the Ramesseum) wearing nemes head-cloth and circlet of uraei (about half now lost), the sculptor has exploited the bichrome nature of the stone to emphasise the division between body and face; the dorsal pillar is inscribed with vertical registers of hieroglyphs - giving the name and titles of the king and part of a dedication to Amun-Ra; in 1817 it was noted that there were traces of colour upon the statue and it may have, therefore, been painted red in antiquity.
persona3_adapted: Head and upper body of a large statue made of pink and grey granite, depicting the powerful Pharaoh Ramses II. This statue was originally one of a pair placed in front of the entrance to the Ramesseum, his memorial temple. The king wears a royal headdress known as a *nemes*—the striped cloth often associated with Egyptian pharaohs—along with a circlet of sacred cobras called *uraei* (though about half of this crown is now lost). The sculptor cleverly used the two-tone nature of the stone to create a clear visual separation between the body and the face. On the back, a central pillar is carved with vertical columns of hieroglyphs, which record the king's name and titles, as well as part of a dedication to the god Amun-Ra. In 1817, observers noted traces of color on the statue, suggesting it may have been painted red in ancient times.

CuratorialText: Head from monumental red granite statue of Amenhotep III(?).
persona3_adapted: Head from a large-scale statue carved from red granite, possibly depicting the pharaoh Amenhotep III.

CuratorialText: Base and lid of the anthropoid wooden inner coffin of Hornedjitef, son of Nekhthorheb, who held a large number of priestly offices. Polychrome painted and gilded face, wig, collar and pectoral, winged scarab across breast, body inscribed with vertical registers of painted hieroglyphs, flanked by deities, the rest of the surface is unelaborated. Interior of lid decorated with a figure of Nut, surrounded by astronomical images of decan stars and constellations. Interior of base also decorated.
persona3_adapted: The base and lid of the human-shaped inner wooden coffin of Hornedjitef, son of Nekhthorheb, who served in many priestly roles. The face, wig, collar, and chest ornament are painted in multiple colors and covered with gold. A winged scarab beetle is depicted across the chest. The body of the coffin is covered with vertical columns of painted hieroglyphs, with gods and goddesses shown on either side. The rest of the surface is left plain. Inside the lid is a painting of the sky goddess Nut, surrounded by images of star groups and constellations used in ancient Egyptian astronomy. The inside of the base is also decorated.

CuratorialText: Brass (heavily leaded zinc-brass) head cast using the lost wax (cire perdue) technique. The head is a little under life size and is made in a naturalistic style. It has a headdress, suggesting a crown, of complex construction. The main tubular section of the crown runs around the head in a three-layer composition. The upper layer has a band of four horizontal rectangles representing flat, discoidal beads surmounted by a red-painted tubular bead and a tassel. The central layer has a row of vertical rectangles representing tubular beads with tassels. The lower layer has a row of rosettes painted in red. The main tubular section of the crown also has a projecting arc around the forehead comprising small tubular beads edged with a row of red-painted feathers. At the back of the crown is a neck cover. The central part of the cover has eighteen vertical elements incised to indicate plaitwork with traces of black paint. The bottom and sides have a row of red-painted rosettes. A crest rises above the central crown at the front. It comprises a conical roundel with central boss surrounded by seven concentric rings, probably representing beads. A plaitwork element rises behind the roundel, it terminates in a pointed ovoid tip; both elements bear traces of black paint. The face is slightly elongated and has vertical incised markings (striations). Two lines of holes run between the lobes of the ears, one going through the angle of the neck and the jaw, the second crossing the jaw below the lower lip. A double line of holes runs across the upper lip. The almond-shaped eyes are small in proportion to the head and the eyebrow-ridge is sharply defined. The lips are not striated. There are grooves round the front of the neck representing skin creases. There are holes pierced through the neck at the front and at both sides. There is a large irregular hole on the right proper side of the jaw.
persona3_adapted:
This head was cast in brass (a type of zinc-brass with a high lead content) using the lost wax method, a technique in which a wax model is encased in a mold, melted away, and replaced with molten metal. Slightly smaller than life-size, the head is rendered in a naturalistic style—meaning it aims to resemble a real human form.

The headdress, which suggests a crown, is built in a complex, layered structure. The main band of the crown wraps around the head in three stacked sections. The top layer features four horizontal rectangles that represent flat, disc-shaped beads, each topped with a red-painted tubular bead and a tassel. The middle layer consists of a row of vertical rectangles, which stand for tubular beads with tassels. The bottom layer has a row of rosettes painted red.

From the main band, an arc projects forward across the forehead, made up of small tubular beads edged with a row of red-painted feathers. At the back of the crown is a neck cover. Its central section has eighteen vertical lines carved into the surface to suggest braided or woven work, with traces of black paint still visible. The bottom and sides of the neck cover are decorated with a row of red-painted rosettes.

At the front of the crown, a crest rises above the central band. It consists of a cone-shaped roundel with a raised central boss, surrounded by seven concentric rings—likely meant to represent beads. Behind this roundel rises a braided element that ends in a pointed, egg-shaped tip; both parts still show traces of black paint.

The face is slightly elongated and marked with vertical incised lines, or striations. Two rows of holes run between the earlobes: one passes through the angle of the neck and jaw, the other crosses the jaw below the lower lip. A double row of holes runs across the upper lip. The almond-shaped eyes are small in proportion to the head, and the brow ridge is sharply defined. The lips are not striated. Grooves around the front of the neck suggest skin creases. Holes have been pierced through the neck at the front and on both sides. There is also a large, irregular hole on the right side of the jaw.

CuratorialText: Goddess. A standing figure of a female deity (Tārā) solid cast in bronze and gilded. The figure has a high tubular coiffure (jatamukuta) held in place by a medallion flanked by makara-s (water creatures). The medallion was probably set with stones, as were the eyes. The earlobes are elongated and the lower body dressed in a tight-fitting cloth knotted at the hips. The upper body is uncovered. The proper right hand is in varadamudrā (gesture of giving), the left hand in katakahastamudrā (the hand pose wherein the tips of the fingers are applied to the thumb so as to form a ring, as if to hold a flower). The two middle fingers of the right hand are missing as are toes from both feet.
persona3_adapted: Goddess. This is a standing figure of a female deity, known as Tārā, cast in bronze and then covered with a thin layer of gold. The figure wears her hair in a tall, tube-like style, held in place by a circular ornament. On either side of this ornament are mythical water creatures called makaras. The ornament itself was probably set with precious stones, as were the figure's eyes. Her earlobes are stretched long, and the lower part of her body is covered by a tight-fitting cloth, tied at the hips. Her upper body is bare. Her right hand is held in a specific pose known as varadamudrā, which is a gesture of giving or blessing. Her left hand is in a pose called katakahastamudrā, where the tips of the fingers touch the thumb to form a ring, as if she were holding a flower. The two middle fingers of her right hand are missing, as are the toes from both feet.

CuratorialText:
The Virgin and child: cartoon; the Virgin half-length, Christ standing with his arms around his mother
Black chalk and/or charcoal, touches of white chalk, pricked and indented for transfer, on two conjoined sheets; framed
persona3_adapted:
The Virgin and Child: a preparatory drawing; the Virgin is shown from the waist up, while the Christ child stands with his arms around his mother.  
This work was created using black chalk and/or charcoal, with touches of white chalk. The outlines have been pricked and indented—techniques used to transfer the design onto another surface. It is drawn on two sheets joined together and is presented in a frame.

CuratorialText: Marble torso of Nike, originally winged, wearing a sleeveless chiton; probably part of an akroterion.
persona3_adapted: Marble torso of Nike, the Greek goddess of victory, who was originally depicted with wings. She wears a sleeveless tunic, known as a chiton. This sculpture was likely part of an akroterion—an ornamental figure placed at the peak or corners of a temple roof.

CuratorialText:
Fragment of a marble corner palmetto with bird foot.
The small fragment originates from the standing, rectangular snapped off corner palmetto above a volute acroterion. One both sides of the middle leave, which protrudes from the corner, three lightly curved and hollow shaped leaves are visible and whose edges are accompanied by a bar. The round endings were emphasised on the outer side by tubercle. Darts appear between the leaves. On the left side of the fragment remains of a curved ovolo moulding of the volute is preserved. The upper and back side show a small pentagonal block with a 45^ (degrees) outstanding, discs-like tunnel. Leaves with a convex back site seem to grow out of the tunnel. On the block and on both sides of the tunnels two claws are visible with bird legs on each the edges
On the darts on the left side traces of red paint was found on the uppermost and the third. Traces of red and blue paint.
The light crystalline marble is fairly sanded on the surface, however, the underside is fractured.
persona3_adapted:
Fragment of a marble corner ornament shaped like a palm leaf, with a bird's foot.

This small piece originally came from a standing, rectangular corner ornament that had broken off from a larger decorative element placed above a spiral-shaped roof decoration. On both sides of the central leaf, which sticks out from the corner, three gently curved, hollow-shaped leaves are visible, each with a raised line running along their edges. The rounded tips of the leaves were emphasized on the outer side by small bumps. Arrow-shaped decorations appear between the leaves. On the left side of the fragment, part of a curved, egg-shaped molding from the spiral decoration is still preserved.

The top and back of the piece show a small, five-sided block with a disc-shaped tunnel projecting out at a 45-degree angle. Leaves with a rounded back seem to grow out of this tunnel. On the block and on both sides of the tunnel, two claws are visible, with bird legs along the edges.

On the arrow-shaped decorations on the left side, traces of red paint were found on the topmost and third ones. Traces of red and blue paint also remain.

The light, crystalline marble has a fairly smooth surface, but the underside is broken and rough.

CuratorialText: Chess piece; walrus ivory; king: bearded; wearing floriated crown, tunic, mantle and brooch; seated upon high-backed chair holding sheathed sword; chair back: two horizontal compartments separated by zigzag band; upper filled with interlaced arcading of round arches; lower filled with symmetrical foliate ornaments; chair sides decorated with interlaced patterns.
persona3_adapted: This chess piece, carved from walrus ivory, depicts a bearded king. He wears a crown decorated with floral patterns, a tunic, a cloak, and a brooch. Seated in a high-backed chair, he holds a sheathed sword. The back of the chair is divided into two horizontal sections by a zigzag band. The upper section features a repeating pattern of interlocking rounded arches; the lower section is filled with symmetrical leaf-like ornaments. The sides of the chair are decorated with interwoven patterns.

CuratorialText: Egyptian terracotta model palmette acroterion. The upper corner broken from a miniature altar, decorated on each side with a half-palmette. The altar was made in a multiple mould, probably of two pieces, but perhaps more. Traces of a matt red slip survive, inside and out and signs of burning within. Brown Nile silt with a light-brown core, abundant fine gold mica and some white inclusions.
persona3_adapted: This fragment is an Egyptian terracotta ornament shaped like a stylized fan of leaves, known as a palmette. It was originally attached to the upper corner of a small altar, which has since broken away. Each side of the ornament is decorated with a half-palmette design. The altar was made using a multi-part mold, likely consisting of two pieces, though possibly more. Traces of a dull red coating, called a slip, survive on both the interior and exterior surfaces, and there are signs of burning inside. The object is made from brown Nile silt clay, which has a lighter brown core and contains abundant fine gold-colored mica, along with some white mineral inclusions.

CuratorialText: Indigo resist-dyed cloth (adire eleko); on factory-made cotton; two panels machine sewn tgether. Each panel is divided into four by seven squares. Hand-painted patterns include two squares with the pillars of Mapo Hall (in Ibadan) between which are four pestles (or spoons).
persona3_adapted: This cloth, known as *adire eleko*, was made using a technique where a resist substance is applied to prevent dye from coloring certain areas, creating patterns. It is made from factory-produced cotton, with two panels machine-sewn together. Each panel is divided into a grid of four rows and seven squares. The designs were painted by hand and include two squares featuring the pillars of Mapo Hall, a landmark building in the city of Ibadan, Nigeria. Between these pillars are four shapes that could represent pestles (tools for grinding) or spoons.

CuratorialText:
Ticket addressed to A. W. Franks to see Falero's painting 'Nightmare' at the Gainsborough Gallery, with image of a creature behind the ticket, with a bat's wing, claw and bone rib cage. 1888
Wood-engraving and letterpress
persona3_adapted:
Ticket for admission to see Falero's painting 'Nightmare' at the Gainsborough Gallery, addressed to A. W. Franks. The ticket features an image of a creature with a bat's wing, claw, and bony rib cage. 1888
Printed using a wood-engraving technique (carving the image into a block of wood) and letterpress (printing from raised, movable type).

CuratorialText:
Ticket to see Frith's painting 'The Railway Station', with central image of a steam train arriving at the station with figures; empty space at the top to add name. 1865
Lithograph
persona3_adapted: A printed admission ticket for viewing William Powell Frith's painting *The Railway Station*, featuring a steam train arriving at the station surrounded by figures. The top of the ticket has a blank space where a name could be added. 1865. This is a lithograph—a print made by drawing on a flat stone or metal plate with a greasy substance, then using that surface to transfer the image onto paper.

CuratorialText:
Paper admission ticket. (whole)
Photographs of, left to right, Klimt painting, the Leopold Museum and Shiele painting; inscription in black above: multicolour print on white background.
Inscription, in black print on white background.
persona3_adapted:
Paper admission ticket (full ticket).  
The ticket features photographs, from left to right, of a painting by Klimt, the Leopold Museum building, and a painting by Schiele. Above these images, there is an inscription printed in black on a white background. The inscription itself is also printed in black on white.

CuratorialText:
Paper admission ticket. (whole)
Photograph, view of the Basilica Cistern in Istanbul showing columns and arched roof; inscription to right in black: multicolour print on pink and white background.
Unidentified historical painting showing forces gathered, tree to left: multicolour print on white background.
persona3_adapted:
Paper admission ticket (full ticket).

A photograph of the Basilica Cistern in Istanbul, showing its columns and arched roof. On the right side, there is black text printed over a pink and white background.

An unidentified historical painting, showing a gathering of military forces with a tree on the left side. This image is a multicolored print on a white background.

CuratorialText:
View of the interior of a tent with visitors gathered before Copley's huge history painting (Guildhall Art Gallery), elaborately framed with pillars and swags, including two young men at left flirting with young women, and two older men at right seated with papers, figures at a desk behind at left; facsimile of the admission ticket to the exhibition, from an engraving by Bartolozzi. c.1850s-60s
Photomechincal reproduction
persona3_adapted: View of the inside of a tent, where visitors are gathered in front of Copley's large-scale historical painting (housed at the Guildhall Art Gallery). The painting is ornately framed with columns and decorative fabric drapes. On the left, two young men are flirting with young women; on the right, two older men sit with papers, while figures are seated at a desk behind them on the left. Also included is a copy of the admission ticket for the exhibition, taken from an engraving by Bartolozzi. This work dates from around the 1850s to 1860s and is a photomechanical reproduction—a print made using a photographic process.

CuratorialText:
Admission ticket to a performance organised by the Guild of Literature and Art, with figure representing the painter Richard Wilson at left, taking a painting to a pawn shop, and at right figure representing Daniel Defoe, with a manuscript of 'Robinson Crusoe' in his pocket, leaving the office of the publisher Edmund Curll with a sad expression on his face, met by his worried wife and daughter; books and pallette with paintbrushes lying in foreground; after Ward. 1851
Mixed method, including etching, aquatint and engraving, on chine collé
persona3_adapted:
Admission ticket to a performance organized by the Guild of Literature and Art. On the left, a figure representing the painter Richard Wilson is shown taking a painting to a pawn shop. On the right, a figure representing Daniel Defoe—with a manuscript of *Robinson Crusoe* in his pocket—leaves the office of the publisher Edmund Curll, wearing a sad expression. He is met by his worried wife and daughter. Books and a palette with paintbrushes lie in the foreground. The image is based on a work by Ward. 1851

Created using a combination of printmaking techniques, including etching (where lines are drawn into a coated metal plate and then acid-etched), aquatint (which creates soft, tonal areas similar to watercolor washes), and engraving (where lines are cut directly into the plate). The print is made on a thin, delicate paper (chine collé) that is bonded to a stronger backing sheet.
