persona3_adapted: Two soldiers lie side by side on the floor of a tent, their heads resting on khaki bags. Both are dressed in desert combat gear. The soldier farther from the artist is reading, while the one closer to the artist has fallen asleep, his book resting on his chest.

CuratorialText:
image: A view across the river Spree with the sun rising over the city in the background. There are fortifications and an observation post on the opposite bank with a vessel moored below, and a British Military policeman with a vehicle on the near side.
History note
Artistic Records Committee Commission, December 1980
persona3_adapted:
A view across the River Spree, with the sun rising over the city in the background. On the opposite bank stand fortifications and an observation post; a vessel is moored below. On the near side, a British military policeman is visible with a vehicle.

This work was created as part of a commission from the Artistic Records Committee in December 1980.

CuratorialText: image: An abstract composition of coiled and twisted girders of all sizes. This drawing explores the distortions made by bombs to building structures. The violence of the blast is evident in the rhythm and the form of the girders. Paule Vézelay was born in Bristol and returned there during the Second World War after over 10 years as an artist in Paris. She never gained an official war artist commission due to her left wing associations and the abstract nature of her work. However with the help of Bristol Museum and Art Gallery she gained a permit to draw the ruins in Bristol.
persona3_adapted: An abstract composition of coiled and twisted metal beams in various sizes. This drawing examines how bombs distort building structures. The violence of the explosion is visible in the pattern and shape of the beams. Paule Vézelay was born in Bristol and returned there during the Second World War after spending more than a decade as an artist in Paris. She never received an official appointment as a war artist—likely because of her left-wing political ties and the abstract style of her work. However, with support from Bristol Museum and Art Gallery, she obtained permission to sketch the ruins in Bristol.

CuratorialText: image: In the foreground two sailors sit with their backs to the viewer on the deck of a ship. A third sailor stands behind them leaning back with his hands in his pockets. A gun protrudes out above them. They are watching two ships anchored ahead of them and clouds of smoke rising from the hills in the distance. The sky is dotted with barrage balloons. The artist has also made several other sketches of ships and sailors in the upper right corner of the image.
persona3_adapted: In the foreground, two sailors sit on a ship’s deck with their backs to us. A third sailor stands behind them, leaning back with his hands in his pockets. Above them, a gun extends outward. They are watching two ships anchored ahead, while clouds of smoke rise from the hills in the distance. The sky is filled with scattered barrage balloons—large balloons tethered to the ground, used as a defense against enemy aircraft. In the upper right corner of the image, the artist has added several smaller sketches of ships and sailors.

CuratorialText: image: British soldiers are in action on a German street, crouching and firing their guns among the rubble of bombed buildings. Two soldiers are lying on a Nazi flag bearing a swastika, the left of the two in the process of firing a Bren Gun. Four other infantrymen stand directly behind them to the left, two armed with rifles and one with a Bren Gun. Other British soldiers are visible in the middle distance.
persona3_adapted: British soldiers are shown in combat on a German street, crouching and firing their weapons amid the rubble of bombed buildings. Two soldiers lie on a Nazi flag marked with a swastika; the one on the left is in the middle of firing a Bren Gun—a type of light machine gun used by the British military. Four other infantrymen stand directly behind them to the left, two carrying rifles and one also armed with a Bren Gun. More British soldiers can be seen further down the street.

CuratorialText: image: two British Royal Army Medical Corps stretcher-bearers stand with their stretchers in a shallow trench in the foreground. The trench leads up to the entrance of an Advanced Dressing Station, marked with an ADS sign, which is housed in the bomb damaged remains of a large house in a ruined village. Other heavily bomb damaged buildings are visible in the background.
persona3_adapted: In the foreground, two stretcher-bearers from the British Royal Army Medical Corps stand with their stretchers in a shallow trench. The trench leads toward the entrance of an Advanced Dressing Station—a frontline medical post—marked by an ADS sign. This station is set up inside the bomb-damaged remains of a large house in a ruined village. In the background, other heavily damaged buildings are visible, underscoring the destruction of the surrounding area.

CuratorialText:
image: the interior of the designers' room in the Old Vic Theatre, Waterloo, London, after suffering the effects of an air raid. The windows have been blown out and internal walls damaged, with the room littered with disturbed papers, designs and a fallen mannequin. The window looks out roughly south towards Lambeth, with the roofs of buildings visible.
Label
Short text of working notes. Inscription verso reads: 'Designers Room - Old Vic Theatre September 1941'.
persona3_adapted:
The photograph shows the interior of the designers' room at the Old Vic Theatre in Waterloo, London, after it was damaged in an air raid. The windows have been blown out and the internal walls are damaged. The room is scattered with papers, design sketches, and a fallen mannequin. Through the window, the view looks roughly south toward Lambeth, where the rooftops of nearby buildings are visible.

The label includes a brief note, written by hand. On the back of the photograph, an inscription reads: 'Designers Room - Old Vic Theatre September 1941'.

CuratorialText: image: Against a gloomy sunset and upon a grassy mound stands a smoking but unmanned French 75mm field gun, facing left. Below running the length of the composition lies the detritus of battle: a broken limber, four German corpses and two dead mules.
persona3_adapted: Against a dim, sunset-lit sky, a French 75mm field gun sits abandoned on a grassy mound, still smoking but with no crew in sight, its barrel pointing left. Stretching across the bottom of the scene lies the wreckage left by battle: a shattered ammunition cart, four dead German soldiers, and two dead mules.

CuratorialText: image: A convoy at sea being bombarded by enemy aircraft. In the centre of the composition is a large tanker, which has black smoke emanating from it. Other ships can be seen to the left and right with aircraft visible above. Large splashes of water in the sea show several near-misses.
persona3_adapted: A convoy at sea comes under attack from enemy aircraft. At the center of the scene, a large tanker releases thick black smoke. Other ships appear on both sides, while planes fly overhead. Large splashes in the water indicate several bombs that have landed dangerously close.

CuratorialText: Powder paint painting, hinged in corners onto a single sheet of white board. Bottom left hinge has previously been cut to allow access to object number. Neatly torn along top and right edges, rather than cut. Previously folded in half vertically.
persona3_adapted: This painting was created using powdered pigment mixed with a binder, a technique known as powder paint. It is attached at the corners with hinges to a single sheet of white board. The hinge at the bottom left was previously cut to allow access to the object's identification number. The top and right edges appear neatly torn rather than cut. The work was also once folded in half vertically.

CuratorialText: image: eight internees stand behind a barbed wire fence at Hutchinson camp near Douglas on the Isle of Man. The word 'Parachutists' is on the wall behind them. Four of the men wear glasses and all but one of them appear to be wearing kippot, indicating their Jewish faith. The other man wears a bowler-style hat.
persona3_adapted: Eight internees are shown standing behind a barbed wire fence at the Hutchinson camp near Douglas on the Isle of Man. Behind them, the word 'Parachutists' is painted on a wall. Four of the men wear glasses, and all but one appear to be wearing kippot—small skullcaps that signify their Jewish faith. The remaining man wears a bowler-style hat.

CuratorialText: Acknowledged as one of the greatest landscape artists of all time, Joseph Mallord William Turner (1775–1851) knew Oxford extremely well. From childhood, he made numerous drawings and watercolours of the streets and colleges and the picturesque views surrounding the city. His celebrated oil painting of the High Street, Oxford is on display in the 19th-Century Art Gallery. To mark the 250th anniversary of J.M.W. Turner's birth on 23 April 1775, explore this selection of his depictions of Oxford from the Ashmolean's collections. The Museum's Western Art Print Room houses just over 100 watercolours and drawings by Turner, as well as many landscape etchings. There are lively studies of animals, birds and fish, as well as evocative views of places that the artist visited on his travels in Britain and Europe, from Margate to Venice.
persona3_adapted: Widely regarded as one of the greatest painters of natural scenery in history, Joseph Mallord William Turner (1775–1851) knew Oxford extremely well. From childhood, he created numerous drawings and watercolours—paintings made with water-based pigments—of the city's streets, colleges, and the charming views surrounding it. His famous oil painting of Oxford's High Street is now on display in the 19th-Century Art Gallery. To mark the 250th anniversary of Turner's birth on 23 April 1775, explore this selection of his depictions of Oxford from the Ashmolean's collections. The Museum's Western Art Print Room holds just over 100 watercolours and drawings by Turner, along with many landscape etchings—prints made from engraved metal plates. There are lively studies of animals, birds, and fish, as well as evocative views of places the artist visited on his travels across Britain and Europe, from Margate to Venice.

CuratorialText: In the second millennium BCE the island of Crete was home to a complex pre-Greek society known as ‘the Minoans’. When archaeologist Sir Arthur Evans started to excavate at the site of Knossos on Crete, in 1900, he frequently connected the things he found with ancient Cretan myths. He named the building at Knossos the ‘Palace of Minos’, after the mythical king of Crete, and connected it to the story of the Labyrinth. The Labyrinth was an ingenious maze commissioned by King Minos to imprison the ferocious Minotaur, a creature with the head of a bull and the body of a man. In the myth, the Minotaur is killed by Theseus, the famous semi-divine hero. Following Theseus's victory, he returned to Greece, and became king and the founder of Athens. The Cretan myths originated in stories which the Greek and Roman inhabitants of the city of Knossos would have told long after the Palace had been destroyed.
persona3_adapted: In the second millennium BCE—roughly four thousand years ago—the island of Crete was home to a sophisticated pre-Greek society known as the Minoans. When the archaeologist Sir Arthur Evans began digging at the site of Knossos on Crete in 1900, he often linked the objects he uncovered to ancient Cretan myths. He named the building at Knossos the "Palace of Minos," after the legendary king of Crete, and associated it with the story of the Labyrinth. According to the myth, the Labyrinth was a cleverly designed maze that King Minos ordered to be built in order to imprison the fierce Minotaur—a creature with the head of a bull and the body of a man. In the story, the Minotaur is killed by Theseus, a famous hero who was part divine. After his victory, Theseus returned to Greece, became king, and was later regarded as the founder of Athens. These Cretan myths originally came from stories that the Greek and Roman inhabitants of Knossos would have told, long after the Palace had fallen into ruin.

CuratorialText:
Landscape painting has a long tradition in East Asian art. However, landscape did not become an important feature of Japanese woodblock prints until the early 1800s. By this time, Japanese citizens were taking part in more outdoor sightseeing activities, such as temple visits and boat trips, and there was a growing demand for souvenir images. In the competitive world of the publishing industry, publishers were constantly trying to develop new products; landscape prints were an appealing novelty.

Technical factors also played an important part in the emergence of the landscape print. Particularly significant was the introduction of the new imported pigment Prussian Blue. Unlike earlier Japanese blues, which faded very quickly, this brilliant colour gave artists more freedom of expression, especially in the depiction of sky and water

Here we feature a selection of highlights from the master of landscape prints, Utagawa Hiroshige, as well as selected works by Hokusai and Kuniyoshi.
persona3_adapted:
Landscape painting has a long history in East Asian art. However, landscapes did not become a major subject in Japanese woodblock prints—a technique of printing images from carved wooden blocks—until the early 1800s. At that time, Japanese people were increasingly enjoying outdoor sightseeing activities, such as visiting temples and taking boat trips, which created a growing demand for souvenir images. In the competitive world of publishing, companies were always looking for new products to offer; landscape prints became an appealing and fresh option.

Technical developments also played a key role in the rise of landscape prints. One especially important factor was the introduction of a new imported pigment called Prussian Blue. Unlike earlier blue pigments used in Japan, which faded quickly, this vivid color gave artists more freedom to express themselves, particularly when depicting sky and water.

Here we present a selection of highlights from Utagawa Hiroshige, widely regarded as the master of landscape prints, along with selected works by Hokusai and Kuniyoshi.

CuratorialText:
The collection of antiquities includes internationally renowned archaeological collections from Prehistoric Europe, the Ancient Near East, Ancient Egypt and Sudan, Minoan Aegean, Mycenaean and Classical Greece, Ancient Cyprus, the Roman world, Medieval and later Europe, as well as the museum's Founding Collection and historic archives.

It also includes one of the oldest, largest and best-preserved collections of casts of Greek and Roman sculpture in the UK.
persona3_adapted:
The collection of antiquities features world-famous archaeological finds from prehistoric Europe, the ancient Near East, ancient Egypt and Sudan, the Minoan and Mycenaean civilizations of the Aegean, classical Greece, ancient Cyprus, the Roman world, medieval and later Europe, as well as the museum's original founding collection and historical archives.

It also holds one of the oldest, largest, and best-preserved collections of plaster casts—reproductions of Greek and Roman sculptures—in the United Kingdom.

CuratorialText: In our Eastern Art collections you will find sculpture, textiles, ceramics and paintings from the Islamic Middle East, China, Japan, Southeast Asia, India and the Himalayas.
persona3_adapted: In our Eastern Art collections, you will find sculptures, textiles, ceramics, and paintings from the Islamic Middle East, China, Japan, Southeast Asia, India, and the Himalayas.

CuratorialText: The Museum’s Heberden Coin Room is one of the leading international coin cabinets with particular strengths in the fields of Greek, Roman, Celtic, Byzantine, Medieval, Islamic and Chinese coinages. It also holds collections of paper money, tokens, jetons and commemorative art medals.
persona3_adapted: The Museum’s Heberden Coin Room is one of the world’s most important collections of coins, with especially strong holdings in Greek, Roman, Celtic, Byzantine, Medieval, Islamic, and Chinese coinage. It also includes collections of paper money, tokens, jetons (small, coin-like tokens used for counting or as gaming pieces), and commemorative art medals.

CuratorialText: The Western Art collections contain outstanding European fine and decorative arts from the Renaissance to the present day. They include Old Master and later paintings, drawings, watercolours, and prints; English and continental ceramics, sculpture, silver, watches, rings and musical instruments. The Ashmolean's exceptional print collection contains around 25,000 drawings and over 250,000 prints and many are accessible through our Western Art Print Room.
persona3_adapted: The Western Art collections feature exceptional European fine and decorative arts—works created for their beauty or practical elegance—from the Renaissance period to the present day. These include paintings, drawings, watercolours, and prints by Old Masters (major European artists working before the early 1800s) as well as later artists; English and European ceramics, sculpture, silver, watches, rings, and musical instruments. The Ashmolean's remarkable collection of prints holds around 25,000 drawings and over 250,000 printed works, many of which can be viewed in our Western Art Print Room.

CuratorialText: The painting resulted from an important early commission for two watercolours to be engraved for the annual calendar published by the University Press, the Oxford Almanack. They were so successful that Tuner eventually completed ten watercolours between 1798 and 1804. This work and one other were later painted in oils for the local print-seller and frame-maker, James Wyatt. Turner consulted Wyatt on the details of the architecture included in the view. He also introduced figures, members of the University and clergy, and some women ‘for the sake of colour’. The painting is one of the most fully documented of all Turner’s works because Wyatt kept his correspondence with the artist which was included in his posthumous sale. The original purpose of the commission was to have the design engraved. Wyatt settled on an oil painting, instead of a watercolour, half the size of Turner’s normal canvases, at a cost of 100 guineas. The painting was exhibited at the Royal Academy in 1812. When, in the 1830s, Turner was choosing views of Oxford for his greatest series of watercolours, he rejected the High Street. He felt that in this painting he had achieved an unparalleled view of technical mastery that he could not repeat.
persona3_adapted:
This painting began as part of an important early commission for two watercolours—paintings made with pigments mixed in water rather than oil—intended to be turned into engravings (prints made from carved surfaces) for the *Oxford Almanack*, the annual calendar published by the University Press. The works were so successful that Turner eventually completed ten watercolours for the project between 1798 and 1804. Later, this piece and one other were recreated in oil paints for James Wyatt, a local seller of prints and picture frames. Turner consulted Wyatt about the architectural details shown in the scene. He also added figures—members of the university and clergy, along with some women—‘for the sake of colour,’ meaning to enhance the visual effect rather than for narrative reasons.

This painting is one of the most thoroughly recorded of all Turner’s works, because Wyatt kept the letters the artist sent him, which were later included in the sale of Wyatt’s estate after his death. The original goal of the commission was to produce a design that could be engraved. Wyatt decided on an oil painting instead of a watercolour, half the size of Turner’s usual canvases, at a cost of 100 guineas (a significant sum at the time). The painting was shown at the Royal Academy in 1812. When, in the 1830s, Turner was selecting views of Oxford for his greatest series of watercolours, he chose not to include the High Street. He felt that in this earlier work he had achieved a level of technical skill that he could not surpass.

CuratorialText: Together with a St Francis (Christ Church, Oxford) and a Risen Christ (National Gallery of Wales), WA1850.13 and WA1850.13.1 come from a lost altar-piece by Granacci, painted around 1520. The four panels may have been placed in the pilasters. Granacci was a follower of Ghirlandaio who was particularly interested in the art of Fra Bartolomeo and Raphael.
persona3_adapted: Together with a painting of St Francis (at Christ Church, Oxford) and a Risen Christ (at the National Gallery of Wales), these two works come from a lost altar-piece—a large religious painting designed for a church altar—created by the artist Granacci around 1520. The four panels may have originally been set into the pilasters, the decorative vertical supports on either side of the altar. Granacci was a follower of the earlier painter Ghirlandaio, but he was especially drawn to the work of Fra Bartolomeo and Raphael, two other Renaissance artists.

CuratorialText: These are pinnacle panels that once belonged to the same unknown altar-piece, which may have been crowned with a central pinnacle panel showing Christ on the Cross. The artist was Florentine, working in a late Gothic manner and an attribution to Bartolomeo di Fruosino has recently been suggested. A painter and miniaturist, Bartolomeo was a pupil of Agnolo Gaddi.
persona3_adapted: These are the top sections of a larger, unknown altarpiece—a painted or carved religious panel placed behind an altar. The altarpiece may have originally been completed by a central top panel showing Christ on the Cross. The artist was from Florence, Italy, and worked in a style that followed the late Gothic tradition, which emphasized elegant, decorative forms and religious symbolism. Recently, experts have suggested that the work might be by Bartolomeo di Fruosino. Bartolomeo was both a painter and a miniaturist—an artist who creates small, detailed illustrations—and he studied under Agnolo Gaddi.

CuratorialText: As a nocturnal landscape and as a brilliantly structured composition, The Hunt in the Forest is a highly original painting. Hunting was an aristocratic pastime with its own rituals, but the idea of a hunt at night is playful and may be symbolic rather than realistic. In preparing this work, the artist Paolo di Dono first mapped out a grid on the panel's surface, fixing a central vanishing point to draw our gaze deep into the composition. In painting the subject, he incorporated details including the huntsmen's spears, the cut branches, logs and the water in the foreground as devices to bring focus to this space and to the receding forms of men and animals. Gold flecks in the foliage of the trees would have complemented the bright colours of the figures against the dark forest, and would have glistened and become animated when illuminated by candlelight. Paolo di Dono (1397-1475) was celebrated in his lifetime as a master of perspective, animals and landscape; his nickname, Uccello (‘Bird’), alludes to his depictions of the natural world. He was a versatile designer, working at times on mosaic and stained-glass commissions and all of these skills have been cleverly combined to create this masterpiece. The Hunt is what is known as a spalliera painting, which would have been mounted at shoulder height – whether as a backboard of a decorated chest or set into the wooden panelling of a room. The original client is unknown, but the painting was clearly intended for a Renaissance palace, perhaps in Urbino where Uccello worked for a time from 1465, or in Florence about 1470.
persona3_adapted: As a night-time landscape and a brilliantly organized composition, *The Hunt in the Forest* is a highly original painting. Hunting was an aristocratic pastime with its own traditions, but the idea of a hunt at night is playful and may be symbolic rather than realistic. In preparing this work, the artist Paolo di Dono first mapped out a grid on the panel’s surface, fixing a central vanishing point—the spot where parallel lines appear to meet in the distance—to draw our gaze deep into the composition. In painting the subject, he included details such as the huntsmen’s spears, the cut branches, logs, and the water in the foreground as tools to focus attention on this space and on the receding forms of men and animals. Gold flecks in the foliage of the trees would have complemented the bright colors of the figures against the dark forest, and would have glistened and seemed to move when lit by candlelight. Paolo di Dono (1397–1475) was celebrated in his lifetime as a master of perspective—the technique of creating an illusion of depth and space on a flat surface—as well as of animals and landscape; his nickname, Uccello (‘Bird’), refers to his depictions of the natural world. He was a versatile designer, working at times on mosaic and stained-glass projects, and all of these skills have been cleverly combined to create this masterpiece. *The Hunt* is what is known as a *spalliera* painting, a type that would have been mounted at shoulder height—whether as the backboard of a decorated chest or set into the wooden paneling of a room. The original client is unknown, but the painting was clearly intended for a Renaissance palace, perhaps in Urbino, where Uccello worked for a time from 1465, or in Florence around 1470.

CuratorialText: The portrait corresponds generally to a number of paintings and prints of Louix XIII of France on a prancing horse. From the type and handling of the paint, it seems to be either by or after a Flemish painter. The appearance of the king suggests a date of about 1622.
persona3_adapted: This portrait resembles several paintings and prints showing King Louis XIII of France riding a horse that is rearing up. Based on the painting style and the way the paint has been applied, it appears to be either the work of a Flemish painter or a copy made after one. The king’s appearance in the image suggests it was created around 1622.

CuratorialText: The Magdalen is recognisable by her jar of ointment, a reference to her traditional identification with the woman who anointed the feet of Christ and dried them with her hair (Luke 7: 36-50). Possibly cut from a larger composition, this small panel has been enlarged by being fitted into another and the figure extended. The artist may have worked in Parma in the early 17th century in the circle of Bartolomeo Schedoni.
persona3_adapted: The Magdalen can be identified by the jar of ointment she holds, which refers to the traditional belief that she was the woman who poured perfume on Christ’s feet and dried them with her hair, as described in the Gospel of Luke (7:36–50). This small painting may have originally been part of a larger scene; it was later enlarged by being set into another panel, and the figure was extended to fit. The artist likely worked in Parma in the early 1600s, within the artistic circle of Bartolomeo Schedoni.

CuratorialText: The landscape is reminiscent of Éragny. It may have been painted in the summer of 1893 or 1894, before Félix Pissarro left Éragny for Belgium. In: Jon Whiteley, Nineteenth-century French Paintings in the Ashmolean Museum, (Oxford: Ashmolean Museum 2022)
persona3_adapted: This landscape is reminiscent of Éragny, a village in northern France where the artist lived. It may have been painted in the summer of 1893 or 1894, before Félix Pissarro left Éragny for Belgium.

CuratorialText: A free copy, dating from the late 17th or early 18th century, of the fresco in one of the spandrels of the painted Loggia di Psiche in the Villa Farnesina, Rome. Raphael and his studio decorated this space in 1518-19 with scenes from the story of Cupid and Psyche. The spandrel from which WA1859.1 was copied may have been painted by Raphael's senior assistant, Giulio Romano. The copyist has altered the format to a square shape.
persona3_adapted: This is an independent copy, made in the late 1600s or early 1700s, of a section of a painted ceiling in Rome. The original was part of a series of wall paintings called the Loggia di Psiche, located in the Villa Farnesina. In 1518–19, Raphael and his workshop filled this space with scenes from the myth of Cupid and Psyche. The specific section copied here—a curved, triangular space between arches—may have been painted by Raphael’s senior assistant, Giulio Romano. The copyist changed the original shape into a square.

CuratorialText: Three of the objects in this still life are found elsewhere in Renoir’s late work: the silver beaker appears alongside a sugar bowl and knife in a small still life said to have been painted at Cagnes in 1910 and again in a similar composition without the knife, also said to have been painted in 1910; the bronze-coloured child on the column was included in a small study, dated c.1905 by the Daubervilles. The faience double salt, a type produced in quantity in the nineteenth century at Desvres in the Pas-de-Calais and at Quimper in Brittany, appears in at least four other sketches. A disembodied image of the same figure, seen from the back, hovers in upper right, probably a reflection in a mirror. Renoir, who worked as a painter at a ceramic manufactory in his early days, seems to have owned a number of pieces of faience of this popular type⁵ and used a variety of coloured and decorated ceramic containers in his flower paintings.
persona3_adapted: Three of the objects in this still life also appear in other works from Renoir’s later career. The silver beaker, for example, is shown with a sugar bowl and knife in a small still life said to have been painted at Cagnes in 1910, and again in a similar composition without the knife, also reportedly from 1910. The bronze-colored child figure on the column appears in a small study dated around 1905 by the Daubervilles. The faience double salt—a type of glazed earthenware salt holder produced in large numbers during the nineteenth century in Desvres (in the Pas-de-Calais region) and Quimper (in Brittany)—can be found in at least four other sketches. A detached image of the same figure, seen from behind, floats in the upper right area of the painting, likely a reflection in a mirror. Renoir, who began his career painting at a ceramics factory, seems to have owned several pieces of this popular type of faience and often used colorful, decorated ceramic containers in his flower paintings.

CuratorialText: Although damaged, this portrait of an unknown woman is of fine quality and has affinities with the work of Andrea in the 1520s. Apart from a short stay in Rome c.1516, Andrea spent most of his career in Siena and in Florence, where he collaborated with his brother Raffaello Piccinelli.
persona3_adapted: Although damaged, this portrait of an unknown woman is of fine quality and shares stylistic similarities with the work of Andrea from the 1520s. Apart from a short stay in Rome around 1516, Andrea spent most of his career in Siena and in Florence, where he collaborated with his brother Raffaello Piccinelli.

CuratorialText: After 1739, Ruysch painted mainly small-scale paintings of bouquets and posies, inscribed with her age, as here. The arrangement of curving stems, full, rounded roses and a number of smaller flowers looks back to the work of Willem van Aelst. This painting was stolen from the Museum in March 1949 and has not been recovered
persona3_adapted: After 1739, Ruysch focused mainly on small paintings of flower bouquets and posies, often noting her age on the canvas, as she does here. The composition—with its curving stems, full, rounded roses, and several smaller flowers—echoes the style of the earlier artist Willem van Aelst. This work was stolen from the Museum in March 1949 and has never been recovered.

CuratorialText: This panel comes from the predella of a large altar-piece of 1433-35 for S. Niccolò in Cafaggio, Florence, whose parts are now scattered in various collections. The Virgin and Child with Four Angels in the centre was flanked by pairs of saints (Sts Benedict and Nicholas of Bari and St John the Baptist and an Evangelist, probably St Matthew). The predella showed scenes from the life of St Nicholas, of which five are known: the birth of the saint; pilgrims at the shrine of the saint; St Nicholas resuscitating three youths; the saint providing dowries for three sisters (three gold balls, his traditional attribute); and banishing the storm. The panels probably originally had quatrefoil frames. Bicci based the design of WA1850.26 on Gentile da Fabriano's Quaratesi altar-piece of 1425 for S. Niccolò oltr'Arno.
persona3_adapted: This panel originally formed part of the lower section, known as the predella, of a large altarpiece created between 1433 and 1435 for the church of San Niccolò in Cafaggio, Florence. The altarpiece is now dismantled, with its various sections held in different collections. At the center of the altarpiece was a scene of the Virgin and Child with Four Angels, flanked on either side by pairs of saints: Benedict and Nicholas of Bari on one side, and John the Baptist and an Evangelist (likely Matthew) on the other. The predella depicted episodes from the life of Saint Nicholas, of which five are still known: his birth; pilgrims visiting his shrine; the saint bringing three youths back to life; his providing dowries for three sisters (symbolized by three gold balls, his traditional identifying emblem); and his calming a storm. The panels were probably originally set within frames shaped like four-leaf clovers, known as quatrefoil frames. The artist, Bicci, based the design of this panel on an altarpiece by Gentile da Fabriano, created in 1425 for the church of San Niccolò oltr'Arno.

CuratorialText: This is clearly the portrait of a professional sculptor, shown in his studio with some of his work on the shelf behind. He holds a terracotta figure of a saint, perhaps the Virgin Mary. The painting has been tentatively attributed to the circle of the de Critz family.
persona3_adapted: This is clearly the portrait of a professional sculptor, shown in his studio with some of his work on the shelf behind him. He holds a clay figure of a saint, possibly the Virgin Mary. Art historians have tentatively suggested that this painting was created by someone in the circle of the de Critz family—meaning it was likely produced by an artist connected to that well-known family of painters, rather than by a specific, identified member.

CuratorialText: The pages of calligraphy, all by one hand, mostly include kanshi (Chinese poems), and are signed with variations of the name Sessō Keigi. Two of the poems bear dates of Meiji 10 (1878) and Meiji 12 (1880), and a few identify the source from which the poem came or where the poem was composed. If the calligraphy was done at the same time as the paintings, this album would be an early work by Sobun before he began participating in the national and international expositions.
persona3_adapted: The pages of calligraphy—all written by the same person—mostly contain kanshi, or Chinese-style poems. They are signed with slight variations of the name Sessō Keigi. Two of the poems are dated to Meiji 10 (1878) and Meiji 12 (1880), and a few others note where the poem came from or where it was composed. If the calligraphy was created around the same time as the paintings, this album would be an early work by Sobun, before he began showing his art in national and international exhibitions.

CuratorialText: The genesis of this painting began in 1857, when it was exhibited as The Mother's Grave. The original composition, known from a drawing in the Ashmolean, showed the boy desolate over the grave of his mother. The landscape was begun in the summer of 1856 in the old churchyard at Chingford, Essex. Around 1862, Hughes altered the background and added the figure of the sister, for which the artist's wife, Tryphena, posed. The detail is used to reinforce the pathos of the subject, so that the ephemeral nature of spider's webs, dew drops, dog roses and dandelion seeds all emphasise the theme of transience. The boy's loss is retold in the lamb separated from its mother by the barrier of the tomb.
persona3_adapted: This painting first appeared in 1857 under the title *The Mother's Grave*. The original version, which we know from a drawing now held at the Ashmolean Museum, showed a young boy alone and grieving at his mother's grave. The landscape background was started in the summer of 1856 in the old churchyard at Chingford, Essex. Around 1862, the artist, Hughes, changed the background and added the figure of the sister, for which his wife, Tryphena, posed. Every detail in the scene is chosen to deepen the emotional impact: the fragile spider's webs, the dew drops, the wild roses, and the drifting dandelion seeds all point to the fleeting, fragile nature of life. The boy's loss is echoed in the image of a lamb separated from its mother by the barrier of the tomb.

CuratorialText: John Tradescant the Younger (1608-1662) succeeded his father as Keeper of the King's Gardens at Oatlands Palace and further enriched the family collection of specimens of natural and man-made curiosity. He is shown here with his friend, Roger Friend, a brewer (for which Zythepsa is the Greek) from Lambeth, who may have contributed the shells to Tradescant's museum.
persona3_adapted: John Tradescant the Younger (1608-1662) took over his father's role as the head gardener for the King's gardens at Oatlands Palace. He continued to expand the family's collection of unusual natural objects and human-made artifacts. In this portrait, he is shown with his friend Roger Friend, a brewer from Lambeth—the Greek word for brewing, Zythepsa, is included in the painting. Friend may have donated the shells that were part of Tradescant's museum.

CuratorialText: As one of the leading members of post-war generation of St Ives artists, Lanyon was primarily interested in the representation of landscape. This work epitomises the tension between abstraction and figuration in an otherwise carefully descriptive work.
persona3_adapted: As a key figure among the artists working in St Ives after the Second World War, Lanyon was mainly concerned with how to depict the landscape. This piece captures the push and pull between abstract forms and recognizable imagery, even though the work is otherwise carefully detailed and descriptive.

CuratorialText: Bundi, Rajasthan, c.1760-70. Gouache with gold on paper. Attended by a drowsy maid, a young noble couple sleep in close embrace on a palace terrace, sheltered by a shamiana or awning. The young prince’s sword and arrows lie close to hand.
persona3_adapted: Bundi, Rajasthan, around 1760-70. Painted with opaque watercolor and gold on paper. A young noble couple sleeps in a close embrace on a palace terrace, attended by a drowsy maid and sheltered by a ceremonial canopy or awning. The prince’s sword and arrows lie nearby, ready at hand.

CuratorialText: This painting was described as A Nun Playing a Piano when it appeared at Christie’s in 1919 and when it was sold by Colnaghi’s to Mrs Weldon in 1936, no doubt because the sitter is wearing a long veil. However, there is no reason otherwise to suppose the sitter is a nun. It is almost certainly a portrait of Desirée-Marie Ribot, known as Louise, the artist’s daughter whom he often used as a model. Born in 1857, her youthful appearance supports the evidence of style which suggests a date in the late 1860s or early 1870s before Ribot’s touch became thicker and more painterly. The dark background and domestic theme recall the work of François Bonvin (1817–1887), an early supporter of Ribot and of Alphonse Legros (1837–1911), who shared Bonvin’s interest in Spanish art.
persona3_adapted: This painting was labeled *A Nun Playing a Piano* when it appeared at Christie’s in 1919 and again when it was sold by Colnaghi’s to Mrs. Weldon in 1936—likely because the woman wears a long veil. However, there is no other reason to think she is a nun. It is almost certainly a portrait of Desirée-Marie Ribot, known as Louise, the artist’s daughter, whom he often used as a model. Born in 1857, her youthful appearance supports what the style suggests: a date in the late 1860s or early 1870s, before Ribot’s brushwork became thicker and more visibly textured. The dark background and domestic scene recall the work of François Bonvin (1817–1887), an early supporter of Ribot, and of Alphonse Legros (1837–1911), who shared Bonvin’s interest in Spanish art.

CuratorialText: The inscription on this painting reads ‘Wind from the west is blowing through layers of curtains on doors, the proud [chrysanthemum] are falling but their beauty remains. In fading moonlight I [stayed] with these flowers while they cooled, versing through this cool evening in autumn.’
persona3_adapted: The inscription on this painting reads: “Wind from the west is blowing through layers of curtains on doors; the proud chrysanthemums are falling, but their beauty remains. In the fading moonlight, I stayed with these flowers while they cooled, composing verses through this cool autumn evening.”

CuratorialText: Sinnott is well known for his complex figurative compositions. He often deals with the human figure in a context. Although the same fluid brush-strokes can be seen in the background and in the figures, the two wrestling men can be distinguished by a lighter touch and a slight variation in the use of paint. The underpainting, in places, appears through the upper layers, accentuating the sense of speed in the painting process.
persona3_adapted: Sinnott is well known for his complex compositions featuring the human form. He often places the human figure within a specific setting or situation. While the same flowing brushstrokes appear in both the background and the figures, the two wrestling men stand out through a lighter touch and a subtle difference in how the paint is applied. In some areas, the initial layer of paint (the layer applied first, before the final colors) shows through the top layers, which highlights the sense of speed and spontaneity in how the painting was made.

CuratorialText: The only securely documented painting by the artist is an altar-piece of 1447 now in the Museo Civico, Padua. A follower of Michele Giambono, Francesco may have collaborated with Antonio Vivarini in Venice. Together with WA1850.10, this panel was once part of an unknown altar-piece possibly for a church in Padua or Venice. St Catherine, of royal birth, wears a crown and carries her martyr's palm; the wheel refers to earlier attempts at her martyrdom.
persona3_adapted: The only painting that can be confidently attributed to this artist is an altar-piece from 1447, now housed in the Museo Civico in Padua. Francesco was a follower of Michele Giambono, meaning he was influenced by or worked in the style of this earlier painter, and he may have collaborated with Antonio Vivarini in Venice. Along with WA1850.10, this panel was once part of an unknown altar-piece, possibly intended for a church in Padua or Venice. Saint Catherine, who was of royal birth, is shown wearing a crown and carrying a palm branch—a traditional symbol of her martyrdom. The wheel beside her refers to earlier attempts to kill her for her Christian faith.

CuratorialText: The grief-stricken figures of the Virgin and St John the Evangelist make this small panel a powerful devotional image; it may have been the right wing of a portable diptych, although no hinge-marks are now visible. The anonymous artist was very close to Jacopo di Cione (to whom WA1921.3 has also been attributed) and would have trained in the large workshop of Orcagna, the leading artist in mid 14th-century Florence
persona3_adapted: The sorrowful figures of the Virgin Mary and St John the Evangelist make this small panel a powerful image meant for private prayer. It may have been the right half of a two-panel painting that could be folded shut for travel, though no marks from hinges remain today. The unknown artist worked very closely with Jacopo di Cione (another painting in the collection, WA1921.3, has also been credited to him) and would have learned his craft in the large workshop of Orcagna, the leading artist in Florence during the mid-1300s.

CuratorialText: Veronese's celebrated Last Supper of 1573 for the refectory of SS Giovanni e Paolo, Venice, caused him to be examined by the Inquisition on the grounds of blasphemy; he changed the title to The Feast in the House of Levi, a subject which allowed for much colourful detail (Luke 6: 27-30). Numerous copies are known of this large painting, now in the Accademia, Venice. WA1855.159 is a reduced copy of fine quality by a mid-18th-century Venetian artist, probably Francesco Fontebasso.
persona3_adapted: Veronese’s famous painting of the Last Supper, created in 1573 for the dining hall of a monastery in Venice, led to him being questioned by the Inquisition on charges of blasphemy. In response, he changed the title to *The Feast in the House of Levi*, a biblical subject that allowed for more lively and colorful details (Luke 6:27–30). Many copies of this large work—now housed in the Accademia museum in Venice—are known to exist. This particular version is a smaller, high-quality copy made by a mid-18th-century Venetian artist, likely Francesco Fontebasso.

CuratorialText: A companion to Opie's portrait of the sitter's husband, Francis Smith (1764-1815), WA1934.422. Both paintings were exhibited at the Royal Academy in 1800.
persona3_adapted: A companion piece to Opie's portrait of the sitter's husband, Francis Smith (1764-1815), WA1934.422. Both paintings were shown at the Royal Academy in 1800.

CuratorialText: Painted in 1886 at a time when both Camille and Lucien Pissarro were working with the pointillist technique. The view, taken from a bend on the path leading from the Pré du Chêne across the Epte from Éragny to Bazincourt, includes the spire of Saint-Martin in Éragny in the centre with the sixteenth-century brick manor house on the right. The Epte runs along the line of trees that crosses the composition. In 1887 Lucien painted an almost identical view in watercolour and bodycolour. The buildings seen from this stretch of the path are now largely concealed by the trees. However, it is clear that in both works Lucien has greatly enlarged the size of the church and manor and suppressed the farm buildings that lie in front of the manor. His father, as Lucien must have known, had painted the same view from the same spot in 1884, using the more improvised style of the early 1880s.
persona3_adapted: Painted in 1886, when both Camille and Lucien Pissarro were using the pointillist technique—a method of applying small, distinct dots of color that blend in the viewer's eye. The scene, taken from a bend in the path that leads from the Pré du Chêne across the Epte River from Éragny to Bazincourt, shows the spire of Saint-Martin in Éragny at the center, with a sixteenth-century brick manor house on the right. The Epte River runs along the line of trees that cuts across the composition. In 1887, Lucien painted an almost identical view using watercolor and bodycolor (a type of opaque watercolor). The buildings visible from this part of the path are now largely hidden by trees. However, it is clear that in both works, Lucien has greatly enlarged the size of the church and manor, while leaving out the farm buildings that stand in front of the manor. His father, as Lucien must have known, had painted the same view from the same spot in 1884, using the more spontaneous style of the early 1880s.

CuratorialText: The painting shows a distant view of the village of Lauris in the Vaucluse, with its distinctive eighteenth-century château and garden terrace on the left and the mountains of the Petit Luberon in the background. Guigou was seated or standing on one of the many sandbanks interspersed with the channels of the river Durance as it passes between Lauris and La Roque-d’Anthéron during the dry season. As in most of the artist’s Provençal landscapes, the air is clear and the sunlight strong. The example of Corot or Daubigny may have had some influence on the choice of the panoramic view that Guigou seems to have favoured above all others, but his manner of painting is distinctive. The sky, sand and river are thinly brushed and enlivened with spots of impasto and small, short strokes with the tip of the brush in the artist’s characteristic manner.
persona3_adapted: The painting offers a distant view of the village of Lauris in the Vaucluse region of southern France. On the left, we see its distinctive eighteenth-century château and garden terrace, while the mountains of the Petit Luberon rise in the background. The artist, Guigou, was likely seated or standing on one of the many sandbanks that appear between the channels of the Durance River during the dry season, as it flows between Lauris and La Roque-d’Anthéron. As in most of Guigou’s landscapes of Provence, the air is clear and the sunlight intense. The choice of a wide, sweeping view—a type of composition Guigou seems to have preferred—may have been influenced by artists like Corot or Daubigny. However, his painting technique is distinctive. The sky, sand, and river are painted with thin layers of color, enlivened by small, thick dabs of paint (a technique called impasto) and short, delicate strokes made with the tip of the brush, in a style that is uniquely his own.

CuratorialText: This painting, which is a cosmic landscape with Daoist undertones, was produced in 1988 for Khoan Sullivan (1919-2003) who wished for something with a predominantly blue colour. The inscription reads ‘To Mr. and Mrs. Sullivan. Affectionately, Chu Teh-Chun 1988.’
persona3_adapted: This painting, a landscape that evokes a sense of the universe and carries subtle references to Daoist philosophy, was created in 1988 for Khoan Sullivan (1919-2003), who had requested a work with a mostly blue color scheme. The inscription on the piece reads, “To Mr. and Mrs. Sullivan. Affectionately, Chu Teh-Chun 1988.”

CuratorialText: One of Lucien's most extrovert works, painted in June 1925 and illustrating the broom in full flower at Le Brusq.
persona3_adapted: One of Lucien's most outwardly expressive works, painted in June 1925 and showing the broom plant in full bloom at Le Brusq.

CuratorialText: Joseph Warton (1722-1800) was educated at Oriel College, Oxford, before entering the church. He was Headmaster of Winchester College between 1766 and 1793 and an accomplished poet. This portrait was painted and exhibited at the Royal Academy in 1777, the year in which Warton was also elected to 'The Club'. He was the brother of Thomas Warton, the Poet Laureate, whose portrait Reynolds also painted (Trinity College, Oxford; Mannings 2000, no. 1838).
persona3_adapted: Joseph Warton (1722-1800) studied at Oriel College, Oxford, before becoming a clergyman. He served as Headmaster of Winchester College from 1766 to 1793 and was also a skilled poet. This portrait was painted and shown at the Royal Academy in 1777, the same year Warton was chosen to join 'The Club' (a famous London dining society for writers, artists, and intellectuals). He was the brother of Thomas Warton, who held the title of Poet Laureate (the official poet of the British monarchy), and whose portrait was also painted by Reynolds (Trinity College, Oxford; Mannings 2000, no. 1838).

CuratorialText: The influence of Vuillard is strongly marked. There is a less finished version of the same composition on the back
persona3_adapted: Vuillard's influence is clearly visible. On the reverse of the canvas, there is a less polished version of the same scene.

CuratorialText: Bollongier was one of the few painters of the first half of the 17th century in Haarlem who specialised in painting flowers. His work seems to have been influenced by the Bosschaert school but with an added naturalism in the arrangement of the flowers. The glass container in this painting was a favourite accessory. It appears first in a work of 1627 and for the last time in a flower piece of 1639 (Rijksmuseum, Amsterdam).
persona3_adapted: Bollongier was one of the few painters working in Haarlem during the first half of the 17th century who focused specifically on painting flowers. His work appears to have been influenced by the Bosschaert school—a group of artists known for their detailed flower paintings—but he brought a more natural, lifelike quality to how the flowers are arranged. The glass container shown in this painting was a favorite object of his. It first appears in a work from 1627 and is seen for the last time in a flower painting from 1639, now held at the Rijksmuseum in Amsterdam.

CuratorialText: When Pharaoh ordered all new-born Hebrew sons to be killed, Moses's mother put him in an ark of bulrushes in the Nile. His sister stayed to watch while Pharaoh's daughter came to the river with her maidens and found the child (Exodus 2: 2-4). WA1950.169 is striking for its emotional intensity, with the focus on Moses's grief-stricken parents. The joyful scene of the finding of Moses was a popular one, whereas this subject is far less common (Poussin earlier treated it c. 1627). Painted in 1654 for Jacques Stella, WA1950.169 was greatly admired by contemporaries for the artist's learned treatment of the subject, his skill in painting reflections and the beauty of the landscape. Pharoah's city is based on Pirro Ligorio's reconstruction of some of the most famous buildings of ancient Rome. A sphinx accompanied by a river-god and cornucopia identifies the Nile. Different explanations, based on Poussin's antiquarian interests and wide-ranging visual references, have been proposed for the significance of the pipes of Pan that hang from a tree behind this group. Two painted copies are recorded in the 17th century; other copies and variations are known (including a tapestry version of the right side) and three engraved versions were made, the first by Stella's niece, Claudine Bouzonnet Stella, in 1672.
persona3_adapted: When Pharaoh ordered that all newborn Hebrew sons be killed, Moses’s mother placed him in a basket made of reeds and set it in the Nile River. His sister stayed to watch as Pharaoh’s daughter came to the river with her attendants and discovered the child (Exodus 2: 2-4). This painting, WA1950.169, is notable for its emotional power, focusing on Moses’s grieving parents. The joyful scene of Moses being found was a popular subject in art, but this particular moment is much rarer (the artist Poussin had treated it earlier, around 1627). Painted in 1654 for Jacques Stella, the work was greatly admired by people of its time for the artist’s learned approach to the story, his skill in painting reflections, and the beauty of the landscape. Pharaoh’s city is based on Pirro Ligorio’s reconstruction of some of the most famous buildings of ancient Rome. A sphinx, accompanied by a river god and a horn of plenty (a cornucopia), identifies the Nile. Various explanations, rooted in Poussin’s interest in ancient artifacts and his wide-ranging visual references, have been proposed for the meaning of the pipes of Pan that hang from a tree behind this group. Two painted copies from the 1600s are known; other copies and variations exist (including a tapestry version of the right side), and three engraved versions were made, the first by Stella’s niece, Claudine Bouzonnet Stella, in 1672.

CuratorialText: The brother of Masaccio, Lo Scheggia was an eclectic artist who painted numerous works for domestic settings. This type of decorative, ceremonial subject is often found on cassone panels. Although the picture is much abraded, it has sufficient affinities with the mature work of Lo Scheggia to make the attribution convincing.
persona3_adapted: Lo Scheggia, the brother of the more famous painter Masaccio, was a versatile artist who created many works intended for private homes. Scenes like this one—decorative and ceremonial in nature—were commonly painted on the front panels of cassone, a type of ornate wedding chest used in Renaissance households. Although the painting’s surface is significantly worn and damaged over time, it still shows enough similarities to Lo Scheggia’s later, more developed style to make the attribution to him seem reliable.

CuratorialText: Lemon studied in Rome and in Paris with Carolus-Duran, but spent his early years in California. He exhibited at the Royal Academy from 1880 and came into contact with Giovanni Costa and his circle of Etruscans in Italy. This slight sketch from nature probably dates from the last years of the 19th century.
persona3_adapted: Lemon studied in Rome and in Paris under Carolus-Duran, but spent his early years in California. He showed his work at the Royal Academy starting in 1880 and became acquainted with Giovanni Costa and his group of artists known as the Etruscans in Italy. This small, informal study done directly from nature likely dates from the final years of the 19th century.

CuratorialText: The subject, the flat and dry handling and the monumental quality of the figures show Shannon's debt to Puvis de Chavannes. The painting has been dated to c.1910 and is a variation on one of the artist's favourite themes.
persona3_adapted: The subject, the flat and dry way the paint is applied, and the monumental quality of the figures show Shannon's debt to Puvis de Chavannes. The painting has been dated to around 1910 and is a variation on one of the artist's favourite themes.

CuratorialText: Views in and around the village of Berck in northern France appear in many of the artist's paintings from 1870 until his death in 1898. This view across the dunes is precisely dated. The unbroken horizon and solitary figure in the foreground emphasize the windswept emptiness of the site.
persona3_adapted: Views of the village of Berck in northern France and its surroundings appear in many of the artist's paintings from 1870 until his death in 1898. This particular scene, looking across the sand dunes, is marked with a specific date. The uninterrupted horizon line and the lone figure in the foreground draw attention to the vast, windswept emptiness of the location.

CuratorialText: Traditionally but wrongly said to be a portrait of Antonio Stradivari, A863a is a portrait of a musician or instrument-maker of the late 16th century and identifications of Claudio Monteverdi (1567-1643) and Gasparo da Salo (1540-1609) have been proposed.
persona3_adapted: Though it has often been described—incorrectly—as a portrait of the famous violin maker Antonio Stradivari, this painting (numbered A863a) actually shows a musician or instrument maker from the late 1500s. Some scholars have suggested it might depict the composer Claudio Monteverdi (1567–1643) or the instrument maker Gasparo da Salò (1540–1609), but these identifications remain uncertain.

CuratorialText: During his later years, Calvert produced many small works in thin oil colours on paper, the restrained colours and muted harmonies evocative of a dream world. They are generally peopled with figures from Greek mythology. Painted on a pink ground.
persona3_adapted: In his later years, Calvert created many small works using thin oil paints on paper. The subdued colors and soft, muted combinations of shades suggest a dreamlike world. These pieces typically feature figures from Greek mythology and are painted on a pink base layer.

CuratorialText: Traditionally attributed to Guido Reni as the Head of a Sibyl, WA1926.9 was recently identified as a copy after Cerrini. The same expressive head appears in his altar-piece of The Holy Family with Sts Catherine of Alexandria and Agnes painted for San Carlo alle Quattro Fontane, Rome (consecrated in 1646) and now in the convent there.
persona3_adapted: Though this painting was long thought to be by Guido Reni and titled *Head of a Sibyl* (a prophetess from ancient mythology), it has recently been recognized as a copy after the artist Giovanni Domenico Cerrini. The same expressive face appears in Cerrini’s altarpiece—a large religious painting placed behind an altar—titled *The Holy Family with Sts Catherine of Alexandria and Agnes*. That work was created for the church of San Carlo alle Quattro Fontane in Rome (consecrated in 1646) and now hangs in the convent attached to that church.

CuratorialText: One of Linnell's most important religious paintings, this was exhibited at the Royal Academy in 1835 and sold to the Art Union for the purposes of engraving in 1838. It was based on sketches made in 1829, when Linnell was closely associated with the 'Ancients' at Shoreham. Samuel Palmer was the model for the disciple on the right. The source is Luke 24: 13-35.
persona3_adapted: One of Linnell's most important religious paintings, this work was shown at the Royal Academy in 1835 and sold to the Art Union in 1838 so that it could be reproduced as an engraving. The painting was based on sketches made in 1829, when Linnell was closely associated with a group of artists known as the 'Ancients' in Shoreham. The figure of the disciple on the right was modeled after Samuel Palmer. The subject comes from the Bible, Luke 24: 13-35.

CuratorialText: Design is an impulse that unifies us across time and culture. This installation unifies two traditionally disparate collecting fields, fashion and textiles with decorative and industrial arts to better understand what underlies our motivations and capacity for design. Ensembles from the Iris Apfel Rare Bird of Fashion collection celebrate the exuberant remixing and inventive styling of one of the world’s most prominent fashion icons, while constellations of unique and culturally significant works of design, fashion, and textiles explore distinctive and resourceful forms of creative expression. A recent gallery highlight is the display of 38 vibrant and flamboyant ensembles from the collection of Boston-based entrepreneur and fashion icon Yolanda Cellucci. Yolanda’s, based in Waltham, Massachusetts, was a hotspot for lavish evening and wedding ensembles and that became a household name throughout the region, and a shorthand for glitz and glamor in the late 20th century. Cellucci generously donated 57 works of fashion and accessories to PEM in 2021, along with archival photographs and materials related to the legacy of her bridal shop.
persona3_adapted: Design is a creative drive that connects people across different eras and cultures. This exhibition brings together two areas that are usually kept separate in museums: fashion and textiles on one side, and decorative and industrial arts on the other. By combining them, we can better understand what inspires us to design and what we are capable of creating. Outfits from the Iris Apfel Rare Bird of Fashion collection celebrate the bold mixing of styles and inventive personal expression of one of the world’s most famous fashion figures. At the same time, groups of unique and culturally meaningful works of design, fashion, and textiles explore creative approaches that are both distinctive and resourceful. A recent highlight of the gallery is the display of 38 vibrant and flamboyant outfits from the collection of Yolanda Cellucci, a businesswoman and fashion icon based in Boston. Her store, Yolanda’s, located in Waltham, Massachusetts, was a popular destination for lavish evening wear and wedding dresses. It became a well-known name across the region and a symbol of glamour and sparkle in the late 20th century. In 2021, Cellucci generously donated 57 fashion pieces and accessories to PEM, along with archival photographs and materials that tell the story of her bridal shop’s legacy.

CuratorialText: Born in Greenbush, New York in 1844, Lewis became the first sculptor of Black and Indigenous (Mississauga) descent to achieve international recognition. Beginning her career in Boston in 1863, she traveled to Rome in 1866 to join the leading American sculptors of her generation, breaking international, racial and gender barriers. "Sometimes the times were dark and the outlook was lonesome, but where there is a will, there is a way,” Lewis recalled in 1878. “That is what I tell my people whenever I meet them, that they must not be discouraged, but work ahead until the world is bound to respect them for what they have accomplished.” Following her death in 1907, Lewis’ legacy endured in Black communities, yet her contribution to American sculpture has largely been underrecognized. Some of her great masterpieces were rediscovered decades later, while others remain lost today. Her vivid, naturalistic stone sculptures feature abolitionists and social reformers, engage with Indigenous identity and give new life to religious and mythological figures.
persona3_adapted: Born in Greenbush, New York in 1844, Lewis became the first sculptor of Black and Indigenous (Mississauga) descent to earn international recognition. She began her career in Boston in 1863 and moved to Rome in 1866 to join the leading American sculptors of her time, breaking barriers related to nationality, race, and gender. "Sometimes the times were dark and the outlook was lonesome, but where there is a will, there is a way," Lewis recalled in 1878. "That is what I tell my people whenever I meet them, that they must not be discouraged, but work ahead until the world is bound to respect them for what they have accomplished." After her death in 1907, Lewis’ legacy continued within Black communities, yet her contribution to American sculpture has largely gone unrecognized. Some of her greatest works were rediscovered decades later, while others remain lost today. Her vivid, lifelike stone carvings depict abolitionists and social reformers, explore Indigenous identity, and breathe new life into religious and mythological figures.

CuratorialText: Home to 3.7 million people, 85 species of animals, 32,000 species of insects and 2 billion migratory birds, the boreal forest spans nearly the entire Northern Hemisphere, just below the Arctic Circle, and makes up one of the world’s last remaining stretches of true wilderness. Discover the biodiversity and global importance of our northernmost forests through first-person stories, commissioned objects, interactive experiences and exquisite photography and videography. The boreal forest is critical to the health of our entire planet. It helps regulate Earth’s climate, is the world’s largest land biome, is a critical water reservoir and holds two to three times as much carbon as tropical forests.
persona3_adapted: Home to 3.7 million people, 85 species of animals, 32,000 species of insects, and 2 billion migratory birds, the boreal forest stretches across nearly the entire Northern Hemisphere, just below the Arctic Circle. It is one of the last truly wild places left on Earth. Explore the rich variety of life and the global significance of these northernmost forests through firsthand accounts, specially commissioned objects, interactive activities, and stunning photography and video. The boreal forest is essential to the health of our entire planet. It helps regulate Earth’s climate, is the largest land-based ecosystem in the world, serves as a crucial source of fresh water, and holds two to three times more carbon than tropical forests.

CuratorialText: Salem’s connections to the Declaration of Independence center around Ezekiel Russell, a local printer who played a key role in publishing and disseminating the Declaration in 1776. This exhibition coincides with the 250th anniversary of the American Revolution as well as the 400th anniversary of the European settlement of Salem. Works on view in the intimate James Duncan Phillips Trust Gallery include two of the earliest broadside editions of the Declaration of Independence alongside Revolutionary-era manuscripts, newspapers, pamphlets and broadsides that showcase the nation’s founding values of democracy: freedom, liberty and equality.
persona3_adapted: Salem’s connection to the Declaration of Independence centers on Ezekiel Russell, a local printer who played a key role in publishing and distributing the Declaration in 1776. This exhibition marks the 250th anniversary of the American Revolution and the 400th anniversary of European settlement in Salem. In the intimate James Duncan Phillips Trust Gallery, visitors will find two of the earliest single-sheet printed versions of the Declaration of Independence, along with Revolutionary-era manuscripts, newspapers, pamphlets, and other printed materials that highlight the nation’s founding democratic values: freedom, liberty, and equality.

CuratorialText: These films honor the lesser-known stories of Salem and offer a behind-the-scenes look at what makes this city so remarkable. Produced over the course of more than seven years by Perry Hallinan, Joe Cultrera and many other filmmakers for presentation at the Salem Film Fest, these short films span topics from breakfast at Red’s to the annual Black Picnic at Salem Willows. For longtime Salem residents, these stories might spark nostalgia; for new residents and tourists, they illuminate Salem’s vibrant communities, bygone businesses and traditions that local filmmakers have long worked to record and preserve. Beyond the Broom showcases the vibrant community of Salem, one with an identity that extends beyond its witch trials history. This exhibition is part of the 2026 celebration of Salem 400+, which commemorates the quadricentennial of the city’s settlement while honoring the preexisting Indigenous community. The initiative aims to engage the Salem community and ensure a vibrant and sustainable city for generations to come.

This exhibition is part of the 2026 celebration of Salem 400+, which marks the 400th anniversary of the city’s founding while also honoring the Indigenous communities who lived here long before. The initiative aims to engage the Salem community and help ensure a lively and sustainable city for generations to come.

CuratorialText: Explore several hundred fascinating objects inside the historic hall, representing a sampling of the many collections that were displayed in the museum during its earliest years. This installation gives voice to early collectors, trading partners and museum visitors about these objects — with some accounts extracted from records in the museum’s vast archive — and shares perspectives from a range of people living today. These captivating stories and works that reflect local and global history, as well as the impact of maritime trade, recenter East India Marine Hall as the heart of the museum. From its inception, this space and the objects in it have elicited empathy, curiosity and wonder and transported visitors to places around the world. This project has been made possible in part by a grant from the National Endowment for the Humanities: Democracy demands wisdom. Major support is provided by Mr. Ulf B. Meibaum III, Jean Verbridge, and Connie and Stan Grayson.

CuratorialText: Renowned for its expansive holdings of decorative arts and paintings, PEM’s American art collection spans four centuries of creative expression in the United States. Rooted in our historic holdings that celebrate the rich and diverse artistic and cultural heritage of Essex County, Massachusetts, the collection has continued to grow to embrace a broader representation of the art of this country. The artists represented in this Seamans gallery use a variety of materials — wood, metal, glass, clay, paint, fiber and more — to convey their creative ambitions. Many of the artistic practices and decorative inspirations behind these works have long histories, with their origins traceable to Europe, Asia and the Americas. These examples of formal perfection, domestic utility and luxury, and innovative twists on cultural traditions tell a larger story of our global connectedness. Together, these works tell compelling stories of American life and the ongoing cultural exchanges between the people of the region, the country and the wider world. Located in the Seamans Gallery.
persona3_adapted: Known for its extensive collection of decorative objects and paintings, PEM’s American art collection covers four centuries of creative work in the United States. The collection began with pieces that celebrate the rich and diverse artistic and cultural heritage of Essex County, Massachusetts, and has since expanded to include a wider range of American art. The artists featured in this Seamans gallery work with many different materials—wood, metal, glass, clay, paint, fiber, and more—to express their creative ideas. Many of the artistic methods and decorative influences behind these pieces have long histories, with roots in Europe, Asia, and the Americas. These objects—some showing perfect craftsmanship, others made for everyday use or luxury, and some offering fresh takes on cultural traditions—together reveal a larger story of how we are all connected across the globe. As a group, these works tell powerful stories about American life and the ongoing exchange of ideas and culture among people in this region, the country, and the wider world. Located in the Seamans Gallery.

CuratorialText: Persian and Turkish architecture, textiles and miniature paintings inspire the precise, stylized floral forms that compose Anila Quayyum Agha’s sculptural chamber of light and shadow. This luminous installation located in the Wheatland Family gallery provides an opportunity to contemplate the differences and commonalities that shape our lives and relationships. Originally from Lahore, Pakistan, and now living in the US, Agha is acutely attuned to the social codes that inform the lives of Muslim women and all immigrants. She describes this work as her effort to create a sense of how women can reclaim and safely open up private space to welcome others. Located in the Wheatland Family Gallery.
