This glowing installation, on display in the Wheatland Family Gallery, invites visitors to reflect on the differences and commonalities that shape our lives and relationships. Originally from Lahore, Pakistan, and now living in the United States, Agha is deeply aware of the unwritten social rules that affect the lives of Muslim women and all immigrants. She describes this work as her attempt to show how women can reclaim and safely open up private space to welcome others.

CuratorialText:
Merchants across the globe went to great lengths to acquire these spectacular commodities. Now known as Asian export art, these objects connected societies and created a complex global economy that continues to shape our world to this day.

PEM’s Asian Export Art collection, foremost in the world, explores cross-cultural exchange as a catalyst for creativity and celebrates the interplay of commerce and creative expression. The gallery features more than 200 works of art made in diverse media by artists in China, Japan, and South Asia. These transcultural objects demonstrate the beauty and ingenuity that can be created through blending artistic traditions, materials, and technologies. Porcelain, textiles, tea, ivory, and silver were the focus of intensive trade activity between Asia and the rest of the world. This installation in the Sean M. Healey Family gallery also addresses the uncomfortable truth that many of these works of art were originally purchased with profits derived from the illegal opium trade. During the 1800s, millions of Indian and Chinese lives were devastated by opium, a foreshadowing of today’s opioid crisis.
persona3_adapted:
Merchants around the world went to extraordinary lengths to obtain these remarkable goods. Today known as Asian export art—objects made in Asia specifically for foreign markets—these items connected different societies and helped create a complex global economy that still influences our world.

PEM’s Asian Export Art collection, the most important of its kind anywhere, examines how cross-cultural exchange can spark creativity and celebrates the relationship between trade and artistic expression. The gallery displays more than 200 works created in a wide range of materials by artists from China, Japan, and South Asia. These objects, shaped by the meeting of different cultures, show the beauty and inventiveness that can emerge when artistic traditions, materials, and techniques are combined. Porcelain, textiles, tea, ivory, and silver were at the center of intense trade between Asia and the rest of the world. This exhibition in the Sean M. Healey Family gallery also confronts a difficult reality: many of these artworks were originally bought with money earned from the illegal opium trade. During the 1800s, millions of people in India and China suffered devastating harm from opium, a crisis that foreshadowed today’s opioid epidemic.

CuratorialText: This exhibition explores America as a place where disparate viewpoints are brought together: America as both longed for and greatly revered. Previously on view in the context of PEM's 2019 Jacob Lawrence: The American Struggle exhibition, Bethany Collins, America: A Hymnal presents a solo exhibition of Collins’ art as an immersive experience. Explore 100 different versions of the popular patriotic anthem “My Country ‘Tis of Thee,” rewritten between the 18th and 20th centuries in support of various American causes. Versions of the lyrics are bound together chronologically in Collins’ artist book, where she burns away the musical notes with a laser — chronicling an idea of America through the history of one song’s varied verses. A range of these powerful voices and expressions are heard throughout the gallery in a sound piece, what Collins calls, “a familiar chaos of dissenting versions of what it means to be … American bound together.” The gallery is wallpapered with a newly commissioned work of flocked flower silhouettes. Each flower contains a meaning as the artist uses floriography, the symbolic language of flowers, to relay messages of hope, longing and loss to further illustrate the evolution and complexity of American experiences. In addition to the artwork on view, a study center offers deeper dives into Collins' artistic approaches and historical sources. Through interactives, PEM collection objects and media components, Bethany Collins, America: A Hymnal inspires us to activate our own civic mindset. Located in the Jurrien Timmer Gallery.
persona3_adapted: This exhibition explores America as a place where very different viewpoints come together: a country that is both deeply desired and greatly respected. Previously shown as part of PEM’s 2019 exhibition *Jacob Lawrence: The American Struggle*, *Bethany Collins, America: A Hymnal* now presents Collins’ work as a single-artist show designed to be an immersive experience. Here, you can explore 100 different versions of the popular patriotic song “My Country ‘Tis of Thee,” which were rewritten between the 1700s and 1900s to support various American causes. These different lyrics are bound together in chronological order in Collins’ artist book, where she uses a laser to burn away the musical notes—creating a record of an idea of America through the history of one song’s many changing verses. Throughout the gallery, a sound piece plays a range of these powerful voices and expressions, which Collins describes as “a familiar chaos of dissenting versions of what it means to be … American bound together.” The gallery walls are covered with a newly commissioned wallpaper featuring silhouettes of flowers in a raised, velvety pattern. Each flower carries a meaning, as the artist uses floriography—the symbolic language of flowers—to convey messages of hope, longing, and loss, helping to show how American experiences have evolved and grown more complex. In addition to the artwork, a study center offers deeper exploration into Collins’ creative methods and historical sources. Through hands-on activities, objects from PEM’s collection, and media components, *Bethany Collins, America: A Hymnal* encourages us to activate our own sense of civic engagement. Located in the Jurrien Timmer Gallery.

CuratorialText: Encompassing everyday objects and the fine arts, the museum’s Japanese collection is distinguished for its range, from paintings and sculpture to decorative arts and costumes and textiles. Spanning Hokkaido in the North to Okinawa in the South, and prehistory to the present, these objects illuminate the varied artistic and cultural traditions of Japan. Toward the end of the 19th century, Edward Sylvester Morse, director of the Peabody Academy of Science (a precursor to PEM), became one of the first Western scholars to visit modern Japan after it opened to travelers. Morse was highly influential in encouraging American interest in Japanese art and culture, and helped create this remarkable Japanese collection.
persona3_adapted: The museum’s Japanese collection spans both everyday objects and fine art, and is notable for its wide variety—from paintings and sculptures to decorative items, costumes, and textiles. Covering regions from Hokkaido in the north to Okinawa in the south, and ranging from prehistoric times to the present day, these objects reveal the rich artistic and cultural traditions of Japan. In the late 1800s, Edward Sylvester Morse, then director of the Peabody Academy of Science (an early version of today’s PEM), became one of the first Western scholars to visit modern Japan after the country opened to foreign travelers. Morse played a key role in sparking American interest in Japanese art and culture, and helped build this remarkable collection.

CuratorialText: From the late Joseon dynasty (1392–1910) to today, PEM’s exceptional collection explores art, culture, and life in Korea during a time of significant transition and global connection. PEM’s early Korean collection was primarily formed by Edward Sylvester Morse, director of the Peabody Academy of Science (a predecessor organization to PEM), who proactively pursued Korean acquisitions during his tenure from 1880 to 1914. Among his most significant acquisitions from this period is a set of eight Korean musical instruments played at the 1893 World’s Columbian Exposition. PEM’s connection with Korea was also strengthened by Yu Kil-Chun (1856-1914), the reformist scholar and politician who came to the U.S. as a member of the first Korean delegation and lived near Salem for nearly two years. Yu Kil-Chun and other early travelers between the U.S. and Korea donated many meaningful and foundational works to the museum’s collection. PEM’s Yu Kil-Chun Gallery of Korean Art and Culture brings to life the compelling stories of these early pioneers, presented alongside the museum’s renowned Korean textile collection, exquisite 19th-century paintings — including the Welcoming Banquet of the Governor of Pyeongan screen — and inspiring contemporary works by Nam June Paik (1932–2006) and other Korean and Korean American artists.
persona3_adapted: From the late Joseon dynasty (1392–1910) to the present day, PEM’s outstanding collection explores the art, culture, and daily life of Korea during a period of major change and growing global connections. The museum’s early Korean collection was largely built by Edward Sylvester Morse, director of the Peabody Academy of Science (an earlier organization that led to PEM), who actively sought out Korean objects during his time at the museum from 1880 to 1914. Among his most important acquisitions from this era is a set of eight Korean musical instruments that were played at the 1893 World’s Columbian Exposition in Chicago. PEM’s ties to Korea were also strengthened by Yu Kil-Chun (1856–1914), a forward-thinking scholar and politician who came to the United States as part of the first official Korean delegation and lived near Salem for nearly two years. Yu Kil-Chun and other early travelers between the U.S. and Korea donated many meaningful and essential works to the museum’s collection. PEM’s Yu Kil-Chun Gallery of Korean Art and Culture brings to life the compelling stories of these early pioneers, displayed alongside the museum’s renowned collection of Korean textiles, exquisite 19th-century paintings—including a large folding screen titled *Welcoming Banquet of the Governor of Pyeongan*—and inspiring contemporary works by Nam June Paik (1932–2006) and other Korean and Korean American artists.

CuratorialText: PEM’s Maritime art collection, which is the finest in the country, frames the sea as an enduring source of opportunity as well as peril, a force that inspires creativity and innovation, and encourages engagement with the wider world. This installation located in the Byrne Family gallery offers a global perspective on our creative response to the sea, from a 17th-century brass Pakistani astrolabe to Valerie Hagerty’s monumental Shipwrecked Armoire with Barnacles (2012) and artworks that reflect Salem’s rich history of maritime trade and exploration. Immersive digital media amplify compelling stories that lay behind unassuming objects, like a calendar stick from 1803 whose carved notches record the long days that Rhode Island native, James Drown, spent shipwrecked and left for dead on Tristan da Cunha, a remote speck island in the South Atlantic.
persona3_adapted: PEM’s Maritime art collection—the finest of its kind in the United States—presents the sea as both a lasting source of opportunity and danger, a force that sparks creativity and innovation, and a call to connect with the wider world. This exhibition in the Byrne Family gallery takes a global look at how people have creatively responded to the sea, from a 17th-century brass astrolabe (an early device used for navigation) made in Pakistan, to Valerie Hagerty’s large-scale work *Shipwrecked Armoire with Barnacles* (2012), and artworks that reflect Salem’s deep history of maritime trade and exploration. Immersive digital media bring to life the powerful stories behind seemingly ordinary objects—such as an 1803 calendar stick, whose carved notches mark the long days that James Drown, a native of Rhode Island, spent shipwrecked and abandoned on Tristan da Cunha, a remote island in the South Atlantic.

CuratorialText: Bringing two extraordinary collections of Native American and American art together for the first time in our institution's history, this long-term installation celebrates artistic achievements across time, space, and worldviews. Located in the Nancy and George Putnam Gallery and Barbara Weld Putnam Gallery, the 250 artworks on display span in time from 10,000 years ago to the present, and demonstrate a range of voices, modes of expression, cultures, and media including: sculpture, paintings, textiles and costumes, furniture, decorative arts, works on paper, installations, video, and a re-envisioned period room. The gallery also includes original documents and objects from the Salem witch trials, one of the most iconic dramas in American history. Between May 1692 and March 1693, the court sentenced 25 innocent people in the Salem region to death. The new installation will be responsive to these urgent times by giving our public an opportunity to grapple with the links, continuities, and disjunctions of our complex histories in America in order to shape a more connected and empathetic present and future. By rethinking America’s histories, visitors are asked to consider how we might envision a better future together.

CuratorialText: These figures, from disparate cultures around the world and across time, embody power as both a fundamental social dynamic and a part of our innate wiring to respond to figures and faces. Curved niches encourage visitors to engage with each artwork, decluttering the field of view and focusing attention. Poetically concise labels heighten the emotional tenor of the experience. Located in the Louise Du Pont Crowninshield Gallery.
persona3_adapted: These figures, drawn from different cultures around the world and from various historical periods, represent power both as a basic force in society and as something rooted in our natural instinct to react to human forms and faces. Curved alcoves invite visitors to focus on each artwork individually, reducing visual distractions and sharpening attention. Brief, poetic descriptions deepen the emotional impact of the experience. This installation is located in the Louise Du Pont Crowninshield Gallery.

CuratorialText: The exhibition starts at “A is for Always Indigenous” to acknowledge the Native communities who have lived for millennia on the land where the museum now sits. It ends with Z is for Zoology and coincides with the return to the galleries of a leatherback turtle specimen captured in 1885, a favorite of longtime visitors. Salem is a city with many stories of local, national and international significance. Alexander Graham Bell completed the first successful long-distance telephone call from Salem in 1877. Parker Brothers produced Monopoly here. And in 2013, President Obama signed legislation recognizing the city as the birthplace of the United States National Guard. Using selections from PEM’s vast collection, Salem Stories features more than 100 works, including paintings, sculpture, textiles, decorative arts, photographs, natural history specimens, manuscripts, posters, books, eyewitness accounts, and even a murder weapon. The A–Z structure creates an accessible and entertaining way to engage with Salem’s history, from past to present day. This is an evolving story, one that will continue to unfold throughout the life of the exhibition. Located in the Peggy and Lloyd Hilborn Gallery.

It ends with “Z is for Zoology,” timed to the return of a leatherback turtle specimen—caught in 1885 and a longtime favorite of returning visitors—to the galleries. Salem is a city rich with stories of local, national, and global importance. In 1877, Alexander Graham Bell made the first successful long-distance phone call from here. Parker Brothers created the board game Monopoly in Salem. And in 2013, President Obama signed a law recognizing the city as the birthplace of the United States National Guard. Drawing from PEM’s vast collection, *Salem Stories* brings together more than 100 works, including paintings, sculptures, textiles, decorative objects, photographs, natural history specimens, manuscripts, posters, books, firsthand accounts, and even a murder weapon. The A-to-Z format offers an accessible and engaging way to explore Salem’s history, from the past to the present day. This is an evolving story—one that will continue to develop throughout the exhibition’s run. Located in the Peggy and Lloyd Hilborn Gallery.

CuratorialText: Bridging myth with social and political history, the Chester and Davida Herwitz Gallery tells the story of nation-building and self-discovery through works by India's most celebrated artistic geniuses of the 20th century. Following independence from British rule in 1947, artists in India aimed to uncover a visual language that was uniquely Indian in inspiration to convey their experiences, struggles, ambitions and dreams. The Prashant H. Fadia Foundation and Deshpande Foundation Gallery features a selection of objects from PEM's extensive collection of historical material from India. Focused primarily on the 19th century, the gallery considers India's long and complex history of foreign occupation, and its troubling impact on the representation of Indian people in art. Featuring some of the earliest objects to come to PEM, including unfired clay sculpture, mica paintings and Kalighat paintings, the gallery considers and questions the timeless tropes of India that persist even today.
persona3_adapted: Bridging myth with social and political history, the Chester and Davida Herwitz Gallery tells the story of nation-building and self-discovery through works by India's most celebrated artistic geniuses of the 20th century. Following independence from British rule in 1947, artists in India aimed to uncover a visual language that was uniquely Indian in inspiration to convey their experiences, struggles, ambitions and dreams. The Prashant H. Fadia Foundation and Deshpande Foundation Gallery features a selection of objects from PEM's extensive collection of historical material from India. Focused primarily on the 19th century, the gallery considers India's long and complex history of foreign occupation, and its troubling impact on the representation of Indian people in art. Featuring some of the earliest objects to come to PEM, including unfired clay sculpture, paintings on mica (a thin, translucent mineral), and Kalighat paintings (a style of watercolor painting on paper that originated in 19th-century Calcutta), the gallery considers and questions the timeless stereotypes of India that persist even today.

CuratorialText: This diverse selection of contemporary glass represents the work of more than 40 of the most influential glass artists from the 20th century to today. Donated to PEM in 2022 by New York-based collectors Carl and Betty Pforzheimer, this collection is remarkable for its range of forms, scale and techniques in glass. The Pforzheimers’ gift significantly expands PEM’s noted historic glass collection while celebrating the extraordinary aesthetic possibilities that arise from the combination of sand and fire.

Donated to PEM in 2022 by New York collectors Carl and Betty Pforzheimer, this collection stands out for its wide variety of shapes, sizes, and methods used in working with glass. The Pforzheimers’ gift greatly strengthens PEM’s well-known historical glass collection, while also celebrating the remarkable artistic potential that comes from combining sand and fire.

CuratorialText: The Pod is part of PEM's Dotty Brown Art & Nature Center, which investigates our place in nature through contemporary art, memorable objects and interactive experiences. Designed for visitors of all ages, the family-friendly center features installations, changing exhibitions, hands-on displays and art-making spaces. Grow your awareness of nature, try on new ways of being, and tap into your own creativity. Discover a life-size wire tree by sculptor Elizabeth Keithline modeled on a live tree from PEM’s campus. Watch dancer Laura Kathrein move as different animals and embody local habitats. And create your own imaginary creature inspired by real animals and fantastical beasts from around the globe. What creative sense will be sparked in you on your visit to The Pod? What comes to mind when you think of a pod? A pea pod, an iPod or maybe a futuristic orb? Jane Winchell, the Sarah Fraser Robbins Director of the Art & Nature Center and curator of The Pod, describes her idea of a pod and what you’ll encounter when you visit our new multisensory exhibition space. There is something for everyone here, ranging from fabulous, nature-inspired contemporary and historic artworks to stations where you can explore nature up close or make your own art. Families with young children will discover the Wonder Zone which includes activity bins, a light table and picture books just for them

CuratorialText: This extraordinary series of events between June 1692 and March 1693 led to the deaths of 25 innocent women, men and children who were wrongfully convicted of crimes. More than 300 years later, the personal tragedies and grievous wrongs that occurred still provoke us to reflect and reckon with the experiences of those involved. Learn the true story of this tragedy as told through the voices and with the possessions of those directly involved. This ongoing installation tells this story through court documents and authentic historic objects presented as tangible fragments directly tied to people in Salem and nearby communities in the late 17th century. A handwritten petition, a carved loom, a walking stick — each illuminates an aspect of individuals who lived through Salem’s witch trials and serves as a reminder of the real people impacted by these harrowing events. The Salem Witch Trials 1692 is organized by the Peabody Essex Museum. This exhibition is made possible by Carolyn and Peter S. Lynch and The Lynch Foundation. We thank Jennifer and Andrew Borggaard, James B. and Mary Lou Hawkes, Chip and Susan Robie, and Timothy T. Hilton as supporters of the Exhibition Innovation Fund. We also recognize the generosity of the East India Marine Associates of the Peabody Essex Museum.

A handwritten petition, a carved loom, a walking stick—each item sheds light on a different aspect of the individuals who lived through Salem’s witch trials and serves as a reminder of the real people impacted by these terrifying events. *The Salem Witch Trials 1692* is organized by the Peabody Essex Museum. CuratorialText: The artist researched the museum’s vast and varied holdings, diving deep to find unexpected connections across time, cultures, and materials. Platacis turned her findings into 210 canvas stencils—all drawn and cut by hand with X-Acto blades. After applying the stencils to the walls, she and her assistant used a variety of spray-paint and graffiti techniques, including drop shadows, high contrast, and layering, to apply color to the walls and give dimensionality and life to her forms. Organic forms and curvilinear lines emerged as unifying design motifs that speak to the natural world. As you move deeper into these galleries, floral forms give way to enchanting fauna and larger-than-life presentations of familiar and unexpected objects. Platacis’ labor-intensive contemporary approach to painting connects to the artistry and skill embedded within the historical objects in PEM’s collection, creating a fascinating interplay between artistic practice then and now. What discoveries await? Located in the H.A. Crosby Forbes and Pamela Cunningham Copeland galleries.
persona3_adapted: The artist explored the museum’s vast and diverse collection, searching for surprising links across different time periods, cultures, and materials. She turned what she found into 210 stencils made of canvas—each one drawn and cut by hand using a precision knife. After placing the stencils on the walls, she and her assistant used a range of spray-paint and graffiti methods, such as adding shadows, strong contrasts, and overlapping layers, to bring color and a sense of depth and life to her shapes. Organic, flowing forms and curved lines became the main visual themes, echoing the natural world. As you move further into these galleries, floral shapes give way to captivating animals and oversized depictions of both familiar and surprising objects. Platacis’s hands-on, time-intensive approach to painting echoes the craftsmanship and skill found in the historical objects from the museum’s collection, creating a fascinating conversation between artistic methods from the past and present. What will you discover? This exhibition is located in the H.A. Crosby Forbes and Pamela Cunningham Copeland galleries.

CuratorialText: As one of the 20th century’s most critically acclaimed and genre-blurring artists, Noguchi created a remarkable design legacy encompassing sculpture, lighting, furniture, ceramics and designs for public spaces such as gardens, playgrounds and plazas. Organized by the High Museum of Art in Atlanta, Isamu Noguchi: "I am not a designer" shines a light on the artist’s limitless creativity and social consciousness. Discover the evolution of Noguchi’s design work, particularly the way he created environments through theatrical set design, furniture and inclusive public spaces — all of which were intentionally designed to improve the way people lived. Many of his designs remain incredibly popular, such as the celebrated coffee table and Akari light sculptures that you may recognize in stores today. As Noguchi once said, “Everything is sculpture. Any material, any idea without hindrance born into space, I consider sculpture.” Noguchi also excelled at working collaboratively and forming close relationships with other creative professionals, particularly with modern dancer and choreographer Martha Graham, American architect R. Buckminster Fuller and Romanian sculptor and painter Constantin Brâncuși. These fruitful creative relationships are also highlighted within the exhibition.
persona3_adapted: As one of the 20th century’s most celebrated artists who mixed different styles and forms, Noguchi created an extraordinary body of design work that includes sculpture, lighting, furniture, ceramics, and public spaces like gardens, playgrounds, and plazas. Organized by the High Museum of Art in Atlanta, the exhibition *Isamu Noguchi: "I am not a designer"* highlights the artist’s boundless imagination and his concern for social issues. The show traces how Noguchi’s design work evolved over time, especially his approach to shaping environments—through stage sets for theater, furniture, and public spaces designed to be open and welcoming to everyone. All of these were created with the goal of improving how people lived. Many of his designs remain widely popular today, including the well-known coffee table and the Akari light sculptures you might recognize in stores. As Noguchi once said, “Everything is sculpture. Any material, any idea without hindrance born into space, I consider sculpture.” Noguchi also worked closely with other creative professionals, forming strong partnerships—especially with modern dancer and choreographer Martha Graham, American architect R. Buckminster Fuller, and Romanian sculptor and painter Constantin Brâncuși. These fruitful collaborations are also featured in the exhibition.

CuratorialText: In the nearly two decades since PEM featured the crowd-pleasing exhibition Origami Now!, contemporary origami has continued to expand into exciting new styles of expression and invention. This multigenerational exhibition in the Dotty Brown Art & Nature Center highlights origami as not just an exciting art form, but also an important influence on new innovations in science, engineering, math and more. See works from leading origami artists, including Michael G. LaFosse, one of the world’s top origami masters and a Massachusetts resident. In addition to showcasing masterworks of origami, PEM will highlight what is emerging in contemporary origami and design, ranging from incredibly complex artistic models and folding nano structures, to origami-inspired emergency shelters. Investigate origami-inspired breakthroughs in robotics, medicine and sustainability. Interactive displays and video tutorials will give visitors of all ages hands-on opportunities to investigate and participate in the artistic process of origami.
persona3_adapted: In the nearly two decades since PEM presented the popular exhibition *Origami Now!*, contemporary origami has continued to evolve into exciting new forms of creative expression and innovation. This exhibition, which brings together artists from different generations, is located in the Dotty Brown Art & Nature Center. It highlights origami not only as a compelling art form, but also as an important influence on new developments in science, engineering, mathematics, and other fields. See works by leading origami artists, including Michael G. LaFosse, one of the world’s top origami masters and a Massachusetts resident. Alongside masterpieces of origami, PEM will showcase what is emerging in contemporary origami and design—from highly complex artistic models and folded nano-scale structures, to emergency shelters inspired by origami techniques. Explore origami-inspired breakthroughs in robotics, medicine, and sustainability. Interactive displays and video tutorials will give visitors of all ages hands-on opportunities to explore and take part in the creative process of origami.

CuratorialText: View iconic contemporary artworks that reach back into millennia-old art practices. The Stars We Do Not See: Australian Indigenous Art is the largest exhibition of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander art ever presented in North America, representing the incredible breadth and depth of Contemporary Indigenous Australian art. The exhibition charts watershed moments in Australian Indigenous art, celebrating a vivid history of creativity that predates the arrival of Europeans by many thousands of years. The exhibition features nearly 200 artworks by more than 130 artists, among them several undisputed masterworks from the National Gallery of Victoria’s collection. Many of these significant works have never before left the country. The Stars We Do Not See is curated especially to introduce North American audiences to customary forms and styles, such as the conceptual map paintings of the Central and Western Desert Regions (sometimes colloquially referred to as “dot paintings”), ochre on bark paintings from far Northern Australia and ambitious experimental weavings. The exhibition also presents powerful works in neon, video and photography that acknowledge and innovate upon tradition.
persona3_adapted: View iconic contemporary artworks that draw inspiration from artistic traditions stretching back thousands of years. *The Stars We Do Not See: Australian Indigenous Art* is the largest exhibition of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander art ever presented in North America, showcasing the remarkable range and depth of contemporary Indigenous Australian art. The exhibition traces key turning points in Australian Indigenous art, celebrating a vibrant creative history that existed long before Europeans arrived. It features nearly 200 artworks by more than 130 artists, including several widely recognized masterpieces from the National Gallery of Victoria’s collection. Many of these important works have never before left Australia. *The Stars We Do Not See* has been specially curated to introduce North American audiences to traditional forms and styles—such as the conceptual map paintings from the Central and Western Desert regions (sometimes called “dot paintings”), paintings made with natural earth pigments on bark from far northern Australia, and ambitious experimental weavings. The exhibition also presents powerful works in neon, video, and photography that both honor and build upon tradition.

CuratorialText: Come and experience the liveliness of a drinking party, the opulence of a royal wedding and poetic evocation of spring on a delicate dish. Discover plants and animals, myths and symbols and decipher the Chinese character for "Double Happiness." With more than 30 highlights from the museum's wide-ranging Chinese collection spanning 3,000 years, this exhibition celebrates China's artistic achievements crystallized in seasonal festivals, religious ceremonies and celebrations. Located in the Yin Yu Tang Interpretive Gallery.

Explore plants and animals, myths and symbols, and learn the meaning behind the Chinese character for "Double Happiness." Featuring more than 30 standout pieces from the museum’s extensive Chinese collection, which spans 3,000 years, this exhibition celebrates China’s artistic accomplishments as they appear in seasonal festivals, religious ceremonies, and celebrations. The exhibition is located in the Yin Yu Tang Interpretive Gallery.

CuratorialText:
Thomas Mifflin (1744-1800) and his wife, Sarah Morris Mifflin (1747?-1790), were the only Philadelphians painted by John Singleton Copley, the greatest artist in the American colonies prior to the Revolution. Mifflin was an ardent patriot and by the time this portrait was made, had established himself as a successful merchant; later he rose to the rank of major general in the Continental Army, and was elected the first governor of Pennsylvania after the United States achieved independence. Sarah Morris, who married Thomas in 1767, was an accomplished, witty, and supportive partner. In the portrait, Sarah is weaving a decorative fringe on a portable loom, which may symbolize the couple's endorsement of the colonists' boycott of English goods.
Famous as one of Copley's finest works soon after it was painted, this portrait shows the artist at the height of his powers. Born in Boston in 1738 into a poor immigrant family, Copley was self-taught. He developed a highly finished style that rendered the features, costumes, and settings of his subjects with remarkable accuracy. Copley always kept a keen eye on his competition, and he may have been inspired to paint both Sarah and Thomas Mifflin on a single canvas by his contemporary Charles Willson Peale, who had recently completed a group portrait of the Philadelphia Cadwalader family that was admired for its portrayal of family unity and affection. Here, Copley depicts not only the features and costumes of his sitters with his famed skill, but creates an image of marriage as an affectionate, equal partnership--an innovative concept in American portraiture at the time.
persona3_adapted:
Thomas Mifflin (1744-1800) and his wife, Sarah Morris Mifflin (1747?-1790), were the only residents of Philadelphia painted by John Singleton Copley, widely considered the most accomplished artist in the American colonies before the Revolution. Mifflin was a passionate supporter of American independence, and by the time this portrait was created, he had already built a successful career as a merchant. He later rose to the rank of major general in the Continental Army and was elected the first governor of Pennsylvania after the United States won its independence. Sarah Morris, who married Thomas in 1767, was an accomplished, witty, and supportive partner. In the portrait, Sarah is weaving a decorative fringe on a small, portable loom—a detail that may symbolize the couple’s support for the colonists’ boycott of British goods.

Recognized as one of Copley’s finest works soon after it was painted, this portrait shows the artist at the peak of his abilities. Born in Boston in 1738 into a poor immigrant family, Copley taught himself to paint. He developed a highly polished style that captured the faces, clothing, and surroundings of his subjects with remarkable precision. Copley always kept a close watch on his rivals, and he may have been inspired to paint both Sarah and Thomas Mifflin together in a single canvas by his contemporary Charles Willson Peale. Peale had recently completed a group portrait of the Philadelphia Cadwalader family, which was praised for how it conveyed family closeness and affection. In this work, Copley not only renders his subjects’ features and clothing with his famous skill, but also creates an image of marriage as a warm, equal partnership—a fresh and unusual idea in American portraiture at the time.

CuratorialText: Better known as a patriot during the American Revolution, Paul Revere was the most prolific and accomplished silversmith in late 1700s Boston. His shop produced domestic and ecclesiastical objects for clients belonging to the same civic, military, political, and religious organizations as Revere. The teapot’s inverted pear shape and engraved ornament of C-scrolls, foliage, and shells epitomizes the Rococo style of his early career. Equally renowned as an engraver of bookplates, trade cards, magazine illustrations, and political prints, Revere probably executed this decoration himself. John Singleton Copley’s famous 1768 portrait of Revere (Museum of Fine Arts, Boston) depicts the silversmith with engraving tools, holding a teapot with the same low, domed cover and slightly pointed, pinecone finial as this example. Could they be one and the same?
persona3_adapted: Better known as a patriot during the American Revolution, Paul Revere was also the most productive and accomplished silversmith in Boston during the late 1700s. His workshop created objects for both domestic and church use, for clients who belonged to the same civic, military, political, and religious groups as Revere himself. The teapot’s inverted pear shape and engraved decoration—featuring curving C-shaped scrolls, leaves, and shells—is a perfect example of the Rococo style, a highly ornamental artistic movement popular in Europe and America during his early career. Revere was equally famous as an engraver of bookplates, business cards, magazine illustrations, and political prints, and he likely executed this decoration himself. John Singleton Copley’s famous 1768 portrait of Revere (Museum of Fine Arts, Boston) shows the silversmith holding engraving tools and a teapot with the same low, domed lid and slightly pointed, pinecone-shaped knob on top as this example. Could they be one and the same?

CuratorialText: Human rights and social issues often figure in Pippin’s work, but this painting is particularly overt in its treatment of racism. Clouds hover over a hooded member of the Ku Klux Klan and a burly white man holding a noose. The two menacing figures stand opposite the Statue of Liberty, here painted brown instead of green. At the center, Mr. Prejudice drives a wedge into a symbolic V for victory, segregating white and black machinists and servicemen. Pippin includes himself as one of the soldiers, wearing a World War I uniform, with his wounded right arm hanging at his side.
persona3_adapted: Human rights and social issues often appear in Pippin’s work, but this painting is especially direct in its treatment of racism. Dark clouds hang over a hooded member of the Ku Klux Klan and a large white man holding a noose. These two threatening figures stand opposite the Statue of Liberty, which is painted brown instead of its usual green. At the center, a figure called Mr. Prejudice drives a wedge into a symbolic V for victory, splitting apart white and Black factory workers and soldiers. Pippin includes himself among the soldiers, wearing a World War I uniform, with his wounded right arm hanging at his side.

CuratorialText: While the bed chambers of chic early Philadelphia houses often featured a high chest (for storing textiles; 1957-129-1) and dressing table (for cosmetics and hair accessories; 2012-59-1), this pairing represents the pinnacle of design, cabinetmaking, and carved ornament. The raised space underneath the high chest distinguishes it as a confection of North America since the British had abandoned that design by the 1730s. While most high chests and dressing tables fit within well-defined groups, the proportions and shaping of the rails and the legs of this pairing relate to only one other dressing table, making it difficult to identify the maker. The choice mahogany, with an even surface and swirling figure, signifies the status of the commission. And a highly skilled carver ornamented it profusely—most conspicuously with a narrative from Aesop’s fable of “The Fox and the Grapes” on the central bottom drawers. Aesop’s moralistic tales were the only fiction Quakers were allowed to read.
persona3_adapted: In the bedrooms of stylish early Philadelphia homes, it was common to find a tall chest for storing textiles and a dressing table for cosmetics and hair accessories. However, this particular set represents the highest level of design, furniture-making, and decorative carving. The open space beneath the tall chest marks it as a distinctly North American creation, since British furniture makers had stopped using that design by the 1730s. While most tall chests and dressing tables fall into clearly defined categories, the proportions and shaping of the horizontal bars and legs of this set match only one other known dressing table, making it difficult to identify the craftsman. The choice of high-quality mahogany, with its smooth surface and swirling grain pattern, signals the importance of the person who commissioned it. A highly skilled carver decorated the piece extensively—most notably with a scene from Aesop’s fable “The Fox and the Grapes” on the central bottom drawers. Aesop’s moral stories were the only fiction that Quakers were permitted to read.

CuratorialText: Pennsylvania-made Kleiderschranken (clothes presses) functioned, as did the earlier models in northern Europe, to hold hanging clothes and store cherished textiles. This schrank is notable for its extraordinary decoration—bold architectural paneling, carving, and lettering that records the name of its owner, Georg Huber of Manheim, Pennsylvania, and the date it was made, 1779, only a year before he married Barbara Oberholtzer. Rather than a lightwood inlay, the lettering and floral designs were made by pouring molten sulfur into channels carved in the walnut. Swiss and German designs inspired the decorative motifs, many of which are commonly found on other Pennsylvania German furniture as well as ceramics and fraktur, and helped to connect the owners and makers to their cultural roots. The wardrobe’s monumental size, bold composition, and profuse ornament demonstrate not only Huber’s aesthetic taste and wealth but also the skills and sensibilities of the Germanic artisans who made it.
persona3_adapted: Pennsylvania-made Kleiderschranken (a type of large wardrobe, or "clothes press") served the same purpose as earlier versions from northern Europe: to hold hanging garments and store treasured fabrics. This particular wardrobe stands out because of its remarkable decoration—bold, paneled designs that resemble architecture, intricate carving, and lettering that records the name of its owner, Georg Huber of Manheim, Pennsylvania, along with the year it was made, 1779, just one year before he married Barbara Oberholtzer. Instead of using lighter wood for the inlaid designs, the letters and floral patterns were created by pouring molten sulfur into channels carved into the walnut. The decorative motifs were inspired by Swiss and German designs, many of which appear frequently on other Pennsylvania German furniture, as well as on ceramics and a type of decorative folk art known as fraktur. These motifs helped connect the owners and makers to their cultural heritage. The wardrobe’s enormous size, bold arrangement of elements, and abundant ornamentation reveal not only Huber’s personal taste and wealth but also the skill and cultural sensibilities of the Germanic craftsmen who built it.

CuratorialText: Inscribed (translation): 1786 G. H. From the earth with sense the potter makes everything. Cadarina Raeder, her dish. German-speaking immigrants to Pennsylvania maintained the artistic traditions they brought from Europe, often retaining art forms and styles over several generations. This dish features decoration made using the centuries-old sgraffito technique, achieved by cutting through a slip coating to expose the red clay underneath. The verse in German around the border, extolling the skill of the potter, also shows an adherence to tradition: language provided an immediate means of holding onto ethnic identity. With such rich ornamentation, this dish was clearly not intended for use.
persona3_adapted: Inscribed (translation): 1786 G. H. From the earth with sense the potter makes everything. Cadarina Raeder, her dish. German-speaking immigrants to Pennsylvania kept alive the artistic traditions they brought from Europe, often preserving these art forms and styles across several generations. This dish is decorated using the centuries-old technique called sgraffito, which involves scratching through a thin layer of liquid clay (known as slip) to reveal the red clay beneath. The German verse around the border, which praises the potter's skill, also reflects a commitment to tradition: language offered an immediate way to hold onto ethnic identity. Given its elaborate ornamentation, this dish was clearly not meant for everyday use.

CuratorialText: In 1795 the Philadelphia artist Charles Willson Peale painted this illusionistic portrait to showcase his artistic skills. At the same time, he wished to represent the roles played by two of his sons at Peale’s Museum, the first American museum created to educate and entertain the public. Titian Ramsay Peale, who crafted natural science displays, gestures at the top of the stairs as his older brother, Raphaelle, an artist with his palette in hand, strides upward just above a real step attached to the base of the canvas. The life-size portrait is known to have startled and delighted visitors, reputedly even George Washington, before the Peale Museum’s collection was dismantled and finally sold in 1854. Acquired by the Philadelphia Museum of Art in 1945, the picture continues to engage and welcome visitors.
persona3_adapted: In 1795, the Philadelphia artist Charles Willson Peale painted this portrait, which creates a striking illusion of reality, to demonstrate his artistic abilities. At the same time, he wanted to show the different roles of two of his sons at Peale’s Museum, the first American museum created to both educate and entertain the public. Titian Ramsay Peale, who built displays of natural science, gestures from the top of the stairs, while his older brother, Raphaelle—an artist holding his paint palette—strides upward just above a real step attached to the bottom of the canvas. This life-size painting reportedly amazed and delighted visitors, even George Washington, before the Peale Museum’s collection was broken up and eventually sold in 1854. Acquired by the Philadelphia Museum of Art in 1945, the picture still draws in and welcomes visitors today.

CuratorialText: A rare early representation of ethnic and religious diversity in the United States, and an outstanding example of Peale’s late naturalistic style, the picture is distinguished by the direct and sympathetic encounter between the artist and his subject and a skilled rendering of details. Yarrow’s knit cap suggests a kufi, a hat traditionally worn by African Muslim men to assert their religion or African identity, but Peale artfully employed its yellow band to highlight his sitter’s steady gaze with its glint of humor and wisdom. Seventy-seven years old when he created this portrait, Peale was seeking to record the traits that he believed supported a long life. In his writings and museum displays, Peale celebrated making wise choices to maintain good health and a positive attitude. He perceived Yarrow’s perseverance in the face of racism and enslavement as a model of resourcefulness, industriousness, and sobriety.
persona3_adapted: A rare early depiction of ethnic and religious diversity in the United States, this painting is also an outstanding example of Peale’s later naturalistic style—meaning he aimed to portray his subjects with lifelike accuracy. What makes the work especially striking is the direct and sympathetic connection between the artist and his subject, along with Peale’s skillful attention to small details. Yarrow’s knit cap resembles a kufi, a hat traditionally worn by African Muslim men to express their religion or African identity. Peale cleverly used the cap’s yellow band to draw attention to his sitter’s steady gaze, which carries a hint of both humor and wisdom. At seventy-seven years old when he created this portrait, Peale was trying to capture the qualities he believed helped people live a long life. In his writings and museum displays, Peale celebrated the importance of making wise choices to maintain good health and a positive outlook. He saw Yarrow’s perseverance in the face of racism and enslavement as a model of resourcefulness, hard work, and self-discipline.

CuratorialText: This chair is part of the elegantly painted furniture Latrobe designed for the Walns' drawing room. Decorated in rich red, yellow, and gilt on a black ground and incorporating classical motifs and symbols, the set of drawing room furniture consisted of a pier table, Grecian sofas, a pair of card tables, window benches, and chairs. Its legs that curved inwards make the chair the earliest American example of a Klismos chair, a forerunner of the classical style that enjoyed widespread popularity in the late 1810s and 1820s. In addition to this chair from the Waln set, the Museum's collection includes six more chairs, a Grecian sofa, a card table, and a pier table with a mirrored back.
persona3_adapted: This chair is part of the elegantly painted furniture that Latrobe designed for the Walns' drawing room. The set is decorated in rich red, yellow, and gold on a black background, and it includes classical motifs and symbols—design elements inspired by ancient Greek and Roman art. The collection consisted of a pier table (a table designed to stand against a wall, often with a mirror), Grecian sofas (sofas with a curved, classical-inspired shape), a pair of card tables, window benches, and chairs. The chair’s legs curve inward, making it the earliest American example of a Klismos chair—a style of ancient Greek chair with curved legs that became a forerunner of the classical style widely popular in the late 1810s and 1820s. In addition to this chair from the Waln set, the Museum’s collection includes six more chairs, a Grecian sofa, a card table, and a pier table with a mirrored back.

CuratorialText:
The Gross Clinic is recognized as one of the greatest American paintings ever made. The young and little-known Eakins created it specifically for Philadelphia’s 1876 Centennial Exhibition, intending to showcase his talents as an artist and to honor the scientific achievements of his native Philadelphia.
Choosing the city’s world-famous surgeon and teacher Dr. Samuel Gross as his subject, Eakins sets the scene in Jefferson Medical College’s surgical amphitheater. Dr. Gross is shown leading a clinic of five doctors operating on the left thigh of a patient. At the same time Gross is demonstrating to students the relatively new surgical procedure he had developed to treat bone infections. In contrast to the recoiling woman to the left—traditionally identified as the patient’s mother—Gross embodies the confidence that comes from knowledge and experience. Casting himself as a witness, Eakins can be seen seated to the right of the tunnel railing, sketching or writing.
persona3_adapted:
The Gross Clinic is widely considered one of the most important American paintings ever created. The artist, Thomas Eakins, was young and not yet famous when he made this work. He painted it specifically for Philadelphia’s 1876 Centennial Exhibition, aiming to demonstrate his artistic skill and to celebrate the scientific accomplishments of his hometown.

Eakins chose as his subject Dr. Samuel Gross, a world-renowned surgeon and teacher from Philadelphia. The scene takes place in the surgical amphitheater of Jefferson Medical College—a room designed for students to observe operations. Dr. Gross is shown leading a team of five doctors as they operate on a patient’s left thigh. At the same time, he is teaching the students about a relatively new surgical technique he had developed to treat bone infections. On the left side of the painting, a woman recoils in distress—traditionally thought to be the patient’s mother. In contrast to her reaction, Dr. Gross embodies the calm confidence that comes from knowledge and experience. Eakins includes himself in the scene as a witness; he can be seen seated to the right of the railing, sketching or writing.

CuratorialText: The dramatic rescue from a foundering ship shown here was made possible by a recent innovation in lifesaving technology, the breeches buoy. Secured firmly to ship and shore, the device permitted the transfer of stranded passengers to safety by means of a pulley that was hauled back and forth by crews at either end. Cropped down to its essentials, Homer's composition thrusts us into the midst of the action with massive waves rolling past, drenching the semiconscious woman and her anonymous savior. The Life Line was immediately recognized by critics as a major contribution to American art, portraying a heroic, contemporary subject with both painterly virtuosity and detailed observation.
persona3_adapted: The dramatic rescue from a sinking ship shown here was made possible by a recent breakthrough in lifesaving technology: the breeches buoy—a device that looks like a ring buoy with a pair of short canvas pants attached. Secured by ropes to both the ship and the shore, it allowed stranded passengers to be moved to safety using a pulley system that crews pulled back and forth from each end. By cutting the scene down to its most essential elements, Homer’s composition plunges us directly into the action. Massive waves roll past, soaking the semiconscious woman and her anonymous rescuer. When it was first exhibited, *The Life Line* was immediately praised by critics as a landmark in American art. It portrays a heroic, contemporary subject with both impressive technical skill in painting and careful attention to realistic detail.

CuratorialText: Mary Cassatt’s flair for digging into an ordinary moment and distilling its emotions stands out in this portrayal of three individuals lulled by a carriage ride into silent reverie. Once titled simply Driving, the painting depicts an outing in the Bois de Boulogne, a large wooded park on the western edge of Paris. The driver of the carriage is Lydia Cassatt, sister of the artist, and the girl seated primly beside her is Odile Fèvre, niece of the painter Edgar Degas. The bored-looking groom is unidentified. Usually associated with domestic interiors and the private lives of women and children, here Cassatt explored an outdoor setting and a degree of autonomy enjoyed by upper-class women. Driving in the park was something the Cassatt sisters enjoyed, particularly after 1879, when the family acquired a small carriage and Bichette, the horse partly depicted here
persona3_adapted: Mary Cassatt had a gift for capturing an ordinary moment and drawing out its emotional depth—something that shines in this scene of three people quietly lost in thought during a carriage ride. Originally titled simply *Driving*, the painting shows an outing in the Bois de Boulogne, a large wooded park on the western edge of Paris. The driver is Lydia Cassatt, the artist’s sister, and the girl sitting neatly beside her is Odile Fèvre, the niece of the painter Edgar Degas. The bored-looking groom is not identified. While Cassatt is best known for scenes set inside homes and for exploring the private lives of women and children, here she turns to an outdoor setting and highlights a degree of freedom that upper-class women could enjoy. Driving in the park was something the Cassatt sisters especially liked after 1879, when their family bought a small carriage and Bichette, the horse partly visible in this painting.

CuratorialText: Tanner painted The Annunciation soon after returning to Paris from a trip to Egypt and Palestine in 1897. The son of a minister in the African Methodist Episcopal Church, Tanner specialized in religious subjects, and wanted to experience the people, culture, architecture, and light of the Holy Land. Influenced by what he saw, Tanner created an unconventional image of the moment when the angel Gabriel announces to Mary that she will bear the Son of God. Mary is shown as an adolescent dressed in rumpled Middle Eastern peasant clothing, without a halo or other holy attributes. Gabriel appears only as a shaft of light. Tanner entered this painting in the 1898 Paris Salon exhibition, after which it was bought for the Philadelphia Museum of Art in 1899, making it his first work to enter an American museum.
persona3_adapted: Tanner painted *The Annunciation* shortly after returning to Paris from a trip to Egypt and Palestine in 1897. The son of a minister in the African Methodist Episcopal Church, Tanner focused on religious subjects and wanted to experience the people, culture, architecture, and light of the Holy Land. Influenced by what he saw, Tanner created an unconventional image of the moment when the angel Gabriel tells Mary she will give birth to the Son of God. Mary is shown as a young teenager dressed in rumpled Middle Eastern peasant clothing, without a halo or other traditional signs of holiness. Gabriel appears only as a shaft of light. Tanner entered this painting in the 1898 Paris Salon, a major official art exhibition, after which it was bought for the Philadelphia Museum of Art in 1899, making it his first work to enter an American museum.

CuratorialText: A father’s love radiates from this iridescent painting of sunlight and youth. Shaped by the realist tradition of Thomas Eakins at the Pennsylvania Academy of the Fine Arts, Daniel Garber adopted Impressionist color and handling during a two-year sojourn in Europe. Returning in 1907, he settled into a farm along Cuttalossa Creek in Bucks County, Pennsylvania, where he harmoniously merged nature, art, and domestic life. In this portrait of his daughter, Tanis, light from the garden outside his studio renders her smock translucent, while the composition’s serene geometry captures the shimmer of an endless summer afternoon.
persona3_adapted: A father’s love shines through this luminous painting of sunlight and youth. Daniel Garber was influenced by the realistic style of Thomas Eakins at the Pennsylvania Academy of the Fine Arts, but he adopted the vivid colors and loose brushwork of Impressionism during a two-year stay in Europe. When he returned in 1907, he settled into a farm along Cuttalossa Creek in Bucks County, Pennsylvania, where he blended nature, art, and family life in a harmonious way. In this portrait of his daughter, Tanis, light from the garden outside his studio makes her smock appear almost see-through, while the calm arrangement of shapes and lines captures the glowing feel of an endless summer afternoon.

CuratorialText: Georgia O’Keeffe once remarked, “What is my experience of the flower if not color?” This painting of two calla lilies is an extraordinary example of her floral compositions, made of sweeping, broad waves of subtly blended hues. The white petals, highlighted in green, are arranged against a pink backdrop, and from each one emerges a bright yellow pistil. Many have interpreted O’Keeffe’s depictions of floral anatomy in relation to sexuality and gender, but the artist always resisted these interpretations, considering them too specific and limiting.
persona3_adapted: Georgia O’Keeffe once said, “What is my experience of the flower if not color?” This painting of two calla lilies is a remarkable example of her flower-based works, built from wide, flowing strokes of gently blended colors. The white petals, touched with green, stand out against a pink background, and from each flower rises a bright yellow pistil—the central part that holds the seeds. Many people have linked O’Keeffe’s close-up views of flowers to ideas about sexuality and gender, but the artist always rejected these readings, finding them too narrow and limiting.

CuratorialText: Wharton Esherick’s carved interiors are among his most important early sculptural work. Inspired by the angular shapes and intersecting lines of the Cubist movement, the artist incorporated the natural patterns and grains of assorted woods into his pieces to emphasize their strong geometric and organic forms. Born in Philadelphia in 1887, Esherick studied painting at the Pennsylvania Academy of the Fine Arts from 1908–10, and discovered wood as a medium for artistic expression when he began to make carved frames for his canvases. By the mid-1920s he was fully committed to sculpture and extended his definition of the medium to the carving of furniture and architectural interiors. His work is often seen as a forerunner to the contemporary studio furniture movement.
persona3_adapted: Wharton Esherick’s carved interiors rank among his most significant early works in sculpture. Inspired by the sharp angles and intersecting lines of Cubism—an early 20th-century art movement that broke objects into geometric shapes—Esherick used the natural patterns and grain lines of different types of wood to highlight the bold, geometric, and organic forms of his pieces. Born in Philadelphia in 1887, Esherick studied painting at the Pennsylvania Academy of the Fine Arts from 1908 to 1910. He first discovered wood as a material for artistic expression when he began carving frames for his own paintings. By the mid-1920s, he had fully committed to sculpture and expanded his understanding of the medium to include carving furniture and entire interior spaces. His work is often seen as an early influence on the modern studio furniture movement, in which artists design and build furniture as a form of artistic expression.

CuratorialText: Andrew Wyeth shared the realist approach of many mid-twentieth-century painters of the American scene, although his process of simplification and synthesis often led to haunting, surrealist effects. His picture of his neighbor Karl Kuerner’s light-filled kitchen holds an air of something missing. Wyeth explained that the painting was about “the winter light, the dishes ready for Karl Kuerner’s lunch—that is, peace, yes, but to me behind it is violence suppressed.” The snarling log outside the window, like the knife on the table, connected in the artist’s mind to Kuerner’s wartime experience, his violent dog, and his strong hand on the entire farm. Said Wyeth, “I wanted to get down to the very essence of the man who wasn’t there.”
persona3_adapted: Andrew Wyeth shared the realistic style of many mid-20th-century American painters who focused on everyday life in the United States. However, his way of simplifying and combining visual elements often created haunting, dreamlike effects. His painting of his neighbor Karl Kuerner’s sunlit kitchen holds a sense that something is absent. Wyeth explained that the work was about “the winter light, the dishes ready for Karl Kuerner’s lunch—that is, peace, yes, but to me behind it is violence suppressed.” The snarling log seen through the window, like the knife on the table, were linked in the artist’s mind to Kuerner’s wartime experiences, his aggressive dog, and his firm control over the entire farm. Wyeth said, “I wanted to get down to the very essence of the man who wasn’t there.”

CuratorialText: In November 1943 this painting was included in Jackson Pollock's stunning solo exhibition at Art of This Century, the innovative New York art gallery run by legendary art patron Peggy Guggenheim. As its title suggests, the work depicts a male figure, embodied in the black columnar form at right, with its mysterious arithmetic graffiti, and a curvy female figure at left, with marvelous catlike eyelashes. Standing on tiny triangular feet, both figures are surrounded by splashed and smeared oil paint that enhances the sense of energy inherent in the two vertical forms, while also foreshadowing the famous "drip" technique that Pollock would develop later that decade.
persona3_adapted: In November 1943, this painting was featured in Jackson Pollock’s striking solo exhibition at Art of This Century, an avant-garde New York gallery run by the legendary art collector and supporter Peggy Guggenheim. As the title suggests, the work shows a male figure, represented by the black column-like shape on the right, marked with mysterious, number-like scribbles, and a curvy female figure on the left, with remarkable cat-like eyelashes. Both figures stand on tiny triangular feet and are surrounded by splashed and smeared oil paint, which heightens the feeling of energy coming from the two upright forms. This energetic use of paint also hints at the famous "drip" method Pollock would develop later in the 1940s.

CuratorialText: Here you will find Benjamin Franklin as you’ve never seen him before. Philadelphia-born artist Roberto Lugo riffs on the traditional representations of Franklin found throughout the city—ones that celebrate him as one of the Founding Fathers of the United States. You can see one such stately portrayal of Franklin directly behind you, in gallery 384. Lugo places Franklin within a constellation of songs, symbols, and rappers’ names—ones that shaped his childhood and adult life. The artist also includes his own self-portrait on the other side of the vase, creating an imagined dialogue between himself and Franklin around the themes of money, history, violence, and success.

(You can see one such dignified portrait of Franklin directly behind you, in gallery 384.) The artist also includes his own self-portrait on the other side of the vase, creating an imagined conversation between himself and Franklin about money, history, violence, and success.

CuratorialText: Florine Stettheimer set this scene like a stage, framed by a grand red staircase at left and a long red drape mimicking a theater curtain at right. The depicted customers of Bendel’s, the famous Manhattan department store, lack the decorum and manners expected of women in this period. Instead, they frantically dive for clearance items, change clothes, inspect garments, and examine their mirrored reflections. Stettheimer lets us see "behind the curtain" to observe these women during a moment of unabashed liberation. The action-packed canvas contains an astonishing thirty-two people and two dogs, including the Pekingese at the forefront who serves as a surrogate for the artist, as indicated by the "FS"-monogrammed sweater. Henri Bendel, the store’s queer owner, stands in the left foreground. Opposite is an androgynous salesperson, whose appearance and pose—slicked-back platinum hair, rosy lips, and delicate hand gesture—align with how white gay men often presented themselves at the time.
persona3_adapted: Florine Stettheimer designed this scene like a stage, with a grand red staircase on the left and a long red curtain that suggests a theater drape on the right. The shoppers shown at Bendel’s, the famous Manhattan department store, do not behave with the restraint and good manners expected of women at the time. Instead, they rush eagerly for sale items, change clothes, inspect garments, and study their own reflections in the mirrors. The lively painting includes an astonishing thirty-two people and two dogs—among them the Pekingese in the foreground, who stands in for the artist, as shown by the sweater with the initials "FS." Henri Bendel, the store’s queer owner, appears in the left foreground. Across from him is an androgynous salesperson, whose appearance and pose—slicked-back platinum hair, rosy lips, and a delicate hand gesture—match the way many white gay men of the time presented themselves.

CuratorialText: Three female immortals wear cloaks and skirts made of grasses and leaves. Their hairy bodies identify them as the Feathered Women (mao nü) of Daoist lore, who live deep in the mountains searching for herbs. Here they show each other what they have gathered. The figure at the far left holds a lingzhi fungus (thought to bestow longevity if ingested) and a basket of plants is draped over her arm. The focus of the painting is on a large health-benefiting ginseng root displayed in the hand of the central immortal. Other herbs depicted include leaves of banana and palm tree carried in the baskets on the women’s backs. The representation of Feathered Women exhibiting precious medicinal items functions as a special talisman, protecting the owner from illness.
persona3_adapted: Three female immortal beings wear cloaks and skirts made of grasses and leaves. Here they are showing each other what they have gathered. The figure on the far left holds a lingzhi fungus, a type of mushroom believed to grant long life if eaten, and a basket of plants hangs from her arm. The painting's main focus is a large ginseng root, known for its health benefits, displayed in the hand of the central immortal. Other plants shown include banana and palm leaves carried in the baskets on the women's backs. The depiction of Feathered Women presenting these precious medicinal items serves as a special charm, meant to protect the owner from illness.

CuratorialText:
This delicate porcelain lantern is so light in the hand that it feels almost weightless. Candles placed within it would have glowed and flickered through the intricate lattice designs, which required great skill to achieve due to the extremely thin clay. It was not until the early 1700s that artists developed this piercing technique.

Auspicious Decoration

The four flowers within the central medallions on the six sides—the camellia, rose, peony, and lily—convey peace, unity, and longevity.
