Candles placed inside would have glowed and flickered through the intricate, lace-like patterns cut into the clay. Achieving these designs required great skill, as the clay had to be extremely thin. Artists did not develop this technique of piercing the clay until the early 1700s.

Auspicious Decoration

The four flowers inside the central oval frames on each of the six sides—the camellia, rose, peony, and lily—symbolize peace, unity, and a long life.

CuratorialText:
Various deva, or guardian deities, are depicted on this fragment from the outer section of a large mural that once decorated the hall of a Buddhist temple. Murals representing such figures, richly dressed in costumes and adorned with jewelry, are typical of northern Chinese temples built from the late Yuan (1271–1368) to the early Ming dynasties (1368–1644), when the ruling elite embraced a mix of Chinese and Tibetan Buddhism. Although there is some loss of color, we can identify several of the deities:
(Far left) The guardian of Buddhist temples and protector of the faith, Weituo (Skanda in Sanskrit), wears a winged helmet. His diamond sword destroys demons and Buddhism’s enemies.
(Center) The Heavenly King of the West brandishes a snake, while the Heavenly King of the North holds an umbrella and stupa/pagoda.
(Right) The protective goddess Mārīcī has three faces—one frontal and two in profile—and multiple arms.
persona3_adapted:
This fragment comes from a large mural that once decorated the hall of a Buddhist temple. It shows various *deva*—a term for guardian deities or divine beings in Buddhist tradition. These figures, richly dressed in elaborate costumes and adorned with jewelry, are typical of northern Chinese temples built from the late Yuan dynasty (1271–1368) to the early Ming dynasty (1368–1644). During this period, the ruling elite embraced a blend of Chinese and Tibetan Buddhist practices. Although some of the original colors have faded, we can still identify several of the deities:

(Far left) Weituo (known as Skanda in Sanskrit), the guardian of Buddhist temples and protector of the faith, wears a winged helmet. He holds a diamond sword, which is said to destroy demons and enemies of Buddhism.

(Center) The Heavenly King of the West brandishes a snake, while the Heavenly King of the North holds an umbrella and a *stupa*—a dome-shaped structure that serves as a Buddhist shrine or reliquary, sometimes also called a pagoda.

(Right) The protective goddess Mārīcī is shown with three faces—one facing forward and two in profile—and multiple arms, emphasizing her supernatural power.

CuratorialText: The Zhihua monastery is one of the great Buddhist temple compounds in the Chinese capital of Beijing. Conceived on a grand scale, the compound was built in the early 1400s by the eunuch Wang Zhen. This ceiling comes from the second of five main halls on the monastery's central axis—the Hall of Great Wisdom. To give visitors a sense of the original context, the Museum installed the ceiling with architectural elements reconstructed from measured drawings made at the original site. In the center is a writhing imperial dragon surrounded by clouds, bracketing, and eight canted panels, each bearing a smaller dragon among clouds. Lotuses, apsaras (Buddhist flying musicians), and other Buddhist religious symbols are carved in the surrounding panels. The Chinese name for this central part of the ceiling is tianjing, or "well of heaven." While most of the original red lacquer is well preserved, much of the rich overlay of gold leaf has been lost. A series of model temples supported by cloud-decorated brackets encompass the central element; within these miniature structures are delicately carved sculptures of Buddhas and bodhisattvas. The remainder of the ceiling consists of square panels painted with stylized lotus flowers that show the influence of Tibetan Buddhism (Lamaism). Each bears a character from a North Indian script (called Lantsha by the Tibetans) that symbolically recreates the universe. The model temples and panels may have been added during repairs made in the Kangxi period (1662–1722).
persona3_adapted:
The Zhihua monastery is one of the great Buddhist temple complexes in Beijing, China. Built on a grand scale in the early 1400s by the eunuch Wang Zhen, the compound originally consisted of five main halls arranged along a central axis. This ceiling comes from the second of those halls, known as the Hall of Great Wisdom. To help visitors imagine how it originally appeared, the Museum has installed the ceiling alongside architectural elements reconstructed from detailed drawings made at the original site.

At the center of the ceiling is a twisting imperial dragon surrounded by clouds, structural brackets, and eight angled panels, each featuring a smaller dragon among clouds. Lotuses, apsaras (Buddhist flying musicians), and other Buddhist religious symbols are carved into the surrounding panels. The Chinese name for this central section is *tianjing*, or "well of heaven." While most of the original red lacquer is well preserved, much of the rich gold leaf that once covered it has worn away.

Around this central element is a series of model temples supported by cloud-decorated brackets. Inside these miniature structures are delicately carved sculptures of Buddhas and bodhisattvas (enlightened beings who delay their own salvation to help others). The rest of the ceiling is made up of square panels painted with stylized lotus flowers, reflecting the influence of Tibetan Buddhism (also called Lamaism). Each panel bears a character from a North Indian script (known as Lantsha to Tibetans), which symbolically recreates the universe. The model temples and panels may have been added during repairs made in the Kangxi period (1662–1722).

CuratorialText: Educator and collector Ernest Fenollosa installed this monumental painting by Kano Hōgai in his home in Tokyo, where it held pride of place. The artist’s highly naturalistic rendering of intertwined dragons reflects a fusion of East and West. While the subject of dragons dates back to ancient China, the three-dimensionality and foreshortening of the animals’ forms reflect Hōgai’s familiarity with the Western use of perspective.
persona3_adapted: Educator and collector Ernest Fenollosa installed this monumental painting by Kano Hōgai in his home in Tokyo, where it held a place of honor. The artist’s highly realistic depiction of intertwined dragons reflects a blend of Eastern and Western influences. While the subject of dragons has roots in ancient China, the three-dimensional quality and the way the animals’ forms appear to recede into space show Hōgai’s familiarity with the Western use of perspective.

CuratorialText: Fifty-eight sixteenth-century granite architectural elements are presented within the museum gallery in an arrangement designed to resemble a South Indian temple hall (mandapam). In 1913 these pieces lay discarded at the small Madanagopalaswamy Temple in Madurai, where a Philadelphia couple purchased them while on their two-year-long honeymoon. But recent research supports that the majority were created for the large Kudal Alagar Temple nearby. There they had been part of a shrine to the goddess Lakshmi, wife of the Hindu deity Vishnu. This shrine had become dilapidated, and the temple authorities dismantled it by 1907, completing its replacement by 1923. Stone slabs between the lion brackets depict several scenes from the Ramayana, the epic tale of one of Vishnu's earthly incarnations. They once formed parts of a carved frieze showing the entire story. The life-size figures projecting from pillars are deities and mythological characters, including from the Ramayana. The many small images on the pillars include gods and heroes as well as humans (like an architect with his measuring stick).
persona3_adapted: Fifty-eight granite architectural elements from the sixteenth century are displayed in the museum gallery, arranged to resemble a South Indian temple hall (known as a *mandapam*). In 1913, these pieces were lying discarded at the small Madanagopalaswamy Temple in Madurai, where a Philadelphia couple purchased them during their two-year honeymoon. However, recent research suggests that most of these elements were originally created for the large Kudal Alagar Temple nearby. There, they had been part of a shrine dedicated to the goddess Lakshmi, the wife of the Hindu deity Vishnu. That shrine had fallen into disrepair, and temple authorities took it apart by 1907, completing its replacement by 1923. Stone slabs placed between the lion-shaped supports show several scenes from the *Ramayana*, the epic story of one of Vishnu's earthly incarnations. These slabs were once part of a carved band (a frieze) that depicted the entire tale. The life-sized figures projecting from the pillars represent gods, goddesses, and mythological characters, including figures from the *Ramayana*. The many small images carved on the pillars include gods, heroes, and even humans—such as an architect holding his measuring stick.

CuratorialText: Enslaved Black and free white craftsmen worked side by side across the southern landscape.  Over the past thirty years researchers at MESDA have identified nearly 6500 craftsmen who enslaved people.  In fact, this fact is among the most common things we know about an identified craftsman.  Often anonymous, those who were enslaved by craftsmen were often at work on building sites, at the potters’ wheels, in cabinet shops, at iron foundries, behind the counters of silversmiths’ shops, and at dozens of other trades.  Their skills are revealed in the objects that they helped to fashion.  Their identities are often lost to history. A line in a legal document, or a brief mention in an advertisement is often all we know about an enslaved craftsman – but careful reading of period documents by MESDA researchers has identified nearly 3000 enslaved craftsmen by name.  The exhibition “Black and White all Mix’d Together”: The Hidden Legacy of Enslaved Craftsmen looks at icons of southern decorative arts through the lens of research conducted by MESDA over more than thirty years.  It recovers the stories of the many named and nameless individuals whose hidden hands shaped the southern landscape.
persona3_adapted: Enslaved Black and free white craftsmen worked side by side across the southern landscape. Over the past thirty years, researchers at MESDA have identified nearly 6,500 craftsmen who enslaved people. In fact, this is one of the most common facts we know about any identified craftsman. Often anonymous, those who were enslaved by craftsmen typically labored on building sites, at potters’ wheels, in cabinet-making workshops, at iron foundries, behind the counters of silversmiths’ shops, and in dozens of other trades. Their skills are revealed in the objects they helped to create. Their identities, however, are often lost to history. A line in a legal document or a brief mention in an advertisement is frequently all we know about an enslaved craftsman—but through careful reading of historical documents, MESDA researchers have identified nearly 3,000 enslaved craftsmen by name. The exhibition “Black and White all Mix’d Together”: The Hidden Legacy of Enslaved Craftsmen examines iconic works of southern decorative art through the lens of more than thirty years of MESDA research. It recovers the stories of the many named and unnamed individuals whose hidden hands shaped the southern landscape.

CuratorialText: North Carolina’s Piedmont region is known around the world for the manufacture of fine furniture.  However, long before the factories of Lexington, Thomasville, Hickory, and High Point came into existence, the Piedmont was home to a diverse group of cabinetmakers who crafted distinctive regional furniture. By the middle of the eighteenth century, immigrants from Pennsylvania, Maryland, and Virginia began pouring into the North Carolina Piedmont in search of inexpensive yet fertile land.  By 1800 about 250,000 Scotch-Irish, English, and German settlers called the region home.  Cabinetmakers immediately utilized the abundant walnut, cherry, pine and poplar trees to produce well-made, stylish furniture for their homes.  In most cases, these craftsmen combined national trends with local idiosyncratic preferences to produce regional furniture with a distinctive style and appearance. This exhibit is drawn from MESDA’s extensive collection of Piedmont North Carolina furniture made between 1780 and 1860.
persona3_adapted: North Carolina’s Piedmont region is famous worldwide for producing high-quality furniture. But long before the furniture factories in towns like Lexington, Thomasville, Hickory, and High Point were established, the Piedmont was home to a diverse group of skilled woodworkers—called cabinetmakers—who created furniture with a distinct regional character. Starting in the mid-1700s, immigrants from Pennsylvania, Maryland, and Virginia began moving into the North Carolina Piedmont, drawn by the promise of affordable, fertile land. By 1800, roughly 250,000 settlers of Scotch-Irish, English, and German descent lived in the area. These cabinetmakers made immediate use of the region’s abundant walnut, cherry, pine, and poplar trees to build sturdy, stylish furniture for their homes. Typically, they blended national design trends with local tastes and preferences, resulting in furniture that had a unique look and feel. This exhibit features pieces from MESDA’s extensive collection of Piedmont North Carolina furniture, all made between 1780 and 1860.

CuratorialText: Masterfully decorated slipware, sculptural bottles, refined creamware, and faience are but part of the rich artistic legacy of North Carolina’s first earthenware potters. During the last half of the eighteenth century, artisans of European descent introduced a variety of Old World ceramics traditions to the Carolina backcountry. From both an artistic and technical standpoint, the work of these craftsmen often surpassed that of their Middle Atlantic and New England contemporaries. North Carolina potters transformed the simplest of materials into vessels of practical utility, astonishing beauty, and deep cultural significance. This exhibit is the first major survey of this work an the context in which it was created.
persona3_adapted: Masterfully decorated pottery with a glossy finish, sculptural bottles, refined creamware (a type of smooth, light-colored earthenware), and faience (a tin-glazed earthenware often painted with colorful designs) are just part of the rich artistic legacy of North Carolina’s first earthenware potters. During the last half of the eighteenth century, artisans of European descent introduced a variety of Old World ceramics traditions to the Carolina backcountry. From both an artistic and technical standpoint, the work of these craftsmen often surpassed that of their Middle Atlantic and New England contemporaries. North Carolina potters transformed the simplest of materials into vessels of practical utility, astonishing beauty, and deep cultural significance. This exhibit is the first major survey of this work and the context in which it was created.

CuratorialText: Salem was the largest of North Carolina’s Moravian communities and from the moment of arrival the earliest settlers documented their landscape with a variety of detailed maps, surveys, drawings, and paintings. Salem was founded in 1766 as a congregation town with the Church overseeing all activities—financial, social, and spiritual. The Church owned all land and leased it to individuals who owned only the improvements. Salem was a bustling trade center with a mill, pottery, tannery, brewery, and store. But the earliest landscapes emphasize the centrality of the Church. They depict the town’s conscious design around a central square, the heart of the community. The square was home to the Gemein Haus (and later Home Church), where religious services were held; the large communal dwellings for Single Brothers and Single Sisters; the schools for young boys and girls; and the store. Gardens and fields appear nestled in and around the most prominent buildings in early views, suggesting the town’s growing success both agriculturally and commercially. As the nineteenth century progressed, the residents lobbied for more personal control. In 1849, the Church agreed to sell land to create the neighboring town of Winston, and in 1856 it abandoned the lease-hold system in favor of private land ownership. Manufacturing enterprises grew, and the railroad’s arrival in 1873 set the stage for tremendous growth in local industries. Salem—once closed to outsiders—became closely intertwined with the town of Winston. The textile and tobacco industries flourished, and by 1900 the twin cities of Winston and Salem had become the largest metropolitan area in North Carolina.

Founded in 1766, Salem was designed as a congregation town, meaning the Church oversaw all aspects of life—financial, social, and spiritual. The Church owned all the land and leased it to individuals, who owned only the buildings and other improvements they made. Salem was a busy trade center, with a mill, pottery, tannery, brewery, and store. Yet the earliest images of the town emphasize the central role of the Church. These pictures show that the town was deliberately arranged around a central square, which served as the heart of the community. The square housed the Gemein Haus (and later the Home Church), where religious services were held; the large communal residences for Single Brothers and Single Sisters; the schools for young boys and girls; and the store. In these early views, gardens and fields appear nestled among the most prominent buildings, suggesting the town’s growing success in both agriculture and commerce. As the nineteenth century progressed, residents pushed for more personal control. In 1849, the Church agreed to sell land to create the neighboring town of Winston, and in 1856 it abandoned the lease-hold system in favor of private land ownership. Manufacturing businesses grew, and the arrival of the railroad in 1873 set the stage for tremendous growth in local industries. Salem—once closed to outsiders—became closely linked with the town of Winston. The textile and tobacco industries flourished, and by 1900 the twin cities of Winston and Salem had become the largest metropolitan area in North Carolina.

CuratorialText:
James River Valley pottery has been the subject of archaeological and collector interest for over half a century.  The James River and its tributaries were home to more than a dozen potteries before the Civil War.  Potters hailing from New York, New Jersey, Pennsylvania, Maryland, Ireland and England, created a rich  hybrid tradition with forms and decorations adapted from their regional styles and wrought in local clays dug from Kaolin to Claymount.

Transcending their status as utilitarian objects, these pots, many exhibited here for the first time, provide a remarkable record and a unique glimpse into a significant and long-lived concentration of pottery manufacturing in eastern Virginia.  Viewed collectively, they present a bold decorative aesthetic rivaling long-recognized and celebrated schools of production seen elsewhere in the commonwealth and the Nation.
Today, many of these important historic sites are threatened by development.  Fortunately, archaeologists and decorative arts scholars are working ahead of the bulldozers to document and understand the important legacy of the James River Potteries before they are lost forever.  At the end of 2013 the Chipstone Foundation journal Ceramics in America will publish an extensive article about the James River Valley stoneware potteries based on this ongoing research.
This exhibition was on view at MESDA from November 2012 through March 2012. It was co-curated by Kurt Russ, Oliver Mueller-Heubach, Marshall
persona3_adapted:
For over fifty years, archaeologists and collectors have studied the pottery made in the James River Valley. Before the Civil War, more than a dozen potteries operated along the James River and its smaller connecting rivers. The potters came from New York, New Jersey, Pennsylvania, Maryland, Ireland, and England. They created a rich, blended tradition, adapting shapes and decorative styles from their home regions and using local clays—from Kaolin to Claymount.

These pots go beyond being simple household tools. Many are displayed here for the first time, and together they offer a remarkable record and a unique window into a major, long-lasting pottery industry in eastern Virginia. Seen as a group, they show a bold decorative style that rivals the better-known and celebrated pottery traditions found elsewhere in Virginia and across the country.

Today, many of these important historical sites are threatened by new construction. Fortunately, archaeologists and scholars who study decorative arts are working ahead of the bulldozers to document and understand the lasting legacy of the James River Potteries before it is lost forever. At the end of 2013, the Chipstone Foundation’s journal *Ceramics in America* will publish a detailed article about the James River Valley stoneware potteries, based on this ongoing research.

This exhibition was on view at MESDA from November 2012 through March 2012. It was co-curated by Kurt Russ, Oliver Mueller-Heubach, and Marshall.

CuratorialText:
An Irish schoolmaster. A German Jewish silversmith. An African Muslim scholar. A multi-national female pastelist. A Scottish cabinetmaker. A potter, a carpenter, an engraver. The lives of these individuals share a common thread: they were “lately from” somewhere else and came to the American South in the 18th and early 19th centuries.

This exhibition features objects created by these craftspeople whose origins spanned continents and oceans. As much as possible, it connects those objects with the words of their makers through letters, inscriptions, autobiographies, account books, advertisements, and even drawings. Together, they tell the story of journeys taken, communities forged in new homes, responses to changing political climates, and the creative blending of traditions into something new.
persona3_adapted:
An Irish schoolmaster. A German Jewish silversmith. An African Muslim scholar. A multi-national female pastelist—an artist who worked with soft, colored chalk. A Scottish cabinetmaker. A potter, a carpenter, an engraver. The lives of these individuals share a common thread: they were “lately from” somewhere else and came to the American South in the 1700s and early 1800s.

This exhibition features objects created by these skilled workers, whose origins spanned continents and oceans. Where possible, it connects those objects with the makers’ own words—through letters, personal inscriptions, autobiographies, financial records, advertisements, and even drawings. Together, these materials tell the story of journeys taken, communities built in new homes, responses to shifting political conditions, and the creative mixing of traditions into something new.

CuratorialText: Early Southerners drank a lot — nearly twenty gallons of alcohol each — every year. They drank when they woke up in the morning, as they ate and worked during the day, and when they socialized at night. But how people drank was as important as what they drank. From imported Madeira sipped at mahogany tables to local whisky out of redware jugs, this exhibit explores the decorative arts that defined the early Southern drinker.
persona3_adapted: Early Southerners drank a great deal—nearly twenty gallons of alcohol per person each year. They drank when they woke up in the morning, while eating and working during the day, and when they socialized at night. But how people drank mattered just as much as what they drank. This exhibit explores the beautifully crafted objects—from imported Madeira wine served at polished mahogany tables to local whiskey poured from handmade red clay jugs—that defined the drinking culture of the early American South.

CuratorialText: this exhibit in MESDA’s Whitehall Gallery brings together rare maps from the 18th and early 19th centuries to explore how maps were made, used, and did work in the early American South. Rivers of Ink, Mountains of Type: Maps at Work in the Early American South includes maps from the MESDA and Old Salem collections as well as major loans from private collections, Salem Congregation, and the Moravian Archives of the Southern Province. The exhibit includes two early surveys of the Wachovia Tract made by Andreas Höger; a rare first-state copy of Joshua Fry and Peter Jefferson’s map of Virginia; two copies of Lewis Evans’s General Map of the Middle British Colonies produced at Ben Franklin’s press in Philadelphia; and surveys by George Washington of lands he owned along the Ohio River.
persona3_adapted: This exhibit in MESDA’s Whitehall Gallery brings together rare maps from the 1700s and early 1800s to explore how maps were made, used, and actively shaped life in the early American South. *Rivers of Ink, Mountains of Type: Maps at Work in the Early American South* includes maps from the MESDA and Old Salem collections, as well as major loans from private collections, Salem Congregation, and the Moravian Archives of the Southern Province. Among the highlights are two early surveys of the Wachovia Tract created by Andreas Höger; a rare first printing of Joshua Fry and Peter Jefferson’s map of Virginia; two copies of Lewis Evans’s *General Map of the Middle British Colonies*, produced at Benjamin Franklin’s press in Philadelphia; and surveys by George Washington of lands he owned along the Ohio River.

CuratorialText: Alabama’s pottery traditions reflect the distinctive combination of people “thrown together” across its landscape. This exhibition explores the human hands that made, held, and preserved these objects. Across the present-day state of Alabama, Indigenous people including the Muscogee (Creek), Choctaw, Cherokee, and Chickasaw, practiced ceramic traditions brought by their ancestors into the region over 10,000 years ago. Colonial and American settlers were both a threat and a market for these craftspeople, who produced hybrid wares that meshed traditional techniques and designs that appealed to these foreign consumers. The geography of Alabama influenced how and where diverse potters and people came together. A tail of the Appalachian Mountains runs from south to north across the middle of the state. Most of the 19th century potters north of the mountains came from Tennessee, Virginia, and the Ohio River Valley. South of the mountains, the majority arrived from Georgia or South Carolina. The Gulf Coast was the site of early interactions between French, Spanish and British colonists and the Indigenous people of Alabama. Whether they came from the north, east, or across the Atlantic, immigrants brought with them enslaved people of African descent, including skilled ceramists. Together, their hands shaped the clay of the American South.
persona3_adapted: Alabama’s pottery traditions reflect the unique blend of people brought together across its landscape. This exhibition focuses on the human hands that created, used, and preserved these objects. Across what is now the state of Alabama, Indigenous groups—including the Muscogee (Creek), Choctaw, Cherokee, and Chickasaw—continued ceramic traditions that their ancestors had brought to the region more than 10,000 years ago. Colonial and American settlers posed both a threat and a source of demand for these craftspeople, who produced hybrid wares that combined traditional techniques and designs with features that appealed to these foreign buyers. Alabama’s geography shaped how and where different potters and communities came together. A spur of the Appalachian Mountains runs from south to north through the middle of the state. Most 19th-century potters living north of these mountains came from Tennessee, Virginia, and the Ohio River Valley. South of the mountains, the majority arrived from Georgia or South Carolina. The Gulf Coast was an early meeting point for French, Spanish, and British colonists and the Indigenous peoples of Alabama. Whether they came from the north, east, or across the Atlantic, immigrants brought with them enslaved people of African descent, including skilled ceramic makers. Together, their hands shaped the clay of the American South.

CuratorialText:
The Carolyn and Mike McNamara Gallery at the Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts (MESDA) exhibits some of the furniture, ceramics, paintings, prints, metalwork, and textiles from the early American South.  From fine art to folk art, these objects tell the stories of the diverse craftsmen and consumers who called the South home for more than four centuries.

The early South is usually divided into three major cultural regions: the Chesapeake, the Lowcountry, and the Backcountry.  The rivers, roads, and natural resources of these regions defined their economic and settlement patterns.  Each was home to a unique blend of ethnicities and religions: Native Americans, British Anglicans and Quakers, Scotch-Irish Presbyterians, French Huguenots, Moravians, German Lutherans and Reformed, and free and enslaved peoples of African descent.  The dynamic interactions between and among these distinct groups flavored the decorative arts of the early South.
persona3_adapted:
The Carolyn and Mike McNamara Gallery at the Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts (MESDA) displays furniture, ceramics, paintings, prints, metalwork, and textiles from the early American South. Ranging from fine art to folk art, these objects reveal the stories of the diverse makers and buyers who called the South home for more than four centuries.

The early South is typically divided into three major cultural regions: the Chesapeake, the Lowcountry, and the Backcountry. The rivers, roads, and natural resources of these areas shaped their economies and where people settled. Each region was home to a distinct mix of ethnicities and religions: Native Americans, British Anglicans and Quakers, Scotch-Irish Presbyterians, French Huguenots, Moravians, German Lutherans and Reformed Christians, as well as free and enslaved people of African descent. The lively interactions among these different groups gave a unique character to the decorative arts—the functional and ornamental objects made by skilled craftspeople—of the early South.

CuratorialText:
The William C. and Susan S. Mariner Southern Ceramics Gallery is home to the most comprehensive collection of antebellum pottery made in Maryland, Virginia, the Carolinas, Georgia, Kentucky, and Tennessee.

Among the decorative arts, pottery is the one media that is most intimately connected to place.  The southern pottery in this gallery reflects the interplay between maker, consumer, culture, and geography.  It is a tangible reflection of how potters responded to specific cultural needs to craft vessels out of local materials that were both useful and beautiful.

This virtual gallery offers two ways to explore the southern ceramics on exhibit in the Mariner Gallery.  The first is through a series of virtual galleries curated by the noted ceramics scholar Robert Hunter, Editor of Ceramics in America that explore important themes—tradition, design, expression, purpose, faith, war, and freedom—as seen through the lens of southern pottery.  You can also browse the objects in the Mariner Gallery by media: earthenware, salt-glazed stoneware, and alkaline glazed stoneware.  Within these pages you are also able to filter the objects by maker, place, and time
persona3_adapted:
The William C. and Susan S. Mariner Southern Ceramics Gallery holds the largest collection of pottery made in the American South before the Civil War, specifically from Maryland, Virginia, the Carolinas, Georgia, Kentucky, and Tennessee.

Among household objects made by hand, pottery is the type most closely tied to its region of origin. The southern pottery in this gallery shows how makers, buyers, culture, and local geography all influenced one another. These objects are physical evidence of how potters responded to specific community needs, shaping local materials into containers that were both practical and attractive.

This virtual gallery offers two ways to explore the southern pottery on display in the Mariner Gallery. The first is through a series of online exhibits curated by Robert Hunter, a well-known ceramics scholar and editor of *Ceramics in America*. These exhibits explore important themes—tradition, design, expression, purpose, faith, war, and freedom—as seen through the lens of southern pottery. You can also browse the objects in the Mariner Gallery by material type: earthenware (pottery fired at lower temperatures), salt-glazed stoneware (a hard, waterproof ceramic with a textured finish), and alkaline glazed stoneware (a type of stoneware with a glossy, often greenish surface). Within these pages, you can also filter the objects by maker, location, and time period.

CuratorialText: The Thomas A. Gray Rare Book and Manuscript Collection is home to rare and important works about the early American South. The collection represents a lifetime of connoisseurship for Tom Gray, a former director of the Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts and cousin of MESDA founder Frank L. Horton. What began as an collection focused on Tom’s home state of North Carolina gradually expanded to include important texts from all seven of MESDA’s southern states. With books and manuscripts spanning four centuries – from 1585 to 1865 – the Gray collection is a perfect complement to the MESDA and Old Salem collections.  Books and manuscripts from the collection are exhibited in the MESDA galleries and are available to researchers.  A selection of highlights are shown below.
persona3_adapted: The Thomas A. Gray Rare Book and Manuscript Collection holds rare and historically significant works about the early American South. The collection reflects a lifetime of careful, expert selection—what specialists call connoisseurship—by Tom Gray, a former director of the Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts and a cousin of the museum’s founder, Frank L. Horton. What began as a collection focused on Gray’s home state of North Carolina gradually expanded to include important texts from all seven southern states that the museum covers. With books and manuscripts spanning four centuries—from 1585 to 1865—the Gray collection perfectly complements the museum’s and Old Salem’s holdings. Items from the collection are displayed in the museum’s galleries and are also available for researchers. A selection of highlights is shown below.

CuratorialText: MESDA’s collection includes architectural woodwork collected from houses in Maryland, Virginia, North Carolina, South Carolina, and Georgia.  This collection was originally assembled by Frank L. Horton to serve as backdrops for period room installations.  Today, in addition to providing scale and context for our study galleries, MESDA’s architectural collections are also studied as important Southern objects in their own right that that can tell stories of style, craftsmanship, migration, ethnicity, and race.
persona3_adapted: MESDA’s collection includes wooden architectural elements—such as doors, moldings, and paneling—salvaged from houses in Maryland, Virginia, North Carolina, South Carolina, and Georgia. These pieces were originally gathered by Frank L. Horton to serve as the setting for rooms decorated in period style. Today, beyond giving a sense of scale and historical context to our study galleries, these architectural objects are studied as significant Southern artifacts in their own right. They offer insights into changing tastes, skilled workmanship, patterns of migration, and the roles of ethnicity and race in the region’s history.

CuratorialText: Old Salem Museums & Gardens and its Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts are home to one of country’s most important collections of early Southern longrifles, swords, and related objects.  Longrifles were some of the most complicated objects crafted in the early American South.  Artistic masterworks, they required craftsmen skilled in woodworking, silversmithing, ironworking, and the mechanical arts.  The rich tradition of early Southern rifle making is kept alive today by the talented craftsmen of the Timothy Vogler Gun Shop, located directly across the street from the Frank L. Horton Museum Center at Old Salem Museums & Gardens.
persona3_adapted: Old Salem Museums & Gardens and its Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts house one of the country’s most significant collections of early Southern longrifles, swords, and related objects. Longrifles were among the most complex items ever made in the early American South. These artistic masterpieces demanded craftsmen skilled in woodworking, silversmithing, ironworking, and the mechanical arts. The rich tradition of early Southern rifle making continues today through the talented artisans at the Timothy Vogler Gun Shop, located directly across the street from the Frank L. Horton Museum Center at Old Salem Museums & Gardens.

CuratorialText: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts and Old Salem Museums & Gardens is home to an exceptional collection of Southern bedcoverings including quilts and coverlets.  The collection also has a significant number of textiles made and used by the women of Salem, North Carolina.
persona3_adapted: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts and Old Salem Museums & Gardens houses an outstanding collection of Southern bedcoverings, including quilts and woven coverlets. The collection also contains a notable group of textiles that were made and used by the women of Salem, North Carolina.

CuratorialText: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts at Old Salem Museums & Gardens is home to an extensive collection of rare books and manuscripts prints that document the people and places of the early American south.  Many of these books and manuscripts are part of the Thomas A. Gray Rare Book and Manuscript Collection.
persona3_adapted: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts at Old Salem Museums & Gardens holds a large collection of rare books and handwritten documents, as well as prints, that record the lives and locations of people in the early American South. Many of these books and manuscripts belong to the Thomas A. Gray Rare Book and Manuscript Collection.

CuratorialText: MESDA is home to an extensive collection of southern brass, copper, and pewter.
persona3_adapted: MESDA houses a large collection of metalwork from the American South, including objects made of brass, copper, and pewter.

CuratorialText: Old Salem Museums & Gardens, its Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts, and The William C. and Susan S. Mariner Southern Ceramics Collection are home to the most comprehensive collection of Southern ceramics in existence.
persona3_adapted: Old Salem Museums & Gardens, along with its Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts and The William C. and Susan S. Mariner Southern Ceramics Collection, holds the largest and most complete collection of pottery and porcelain made in the American South that exists today.

CuratorialText: MESDA is home to a collection of clocks, surveyors compasses, and sundials that span all seven of our constituent states.
persona3_adapted: MESDA houses a collection of clocks, surveyors' compasses, and sundials from all seven of the states that make up our region.

CuratorialText: German speaking communities throughout the South created richly illuminated documents celebrating births, baptisms, marriages, and deaths.
persona3_adapted: German-speaking communities across the American South created elaborately decorated documents to celebrate life events such as births, baptisms, marriages, and deaths.

CuratorialText: For over forty years the Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts has collected many of the finest examples of southern-made furniture.  These objects demonstrate how the early South evolved, inform us about the diverse peoples who came here, and illuminate the artisans who were integral to the process. Whether they were English, Scottish, Irish, German, African, or French, Southern cabinetmakers utilized their materials at hand, both native and imported, to create furniture that speaks to us about their heterogeneous backgrounds and the multifaceted aspects of their everyday lives. These craftsmen contributed to the dynamic amalgamation of art, culture, and history that forms the basis for MESDA’s ongoing study of the early South.
persona3_adapted: For over forty years, the Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts has gathered many of the finest examples of furniture made in the American South. These objects show how the early South developed over time, tell us about the many different groups of people who came to the region, and highlight the skilled makers who were essential to that process. Whether they were English, Scottish, Irish, German, African, or French, Southern furniture makers used the materials available to them—both local and imported—to create pieces that reveal their diverse backgrounds and the many different aspects of their daily lives. These craftspeople contributed to a rich and ever-changing blend of art, culture, and history that forms the foundation of MESDA’s ongoing research into the early South.

CuratorialText: MESDA’s collection of iron represents the work of furnaces and blacksmiths across all seven southern states.
persona3_adapted: MESDA’s collection of iron objects showcases the work of furnaces and blacksmiths throughout all seven southern states.

CuratorialText: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts at Old Salem Museums & Gardens is home to one of the most important collections of southern of maps.  On the surface maps appear scientific and authoritative, useful devices for governmental officials and colonial expansionists to claim ownership of land and exert power and authority over competing nationalities.  Below the surface, however, maps often provide tremendous insights into a myriad of social, political, and economic forces that shaped Southern culture.
persona3_adapted: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts at Old Salem Museums & Gardens holds one of the most significant collections of southern maps. At first glance, maps seem like scientific and reliable tools—useful for government officials and colonial expansionists to claim land and assert power over rival nations. But beneath this surface, maps often reveal deep insights into the many social, political, and economic forces that helped shape Southern culture.

CuratorialText: Music is an important part of Southern culture.  In Salem, North Carolina, music was intrgal to Moravian worship and daily life.  Salem had its own orchestra by the 1780s and brass bands played regularly as part of religious services and to announce births and deaths in the community.  Old Salem Museums & Gardens collection of musical instruments and related materials reflects the important role played by music in the community.  Other objects in the collection reflect musical craftsmanship elsewhere in the American South.
persona3_adapted: Music is an essential part of life in the American South. In Salem, North Carolina, music was deeply woven into both the religious practices and everyday routines of the Moravian community. By the 1780s, Salem had its own orchestra, and brass bands performed regularly—not only during church services but also to mark important life events, such as births and deaths. The collection of musical instruments and related items at Old Salem Museums & Gardens highlights how central music was to this community. Other objects in the collection also showcase the skill of instrument makers and musicians elsewhere in the American South.

CuratorialText: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts has led the way in researching and collecting work by Southern artisans, allowing us to better understand the rich cultural complexity of artistic production in the South. While it is true that with only a handful of significant urban centers, fewer painters settled and remained in the South, the painting collection at MESDA ably demonstrates the diversity of training and styles with works by European-trained and native-born artists living in urban and rural settings
persona3_adapted: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts has been a pioneer in studying and collecting work by Southern craftspeople, helping us better understand the rich cultural complexity of art-making in the South. It is true that, with only a few major cities, fewer painters chose to settle and stay in the region. However, the museum’s painting collection clearly shows a wide range of training and artistic approaches, featuring works by both European-trained and American-born artists who lived in cities and rural areas alike.

CuratorialText: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts at Old Salem Museums & Gardens is home to an extensive collection of prints that document the people and places of the early American south.  These views documented English perceptions of America and served as vehicles to convey a host of attitudes and values and provide tremendous insights into a myriad of social, political, and economic forces that shaped Southern culture.
persona3_adapted: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts at Old Salem Museums & Gardens holds a large collection of prints—images made by transferring ink from a carved surface onto paper—that record the people and places of the early American South. These images captured how the English saw America and acted as tools for expressing a range of beliefs and values. They offer deep understanding of the many social, political, and economic forces that helped shape Southern culture.

CuratorialText: Pastel portrait of Frances L’Escott. Half-length pastel portrait of young woman with body turned slightly to the right and head slightly to the left. She has auburn hair, brown eyes, with loosely fitted ecru and blue gown. The original wood frame retains its original spruce back with the following inscriptions: “Henrietta Johnston Fecit/ South Carolina Ano 1716.” “I/ Frances Lescott/ Married Peter Villep—.”
persona3_adapted: Pastel portrait of Frances L’Escott. This is a half-length pastel drawing of a young woman, her body angled slightly to the right and her head turned a bit to the left. She has auburn hair and brown eyes, and wears a loosely fitted gown in ecru and blue. The original wooden frame still has its original spruce backing, which bears the following handwritten notes: “Henrietta Johnston Fecit/ South Carolina Ano 1716.” and “I/ Frances Lescott/ Married Peter Villep—.”

CuratorialText: Portrait of man, bust length, seated in a red upholstered chair, body half-left, eyes and head forward, brown hair, blue-gray eyes, ruddy complexion, wearing a black coat, yellow vest and white stock and tie, brass buttons. The background is a greenish-gray.
persona3_adapted: This portrait shows a man from the chest up, seated in a red upholstered chair. His body is turned slightly to the left, but he faces the viewer directly. He has brown hair, blue-gray eyes, and a ruddy complexion. He wears a black coat, a yellow vest, and a white stock and tie, with brass buttons visible. The background is a muted greenish-gray.

CuratorialText: Portrait of man in brown coat and yellow vest with white stock. He has blue-gray eyes and dark hair. He is a half-length figure, facing half left, posed with hand in his vest. He stands in front of a column and what must be a balustraded porch with green trees and a greenish ground in the distance.
persona3_adapted: This portrait shows a man wearing a brown coat, a yellow vest, and a white neckcloth. He has blue-gray eyes and dark hair. The painting captures him from the waist up, his body turned slightly to the left, with one hand tucked into his vest. He stands before a column, and behind him is what appears to be a porch with a railing, with green trees and a greenish landscape stretching into the distance.

CuratorialText: Portrait of a woman seated in a Chippendale type chair, half-length, facing forward and dressed with a white high crowned cap, gray dress and white neck piece with a black ribbon and jewel at her throat. She has brown eyes, dark hair. She holds a reddish-brown book in her right hand and is seated at a column base to the right, with red drapery and tassel, and with a balustraded rail and trees in the background to the left.
persona3_adapted: This portrait shows a woman seated in a chair designed in the Chippendale style—a type of ornate, mid-18th-century English furniture. She is shown from the waist up, facing the viewer directly. She wears a tall white cap, a gray dress, and a white collar or neckpiece, accented with a black ribbon and a jewel at her throat. Her eyes are brown, and her hair is dark. In her right hand, she holds a reddish-brown book. She sits beside the base of a column on the right side of the image. Behind her, on the left, there is red fabric with a decorative tassel, and in the background, a railing and trees are visible.

CuratorialText: Full length portrait of a man in blue uniform with red trim at lapel and cuffs, white vest and breeches with black boots trimmed with gold at the top, white epaulets and neck piece, and black tircorner hat decorated with silver cord. He is posed with sword in right upraised hand and a dagger-like knife in the left, facing half-left, with a tree at either side to frame a fight between American troops and Indians in the background. The soldiers in the back wear round hats, short blue jackets with red facings, belly boxes, and linen overalls. The retreating Indians to the left rear of the painting appear with topknots or scalp locks, and wear battle dress of breechcloths and leggings.
persona3_adapted: This is a full-length portrait of a man dressed in a blue military uniform with red trim at the collar and cuffs. He wears a white vest and breeches, black boots with gold trim at the top, white shoulder ornaments (epaulets), a white neckpiece, and a black three-cornered hat decorated with silver cord. He stands facing slightly to the left, holding a sword raised in his right hand and a dagger-like knife in his left. Trees on either side frame the scene, while in the background, a fight unfolds between American soldiers and Native Americans. The soldiers in the back wear round hats, short blue jackets with red trim, cartridge boxes worn on the belt (belly boxes), and linen trousers. On the left side of the painting, the retreating Native American figures have topknots or scalp locks and wear battle attire consisting of loincloths (breechcloths) and leggings.

CuratorialText: Charcoal drawing of the profile of Joseph Lewis, Jr., facing left on pink paper. The drawing shows a gentleman with light hair tied at the back with dark ribbon who is wearing a dark high coat with a white stock. The painting is mounted in the original gold frame with black and gold painted glass.
persona3_adapted: Charcoal drawing on pink paper of Joseph Lewis, Jr., shown in profile facing left. The subject is a gentleman with light hair tied back with a dark ribbon, wearing a dark high-collared coat and a white neckcloth. The work is displayed in its original gold frame, which features black and gold painted glass.

CuratorialText: Watercolor on paper of a plantation with an inscription at the base of the painting. The house is a white frame two-story central hall structure with its chimneys on the rear elevation. A fence runs in front of the house with a bridge to the right in the background, indicating an inlet of water. An outbuilding stands behind some trees standing behind the bridge. Several large trees are located in the left foreground, along with some moss, grass and other foliage. A path leads to the house, and two men, one mounted upon a horse, the other standing below him are talking to one another. A body of water is in the background.
persona3_adapted: A watercolor painting on paper depicts a plantation, with a label written at the bottom of the artwork. The main house is a two-story white wooden building, with a central hallway and chimneys located at the back. A fence stretches across the front of the house, and in the background to the right, a bridge suggests the presence of a narrow inlet of water. Behind the bridge, an outbuilding is partially hidden by trees. In the lower left foreground, several large trees appear alongside moss, grass, and other plants. A path leads up to the house, where two men are speaking with one another—one seated on a horse, the other standing beside him. A body of water can be seen in the background.

CuratorialText: Charcoal drawing, profile of man, facing right, on faded pink paper. The gentleman wears the uniform of an officer, a dark coat with light facings with buttons at the outside of long buttonholes on the lapel and collar, and epaulets of gold bullion. A black inner collar is over a white stock. The man’s hair is graying and is tied in a knot at the back with a black ribbon.
persona3_adapted: A charcoal drawing on faded pink paper shows a man in profile, facing to the right. He is dressed in an officer’s uniform: a dark coat with lighter trim, buttons placed along the outer edges of long buttonholes on the lapel and collar, and gold-threaded shoulder ornaments called epaulets. A black inner collar sits above a white neckcloth, known as a stock. The man’s graying hair is pulled back and tied in a knot with a black ribbon.

CuratorialText: Oil on canvas portrait of a gentleman with dark eyes, dark complexion and a shaven head, who is facing half-right with hands folded resting on a table . His right hand holds a quill pen and the left is holding a document with a name written thereon. He wears a white loose collar shirt with deep red robe and turban of the same red material, and sits in a chair upholstered in a red-brown fabric with brass tacks. The background is a dark green drapery to the left and books on the right.
persona3_adapted: This oil painting on canvas shows a gentleman with dark eyes, a dark complexion, and a shaven head. He faces slightly to the right, with his hands folded and resting on a table. In his right hand, he holds a quill pen, and in his left, a document with a name written on it. He wears a loose white collared shirt, a deep red robe, and a turban made from the same red fabric. He sits in a chair upholstered in a red-brown material, decorated with brass tacks. The background features a dark green curtain on the left side and a stack of books on the right.

CuratorialText: Bust portrait, oil on canvas, of woman wearing a pearl necklace and blue dress with lace trim, in mahogany architectural frame with cut-out corners.
persona3_adapted: A painted portrait, shown from the chest up, of a woman wearing a pearl necklace and a blue dress with lace trim. The work is done in oil on canvas and is set in a dark reddish-brown wooden frame with decorative cut-out corners.

CuratorialText: Painting, oil on canvas, of Richard Sprigg of Strawberry Hill, Maryland. Young man facing forward, his body is slightly to the right, his left hand in his vest, wearing a dark beige coat and light silk vest, with white stock. He has a ruddy complexion, dark eyes, brown hair. The background varies from greenish-gray to the lower right to very dark gray in upper left and has lunettes in the corners.
persona3_adapted: Portrait of Richard Sprigg of Strawberry Hill, Maryland, painted in oil on canvas. The young man faces forward, his body turned slightly to the right, with his left hand tucked into his vest. He wears a dark beige coat and a light silk vest, with a white stock—a type of neckcloth—around his neck. His complexion is ruddy, with dark eyes and brown hair. The background shifts from a greenish-gray in the lower right to a very dark gray in the upper left, and features small, arched, window-like shapes—called lunettes—in the corners.

CuratorialText: Oil on canvas portrait of a woman facing half-right. The subject has dark hair combed upward to expose her left ear, and pearls in her hair that hang down over her right shoulder. She wears a blue silk cross-bodice dress tied with a dark red sash. The pinned-up sleeves of the garment revealing a light under-garment. She is posed with her left arm upon a table, the right at her hip, and foliage painted in the background.
persona3_adapted: Oil on canvas portrait of a woman, turned slightly to the right. Her dark hair is swept upward, revealing her left ear, and pearls woven into her hair fall over her right shoulder. She wears a blue silk dress with a crisscross bodice, fastened with a dark red sash. The sleeves are pinned up, exposing a lighter garment beneath. She rests her left arm on a table, her right hand on her hip, with foliage painted in the background.

CuratorialText: A watercolor “en grisaille” of Williamsville, a home in Hanover County, Virginia. The house was built between 1794 and 1803 for William Pollard, II, the second Clerk of Court of Hanover County. It passed to his son George William Pollard in 1840 and remained in the Pollard family until after the death of Harry Todd Pollard, the amateur artist who drew and signed the watercolor.
persona3_adapted: A watercolor painting in shades of gray—a technique known as "grisaille"—depicts Williamsville, a home in Hanover County, Virginia. The house was built between 1794 and 1803 for William Pollard, II, who served as the second Clerk of Court of Hanover County. The property was inherited by his son, George William Pollard, in 1840, and stayed within the Pollard family until after the death of Harry Todd Pollard, the amateur artist who drew and signed this watercolor.

CuratorialText: Painting, done in oil on mahogany panel, of a young man facing half-right. The gentleman has dark hair and side-burns, and is dressed in a white shirt, tie and vest, and a black coat supporting a high collar. There is an inscription on the back of the panel.
persona3_adapted: This oil painting on a mahogany panel shows a young man facing slightly to the right. He has dark hair and sideburns, and wears a white shirt, tie, and vest, with a black coat and a high collar. An inscription is written on the back of the panel.

CuratorialText: Oil on canvas painting of a man facing half-left. The work is a half-length portrait. The subject has gray hair, dark gray eyes, wears a dark brown coat with a high turned-down collar, a yellow vest, and white stock at the neck.
persona3_adapted: Oil on canvas painting of a man facing slightly to the left. The work shows him from the chest up. He has gray hair and dark gray eyes, and wears a dark brown coat with a high, folded-down collar, a yellow vest, and a white neckcloth.

CuratorialText: This painting depicts a bend in the Cumberland River near the Hermitage, the home of Andrew Jackson (1767-1845). It was a scene that the artist returned to regularly. Another version of this composition is in the collection of the Greenville County Museum of Art.
persona3_adapted: This painting shows a bend in the Cumberland River near the Hermitage, the home of Andrew Jackson (1767–1845). The artist returned to this scene many times, and a similar version of the work is held in the collection of the Greenville County Museum of Art.

CuratorialText: Painting, oil on canvas, of Charles Town showing the city waterfront as seen from across the Cooper River. A large sailing ship is in the left foreground, the opposite shore in foreground with various figures along the shore.
persona3_adapted: Painting, oil on canvas, depicting the waterfront of Charles Town as seen from across the Cooper River. In the left foreground, a large sailing ship is visible, while the opposite shore appears in the foreground with several figures along the water's edge.

CuratorialText: Painting, oil on canvas, waist-length portrait of a lady wearing a gray satin dress with gray stomacher in front and white lace at neck and sleeves, her hair is pulled tightly back, a loop of pearls in it on the left side of the portrait. Her right hand holds a pink rose.
persona3_adapted: A waist-length portrait of a woman, painted in oil on canvas. She wears a gray satin dress with a matching panel at the front, known as a stomacher, and white lace at the neck and sleeves. Her hair is pulled tightly back, with a loop of pearls on the left side of the portrait. In her right hand, she holds a pink rose.

CuratorialText: Painting, oil on canvas, of a man facing half-left and seated in a green painted chair. The man has a high foreheard, dark hair, dark blue eyes, ruddy complexion, and is wearing a dark coat mounted with gray buttons, a white stock, and a yellow vest. A red and pearl pin is on his neck cloth. The painting is signed in the lower left corner.
persona3_adapted: This oil painting on canvas shows a man seated in a green chair, his body turned slightly to the left. He has a high forehead, dark hair, dark blue eyes, and a ruddy complexion. He wears a dark coat with gray buttons, a white neckcloth, and a yellow vest. A pin combining red and pearl tones is fastened to his neckcloth. The artist’s signature appears in the lower left corner.
